name: Banded Crunch Isometric Hold
description: The banded crunch isometric hold is an abdominal exercise where you maintain a crunch position against constant band resistance, emphasizing time under tension for the rectus abdominis.
instructions: Lie on your back with the banded crunch device or resistance band anchored overhead. Lift your shoulders off the ground, curling up towards your knees, and hold the position. Maintain a stable posture and engage your core, keeping constant tension on the band. Hold for the desired time, typically 30-60 seconds, while breathing naturally.

name: Banded Crunch
description: The banded crunch is an abdominal exercise where a resistance band provides added load and continuous tension through spinal flexion, emphasizing the rectus abdominis (six-pack muscles).
instructions: Anchor the resistance band at a stable point above you. Lie on your back with the band attached to your shoulders or chest, knees bent and feet flat. Lift your shoulders off the ground, curling up towards your knees, keeping the band taut. Slowly lower back down to the starting position, maintaining control throughout the movement.

name: Crunch
description: The crunch is a core exercise targeting the rectus abdominis and obliques. Lying on your back with knees bent and feet on the floor, you lift your shoulders and upper back off the ground by bracing and shortening the abs, then lower under control. It can be performed for time or reps as part of an ab-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet on the floor. Lift your shoulders and upper back off the ground by bracing and shortening your abs. Lower your shoulders back down to the starting position under control. Keep your lower back pressed into the floor throughout the movement.

name: Decline Band Press Sit-Up
description: The decline band press sit-up is a core-focused strength exercise performed on a decline surface while pressing resistance bands. It primarily targets the rectus abdominis and deep core muscles, with additional engagement of the shoulders and upper back. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions as part of a core segment in a workout.
instructions: Sit on a decline bench with feet secured. Hold a resistance band in each hand, with the band anchored above your head. Press the band forward as you sit up, keeping your core engaged and shoulders down. Lower your body back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Band Low-to-High Twist
description: The band low-to-high twist is a rotational core exercise that targets the upper abdominals and obliques. Using a resistance band anchored low, you rotate your torso diagonally upward with control, avoiding rapid or jerky twisting to protect the spine. It is typically performed for 8-15 controlled reps per side as part of a core-focused workout.
instructions: Anchor the resistance band at a low point. Hold the band with both hands and twist your torso diagonally upward, keeping your arms straight. Rotate with control, avoiding jerky movements, and focus on using your core muscles. Return to the starting position slowly and repeat on the other side.

name: Barbell Roll-Out
description: The barbell roll-out is an advanced core stability exercise where you kneel on the floor, grip a barbell loaded with rotating collars, and slowly roll it forward while maintaining a rigid, braced torso. The movement heavily challenges the abdominals and entire anterior core as you extend and then pull the barbell back under your shoulders. It is more difficult than most ab-wheel variations and often requires building up with regressions before performing full reps from the knees or standing.
instructions: Kneel on the floor with toes tucked under and grip the barbell with rotating collars. Maintain a rigid, braced torso and slowly roll the barbell forward, extending your body. Keep your core muscles engaged and control the movement as you roll out and then pull the barbell back under your shoulders. Lower your body slowly and deliberately to avoid injury.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Ab Rollout - On Knees
description: The barbell ab rollout from the knees is a core-strengthening exercise where you kneel on the floor, grip a barbell with rotating collars, and slowly roll it forward while maintaining a tight, braced torso, then pull back to the start using your abdominal muscles. It heavily challenges the abs and overall core stability and is more difficult than most standard ab roller variations.
instructions: Kneel on the floor with your hands gripping a barbell with rotating collars. Roll the barbell forward slowly, keeping your torso tight and braced. Lower your body until your arms are fully extended, then pull the barbell back to the starting position using your abdominal muscles. Keep your core engaged throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Bar Press Sit-Up
description: The decline bar press sit-up is a weighted core exercise performed on a decline surface while holding a barbell. As you perform a sit-up, you press the barbell upward, heavily engaging the abs with emphasis on the lower abs, while also involving the shoulders and upper back to stabilize and move the weight. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions as part of a core-focused strength routine.
instructions: Sit on a decline bench with your feet secured. Hold a barbell over your chest with both hands. Perform a sit-up, lifting your torso off the bench, and press the barbell upward, extending your arms fully. Lower your torso back down to the starting position, maintaining control throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bench Barbell Roll-Out
description: The bench barbell roll-out is a core-strengthening exercise performed with the knees on a bench and hands gripping a barbell on the floor. From a kneeling position, you roll the barbell forward under control, extending your hips and shoulders while keeping the abs braced, then pull back to the start using your core. Elevating the knees on a bench increases upper-body demand compared with a standard floor roll-out.
instructions: Kneel on a bench with your hands gripping a barbell on the floor. Roll the barbell forward under control, extending your hips and shoulders while keeping your abs braced. Keep your core tight and use it to pull the barbell back to the starting position. Lower your body slowly and deliberately to maintain control throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Side Bend
description: Stand upright holding a barbell across your upper back and shoulders. With your core braced and hips facing forward, slowly bend sideways at the waist, lowering the barbell toward one side while keeping your torso in the same plane. Pause briefly at a comfortable stretch, then use your obliques to return to the starting position. Repeat on the other side, avoiding twisting or excessive range of motion.
instructions: Stand upright with a barbell across your upper back and shoulders. Bend sideways at the waist, lowering the barbell to one side while keeping your torso straight. Pause briefly, then use your obliques to return to the starting position. Repeat on the other side, keeping your core braced and hips facing forward.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated Bar Twist
description: The seated bar twist is a core exercise targeting the obliques by rotating the torso while holding a light bar across the shoulders. Performed for higher repetitions with an empty barbell or light bar, it helps build rotational core strength and resiliency and can be added during or at the end of a workout.
instructions: Sit on floor with feet flat, holding a light bar across shoulders. Twist torso to left, keeping feet and hips facing forward, then return to center. Repeat on right side, twisting torso to right while maintaining proper form. Continue alternating sides for desired number of repetitions.

name: Single-arm Landmine Pull and Press
description: An explosive rotational barbell exercise using a landmine setup. Starting from the floor, you pull the bar up with one arm while rotating through the hips and torso, then transition into an overhead or diagonal press. Emphasis is on speed, control, and core-driven rotation rather than maximal load.
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the bar with one arm, keeping core engaged and body straight. Pull the bar up, rotating through hips and torso, then press it overhead or diagonally, emphasizing speed and control. Lower the bar back to the starting position, maintaining core stability throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Plate Sit-Up
description: A weighted core exercise performed on a decline surface while holding a plate (or similar load) to target the rectus abdominis and deep core muscles through a larger range of motion than a standard floor sit-up.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with your knees bent and feet secured. Hold a weight plate across your chest or at your shoulders. Slowly lift your torso up towards your knees, curling up and squeezing your abdominal muscles. Lower your torso back down to the starting position in a controlled manner.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Advanced Kettlebell Windmill
description: A kettlebell windmill variation that targets the core and shoulders by holding a kettlebell overhead while hinging at the hips and rotating the torso to lower the opposite hand toward the floor, emphasizing oblique and shoulder stability.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell overhead with one hand, standing tall with feet shoulder-width apart. Hinge at the hips, keeping the kettlebell overhead, and rotate your torso to lower the opposite hand toward the floor. Keep your core engaged and focus on controlled movement, avoiding jerky motions. Return to the starting position, squeezing your core and shoulders to maintain stability.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Windmill
description: The kettlebell windmill is a dynamic single-kettlebell exercise that targets the core while challenging shoulder and hip mobility and stability. From a standing position with the kettlebell locked out overhead, you hinge at the hips and rotate to lower your torso toward the floor, then return to standing under control. It is best used for functional strength and mobility work rather than high-fatigue conditioning.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell overhead with arm locked. Hinge at the hips and rotate your torso to lower toward the floor, keeping the kettlebell overhead. Keep your core engaged and control the movement as you lower and return to standing. Return to the starting position, maintaining stability and balance throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Pass Between The Legs
description: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in one hand. Hinge slightly at the hips and pass the kettlebell between your legs from one hand to the other in a controlled circular pattern, keeping your core braced and torso stable throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in one hand. Hinge slightly at the hips, keeping your core braced and torso stable. Pass the kettlebell between your legs from one hand to the other in a controlled circular pattern. Keep the movement slow and controlled, focusing on maintaining good posture throughout.

name: Kettlebell 3-Point Leg Extension
description: The kettlebell 3-point leg extension is a dynamic core exercise where you extend your legs around and over a kettlebell placed between them, moving in three directions to challenge the abs and obliques. It resembles a combination of a V-up and reverse crunch, emphasizing control through the midsection while the legs trace a path over the kettlebell without touching it.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and a kettlebell between your legs. Lift your shoulders off the ground and extend your legs straight up towards the ceiling. Move your legs in a circular motion around the kettlebell, tracing a path in three directions without touching it, keeping your core engaged and controlled throughout the movement. Lower your legs back down to the starting position, maintaining control and avoiding any jerky motions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Double Kettlebell Swing
description: A hip-dominant explosive movement where you swing two kettlebells between your legs and up to chest height using powerful hip extension, engaging the core and posterior chain.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand. Hinge hips and swing kettlebells back between legs, keeping arms straight. Explosively drive hips forward, swinging kettlebells up to chest height, squeezing glutes and engaging core. Control the kettlebells as they swing back down, repeating the motion for desired reps.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Burpee Over Kettlebell
description: The burpee over kettlebell is an advanced conditioning variation where you perform a standard burpee and then laterally jump over a kettlebell. It increases lower-body power, core engagement, and overall athleticism while adding a novel stimulus to traditional burpees.
instructions: Start in a standing position with a kettlebell on the ground beside you. Drop down into a squat position and place your hands on the ground, then kick your feet back into a plank position. Perform a push-up, then quickly return your feet to the squat position and stand up, before laterally jumping over the kettlebell. Land softly on both feet and immediately repeat the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Toe-Touch
description: A weighted abdominal exercise where you lie on your back, hold a kettlebell with straight arms above your chest, and lift your legs and torso to touch the kettlebell to your toes, increasing core demand compared to the bodyweight version.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat. Hold a kettlebell with straight arms above your chest. Lift your legs and torso, keeping arms straight, to touch the kettlebell to your toes. Lower back down to the starting position, controlling the movement to engage your core muscles.

name: Kettlebell Swing
description: The kettlebell swing is a hip-hinge power exercise that targets the posterior chain, building explosive strength in the hamstrings and glutes while also engaging the back and core for stability. It can be used for strength, power, or cardiovascular conditioning.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly outward, and kettlebell on ground between feet. Hinge hips and grab kettlebell handle with both hands, keeping back straight and core engaged. Swing kettlebell back between legs, then up to chest height, using hips and legs to generate power, not arms. Control the swing and repeat, maintaining a smooth and explosive motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Two-Way Kettlebell Swing
description: The two-way kettlebell swing alternates a chest-height (Russian) swing with an overhead (American) swing. Both variations emphasize a powerful hip hinge to drive the kettlebell, targeting the hamstrings, glutes, and core, while the overhead swing increases demand on the shoulders and upper back. Perform for time or reps, maintaining a neutral spine and strong core. If overhead swings cause shoulder or lower back discomfort, stick to chest-height swings only.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell with both hands. Hinge your hips to swing the kettlebell back between your legs, then drive your hips forward to swing it up to chest height. Alternate with an overhead swing, swinging the kettlebell up to full extension above your head, keeping your core strong and spine neutral. Return to the starting position, repeating the sequence and switching between chest-height and overhead swings.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Crab Reach
description: A kettlebell pressing exercise performed from a modified single-arm bridge (crab) position, pressing the kettlebell overhead while stabilizing through the shoulders, back, glutes, and core.
instructions: Start in a modified single-arm bridge position with one hand and both feet on the ground. Hold a kettlebell in the other hand, with arm bent and kettlebell at shoulder height. Press the kettlebell straight up over your shoulder, extending your arm fully. Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Taylor Single-Arm Kettlebell Swing
description: A dynamic hip-hinge exercise where you swing a kettlebell with one arm between the legs and up to chest height, emphasizing explosive hip drive and core stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in one hand. Hinge hips and swing the kettlebell back between legs, keeping arm straight. Explosively drive hips forward to swing the kettlebell up to chest height, squeezing core and keeping arm straight. Lower the kettlebell back between legs, repeating the motion with control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Kettlebell Up to Elbow Down
description: An intermediate kettlebell core exercise where you move from a lying or reclined position up to the elbow and back down, emphasizing controlled trunk rotation and core stability.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat. Hold a kettlebell in one hand with arm extended overhead. Lift your torso up to elbow height, rotating your trunk and keeping your core tight, then slowly lower back down to the starting position. Repeat on the other side to maintain balanced strength.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Double-Kettlebell Ski
description: From a hip-width stance, hinge at the hips while holding a kettlebell in each hand at your sides. With a strong, braced core, swing both kettlebells back between your legs, then drive your hips forward explosively to swing them up to about chest height, keeping your arms relaxed and torso stable. Control the swing back down and repeat, maintaining a neutral spine and using your hips and core to power the movement rather than your arms.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand at your sides. Hinge at the hips and swing the kettlebells back between your legs, keeping your core strong and braced. Drive your hips forward to swing the kettlebells up to chest height, keeping your arms relaxed and torso stable. Control the swing back down, repeating the movement and using your hips and core to power it.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell V-Sit Cross Jab
description: The dumbbell V-sit cross jab is a hybrid core and upper-body exercise performed in a V-sit position while punching across the body with dumbbells. It targets the abs and obliques while also engaging the shoulders and arms, making it useful for time-efficient strength and conditioning circuits with a cardiovascular component.
instructions: Sit in a V-sit position with feet lifted off the ground. Hold a dumbbell in each hand, with arms extended and palms facing each other. Punch one arm across your body, keeping the other arm stable, then quickly alternate with the other arm. Repeat, controlling the movement and keeping your core engaged throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Spell Caster
description: The dumbbell spell caster is a rotational core exercise performed with light dumbbells, targeting the abs and obliques while also engaging the shoulders, back, hips, and legs in a coordinated, full-body motion. It is typically done for moderate to high reps per side, focusing on control and smooth rotation rather than heavy loading.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, engaging core and keeping back straight. Rotate torso from side to side, keeping arms straight and letting dumbbells guide the motion, focusing on smooth and controlled movement. Switch directions after completing the desired number of reps on each side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Side Bend
description: The dumbbell side bend is an oblique-focused exercise performed standing with a dumbbell in one hand, bending the torso sideways toward the weight and then returning to an upright position. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions and can be performed one side at a time or alternating sides.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in one hand. Bend your torso sideways toward the weight, keeping your arms straight and core engaged. Lower yourself until you feel a stretch in your obliques, then return to an upright position. Repeat on the other side by switching the dumbbell to your opposite hand.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Crunch
description: The dumbbell crunch is an intermediate abdominal exercise where a dumbbell is held to increase resistance during a standard crunch, providing a greater strength and hypertrophy stimulus to the core.
instructions: Lie on a flat surface with knees bent and feet planted. Hold a dumbbell close to your chest. Lift your shoulders off the ground, curling up towards your knees, then lower back down to the starting position. Breathe naturally and avoid straining your neck.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Fix Dumbbell Sprawl
description: From a standing position holding dumbbells at your sides, hinge at the hips and place the dumbbells on the floor. Jump or step your feet back into a high plank while gripping the dumbbells, keeping your core braced and body in a straight line. Jump or step your feet back toward your hands, then stand up explosively, returning to the starting position. Repeat for the desired reps while maintaining a tight core and neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with dumbbells at your sides. Hinge at the hips and place the dumbbells on the floor, then jump or step your feet back into a high plank position. Jump or step your feet back toward your hands, then stand up explosively to the starting position. Keep your core braced and body in a straight line throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Fix Alternating Renegade Row
description: From a high plank position with hands gripping dumbbells under the shoulders and feet set wide for stability, brace your core and keep your body in a straight line from head to heels. Row one dumbbell toward your ribcage by driving the elbow back while minimizing hip rotation, then lower it under control and repeat on the opposite side, alternating arms each rep.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands gripping dumbbells and feet wide for stability. Brace your core and keep your body straight from head to heels. Row one dumbbell toward your ribcage by driving your elbow back, then lower it under control. Alternate arms with each rep, rowing with one arm and then the other.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Mountain Climber
description: From a high plank position with hands gripping dumbbells directly under the shoulders, drive one knee toward the chest, then quickly switch legs in a running motion while maintaining a strong, braced core and flat back. Keep hips level and avoid sagging or piking as you alternate legs at a controlled, rhythmic pace.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands gripping dumbbells under your shoulders. Drive one knee toward your chest, then quickly switch to the other knee in a running motion. Keep your core braced, back flat, and hips level, avoiding any sagging or piking. Alternate legs at a controlled, rhythmic pace.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Russian Twist
description: Sit on the floor holding a dumbbell with both hands, lean back slightly to engage your core, lift your feet if able, and rotate your torso to move the dumbbell from side to side in a controlled twisting motion.
instructions: Sit on the floor with your feet lifted and back slightly leaned. Hold a dumbbell with both hands in front of your chest. Rotate your torso to move the dumbbell from side to side in a controlled twisting motion. Keep your core engaged and movements slow and deliberate.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Fix Dumbbell Pull-Over
description: Lie on a bench holding a dumbbell with both hands above your chest, arms extended. Keeping a slight bend in your elbows, slowly lower the dumbbell in an arc behind your head until you feel a stretch through your upper body and core, then pull it back over your chest using controlled motion. Focus on keeping your ribs down and abs braced to avoid excessive arching of the lower back.
instructions: Lie on a bench holding a dumbbell with both hands above your chest, arms extended. Lower the dumbbell in an arc behind your head, keeping a slight bend in your elbows, until you feel a stretch. Pull the dumbbell back over your chest using controlled motion, focusing on keeping your ribs down and abs braced. Keep your core engaged to avoid arching your lower back.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Sit-Up
description: Lie on your back on a mat with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, holding a dumbbell close to your chest. Brace your core and perform a controlled sit-up, lifting your torso until you are upright without pulling with your arms. Slowly lower back down to the starting position, keeping the dumbbell stable and your lower back from arching excessively.
instructions: Lie on your back on a mat with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, holding a dumbbell close to your chest. Brace your core and perform a controlled sit-up, lifting your torso until upright. Keep your arms stable and avoid pulling with them, focusing on using your core muscles. Slowly lower back down to the starting position, maintaining control and stability throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Front Squat
description: Hold a pair of dumbbells at shoulder height with elbows pointing slightly forward and feet about shoulder-width apart. Brace your core, keep your chest up, and sit your hips back and down into a squat until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor, keeping your heels flat. Drive through your mid-foot and heels to stand back up, maintaining an upright torso and tight core throughout.
instructions: Hold dumbbells at shoulder height with elbows forward and feet shoulder-width apart. Sit your hips back and down into a squat, keeping chest up and core braced, until thighs are parallel to the floor. Keep heels flat and drive through mid-foot and heels to stand up. Maintain an upright torso and tight core throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Fix Full Man Maker
description: A full-body dumbbell complex combining a push-up, row, clean, front squat, and overhead press to build strength, core stability, and conditioning.
instructions: Start in a plank position with dumbbells under hands. Perform a push-up, then row one dumbbell to side, followed by cleaning it to shoulder level. Immediately drop into a front squat, lowering body until thighs are parallel to ground, then stand and press dumbbell overhead. Repeat on other side, alternating arms with each repetition.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell T-hold
description: The dumbbell T-hold is an isometric side plank variation where you support your body on one forearm or hand while holding a dumbbell straight up toward the ceiling with the top arm, forming a T-shape. This position primarily challenges the obliques and overall core stability while also engaging the shoulders and upper back to maintain the hold. It is typically performed for timed holds but can be made dynamic by rotating the torso and dumbbell under the body and back to the T position for repetitions.
instructions: Lie on your side with feet stacked and forearm on the ground for support. Hold a dumbbell straight up with your top arm, forming a T-shape with your body. Engage your core and maintain a straight line from head to heels. Hold this position for the desired time or rotate your torso and dumbbell under your body and back to the T position for repetitions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Hollow-Body Floor Press
description: The dumbbell hollow-body floor press combines a dumbbell floor press with a hollow-body hold, building upper-body pressing strength while challenging core stability and control.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest, with arms extended and palms facing forward. Engage your core and press the dumbbells upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower them back down to the starting position. Keep your lower back pressed into the floor throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Crusher
description: A dumbbell complex combining a squat, curl, and overhead triceps press in one continuous sequence to train the lower body and upper body while elevating heart rate.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at sides. Lower into a squat, keeping back straight and knees behind toes. From the squat, curl the dumbbells up to shoulders, then press them overhead, extending arms fully. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the sequence.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm Dumbbell Crunch
description: The single-arm dumbbell crunch is an intermediate core exercise where you perform a traditional crunch while holding a dumbbell in one hand. The offset load increases demand on the rectus abdominis and challenges the obliques and deep core muscles to resist rotation and stabilize the spine.
instructions: Lie on a flat surface with knees bent and feet planted. Hold a dumbbell in one hand, extended overhead, and lift your shoulders off the ground, curling up towards your knees. Keep your core muscles engaged and avoid twisting or straining. Lower your shoulders back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Dumbbell Straight-Legged Hip Raise
description: A core-focused hip raise performed with straight legs while holding a dumbbell to increase resistance. This exercise targets the abs and hip flexors while engaging the glutes and improving body control and coordination.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and hold a dumbbell in one hand. Lift your straight legs towards the ceiling, keeping them straight and engaged. Raise your hips off the ground, squeezing your glutes and lower back muscles, then slowly lower back down to the starting position. Repeat with the dumbbell in the other hand to balance the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated Leg Tuck with Dumbbell
description: The seated leg tuck with dumbbell is a core-focused strength exercise performed sitting on a bench while holding a dumbbell between the feet or ankles and bringing the knees toward the chest. It is typically used as a high-repetition burnout at the end of an ab workout.
instructions: Sit on a bench with your feet flat on the floor. Hold a dumbbell between your feet or ankles. Lift your feet off the ground, keeping the dumbbell in place, and bring your knees toward your chest. Slowly lower your legs back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hanging Leg Raise with Dumbbell
description: Hang from a pull-up bar with arms fully extended, holding a dumbbell between your feet. Brace your core and, without swinging, raise your straight legs until they are parallel to the floor or higher, keeping the dumbbell secure. Pause briefly at the top, then lower your legs under control to the starting position while maintaining a stable torso and steady grip.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with arms fully extended, holding a dumbbell between your feet. Raise your straight legs until they are parallel to the floor or higher, keeping the dumbbell secure and your core braced. Pause briefly at the top, then lower your legs under control to the starting position. Maintain a stable torso and steady grip throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Get-Up Sit-Up
description: Lie on your back holding a dumbbell in one hand directly above your shoulder with your arm extended. Bend the knee on the same side, keeping the opposite leg straight on the floor. Brace your abs and press the dumbbell toward the ceiling as you sit up, driving through your bent leg and bringing your torso upright while keeping the arm locked out and the weight stacked over your shoulder. Control the movement back down to the starting position without letting the dumbbell drift forward or backward. Repeat for reps, then switch sides.
instructions: Lie on your back holding a dumbbell in one hand above your shoulder with arm extended. Bend the knee on the same side as the dumbbell, keeping the other leg straight. Sit up, pressing the dumbbell towards the ceiling and driving through your bent leg, keeping your arm locked out. Control the movement back down to the starting position, then repeat on the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Right Side Plank with Left Triceps Press
description: Assume a right side plank position, supporting your body on your right forearm and the side of your right foot while keeping your body in a straight line. Hold a dumbbell in your left hand with your arm extended toward the ceiling. Bend your left elbow to lower the dumbbell toward your shoulder, then press it back up to full extension while maintaining the side plank. Keep your core braced, hips lifted, and avoid rotating your torso throughout the movement.
instructions: Assume a right side plank position on your right forearm and the side of your right foot. Hold a dumbbell in your left hand with your arm extended toward the ceiling. Lower the dumbbell by bending your left elbow, then press it back up to full extension. Keep your core braced and hips lifted throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Left Side Plank with Right Triceps Press
description: Assume a left side plank position, supporting your body on your left forearm and the side of your left foot while keeping your body in a straight line. Hold a dumbbell in your right hand with your arm extended toward the ceiling. Bend your right elbow to lower the dumbbell toward your head, then press it back up to full extension while maintaining the side plank. Keep your core braced, hips lifted, and avoid rotating your torso throughout the movement.
instructions: Assume a left side plank position on your left forearm and the side of your left foot. Hold a dumbbell in your right hand with your arm extended toward the ceiling. Lower the dumbbell by bending your right elbow, then press it back up to full extension. Keep your core braced and hips lifted throughout the movement.

name: Smith Machine Straight-Legged Hip Raise

The fixed bar path reduces balance demands compared to free weights, allowing you to focus on controlled core contraction for moderate to high repetitions.

The exerciser resists the push and prevents the legs from touching the ground, emphasizing abdominal strength and stability.
instructions: Lie on your back with straight legs. Have your partner stand over you, ready to push your legs down. Raise your legs toward your partner, then have them push your legs down at varying angles, while you resist the push and keep your legs straight. Keep your core engaged to prevent your legs from touching the ground.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hanging Leg Raise with Throw Down
description: A partner-assisted hanging leg raise where you lift your legs to hip height or higher while hanging, then your partner forcefully pushes your legs downward, requiring you to resist and control the eccentric phase to increase abdominal engagement.
instructions: Hang from a bar with an overhand grip. Lift your legs to hip height or higher, keeping them straight. Your partner will then forcefully push your legs downward, and you must resist and control the movement, focusing on engaging your abdominal muscles. Lower your legs slowly to the starting position, maintaining control throughout the entire range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Partner Sit-Up with High-Five
description: A partner core exercise where both partners lie on their backs, perform a sit-up simultaneously, and high-five each other at the top of the movement before returning to the starting position.
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground. Have your partner lie next to you in the same position. Perform a sit-up simultaneously with your partner, meeting at the top to high-five each other. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid straining your back.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball Knee Roll-In
description: The exercise ball knee roll-in is an advanced core move performed from a plank position with the feet on an exercise ball and hands on the floor. From a stable plank, the knees are drawn in toward the chest by actively engaging the abdominals, then extended back to the starting position under control.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands on the floor and feet on an exercise ball. Engage your core and draw your knees towards your chest, keeping your back straight. Slowly roll the ball back to the starting position, controlling the movement with your abdominal muscles. Keep your body stable and rigid throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Partner Lying Leg Raise with Throw Down
description: A partner core exercise where one person lies on their back and raises their straight legs toward the standing partner, who then pushes the legs back down. The person on the floor resists the push and prevents the legs from touching the ground, emphasizing abdominal strength and stability.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead for support. Raise your straight legs toward your standing partner, keeping them rigid. Your partner will push your legs back down, but resist the push and control the descent, keeping your legs straight and off the ground. Keep your core engaged throughout the exercise to maintain stability and strength.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Crab Toe-Touch
description: The crab toe-touch is a full-body bodyweight exercise that targets the glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps, and core while challenging coordination and balance. From a crab position, you alternately reach one hand toward the opposite foot, engaging the abs and obliques as you stabilize through the shoulders and hips.
instructions: Start in a crab position with hands and feet on the ground. Lift hips off the ground, engaging your core and glutes. Alternate reaching one hand toward the opposite foot, keeping your shoulders and hips stable. Return to the starting position and repeat with the other hand and foot.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Side Kick-Through
description: The side kick-through is a dynamic, full-body bodyweight movement performed from a bear crawl position. You rotate your hips and torso while extending one leg out to the side, keeping your hips elevated and core braced throughout. It challenges core stability, shoulder strength, and coordination, and is often used in bodyweight circuits, animal-flow style training, or as part of a dynamic warm-up or mobility sequence.
instructions: Start in a bear crawl position with hands under shoulders and toes under hips. Rotate your hips and torso to one side, extending one leg out to the side while keeping your hips elevated and core braced. Keep your leg straight and your foot flexed, holding for a brief moment before returning to the starting position. Alternate sides to complete the exercise, maintaining control and stability throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Partner Target Sit-Up
description: A core exercise performed with a partner where one person does sit-ups while reaching toward the partner’s hands as a moving target, increasing range of motion and difficulty. Can be done for time or repetitions in any ab-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat. Have your partner stand over you with hands held out as targets. Perform a sit-up, reaching up to touch your partner's hands, which should be moving slightly to increase the challenge. Control your return to the starting position, repeating for desired repetitions or time.

name: Jump Lunge
description: The jump lunge is an explosive lower-body bodyweight exercise where you alternate lunges mid-air, powerfully jumping up from a lunge position and switching legs before landing. It targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, and is commonly used in power, athleticism, and fat-loss workouts, performed for time or repetitions.
instructions: Start in a lunge position with one foot forward and the other back. Lower your body until your back knee almost touches the ground, then explosively jump up. Switch your legs mid-air, landing in a lunge position with the opposite foot forward. Keep your core engaged and control your landing to maintain balance.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Side Plank Hip Dip
description: A dynamic side plank variation where you lower and lift your hips toward the floor to intensify engagement of the obliques and deep core muscles. Performed for time or repetitions, it challenges lateral core stability and shoulder endurance.
instructions: Start in a side plank position with feet stacked and hands under shoulders. Lift hips off the ground, engaging your core and keeping your body in a straight line. Lower your hips toward the floor, then lift back up to the starting position, repeating for desired repetitions. Keep your shoulders stable and core engaged throughout the movement.

name: Elbow Plank Jack
description: The elbow plank jack is an isometric core exercise with a dynamic lower-body component. From an elbow plank position, the feet jump out wide and back together while the torso stays stable, emphasizing the deep core muscles, especially the transverse abdominis. It can be performed for time or repetitions in the core portion of a workout.
instructions: Start in an elbow plank position with forearms on ground and body in a straight line. Engage core muscles to maintain stability. Jump feet out wide and then back together, keeping torso still and stable. Repeat for desired time or repetitions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball V-Up
description: The exercise ball V-up is an intermediate core movement where you pass a stability ball between your hands and feet while performing V-ups, powerfully targeting the rectus abdominis and overall core stability. It can be done for reps or time in ab-focused or bodyweight circuits.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight, holding a stability ball. Lift your arms and legs off the ground, balancing on your glutes. Pass the stability ball from your hands to your feet, then quickly return it to your hands as you lift your torso and legs into a V shape. Lower back down to the starting position and repeat.

name: Standing Torso Twist Stretch
description: The standing torso twist stretch is a dynamic stretch performed from a standing position, rotating the upper body side to side to warm up the lats and core, especially the obliques, before activities that involve twisting.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Keep arms extended out to sides at shoulder height. Twist torso to left, keeping feet and hips facing forward, then twist to right, repeating the motion. Continue alternating sides in a slow and controlled manner.

name: Slalom Jumps
description: Slalom jumps are an explosive lateral plyometric exercise performed with bodyweight. You jump side to side over an imaginary line or small object, landing softly with bent knees and quickly rebounding into the next jump. This movement primarily targets the quadriceps and glutes, with strong involvement from the hamstrings and calves, and challenges cardiovascular endurance when performed for higher reps or time.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Jump sideways over an imaginary line, landing softly on the balls of your feet with bent knees. Quickly rebound and jump to the other side, repeating the motion in an alternating pattern. Keep your weight light on your feet and your knees bent to absorb the impact and prepare for the next jump.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bear Crawl
description: The bear crawl is a bodyweight crawling exercise performed on hands and feet with hips low and core braced. It delivers full-body conditioning, challenging the shoulders, core, and legs while elevating heart rate. It can be used as a dynamic warm-up, in short bursts for power or circuits, or for longer durations as conditioning.
instructions: Start on hands and feet with knees slightly bent and hips low. Keep your core braced and back straight, engaging your abdominal muscles. Crawl forward, taking small steps with your hands and feet, keeping your hips stable and low to the ground. Maintain a smooth, controlled movement, avoiding arching your back or letting your hips sag.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Crab Single-Arm Reach
description: The crab single-arm reach is a full-body bodyweight exercise performed from a reverse tabletop position, lifting the hips while alternating overhead arm reaches to promote shoulder and hip mobility, core activation, and dynamic warm-up.
instructions: Start in a reverse tabletop position with hands and feet on ground. Lift hips up, keeping core engaged and body straight. Reach one arm up and over, keeping it straight, while maintaining balance and stability. Alternate arms with each repetition, controlling movements to promote smooth and dynamic motion.

name: Side Lunge Cross-Body Heel-Touch
description: A lateral lunge variation where you step out to the side, bend the working leg, and reach the opposite hand toward the heel, targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings with a slight rotational core challenge.
instructions: Stand with feet together. Take a large step to one side and lower your body, bending the working leg and keeping the other leg straight. Reach the opposite hand toward the heel of the bent leg, touching it lightly. Push back to the starting position and repeat on the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Alligator Crawl
description: The alligator crawl is a bodyweight crawling exercise performed with the hips slightly elevated and the knees tracking outside the elbows. It challenges the core to stabilize the torso while the shoulders and hips drive the body forward, making it useful as a full-body warm-up, for short power bursts in circuits, or for longer bouts of conditioning.
instructions: Start on hands and knees with hips slightly elevated. Keep knees outside elbows and engage core to stabilize torso. Crawl forward by driving shoulders and hips, keeping body low and controlled. Move slowly and deliberately to maintain proper form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Lying Cross-Over Lower Back Stretch
description: The lying cross-over lower back stretch is a bodyweight mobility exercise performed lying on your back while rotating your legs across your body. It gently stretches the lower back, hips, and torso, and is commonly used in warm-ups or general mobility routines.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended out to the sides. Bring one knee towards your chest and cross it over your other leg, keeping your foot flexed. Gently lower your knee towards the ground, stretching your lower back and hips, and hold for a few seconds. Repeat on the other side to maintain balance and symmetry.

name: Half Bird Dog
description: The half bird dog is a core stability exercise performed on hands and knees, where you extend and raise one arm or one leg at a time to strengthen the abs and posterior chain while promoting spinal stability.
instructions: Start on hands and knees with a flat back. Lift one arm straight out in front of you, keeping it at shoulder height. At the same time, lift the opposite leg straight out behind you, keeping it at hip height. Hold briefly, then lower back down and repeat on the other side.

name: Shoulder Tap
description: From a high plank (top of push-up position), brace your core and widen your feet for stability. Without letting your hips rotate, lift one hand to tap the opposite shoulder, then return it to the floor. Repeat with the other hand. One tap with each hand equals one rep.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with feet wide for stability. Brace your core to maintain a straight line from head to heels. Lift one hand to tap the opposite shoulder, keeping your hips still, then return it to the floor and repeat with the other hand. Alternate hands to complete one rep.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Double Crunch
description: The double crunch is a bodyweight core exercise that targets the rectus abdominis by simultaneously bringing the knees and elbows toward each other while keeping the lower back in contact with the ground.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and hands behind head. Lift shoulders off the ground, curling up towards knees. Bring elbows towards knees, squeezing abdominal muscles. Lower back down to starting position slowly.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Flutter Kick
description: The flutter kick is a core exercise performed lying on your back with your legs extended and lifted slightly off the ground, moving them in a quick alternating up-and-down motion. It primarily targets the rectus abdominis while also engaging the hip flexors and lower back for stabilization.
instructions: Lie on your back with legs extended and lifted slightly off the ground. Lift your head and shoulders off the ground, engaging your core. Move your legs in a quick alternating up-and-down motion, keeping them straight. Keep your core tight and maintain control throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Sprawl
description: An explosive bodyweight movement similar to a burpee, where you quickly drop into a plank position and then return to standing without a jump or push-up. It challenges the core while engaging both upper and lower body, making it effective for fat loss and athletic conditioning when performed for time or high reps.
instructions: Start in a standing position with feet shoulder-width apart. Drop down into a plank position, placing hands on the ground and kicking feet back into a straight line from head to heels. Quickly return to standing by pulling feet forward and standing up tall. Keep core engaged and movements explosive throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Spider Plank Jack
description: The spider plank jack is a bodyweight core exercise performed from a straight-arm plank, combining a spider crunch with a plank jack to challenge core stability, hip mobility, and conditioning.
instructions: Start in a straight-arm plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Bring one knee up towards chest, touching foot to ground beside hand, while keeping core engaged. Quickly switch legs in a jumping motion, mimicking a jack, and continue alternating legs. Keep torso stable and maintain proper plank form throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Pillar-To-Plank
description: Start in a tall pillar position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line from head to heels. Brace your abs and glutes, then lower one forearm to the floor at a time to transition into a forearm plank, keeping hips level. Press back up to the tall pillar position one arm at a time without twisting through the torso. Repeat for reps, alternating the lead arm while maintaining a tight core and neutral spine throughout.
instructions: Start in a tall pillar position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line. Lower one forearm to the floor at a time to transition into a forearm plank, keeping hips level. Press back up to the tall pillar position one arm at a time, avoiding torso twist. Repeat, alternating the lead arm while maintaining a tight core and neutral spine.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Alternating High Knee Raise
description: From a standing position, alternately drive one knee up toward hip or waist height while bracing your core and maintaining an upright posture. Lower the leg under control and repeat on the opposite side, focusing on controlled core engagement rather than speed.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and engage your core. Drive one knee up toward hip height, keeping posture upright and core braced. Lower the leg under control and repeat on the opposite side. Focus on controlled movements, prioritizing core engagement over speed.

name: Sumo Squat Pulse
description: Stand with your feet wider than shoulder-width apart and toes turned out. Lower into a deep sumo squat, keeping your chest up and core braced. From the bottom position, perform small up-and-down pulses without fully standing, maintaining tension in your legs and glutes. Keep your knees tracking over your toes and avoid letting them cave inward. Complete all pulses before returning to a full standing position.
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width apart and toes turned out. Lower into a deep sumo squat, keeping chest up and core braced. From the bottom, perform small up-and-down pulses without standing, keeping knees over toes. Return to standing after completing all pulses.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Line-Drill Foot Work
description: A fast-paced footwork drill performed over an imaginary or marked line, focusing on rapid, alternating steps to improve core stability, coordination, and lower-body reactivity.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart beside an imaginary or marked line. Start with one foot on each side of the line, then quickly alternate feet over the line in a rapid, stepping motion. Keep your knees slightly bent and core engaged to maintain balance and stability. Continue for the desired duration, focusing on fast and controlled footwork.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Plank Fire Hydrants
description: From a high plank position, lift one bent leg out to the side in a fire-hydrant motion while keeping your core braced and hips level, then return to plank and repeat on the other side.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Lift one bent leg out to the side, keeping knee at 90 degrees, while engaging your core and keeping hips level. Return to plank position and repeat on the other side, alternating legs in a controlled motion. Keep your core braced throughout the exercise to maintain stability.

name: Banded Jumping Jack
description: A plyometric jumping jack performed with a resistance band looped around the thighs just above the knees. The band increases tension on the hips and glutes, making it an effective warm-up or conditioning drill for lower-body training and athletic movements.

Jump your feet out to the sides while raising your arms above your head, then quickly return to the starting position, repeating in a rapid and continuous motion. Keep your core engaged and land softly on the balls of your feet to maintain control and balance.

name: Shin Hug
description: The shin hug is a bodyweight core exercise similar to a V-up, where you curl your upper body and legs toward each other until your ribs meet your thighs, emphasizing controlled abdominal contraction.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight. Lift your shoulders and legs off the ground, curling your upper body and legs toward each other. Continue to curl until your ribs meet your thighs, squeezing your abdominal muscles. Lower your body back down to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Bear Crawl Up-Down
description: The bear crawl up-down is a static bear crawl hold where you alternate lowering one forearm to the ground at a time and then pressing back up to your hands. This variation increases upper-body demand, core stability, and coordination while challenging the cardiovascular system. It can be used as a full-body warm-up or in a bodyweight conditioning circuit.
