Press back up to your hand, then repeat with the other forearm, alternating sides in a slow and controlled manner. Keep your body low and level throughout the exercise.

name: Bear Crawl Hold
description: A static bear crawl position held for time, with hands under shoulders and knees under hips hovering just off the floor. This hold builds full-body tension and challenges the shoulders, core, and legs, making it useful as a warm-up, core exercise, or progression toward dynamic bear crawls.
instructions: Start on hands and knees with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Lower your body until your knees almost touch the ground, hovering just above it. Engage your core and maintain a straight line from head to heels, holding this position for the desired time. Keep your body rigid and stable, avoiding any sagging or arching.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Traveling Bear Crawl
description: The traveling bear crawl is an advanced variation of the bear crawl performed moving laterally instead of forward and backward. With hips low and knees hovering off the floor, you crawl side to side, keeping the core braced and shoulders stable. This intense full-body movement challenges the cardiovascular system while heavily engaging the shoulders, core, and legs, and can be used as a dynamic warm-up, in power circuits, or for conditioning intervals.
instructions: Start in a bear crawl position with hips low and knees slightly off the ground. Keep your core braced and shoulders stable, engaging your entire body. Crawl laterally to one side by taking small steps with your hands and feet, maintaining a low and stable position. Continue alternating sides, moving slowly and controlled to avoid losing form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-leg V-up
description: The single-leg V-up is a bodyweight core exercise performed lying on your back, raising one leg and your torso simultaneously to meet in a V position. It targets the abdominals and hip flexors unilaterally and can be done for time or repetitions in ab-focused workouts.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead. Lift one leg straight up towards the ceiling and simultaneously lift your torso, keeping arms straight, to meet your leg in a V position. Lower your leg and torso back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement. Repeat on the other side to target both legs evenly.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Alternating Single-Leg Sprawl
description: The alternating single-leg sprawl is an explosive bodyweight movement where you drop from standing into a single-leg plank position, alternating legs each rep. It challenges the core, shoulders, and legs while elevating heart rate, making it useful in fat-loss or athleticism-focused circuits.
instructions: Start in a standing position with feet hip-width apart. Drop down into a single-leg plank position, landing on one hand and one foot while keeping the other leg lifted. Quickly switch to the other leg, alternating sides with each rep. Keep your core engaged and body in a straight line from head to heel throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Wide Mountain Climber
description: The wide mountain climber is a dynamic bodyweight exercise performed from a high plank position, driving one knee at a time toward the outside of the same-side elbow. This variation increases demand on the core, hips, and shoulders while elevating heart rate, making it useful for conditioning, warm-ups, and core training without equipment.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Drive one knee toward the outside of the same-side elbow, keeping core engaged. Quickly switch to the other knee, mimicking a running motion. Continue alternating knees at a rapid pace, maintaining proper form and engagement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Plank Leg Raise
description: The plank leg raise is an isometric core exercise performed in a plank position while alternately lifting one leg at a time. It emphasizes the deep core muscles, especially the transversus abdominis, while also engaging the glutes and hips to maintain stability and alignment. It can be performed for time or repetitions in the core-focused portion of a workout.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Engage your core by drawing your belly button towards your spine. Lift one leg off the ground, keeping it straight, and hold for a brief moment before lowering it back down. Alternate legs to continue the exercise, maintaining a stable plank position throughout.

name: Glute Bridge Step-Out
description: The glute bridge step-out is a bodyweight lower-body exercise performed from a bridge position while alternately stepping the feet out and back in. It emphasizes the glutes and hamstrings while also engaging the core to maintain pelvic stability. It can be used as a dynamic warm-up, activation drill, or accessory movement for lower-body training.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the ground. Lift your hips up into a bridge position, squeezing your glutes and keeping your core engaged. Step one foot out to the side, keeping your knee bent at a 90-degree angle, then return to the starting position and repeat with the other foot. Continue alternating feet, maintaining a stable pelvis and controlled movement throughout the exercise.

name: Knee Up-Down
description: The knee up-down is a bodyweight exercise that mimics getting up and down from a kneeling position, combining elements of a squat and lunge. It is often used for time or reps in dynamic warm-ups or bodyweight circuits to build lower-body strength and core stability.
instructions: Start in a standing position with feet shoulder-width apart. Lower your body into a kneeling position, keeping your back straight and core engaged. From the kneeling position, step one foot forward into a lunge, then return to standing, alternating legs with each repetition. Keep your movements controlled and focused on proper form.

name: Reach Crunch
description: The reach crunch is a bodyweight core exercise where you lie on your back with arms extended toward the ceiling and perform a controlled crunch, reaching your hands upward to intensify abdominal engagement. It is typically used in ab-focused circuits and can be done for time or repetitions.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the ground. Extend your arms straight up towards the ceiling. Lift your shoulders off the ground, reaching your hands upwards, and curl up towards your knees. Lower your shoulders back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: V-up plus knee-to-chest combo
description: The V-up plus knee-to-chest combo is a core-focused exercise that combines a traditional V-up with a knee-to-chest crunch in one continuous sequence. Starting from a lying position, you simultaneously lift your upper body and legs to form a V, then draw the knees toward the chest before extending again and lowering under control. This movement emphasizes the abdominals and hip flexors while also engaging the lower back and obliques for stability, making it suitable for intermediate-level core training performed for time or repetitions.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead. Lift your upper body and legs to form a V, keeping core engaged. Draw your knees toward your chest, then extend and lower under control, repeating the sequence. Focus on slow and controlled movements to target your core and hip flexors.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Side Plank Lateral Raise
description: The side plank lateral raise combines an isometric side plank hold with a dumbbell lateral raise to challenge the core and shoulders simultaneously. Performed with light weight to maintain balance, it efficiently targets the obliques, abs, and shoulder stabilizers while improving lateral core strength and overall stability.
instructions: Start in a side plank position with feet stacked and hands under shoulders. Hold a light dumbbell in the top hand with arm extended. Lift the dumbbell out to the side until it's at shoulder height, then lower it back down to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and body stable throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Superhero Burpee
description: The superhero burpee is an advanced burpee variation where, from the bottom position, you extend your arms straight overhead in front of you as if in a Superman flight position before pushing back up. This increases the challenge to the upper back, shoulders, and core while still providing a full-body, high-intensity movement suitable for fat-loss or athleticism-focused workouts.
instructions: Start in a standing position. Drop down into a squat, placing hands on the ground, and kick back into a plank position. From the plank, extend arms straight overhead, then do a push-up, before quickly returning your feet to the squat position and standing up. Jump up in the air, landing softly on the balls of your feet to complete the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hollow-body Abduction
description: An isometric core exercise performed in a hollow-body position while abducting the legs to increase demand on the rectus abdominis and surrounding core musculature. Typically held for time or performed for controlled repetitions in ab-focused workouts.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight. Engage your core to lift your shoulders and legs off the ground, holding a hollow-body position. Slowly lift your legs out to the sides, keeping them straight, and hold for the desired time or repeat for controlled repetitions. Maintain a stable core and controlled breathing throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Tip-toe Crunch
description: The tip-toe crunch is an intermediate core exercise performed lying on your back with your toes lightly touching or hovering near the ground. This position reduces hip flexor involvement and increases emphasis on the abs as you curl your shoulders and upper back off the floor. It can be done for reps or time as part of a core-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet lightly touching the ground. Lift your toes slightly, keeping them hovering near the floor. Curl your shoulders and upper back off the floor, squeezing your abs as you lift. Lower back down to the starting position, repeating for desired reps or time.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Knee-to-Nose Pulse
description: The knee-to-nose pulse is a core-focused bodyweight exercise performed from a straight-arm plank. One knee is driven toward the nose and held briefly in the bent position, then pulsed in small controlled movements to increase time under tension in the abs and hip flexors while maintaining a strong plank position.
instructions: Start in a straight-arm plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Engage core and lift one knee toward nose, holding briefly in bent position. Pulse the knee in small controlled movements, keeping the rest of the body still and core muscles tight. Maintain a strong plank position throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Plank Walk-Out
description: From a standing position, hinge at the hips to place your hands on the floor, then walk your hands forward into an extended plank with the hands in front of the head, pause while bracing the core, then walk the hands back toward the feet and return to standing.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hinge at the hips and place hands on the floor, then walk hands forward into a plank position. Pause and brace your core, then walk hands back toward your feet and return to standing. Keep your body straight and core engaged throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Long Sit-Up
description: The long sit-up is an advanced sit-up variation performed with the arms extended overhead, increasing the lever length and making it more challenging for the abs to contract and lift the torso. It can be done for time or repetitions, with feet either anchored or unanchored.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and feet flexed. Lift your torso off the ground, keeping arms straight, and curl up towards your knees. Focus on contracting your abdominal muscles to control the movement. Lower your torso back down to the starting position slowly.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated Leg Lift
description: The seated leg lift is a bodyweight core exercise performed from a seated position, lifting the legs to engage the abs and hip flexors. It can be done for time or repetitions in the core portion of a workout.
instructions: Sit on the floor with your back straight and hands behind you for support. Lift your legs off the ground, keeping them straight or slightly bent. Raise your legs 6-8 inches off the ground, squeezing your abs and holding for a brief moment. Lower your legs back down to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid straining your back.

name: Gorilla Burpee
description: The gorilla burpee is a full-body burpee variation that adds a reverse lunge on each leg and a full squat before the standard burpee. This increases the challenge on the lower body—especially the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings—while still demanding core stability and upper-body involvement. It can be performed for time or repetitions in fat-loss or athleticism-focused circuits.
instructions: Start in a standing position with feet shoulder-width apart. Drop down into a squat position and place hands on the ground, then kick back into a plank position. From the plank, perform a reverse lunge on each leg, alternating sides, before doing a push-up. Quickly return to the squat position and stand up, repeating the process for the desired number of repetitions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Knee-in to V-out
description: A floor-based bodyweight core exercise that combines a reverse crunch and V-up. From a reclined position, you draw your knees toward your chest, then extend your legs into a wide V shape while maintaining core tension, targeting the rectus abdominis.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead. Draw your knees toward your chest, lifting your hips off the ground. Extend your legs into a wide V shape, keeping your core tight and lower back pressed into the floor. Slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position, maintaining control throughout the movement.

name: Bear Crawl Push-Up
description: The bear crawl push-up combines a bear crawl hold position with a push-up, creating an intense full-body movement that challenges coordination, shoulders, core, and legs while elevating heart rate. Performed on all fours with knees hovering just off the ground, you maintain a strong, braced torso as you bend the elbows to lower the chest toward the floor and press back up, all while keeping the hips low and stable. It can be used as a dynamic full-body warm-up or as part of a bodyweight conditioning circuit.
instructions: Start on all fours with knees hovering just off the ground. Maintain a strong, braced torso and keep hips low and stable. Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows, then press back up to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and movements controlled throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Plank Reach
description: The plank reach is a core stability exercise where you hold a plank position and alternately extend one arm forward. This challenges the entire core, with extra emphasis on the obliques, as the body resists rotation and shifting. It can be performed for time, with pauses in the reach, or for controlled repetitions.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Engage your core and maintain a straight line from head to heels. Reach one arm forward, keeping it straight, and then return to the starting position. Alternate arms with each repetition, keeping your core stable and body steady throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Eiffel Tower Leg Raise
description: The Eiffel Tower leg raise is a bodyweight core exercise that primarily targets the rectus abdominis by having you raise your legs and trace a pyramid shape in the air. Similar to a V-up but with added leg movement, it increases time under tension for the abs and challenges coordination and control. Perform for controlled reps or time as part of a core-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight. Lift your legs and torso off the ground, keeping your arms and legs straight, and raise your legs to form the peak of the "Eiffel Tower". Slowly lower your legs to one side, then the other, tracing a pyramid shape in the air. Lower back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Straight-Arm Plank
description: The straight-arm plank is a core stability exercise performed in the top of a push-up position with elbows fully extended and palms on the floor. The body is held in a straight line from head to heels to strengthen the abs, obliques, shoulders, and lower back.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart and palms on the floor. Engage your core and straighten your arms, keeping your elbows fully extended. Hold your body in a straight line from head to heels, squeezing your abs and keeping your shoulders down. Maintain this position for the desired amount of time, breathing naturally.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Plank Up-Down
description: The plank up-down is a bodyweight core exercise where you transition one arm at a time between a high plank on the hands and a low plank on the elbows. This movement increases demand on the triceps and core compared to a standard plank and can be performed for time or repetitions.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Lower one arm to elbow, keeping core engaged and body straight. Quickly switch to the other arm, returning the first arm to the high plank position. Alternate arms for desired time or repetitions, maintaining a stable core throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Frog Kick
description: The frog kick is an explosive bodyweight movement starting from a straight-arm plank, then hopping the legs forward toward the hands. It targets the core, legs, and shoulders while providing high-intensity conditioning, and is typically performed for time or reps in athleticism or fat-loss circuits.
instructions: Start in a straight-arm plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Engage your core and keep your body straight. Explosively hop your legs forward toward your hands, landing softly on the balls of your feet. Immediately return to the starting plank position and repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Front Kick-Through
description: The front kick-through is a dynamic bodyweight movement starting from a bear crawl position, then rotating the hips and kicking one leg forward while supporting the body on the opposite hand and foot. It challenges core stability, shoulder strength, and coordination, and is often used in circuits, animal-flow style training, or as part of a dynamic warm-up.
instructions: Start in a bear crawl position with hands and feet on the ground. Rotate your hips and kick one leg forward, keeping it straight. Support your body on the opposite hand and foot, engaging your core for stability. Return to the starting position and repeat with the other leg.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Roman Chair Side Ups
description: Position yourself sideways on a Roman chair or hyperextension bench with your hips supported and your upper body hanging off the side. Cross your arms over your chest or place your hands lightly behind your head. Lower your torso sideways toward the floor in a controlled motion, then contract your obliques to raise your torso back to the starting position. Repeat for reps, then switch sides.
instructions: Position yourself sideways on a Roman chair with hips supported and upper body hanging off. Cross your arms over your chest or place hands lightly behind your head. Lower your torso sideways toward the floor in a controlled motion, then contract your obliques to raise back up. Switch sides after completing the desired number of reps.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Leg Lift with Twist
description: A bodyweight core exercise performed on a decline surface where you lift your legs and add a twisting motion to emphasize the obliques and lower portion of the rectus abdominis.
instructions: Lie on a decline surface with hands grasping the edge for support. Lift your legs straight up towards the ceiling, keeping them straight. As you lift, twist your legs to one side, targeting your obliques, then return to the starting position and repeat on the other side. Lower your legs slowly to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Butterfly Crunch
description: A bodyweight core exercise performed lying on your back with the soles of your feet together and knees dropped out to the sides. From this butterfly position, you curl your upper back off the floor to target the rectus abdominis, performing controlled reps for time or repetitions in an ab-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on your back with the soles of your feet together and knees dropped out to the sides. Engage your core and curl your upper back off the floor, lifting your shoulders and head. Focus on controlled, slow movements to target the rectus abdominis. Lower your back down to the starting position and repeat for desired reps.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Cross-Body Mountain Climber
description: A dynamic bodyweight core exercise performed from a high plank, driving each knee toward the opposite elbow in a controlled, alternating motion. It challenges the abs and obliques while also engaging the shoulders and hips, and can be used for conditioning or as part of a warm-up.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Engage core and lift right knee toward left elbow, then quickly switch to lift left knee toward right elbow. Continue alternating legs in a controlled, rapid motion, keeping hips stable and core tight. Keep the movement smooth and fluid, aiming for a steady pace.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Lying Leg Lift
description: The lying leg lift is a bodyweight core exercise performed while lying on your back, lifting and lowering straight legs to target the rectus abdominis or six-pack muscles. It can be done for reps or time in the core portion of a workout.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead or hands under your glutes for support. Lift your straight legs off the ground, keeping them straight and core muscles engaged. Lift your legs 6-8 inches off the ground, then slowly lower them back down to the starting position without touching the floor. Repeat for desired reps or time, focusing on controlled movements and core engagement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hanging Alternating Single-Leg Raise
description: A hanging core exercise where you raise one leg at a time while hanging from a bar, alternating sides each rep to target the abs and hip flexors with additional engagement of the lats.
instructions: Hang from a bar with an overhand grip. Lift one leg up towards the ceiling, keeping it straight, and then lower it back down. Alternate legs with each rep, raising one leg while the other remains still. Keep your core engaged and body stable throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Plank Walk
description: From a high plank position, walk your hands and feet laterally to one side for several steps while maintaining a straight line from head to heels, then reverse the direction. Keep your core braced and hips level throughout to challenge abdominal stability and shoulder control.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Engage your core and keep hips level, maintaining a straight line from head to heels. Walk your hands and feet laterally to one side for several steps, keeping your body stable. Reverse direction and walk back to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Plank Push-Up
description: The plank push-up (plank up-down) is a core-focused bodyweight exercise where you transition one arm at a time between a high plank on the hands and a low plank on the forearms. This movement increases demand on the triceps and abs compared to a standard plank and can be performed for time or repetitions in strength or core workouts.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Lower one forearm to the ground, then the other, forming a low plank position. Push back up to the starting position, one arm at a time, returning to the high plank. Repeat, alternating arms with each repetition.

name: Alternating Lying Leg Raise
description: A bodyweight core exercise performed lying on your back, lifting one straight leg at a time in an alternating pattern to target the rectus abdominis while maintaining a braced core and neutral lower back.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead or hands under your lower back for support. Lift one straight leg 6-8 inches off the ground, keeping the other leg flat. Alternate legs in a slow and controlled manner, maintaining a braced core and neutral lower back throughout the exercise. Keep your movements smooth and deliberate, avoiding any jerky or bouncy motions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Leg Lift
description: A bodyweight core exercise performed on a decline surface, lifting and lowering the legs to target the rectus abdominis with emphasis on the lower abs.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with hands gripping the edge for support. Lift your legs straight up towards the ceiling, keeping them straight and core engaged. Lower your legs back down to the starting position, avoiding arching your back. Control the movement throughout the entire range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Prison Push-Up
description: A bodyweight push-up variation performed from a plank position, lowering the chest toward the floor and then returning to the top while maintaining a tight core, emphasizing abdominal engagement and overall trunk stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart and toes on the ground. Engage your core and maintain a straight line from head to heels. Lower your chest toward the floor, keeping your elbows close to your body, and then push back up to the starting position. Keep your abdominal muscles tight throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: McGill Modified Curl-Up
description: Lie on your back with one knee bent and the other leg straight. Place your hands under the natural curve of your lower back to maintain a neutral spine. Brace your core, then gently lift your head, shoulders, and upper back just slightly off the floor without rounding the lower back. Hold briefly while maintaining tension in the abs, then lower under control and repeat.
instructions: Lie on your back with one knee bent and the other leg straight. Place your hands under your lower back to maintain a neutral spine. Brace your core, then lift your head, shoulders, and upper back slightly off the floor without rounding your lower back. Hold briefly, then lower under control and repeat.

name: Cross-Body Toe-Touch
description: The cross-body toe-touch is a dynamic variation of the toe-touch stretch where one hand at a time reaches toward the opposite foot. It is commonly used in warm-ups to engage the core and posterior chain while improving flexibility and coordination.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Bend to one side, reaching with one hand towards the opposite foot, keeping knees slightly bent. Touch toe briefly, then return to standing position. Alternate sides with each repetition.

name: Toy Soldier
description: The toy soldier is a dynamic kicking exercise where you alternately swing each leg straight in front of you and reach the opposite hand to the foot. It primarily warms up and strengthens the hip flexors and legs while engaging the core and improving coordination and mobility before training.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Lift one leg straight out in front of you, keeping it straight, and reach the opposite hand to touch your foot. Alternate legs in a continuous motion, mimicking a marching action, and keep your core engaged for balance. Keep your movements controlled and swift to maintain rhythm and target the hip flexors and legs.

name: Natural Glute Ham Raise with Stability Ball
description: Kneel on the floor with your feet anchored against a wall or sturdy object and a stability ball placed in front of you for support. Keeping your body straight from knees to shoulders and your core braced, slowly lower your torso toward the floor by extending at the knees, using the stability ball lightly with your hands to control the descent. At the bottom of the movement, contract your hamstrings and glutes to pull your body back up to the starting position, using the ball as needed for assistance and balance.
instructions: Kneel on the floor with feet anchored against a wall and a stability ball in front of you. Lower your torso toward the floor by extending at the knees, using the ball for control. At the bottom, contract your hamstrings and glutes to pull back up to the starting position, using the ball as needed for balance. Keep your body straight and core braced throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Lateral Ape
description: The lateral ape is a dynamic, low-squat lateral hopping exercise that targets the lower body and core while challenging hip mobility, coordination, and athleticism. It can be used in warm-ups, conditioning circuits, or agility-focused sessions.
instructions: Start in a low squat position with feet shoulder-width apart. Lower your body down slightly, then explosively hop to one side while keeping your back straight and core engaged. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately repeat on the other side, alternating hops in a lateral direction. Keep your movements quick and controlled throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hollow-body Knee Tuck
description: The hollow-body knee tuck is a floor-based core exercise that combines a hollow-body hold with a knee tuck motion. Lying on your back, you lift your shoulders and legs slightly off the ground to create a hollow position, then draw your knees toward your chest while maintaining core tension. This movement emphasizes the rectus abdominis while also engaging the hip flexors and obliques for stability. It can be performed for repetitions or timed holds within ab-focused or circuit workouts.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead. Lift your shoulders and legs slightly off the ground to create a hollow position. Draw your knees toward your chest while maintaining core tension, then return to the hollow position. Repeat for desired repetitions or hold for a set time.

name: Hanging Crunch
description: The hanging crunch is an intermediate abdominal exercise that targets the rectus abdominis by curling the spine while hanging from a bar or supported in ab straps. Compared to a standard hanging knee raise, it emphasizes a stronger crunch at the top of the movement for greater core activation.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Tyler Holt Hanging Toes-To-Bar
description: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, arms fully extended and core braced. Keeping your legs straight, raise your feet in a controlled motion until your toes touch the bar between your hands, then lower them back down under control without swinging. Maintain tension in your abs throughout and avoid using momentum.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, arms fully extended and core braced. Raise your straight legs in a controlled motion until your toes touch the bar. Lower your legs back down under control without swinging, keeping tension in your abs. Maintain a stable body position throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Side Plank
description: The side plank is an isometric core exercise emphasizing the obliques and deep core muscles to build lateral stability and spinal support. Performed for time on one side at a time, it is often included in core-stability routines such as the McGill "big three."
instructions: Lie on your side with feet stacked and hands under your shoulders. Lift your hips off the ground, engaging your core and keeping your body in a straight line. Hold this position for the desired time, breathing naturally and maintaining control. Switch sides to work the other obliques.

name: Bird Dog
description: The bird dog is a core stability exercise performed from a hands-and-knees tabletop position, extending the opposite arm and leg to strengthen and stabilize the posterior chain and trunk.
instructions: Start on hands and knees in a tabletop position. Engage your core and lift your right arm and left leg off the ground, holding them straight. Hold for a brief moment, then lower and repeat with the left arm and right leg. Keep your movements slow and controlled throughout the exercise.

name: Single-Leg Curl Up
description: The single-leg curl up is a bodyweight core exercise that emphasizes abdominal bracing and spinal stability. One leg is kept straight while the other is bent, and the torso is gently curled up without flexing the lower back, reinforcing proper core engagement for strength training and injury prevention.
instructions: Lie on your back with one leg straight and the other bent at a 90-degree angle. Engage your core muscles by drawing your belly button towards your spine. Slowly curl your torso up, keeping your lower back pressed into the ground, and hold for a brief moment. Lower back down to the starting position, switching legs to repeat on the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-Kettlebell Windmill
description: The single-kettlebell windmill is a dynamic strength exercise that targets the core while challenging shoulder and hip stability and mobility. From a standing position with the kettlebell locked out overhead, you hinge at the hips and rotate to lower your torso toward the floor, then return to standing under control. It is best used for functional core and shoulder work rather than high-fatigue conditioning.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell overhead with one arm. Hinge at the hips and rotate your torso, lowering it toward the floor while keeping the kettlebell locked out. Keep your core engaged and control the movement as you lower and return to standing. Return to the starting position, squeezing your core and stabilizing your shoulder.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Foam Roll Thigh
description: Lie face down with a foam roller positioned under the front of your thigh. Support your upper body on your forearms and slowly roll your thigh over the foam roller from just above the knee to just below the hip, pausing on tender spots. Repeat on the other leg. This helps release tension in the quadriceps and hip flexors.
instructions: Lie face down with a foam roller under the front of your thigh. Support your upper body on your forearms and slowly roll your thigh over the foam roller from just above the knee to just below the hip. Pause on tender spots to release tension. Repeat on the other leg.

name: Quadruped Thoracic Rotation
description: From an all-fours position, place one hand lightly behind your head and rotate your upper back to bring your elbow toward the supporting arm, then open your chest by rotating upward, following your elbow with your eyes. Focus on moving through the upper back while keeping your hips stable and core engaged.
instructions: Start on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Place one hand lightly behind your head and rotate your upper back to bring your elbow toward the supporting arm. Keep your hips stable and core engaged as you rotate, following your elbow with your eyes to open your chest. Return to the starting position and repeat on the other side.

name: Foam Roll Lats
description: Lie on your side with a foam roller positioned under your armpit along the side of your upper back (lat area). Gently roll your body up and down and slightly forward and back to massage the lat muscles, pausing on tender spots and breathing deeply. Perform slowly and controlled on each side.
instructions: Lie on your side with a foam roller under your armpit along the side of your upper back. Gently roll up and down and slightly forward and back to massage the lat muscles. Pause on tender spots and breathe deeply, moving slowly and controlled. Repeat on the other side.

name: Ab Wheel Roll-Out
description: The ab wheel roll-out is a core-strength exercise performed with an ab wheel, emphasizing the abdominals and overall midsection stability. From a kneeling position, you roll the wheel forward under control, extending your body while maintaining a braced core, then pull back to the start. Beginners can reduce the range of motion or perform static wheel planks until sufficient strength is developed.
instructions: Start on your hands and knees with the ab wheel in front of you. Grasp the wheel handles firmly and roll it forward, extending your body while keeping your core muscles engaged. Maintain control throughout the movement, avoiding arching your back. Return to the starting position by pulling the wheel back towards you.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bench Mountain Climber
description: A bodyweight core exercise performed with the hands elevated on a bench, reducing shoulder load while targeting the abs and hip flexors. From a high plank with hands on the bench, alternate driving knees toward the chest in a quick, controlled running motion, maintaining a stable torso and neutral spine.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands on the bench. Place hands shoulder-width apart and engage core muscles to maintain a stable torso. Alternate driving knees toward the chest in a quick, controlled running motion, keeping spine neutral. Keep the movement fast and controlled to target the abs and hip flexors.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Feet-Elevated Bench Side Plank
description: An advanced side plank variation performed with the feet elevated on a bench. This increases the load on the obliques, shoulders, and lateral hip musculature compared to a standard floor side plank, challenging core stability and lateral strength.
instructions: Place your feet on a bench and lie on your side with your hands under your shoulders. Lift your hips off the ground, forming a straight line from head to heels. Engage your core and hold the position, keeping your body stable and your shoulders down. Hold for the desired duration, then slowly lower your hips back down.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Cross-body Sit-up
description: The cross-body sit-up is a bodyweight core exercise where you lie on your back and bring the opposite elbow toward the opposite knee, emphasizing the obliques and rectus abdominis. Typically performed alternating sides for reps or time, it challenges rotational core strength and trunk flexion without added resistance.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat. Lift your torso, bringing one elbow towards the opposite knee. Alternate sides with each rep, keeping core engaged and movements controlled. Focus on slow and deliberate movements to target the obliques and rectus abdominis.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Kayak
description: The decline kayak is a core exercise performed on a decline bench with the feet anchored, mimicking a paddling motion while holding a bar or pipe. It primarily targets the obliques and abs and is typically done for moderate to high repetitions as part of an ab-focused workout or circuit.
instructions: Anchor your feet on the decline bench and hold a bar or pipe with both hands. Mimic a paddling motion by twisting your torso from side to side. Keep your core engaged and maintain control throughout the movement. Focus on quick, precise twists to target your obliques and abs.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Sit-Up Twist
description: The decline sit-up twist is a bodyweight core exercise performed on a decline surface to emphasize the obliques and rectus abdominis. From a declined position, you perform a sit-up while rotating the torso at the top of the movement to engage the obliques. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions as part of a core-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with hands behind your head. Lift your torso up, curling towards your knees, and rotate to one side. Twist to the other side on the next repetition, alternating sides with each sit-up. Control the movement as you lower back down to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Flat Bench Leg Raise
description: A lying leg raise performed on a flat bench to target the rectus abdominis by lifting the legs under control while keeping the lower back stable against the bench.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your hands grasping the edge for support. Lift your legs straight up towards the ceiling, keeping them straight and core engaged. Raise your legs until they are at a 90-degree angle to the bench, then slowly lower them back down to the starting position. Keep your lower back pressed against the bench throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Chin-Up Crunch
description: The chin-up crunch combines a chin-up with a knee raise or crunch, targeting the core while also engaging the upper body pulling muscles.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip. Lift your body up to perform a chin-up, squeezing your lats at the top. As you reach the top of the chin-up, lift your knees up towards your chest to perform a crunch. Lower your body back down to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Gorilla Chin Oblique Crunch
description: A hanging core exercise where you perform a chin-up on a bar while simultaneously driving your knees toward one side of your torso to target the obliques. Each rep alternates sides, combining upper-body pulling strength with rotational core work.
instructions: Hang from a bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Perform a chin-up, lifting knees towards one side of your torso. Alternate sides with each rep, targeting the obliques. Control the movement and avoid swinging.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Half Turkish Get-Up
description: The half Turkish get-up is a kettlebell-style core movement performed with bodyweight or load that trains shoulder stability, trunk strength, and hip control through the first half of the traditional Turkish get-up. Starting from a lying position, you drive through the planted foot and elbow to come up to a supported seated or tall-post position, then reverse the motion under control. It can be used in lower rep ranges for strength or higher reps for technique practice and conditioning.
instructions: Start by lying on your side with your bottom arm bent and supporting your body. Drive through your planted foot and elbow to lift your hips and torso up to a supported seated position. Keep your top arm straight and your eyes focused on your hand. Slowly reverse the motion to return to the starting position under control.

name: Hanging Oblique Crunch
description: The hanging oblique crunch is an intermediate abdominal exercise performed while hanging from a bar or using ab straps. You lift and twist your knees toward one side of your torso to target the rectus abdominis and obliques, then alternate sides for each repetition.
instructions: Hang from a bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Lift knees up toward chest, then twist to one side, touching knees to torso. Alternate sides with each repetition, controlling the movement to target obliques. Lower knees back down to starting position slowly.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hollow-Body Hold
description: The hollow-body hold is an isometric core exercise that builds strength and stability through the abs and surrounding trunk muscles. Common in gymnastics and CrossFit, it teaches full-body tension and bracing for athletes and lifters. It is typically held for time and can be used as a warm-up activation drill, in circuits, or as equipment-free core training.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight. Engage your core by drawing your belly button towards your spine and squeezing your glutes. Lift your shoulders and legs off the ground, holding a straight line from head to heels. Hold this position for the desired time, maintaining control and tension throughout your entire body.

name: Parallel Bars Knee Raise
description: A bodyweight core exercise performed on parallel bars from the top of a dip position, lifting the knees toward the chest to target the abs with controlled movement.
instructions: Start in a dip position on parallel bars with hands grasping the bars and arms straight. Lift your knees up towards your chest, keeping your upper body still. Raise your knees as high as you can, then slowly lower them back down to the starting position. Control the movement throughout to target your core muscles effectively.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Parallel Bars Leg Raise
description: The parallel bars leg raise is an intermediate bodyweight core exercise performed in the top position of a dip on parallel bars. From a strong support position with arms locked out and shoulders stabilized, the legs are lifted under control to engage the abs, with minimal swinging. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions as part of ab-focused training, though many lifters must build strength and control before reaching higher rep ranges.
instructions: Hang from parallel bars with arms locked and shoulders stable. Lift your legs straight up, keeping them together, to engage your core. Raise your legs until they are at a 90-degree angle to the floor, then lower them back down under control. Avoid swinging your legs and focus on slow, deliberate movements.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Spider Crunch
description: The spider crunch is a core exercise performed from a straight-arm plank, driving one knee toward the same-side elbow with the knee tracking wide to emphasize the obliques and abs. It can be done for reps or time as bodyweight ab training or conditioning.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Toe-touch
description: The toe-touch is a bodyweight abdominal exercise performed lying on your back, lifting your legs and reaching your hands toward your toes to emphasize the rectus abdominis.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead. Lift your legs straight up towards the ceiling, then lift your shoulders off the ground. Reach your hands towards your toes, keeping your legs straight and core engaged. Lower your legs and arms back down to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid straining your back.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hanging Toes-To-Bar
description: An advanced abdominal exercise performed while hanging from a pull-up bar and lifting the legs until the toes touch the bar, challenging core, lats, and shoulder strength and control.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Engage your core and lift your legs straight up towards the bar. Keep your legs straight and control the movement as you touch your toes to the bar. Lower your legs back down to the starting position in a slow and controlled manner.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30 V-Up
description: A bodyweight core exercise where you simultaneously lift your upper body and legs to form a V shape, powerfully targeting the rectus abdominis while also engaging the hip flexors and stabilizing muscles of the trunk.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight. Lift your upper body and legs simultaneously, forming a V shape with your body. Keep your core engaged and hold for a brief moment at the top of the movement. Lower your body back down to the starting position, controlling the descent to maintain proper form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Cocoon Crunch
description: The cocoon crunch is a bodyweight core exercise that targets the rectus abdominis by simultaneously bringing the knees toward the chest and the arms from overhead to the center. It can be performed for time or repetitions as part of an ab-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight. Lift your arms and legs off the ground, bringing your knees toward your chest. As you lift, curl up and bring your arms forward to meet your knees, squeezing your abdominal muscles. Lower your arms and legs back to the starting position, controlling the movement to target your core.

name: Bench Oblique Crunch
description: The bench oblique crunch is an intermediate core exercise performed lying sideways on a bench to emphasize the obliques. With a controlled, small range of motion, you lift your upper body by contracting the obliques while keeping the lower body stable. It can be performed as an active hang, keeping the shoulders packed and upper-back muscles engaged, or as a passive hang, allowing the shoulders and upper body to relax and stretch. This movement helps build grip strength, shoulder stability, and can improve overhead mobility when performed with proper technique.
instructions: Find a sturdy pull-up bar and grasp it with both hands, shoulder-width apart. Hang from the bar with your arms fully extended, engaging your upper-back muscles to pack your shoulders. Keep your core tight and your body straight, or relax your shoulders and upper body for a passive hang. Hold the position for the desired amount of time, focusing on grip strength and shoulder stability.

name: Total Fitness Hollow Hold

Common in gymnastics and CrossFit, it teaches full-body tension and bracing for lifters and athletes. description: A partner-based Russian twist performed seated on the floor, rotating the torso side to side while passing a medicine ball to a partner. This move emphasizes the obliques and overall core stability and can be performed for repetitions or time in an ab-focused workout.
instructions: Sit on the floor with knees bent and feet flat, back straight, and hold a medicine ball. Twist torso to left, passing the medicine ball to your partner, then twist to right to receive the ball back. Keep movements slow and controlled, engaging core throughout the exercise. Focus on rotating torso, not just arms, to target obliques effectively.

name: Medicine Ball Half Moon
description: The medicine ball half moon is a dynamic core exercise where you lie on the floor and move your legs in a controlled arc over a stationary medicine ball, engaging both the rectus abdominis and obliques. It is typically performed for reps or time as part of ab-focused or medicine ball and bodyweight circuits.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, positioning a medicine ball near your hips. Lift your feet off the ground and hold them together, then slowly move them in a half-moon shape over the medicine ball. Keep your lower back pressed into the floor and engage your core throughout the movement. Return to the starting position and repeat for desired reps or time.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball Roll-Out
description: The exercise ball roll-out is a core-focused movement performed from a plank-style position with the forearms or hands on a ball. From a strong, neutral spine, the ball is slowly rolled forward as far as control allows, then pulled back to the start. The instability of the ball significantly challenges the abdominals, obliques, and shoulder stabilizers. Range of motion can be reduced for those building strength or learning proper control.
instructions: Start in a plank position with forearms or hands on the exercise ball. Engage your core and maintain a neutral spine. Slowly roll the ball forward, keeping control, then pull it back to the starting position. Keep movements slow and deliberate to challenge your core and maintain stability.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Medicine Ball Pass
description: A partner-based core exercise where two people stand facing each other and pass a medicine ball back and forth using controlled chest or overhead throws, engaging the abdominals to stabilize the torso throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand facing your partner with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the medicine ball at chest level and pass it to your partner using a controlled throw. Receive the ball from your partner, engaging your core to stabilize your torso, and immediately pass it back. Keep your movements smooth and controlled throughout the exercise.

name: Partner Medicine Ball Sit-Up
description: A partnered abdominal exercise where two people perform sit-ups facing each other, passing a medicine ball back and forth at the top of each rep. Each partner alternates between a weighted and unweighted sit-up, making it more challenging than a standard sit-up and suitable for time or reps in an ab-focused workout.
instructions: Sit on the floor facing your partner with knees bent and feet flat. Hold a medicine ball and perform a sit-up, passing the ball to your partner at the top of the movement. Your partner then performs a sit-up without the ball, while you lower back down. Alternate passing the ball back and forth at the top of each rep, switching between weighted and unweighted sit-ups.

name: Medicine Ball Russian Twist
description: A seated rotational core exercise where you hold a medicine ball in front of your chest, lean back slightly, lift or keep heels on the floor, and twist your torso to move the ball from side to side. It primarily targets the obliques and overall core stability.
instructions: Sit on floor with knees bent and feet lifted or heels on ground. Hold a medicine ball in front of your chest and lean back slightly. Twist your torso to left and right, moving the ball from side to side, keeping arms straight and core engaged. Keep movements slow and controlled to target your obliques and core.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Medicine Ball Slam
description: An explosive full-body movement where you lift a medicine ball overhead and powerfully slam it into the ground to build power, strength, and speed, primarily targeting the abdominals with significant involvement from the legs, shoulders, triceps, and lats.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest level. Lift the medicine ball overhead, extending arms fully and engaging core muscles. Slam the medicine ball into the ground in front of you, using your entire body to generate power. Catch the ball on the rebound and repeat, maintaining control throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Medicine Ball Twist
description: The medicine ball twist targets the abdominal muscles, especially the obliques, by having you hold a medicine ball close to your chest and rotate your torso from side to side for reps or time, similar to a Russian twist.
instructions: Sit on floor with knees bent and feet flat. Hold a medicine ball close to your chest with both hands. Twist your torso from side to side, touching the ball to the ground beside you each time. Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Oblique Crunch
description: The decline oblique crunch is a bodyweight core exercise performed on a decline surface to emphasize the obliques and rectus abdominis. Lying back on a decline bench with feet secured, you crunch up and rotate the torso to one side, then lower under control and repeat for high repetitions.
instructions: Lie back on a decline bench with feet secured. Crunch up and rotate your torso to one side, lifting your shoulders off the bench. Lower your torso back down under control, then repeat on the other side. Focus on slow and controlled movements to target your obliques and core muscles.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Sit-Up
description: The decline sit-up is a bodyweight core exercise performed on a decline surface to emphasize the rectus abdominis, often used for moderate to high repetitions in core-focused workouts.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with your knees bent and feet secured. Place your hands behind your head or across your chest for support. Lift your torso up towards your knees, curling up and squeezing your abdominal muscles. Lower your torso back down to the starting position in a controlled movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hanging Windshield Wiper
description: The hanging windshield wiper is an advanced core exercise performed while hanging from a bar, rotating the legs side to side to challenge the abs, obliques, and deep core, while also demanding significant strength from the lats, biceps, and grip.
instructions: Hang from a bar with an overhand grip, engaging your lats and core. Lift your legs straight out in front of you, then rotate them from side to side in a slow and controlled motion. Keep your upper body stable and focus on using your core to generate the movement. Lower your legs back to the starting position and repeat, maintaining a tight grip on the bar throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball Pull-In
description: An advanced core exercise performed from a plank position with the shins on a stability ball, pulling the knees toward the chest to engage the abdominals and hip flexors while maintaining a stable upper body.
instructions: Start in a plank position with shins on the stability ball. Engage your core and maintain a straight line from head to heels. Pull your knees toward your chest, keeping your upper body stable and your hips down. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid bouncing the ball.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball Crunch
description: The exercise ball crunch is an abdominal exercise performed while lying back on a stability ball to increase range of motion. The ball allows the spine to extend and flex more than on the floor, creating a greater stretch and contraction of the abs on each repetition. It can be done with a slow tempo for focused contractions or for higher repetitions as part of a core workout.
instructions: Lie back on a stability ball with feet flat on floor. Place hands behind head for support, but avoid pulling on neck. Lift torso up towards ceiling, curling up and squeezing abdominal muscles. Lower back down slowly to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Feet-Elevated Crunch
description: The feet-elevated crunch is a core exercise that reduces hip flexor involvement by elevating the feet, placing more emphasis on the abs through a shorter but intense range of motion.
instructions: Lie on a flat surface with knees bent and feet elevated on a bench or platform. Place hands behind head for support, not pulling on neck. Lift shoulders off the ground, curling up towards knees, then lower back down to starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Weighted Ball Side Bend
description: Stand upright holding a medicine ball at your side or in front of your chest. Brace your core and slowly bend sideways at the waist, lowering the ball toward the floor while keeping your hips stable and chest facing forward. Pause briefly at the bottom, then use your obliques to return to the starting position. Repeat on both sides, moving in a controlled manner and avoiding any twisting or jerking.
instructions: Stand upright holding a medicine ball at your side. Bend sideways at the waist, lowering the ball toward the floor while keeping your hips stable and chest facing forward. Pause briefly, then use your obliques to return to the starting position. Repeat on both sides, moving in a controlled manner.

name: Swiss Ball Ab Roll-Out
description: The Swiss Ball Ab Roll-Out is a core-strengthening exercise performed in a plank-style position with the forearms or hands on a stability ball. From a strong, neutral spine, you slowly roll the ball forward, extending your body while bracing the abs and maintaining control through the shoulders and upper back, then pull the ball back to the starting position. The instability of the ball significantly increases core engagement and difficulty, so beginners should start with a shorter range of motion.
instructions: Start in a plank position with forearms or hands on the Swiss Ball. Engage your core and maintain a neutral spine. Slowly roll the ball forward, extending your body while keeping your abs and shoulders stable. Return to the starting position by pulling the ball back with control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball Squat and Twist
description: A combination lower-body and core exercise where you hold an exercise ball close to your chest, perform a squat, and twist your torso at the top of the movement to engage the obliques. Typically done for higher reps or time as part of a bodyweight and exercise ball circuit.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding an exercise ball close to your chest. Lower your body into a squat, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes. As you stand up, twist your torso to one side, keeping the exercise ball close to your chest, then repeat on the other side. Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on controlled movements and proper form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Swiss Ball V-Up With Foot Pass
description: A core-focused V-up variation where you pass a ball between your hands and feet while lifting your upper and lower body into a V position, emphasizing the rectus abdominis.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and hold a Swiss ball with your hands. Lift your shoulders and legs off the ground, passing the ball to your feet. Hold the V position for a moment, then pass the ball back to your hands. Lower your body back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball Pike
description: The exercise ball pike is an intermediate core movement where you start in a plank with your feet elevated on a ball, then lift your hips toward the ceiling into a pike position before returning to the starting plank. It challenges core strength, shoulder stability, and balance, making it an effective centerpiece for ab-focused workouts or circuits.
instructions: Start in a plank position with your hands on the ground and your feet on an exercise ball. Engage your core and lift your hips toward the ceiling, forming a pike position. Keep your body straight from head to heels, squeezing your abs and shoulders. Slowly lower back down to the starting plank position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball Bird Dog
description: The exercise ball bird dog is a core stability movement performed with the torso supported on an exercise ball while opposite arm and leg extend. It targets the glutes and postural muscles of the upper back and core, and serves as a regression to the traditional bird dog. It can be used for warm-ups, mobility, or recovery, performed for time or repetitions.
instructions: Start by placing your torso on an exercise ball, engaging your core for stability. Position your hands under your shoulders and your knees under your hips, with your feet hip-width apart. Lift your right arm and left leg off the ground, extending them while keeping your core stable. Hold briefly, then lower and repeat with the opposite arm and leg.

name: Exercise Ball Superman
description: The exercise ball superman is an intermediate posterior-chain exercise performed prone over a stability ball. With the core braced, you extend the hips and raise the chest and arms to engage the spinal erectors, glutes, hamstrings, and upper back. It can be performed for time or repetitions as part of a warm-up, strength session, or mobility/recovery routine.
instructions: Lie on a stability ball with your chest down and engage your core. Place your hands on the floor in front of the ball for balance. Slowly lift your arms, chest, and hips off the ball, squeezing your glutes and lower back muscles. Hold for a brief moment, then slowly return to the starting position.

name: BOSU Cross-Body Crunch
description: The BOSU cross-body crunch is a dynamic core exercise performed on a stability surface to challenge the abs and obliques. Lying back on the dome with feet on the floor, you lift your upper body and bring one elbow toward the opposite knee in a twisting motion, alternating sides for controlled repetitions or time.
instructions: Lie back on the BOSU dome with feet on the floor. Lift your upper body and bring one elbow toward the opposite knee in a twisting motion. Alternate sides with controlled movements, keeping your core engaged. Focus on slow and deliberate repetitions to target your abs and obliques.

name: AM Ball Crunch
description: An exercise ball crunch performed with the lower back supported on a stability ball, allowing greater range of motion to fully stretch and contract the abdominals through each repetition.
instructions: Sit on a stability ball with your lower back supported and feet flat on the floor. Place your hands behind your head for support, but avoid pulling on your neck. Lift your torso up, curling forward and squeezing your abdominal muscles. Lower your torso back down to the starting position, controlling the movement to target your core muscles.

name: Bosu Cable Reverse Crunch
description: Lie back on a BOSU or stability surface with a cable attached to your ankles, then curl your hips and knees toward your chest to lift your pelvis off the surface, emphasizing the lower abs while keeping your core braced and movement controlled.
instructions: Lie back on a BOSU or stability surface with a cable attached to your ankles. Curl your hips and knees toward your chest, lifting your pelvis off the surface. Keep your core braced and movement controlled, emphasizing the contraction of your lower abs. Return to the starting position in a slow and controlled manner.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: BOSU Knee Tuck
description: A dynamic core exercise performed with your hands on the floor and feet on a BOSU or similar unstable surface, pulling the knees toward the chest to target the rectus abdominis and overall core stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands on the floor and feet on the BOSU ball. Engage your core and keep your body straight. Pull your knees toward your chest, lifting your hips off the BOSU ball. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to maintain balance and stability.

name: Feet-Elevated Side Plank on Ball
description: An advanced side plank variation where the feet are elevated on a ball to increase core and shoulder demand while challenging balance. Hold the body in a straight line from shoulders to feet, keeping the hips lifted and the obliques engaged throughout the hold.
instructions: Place your feet on a stability ball with your hands under your shoulders. Lift your hips off the ground, engaging your core and keeping your body in a straight line. Hold this position, keeping your obliques tight and your balance controlled. Maintain the hold for the desired duration, breathing naturally.

name: Hanging Exercise Ball Leg Raise
description: The hanging exercise ball leg raise is an intermediate core movement performed while hanging from a bar and holding a medicine ball between the legs. It primarily targets the abs and hip flexors while also engaging the forearms and shoulders for stabilization.
instructions: Hang from a bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Hold a medicine ball between your legs, squeezing it gently. Raise your legs straight up towards the ceiling, keeping the medicine ball in place, then slowly lower them back down to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and body stable throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Taylor Exercise Ball Stir-The-Pot
description: From a plank position with forearms on an exercise ball, brace your core and slowly make small circular stirring motions with your forearms while keeping your body in a straight line. Move in both clockwise and counterclockwise directions without letting your hips sag or rise.
instructions: Start in a plank position with forearms on an exercise ball. Brace your core to maintain a straight line from head to heels. Make small circular stirring motions with your forearms, moving in both clockwise and counterclockwise directions. Keep your body stable and engaged, avoiding hip sag or rise throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Feet-Elevated Plank on Ball
description: A straight-arm plank variation where the feet are elevated on a ball to increase core and shoulder stabilization demands. The body is kept in a rigid line from shoulders to heels, emphasizing the abs, shoulders, and overall trunk stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands on the ground and feet on a stability ball. Engage your core and keep your body in a straight line from shoulders to heels. Slowly lift your hips to a stable position, squeezing your abs and keeping your shoulders down. Hold this position for the desired time, maintaining control and a rigid body line.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Band Hip Adductions
description: Attach a resistance band to a sturdy anchor at ankle height and loop the other end around your inside ankle. Stand sideways to the anchor with feet hip-width apart and hold onto a support if needed. Keeping your torso upright and core braced, draw the working leg across your body toward the midline against the band’s resistance, then slowly return to the starting position with control. Repeat for reps, then switch sides.
instructions: Stand sideways to the anchor with feet hip-width apart. Loop the resistance band around your inside ankle and hold onto a support if needed. Draw the working leg across your body toward the midline against the band's resistance, then slowly return to the starting position with control. Repeat for reps on one side, then switch to the other side.

name: Lateral Band Walk
description: Place a resistance band around your lower legs, just above the ankles or knees. With a slight bend in your knees and hips pushed back, step laterally to one side while keeping constant tension on the band. Maintain a strong core and avoid your knees collapsing inward as you take controlled side steps in both directions.
instructions: Place a resistance band around your lower legs, just above the ankles or knees. Stand with a slight bend in your knees and hips pushed back, maintaining a strong core. Step laterally to one side, keeping constant tension on the band, and then bring the other leg to meet it, avoiding knee collapse. Repeat in both directions, taking controlled steps to work your legs and glutes.

name: Iliotibial Band SMR
description: Iliotibial band self-myofascial release (SMR) uses a foam roller to apply pressure along the outside of the upper leg to help reduce tightness and discomfort in the IT band area. The individual typically lies on their side with the roller under the outer thigh and slowly rolls to find tender spots, pausing briefly on them to encourage the tissue to relax. It is often paired with SMR or stretching for the glutes and quadriceps as part of a lower-body recovery or mobility routine.
instructions: Lie on your side with the foam roller under your outer thigh. Slowly roll the foam roller along the length of your outer thigh, applying pressure to the IT band area. Pause briefly on tender spots to encourage relaxation, then continue rolling. Focus on slow, controlled movements to effectively release tension in the IT band.

name: Adductor SMR
description: Adductor self-myofascial release (SMR) uses a foam roller to apply pressure along the inner thigh muscles to help reduce tightness and improve tissue quality. The individual typically lies on the floor with the inner thigh positioned on the foam roller, slowly rolling and pausing on tender spots to encourage the adductors to relax.
instructions: Lie on the floor with the foam roller under your inner thigh. Position the roller where you feel tightness or tension. Slowly roll the foam roller along your inner thigh, pausing on tender spots for 20-30 seconds to release tension. Breathe deeply and relax, allowing the adductor muscles to release and relax.

name: Lying Bent Leg Groin Stretch
description: Lie on your back with one knee bent and the foot flat on the floor, then let the bent knee fall outward to the side to gently stretch the inner thigh and groin. Hold the position without bouncing, keeping your lower back relaxed against the floor and breathing steadily throughout the stretch.
instructions: Lie on your back with one knee bent and foot flat on the floor. Let the bent knee fall outward to the side, stretching the inner thigh and groin. Keep your lower back relaxed against the floor and hold the position without bouncing. Breathe steadily throughout the stretch, holding for the desired time before switching sides.

name: Lateral Box Jump
description: A plyometric jump where you explode laterally onto a box or step and then step or jump back down, building lateral power, agility, and lower-body strength.
instructions: Stand beside a box or step with your feet shoulder-width apart. Bend your knees slightly and swing your arms to generate power, then explosively jump sideways onto the box. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately step or jump back down to the starting position. Repeat on the other side to target both legs and improve overall agility.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Thigh Adductor Machine
description: A seated machine exercise targeting the inner thighs by bringing the legs together against resistance, typically performed for moderate to high repetitions as part of lower-body training.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your feet on the footrests and knees bent. Place your legs on the padded levers with feet pointing outward. Squeeze your legs together to lift the weight, then slowly return to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Thigh Abductor
description: The thigh abductor is a lower-body strength exercise that targets the outer hips and glutes, typically performed for moderate to high repetitions as part of a lower-body workout.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart beside the machine. Place outer thigh against the pad and grasp handles for support. Lift the pad away from the center, squeezing outer hips and glutes, then control the return to starting position. Repeat for desired repetitions.

name: Standing Hip Circles
description: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and hands on your hips. Shift your weight evenly through both feet, then slowly draw large circles with your hips, moving them forward, to the side, back, and around in a smooth, controlled motion. Complete several circles in one direction, then reverse. Keep your torso upright and core lightly engaged to focus the movement through the hip joints.
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and hands on your hips. Shift your weight evenly through both feet. Draw large circles with your hips, moving them forward, to the side, back, and around in a smooth motion. Keep your torso upright and core lightly engaged to focus the movement through the hip joints.

name: Groiners
description: From a push-up position, step one foot up outside the same-side hand, dropping the hips toward the floor to stretch the inner thigh and hip. Alternate sides in a dynamic, controlled manner to mobilize the hips and adductors.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart. Step one foot up outside the same-side hand, lowering your hips toward the floor to stretch the inner thigh and hip. Alternate sides in a controlled manner, stepping the other foot up outside the opposite hand. Keep movements slow and deliberate to maintain balance and target the adductors.

name: Fire Hydrant
description: From an all-fours position, lift one bent leg out to the side while keeping your core braced and hips level, then lower with control. This targets the outer hips and glutes and helps improve hip stability.
