instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with a straight-bar attachment at shoulder height. Grasp the bar with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Curl the bar up towards your shoulders, keeping your upper arms still, then lower it back down to the starting position slowly. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the exercise.

name: Standing Reverse-Grip Cable Curl
description: A cable-based curl performed standing with an overhand (pronated) grip on a straight or EZ-style cable attachment. The constant tension from the cable emphasizes the forearms and brachialis while still training the biceps. Typically used with moderate to high reps in upper-body or arm-focused workouts.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with the cable at chest height. Hold the straight or EZ-style cable attachment with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Keeping your upper arms still, curl the cable towards your shoulders, squeezing your biceps at the top. Lower the cable back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Lying High-Cable Biceps Curl
description: The lying high-cable biceps curl is a single-joint exercise performed face-up, pulling the cable handles toward the head to isolate and strengthen the biceps. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions as part of an arm-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench under a high-cable machine with feet planted on the floor. Grasp the cable handles with palms facing forward. Pull the handles towards your head, keeping upper arms still, and curl the handles up to shoulder height. Lower the handles back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging.

name: High Cable Curls
description: The high cable curl is a single-joint exercise targeting the biceps using a cable machine, typically performed for moderate to high repetitions during arm-focused workouts.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the cable at shoulder height. Grasp the handle with an underhand grip, keeping your upper arms still. Curl the handle down towards your shoulders, squeezing your biceps at the top. Lower the handle back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Seated Cable Biceps Curl
description: The seated cable biceps curl is a single-joint arm exercise that isolates the biceps. The seated position reduces momentum, allowing for strict form and continuous tension from the cable. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions to create a strong burn and pump in the biceps as part of an arm-focused workout.
instructions: Sit at cable machine with feet flat on floor. Grasp cable bar with palms facing forward, keeping upper arms still. Curl the bar up towards shoulders, squeezing biceps at the top. Lower the bar back down to starting position, controlling the movement to maintain tension.

name: Double Arm Cable Biceps Curl
description: Stand facing a cable machine with a straight bar attached to the low pulley. Grip the bar with an underhand shoulder-width grip, brace your core, and keep your elbows close to your sides. Curl the bar up toward your shoulders by flexing your biceps, avoiding swinging or using momentum. Squeeze at the top, then slowly lower the bar back to the starting position with control.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a straight bar at a low pulley. Grip the bar with an underhand shoulder-width grip and keep your elbows close to your sides. Curl the bar up toward your shoulders by flexing your biceps, squeezing at the top. Lower the bar back to the starting position with control, avoiding swinging or momentum.

name: AM Cable Biceps Curl
description: The AM cable biceps curl is a standing curl performed with a straight bar attached to a low cable. It keeps constant tension on the biceps throughout the range of motion and is often used as a burnout move for moderate to high reps (8-12) in upper-body or arm-focused workouts.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the straight bar with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Curl the bar up towards your shoulders, keeping your upper arms still, then slowly lower it back to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the movement.

name: Single-arm cable biceps curl
description: A single-joint isolation exercise using a cable machine to target the biceps with strict, controlled curls performed one arm at a time, typically for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the cable at shoulder height. Hold the cable handle with one hand, palm facing forward, and keep your upper arm still. Curl the handle towards your shoulder, keeping your elbow close to your body, then slowly lower it back to the starting position. Repeat with the other arm to complete the exercise.

name: 30 Arms Squatting Cable Straight-Bar Curl
description: From a squat position facing a low cable pulley with a straight bar attached, hold the bar with an underhand grip and curl it toward your shoulders while maintaining the squat. Keep your elbows close to your sides, squeeze the biceps at the top, then lower the bar under control without letting your torso lean forward.
instructions: Face a low cable pulley with a straight bar attached and assume a squat position. Hold the bar with an underhand grip, keeping your elbows close to your body. Curl the bar toward your shoulders, squeezing your biceps at the top, then lower it under control without leaning forward. Maintain the squat position throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30° Arms High Cable Curl
description: Stand between two high cable pulleys with arms raised out to the sides at about 30 degrees above shoulder height, elbows slightly bent. Grip the handles with palms facing up and curl the handles toward your head by flexing the biceps, keeping upper arms fixed and shoulders stable. Squeeze at the top, then slowly extend the elbows back to the starting position under control.
instructions: Stand between two high cable pulleys with arms raised at 30 degrees above shoulder height. Grip the handles with palms facing up and curl the handles toward your head by flexing the biceps. Keep your upper arms fixed and shoulders stable throughout the movement. Lower the handles back to the starting position slowly and under control.

name: Cable Straight-Bar Curl
description: Stand facing a cable machine with a straight bar attached to the low pulley. Grip the bar with an underhand shoulder-width grip, keep your elbows close to your sides, and curl the bar up toward your shoulders by flexing your biceps. Squeeze at the top, then lower the bar under control to full elbow extension without swinging your body.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a straight bar attached to the low pulley. Grip the bar with an underhand shoulder-width grip, keeping your elbows close to your sides. Curl the bar up toward your shoulders by flexing your biceps, squeezing at the top. Lower the bar under control to full elbow extension, avoiding body swing.

name: Cable Concentration Curl
description: Sit or stand beside a low cable pulley with a single handle attached. Brace your upper arm against the inside of your thigh (or keep it fixed by your side), then curl the handle up toward your shoulder, squeezing the biceps at the top before lowering under control. This isolates the biceps similarly to a dumbbell concentration curl but uses constant cable tension.
instructions: Sit or stand beside a low cable pulley with a single handle attached. Place your upper arm against the inside of your thigh to keep it stable. Curl the handle up toward your shoulder, squeezing your biceps at the top. Lower the handle back down under control to the starting position.

name: 30 Arms BFR High Cable Curl
description: An isolation biceps curl performed on a high cable, typically used with blood flow restriction (BFR) to increase muscle activation with lighter loads. Stand facing away or toward the cable, elbows kept high and slightly in front of the body, and curl the handle toward your head while keeping constant tension on the biceps.
instructions: Stand facing the cable with elbows high and slightly in front of your body. Hold the handle with your palms facing away from you. Curl the handle toward your head, keeping constant tension on your biceps and your elbows in place. Keep your upper arms still and only move your forearms to complete the curl.

name: BFR Cable Rope Hammer Curl
description: The blood flow restriction (BFR) cable rope hammer curl is an arm exercise performed on a cable stack using a rope attachment and BFR bands placed high on the upper arms. It targets the biceps and forearms with a neutral (hammer) grip, typically using light loads for high repetitions to create a strong muscle pump and promote hypertrophy.
instructions: Stand facing a cable stack with the rope attachment at shoulder height. Place BFR bands high on your upper arms and hold the rope with a neutral grip, palms facing each other. Curl the rope towards your shoulders, keeping your upper arms still, then slowly return to the starting position. Focus on squeezing your biceps and forearms throughout the movement.

name: Machine Preacher Curls
description: The machine preacher curl is a beginner-friendly isolation exercise that targets the biceps using a preacher curl machine to provide guided resistance and support.
instructions: Sit at the preacher curl machine with arms resting on the pad. Grasp the bar with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Curl the bar up towards your shoulders, squeezing your biceps at the top. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Single-arm Machine Preacher Curl
description: The single-arm machine preacher curl is an isolation exercise targeting the biceps using a selectorized or plate-loaded curl machine with a preacher-style pad. The arm is braced on the angled pad to minimize shoulder involvement and momentum, allowing strict elbow flexion through a controlled range of motion. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8-12 or more per set, within an upper-body or arm-focused workout to build biceps size and strength.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your arm resting on the preacher-style pad. Grasp the handle with your palm facing forward and brace your arm on the pad. Curl the handle up towards your shoulder, keeping your upper arm still, and then lower it back down to the starting position. Focus on slow and controlled movements to target your biceps.

name: Machine Preacher Curl
description: The machine preacher curl is an isolation exercise for building size and strength in the biceps using a selectorized or plate-loaded machine. The upper arms rest on an angled pad to limit momentum and shoulder involvement, placing constant tension on the biceps through a controlled curling motion. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions as part of an upper-body or arm-focused workout.
instructions: Sit at the machine with upper arms resting on the angled pad. Grasp the bar with palms facing forward and lift the weight up by curling your arms, keeping upper arms still. Lower the weight back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Focus on squeezing your biceps at the top of the curl.

name: Smith Machine Drag Curl
description: The Smith machine drag curl is a biceps-focused curl variation performed on a Smith machine. The bar is kept close to the torso and dragged upward, emphasizing elbow flexion and biceps contraction with reduced shoulder involvement. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions (8–15 per set) as part of an arm or upper-body workout.
instructions: Stand in a Smith machine with the bar at thigh level. Hold the bar with an underhand grip, keeping your upper arms still and close to your torso. Drag the bar up your thighs, curling your elbows and squeezing your biceps. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: 30 Arms BFR Machine Preacher Curl
description: An isolation biceps curl performed on a preacher-style machine while using blood flow restriction (BFR) cuffs to increase training stimulus with lighter loads.
instructions: Sit at the 30 Arms BFR Machine with arms resting on the preacher curl pad. Place BFR cuffs on upper arms, inflating to the recommended pressure. Grasp the bar with hands shoulder-width apart and curl the bar up towards your shoulders, keeping upper arms still. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Seated Biceps Stretch
description: Sit upright and extend one arm straight in front of you with the palm facing up. Gently pull back on the fingers with the opposite hand until you feel a stretch along the front of the arm and into the biceps. Hold, then switch sides, keeping shoulders relaxed and posture tall throughout.
instructions: Sit upright with good posture. Extend one arm straight in front of you with palm facing up. Gently pull back on the fingers with your opposite hand until you feel a stretch in your biceps. Hold and then switch arms to stretch the other side.

name: EZ-Bar Curl
description: The standing EZ-bar curl is a biceps-focused exercise performed with an EZ-bar, typically for moderate to high repetitions in upper-body or arm workouts.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the EZ-bar with palms facing forward. Hold the bar at shoulder width, with arms extended and hands evenly spaced. Curl the bar up towards shoulders, keeping upper arms still and only moving forearms. Lower the bar back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging or jerking.

name: Reverse Barbell Preacher Curls
description: The reverse barbell preacher curl is a biceps curl variation performed with an overhand (pronated) grip on a barbell while the upper arms rest on a preacher bench. The overhand grip shifts emphasis from the biceps to the forearms and brachialis, helping develop overall arm thickness and grip strength. Keep the wrists neutral, control the lowering phase, and avoid swinging or lifting the elbows off the pad.
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench with upper arms resting on the pad. Grasp a barbell with an overhand grip, keeping wrists neutral. Lower the barbell down slowly, controlling the movement, then curl it back up to the starting position. Keep elbows on the pad throughout the exercise.

name: Close-Grip EZ-Bar Curl with Band
description: Stand holding an EZ bar with a close underhand grip while a resistance band adds extra tension. Keep your elbows close to your sides and curl the bar up toward your shoulders, squeezing the biceps at the top, then lower under control to full extension.
instructions: Stand holding an EZ bar with a close underhand grip. Keep your elbows close to your sides and curl the bar up toward your shoulders. Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement. Lower the bar under control to full extension, keeping tension in the band.

name: Standing EZ-Bar Curl
description: The standing EZ-bar curl is an intermediate biceps exercise performed while standing and curling an EZ-shaped bar to build arm size and strength. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions in upper-body or arm-focused workouts.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding an EZ-bar with palms facing forward. Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Curl the bar up towards shoulders, keeping upper arms still, then lower it back down to the starting position. Keep core engaged and back straight throughout the exercise.

name: 30 Arms Standing Overhand EZ-Bar Curl
description: Stand holding an EZ bar with an overhand (pronated) grip at shoulder-width, arms extended and elbows close to your sides. Keeping your upper arms stationary and wrists neutral, curl the bar up by flexing your elbows until your forearms are just short of vertical. Squeeze the biceps and forearms at the top, then lower the bar under control to the starting position without swinging your torso.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding an EZ bar with an overhand grip. Keep your upper arms still and wrists neutral, then curl the bar up by bending your elbows. Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement, then lower the bar back down to the starting position under control. Keep your torso stable throughout the exercise.

name: EZ-Bar Preacher Curl
description: An isolation curl performed with an EZ bar on a preacher bench to target the biceps while minimizing shoulder involvement and momentum.
instructions: Sit at the preacher bench with arms resting on the pad. Grasp the EZ bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder width. Curl the bar up towards your shoulders, squeezing your biceps at the top. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: 30 Arms Standing EZ-Bar Curl
description: Stand holding an EZ bar with an underhand grip, hands just outside hip-width. Keeping your elbows close to your sides and torso upright, curl the bar up toward your shoulders by flexing your biceps, then lower under control to full elbow extension. Avoid swinging your body or letting your elbows drift forward.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding an EZ bar with an underhand grip. Keep your elbows close to your sides and torso upright. Curl the bar up toward your shoulders by flexing your biceps, then lower under control to full elbow extension. Avoid swinging your body or letting your elbows drift forward.

name: Calf Raises with Resistance Bands
description: Stand with the balls of your feet on the floor and a resistance band anchored under your feet, holding the ends in your hands or around your shoulders. Keeping your legs straight and core braced, press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing your calves at the top. Lower your heels under control back to the starting position and repeat for reps.
instructions: Stand with the balls of your feet on the floor and a resistance band anchored under your feet. Hold the ends of the band in your hands or around your shoulders. Press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing your calves at the top. Lower your heels under control back to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Peroneals Stretch
description: A stretch targeting the peroneal muscles along the outer lower leg to improve ankle mobility and reduce tightness in the calves and surrounding tissues.
instructions: Sit on floor with affected leg crossed over other leg. Place hand on knee of crossed leg and pull it toward opposite shoulder. Hold stretch for 15-30 seconds, feeling stretch on outer lower leg. Switch legs and repeat to maintain even flexibility.

name: Posterior Tibialis Stretch
description: A stretch targeting the posterior tibialis and surrounding calf muscles by gently pulling the foot into eversion and dorsiflexion to relieve tightness along the inner lower leg and ankle.
instructions: Sit on floor with affected leg crossed over other leg. Place hand on foot and gently pull it outward and up, stretching inner lower leg and ankle. Hold stretch for 15-30 seconds, feeling gentle pull in calf and inner leg. Repeat on other side to maintain even flexibility.

name: Calf SMR
description: Calf self-myofascial release (SMR) uses a foam roller or similar tool to apply pressure to tight or sore calf muscles. The individual typically sits on the floor with the roller under the lower leg and slowly rolls along the length of the calf, pausing on tender spots to encourage the muscle to relax. This technique is often used before or after workouts to reduce tightness and improve lower-leg mobility, and can be paired with quadriceps SMR or calf stretching.
instructions: Sit on the floor with the foam roller under your lower leg. Place the roller under your calf and slowly roll it along the length of the muscle. Pause on tender spots and hold for a few seconds to release tension. Roll back and forth, applying moderate pressure, to relax the muscle and improve mobility.

name: Rocking Standing Calf Raise
description: Stand upright holding a barbell across your upper back with feet hip-width apart. Shift your weight forward onto the balls of your feet and rise up into a calf raise, squeezing your calves at the top. Then slowly rock your weight back toward your heels while maintaining control, lowering your heels toward the floor before repeating the forward rocking motion. Keep your core braced, knees soft, and movements smooth throughout the exercise.
instructions: Stand upright with a barbell across your upper back and feet hip-width apart. Shift your weight forward onto the balls of your feet and rise up into a calf raise, squeezing your calves. Slowly rock back onto your heels, lowering them toward the floor, then repeat the forward motion. Keep your core braced and knees soft throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Standing Barbell Calf Raise
description: The standing barbell calf raise targets the calf muscles, especially the gastrocnemius, by lifting the heels while supporting a barbell across the upper back. Performed standing, it can be done for reps or time and may also be done in a Smith machine for added stability.
instructions: Stand on a step or platform with a barbell across your upper back. Hold the barbell with your hands at shoulder width and your feet shoulder-width apart. Lift your heels as high as possible, then lower them back down to the starting position. Keep your knees straight and core engaged throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Seated Calf Raise
description: Sit on a bench with your feet flat on the floor and a barbell resting across your thighs, just above the knees. Lift your heels by pressing through the balls of your feet, contracting your calves at the top, then slowly lower your heels back toward the floor under control. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping your core braced and upper body stable.
instructions: Sit on a bench with feet flat on the floor and a barbell across your thighs. Lift your heels by pressing through the balls of your feet, contracting your calves at the top. Slowly lower your heels back toward the floor under control. Keep your core braced and upper body stable throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Standing Calf Raise
description: A standing calf exercise performed in a Smith machine to isolate and strengthen the gastrocnemius muscles using controlled, moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Stand under the Smith machine bar with feet shoulder-width apart on the edge of a step or platform. Raise up onto tiptoes, then lower heels back down to the starting position, squeezing calf muscles at the top. Keep knees straight and core engaged throughout the movement. Control the return to starting position to avoid bouncing or jerking.

name: Calf Raise On A Dumbbell
description: Stand with the balls of your feet balanced on a dumbbell handle, holding onto a stable surface for support if needed. Keeping your knees slightly soft and torso upright, lower your heels toward the floor to feel a stretch in your calves, then press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible. Pause briefly at the top, squeezing your calves, then lower under control and repeat for the desired reps.
instructions: Stand on a dumbbell handle with the balls of your feet. Hold onto a stable surface for support if needed. Lower your heels to the floor, then press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing your calves at the top. Repeat for the desired reps, keeping your knees slightly soft and torso upright.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Standing Dumbbell Calf Raise
description: The standing dumbbell calf raise targets the calf muscles, especially the gastrocnemius, by lifting your heels off the ground while holding dumbbells. It can be performed with one or two dumbbells, using a free hand to hold a stable object if additional balance support is needed.
instructions: Stand on the floor with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand. Lift your heels off the ground, raising up onto your tiptoes. Slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Keep your knees straight and core engaged to maintain balance and stability.

name: Single-leg Standing Dumbbell Calf Raise
description: Stand tall holding one or two dumbbells at your sides. Shift your weight onto one leg, using your free hand to lightly hold a stable object if needed for balance. From a flat foot, press through the ball of your foot to raise your heel as high as possible, squeezing the calf at the top. Pause briefly, then lower your heel under control to a full stretch and repeat for reps before switching legs.
instructions: Stand tall holding dumbbells at your sides. Shift your weight onto one leg, using your free hand for balance if needed. Raise your heel by pressing through the ball of your foot, squeezing your calf at the top. Lower your heel under control to a full stretch and repeat.

name: Dumbbell Walking Calf Raise
description: The dumbbell walking calf raise is a dynamic lower-leg exercise where you walk forward on the balls of your feet while holding dumbbells at your sides, lifting your heels with each step to target the calf muscles.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding dumbbells at your sides. Take a step forward with one foot, lifting your heel off the ground as you transfer your weight onto the ball of that foot. Bring the other foot forward to meet the first foot, repeating the heel lift with each step. Keep your knees straight and focus on lifting your heels to target your calf muscles.

name: Balance Board
description: Stand on a balance board with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent, maintaining an upright posture while keeping the board as level as possible. Focus on engaging your calves and core to control small shifts in weight and stabilize the board without letting the edges touch the ground.
instructions: Stand on the balance board with feet hip-width apart. Keep your knees slightly bent and maintain an upright posture. Engage your calves and core to control small shifts in weight and stabilize the board. Keep the board level, avoiding letting the edges touch the ground.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Weighted Donkey Calf Raise
description: A calf-focused strength exercise performed by hinging at the hips with the torso supported (often on a bench or similar surface) while a weight is attached via a belt around the hips. The movement involves raising and lowering the heels through a full range of motion to target the gastrocnemius and overall calf development.
instructions: Stand on a step or platform with hips under a weighted belt. Hinge at the hips and place hands on a bench for support. Raise your heels as high as possible, then lower them back down to the starting position, squeezing your calf muscles at the top of the movement. Keep your knees straight throughout the exercise.

name: Anterior Tibialis Self-Myofascial Release (SMR)
description: Beginner-friendly self-myofascial release targeting the front of the lower leg (anterior tibialis) by gently rolling along the muscle to reduce tightness and improve ankle mobility.
instructions: Sit on floor with affected leg straight out in front. Place foam roller under front of lower leg, just above ankle. Slowly roll foam roller up and down, applying gentle pressure to release tension in the muscle. Focus on tender areas, holding for a few seconds to allow relaxation.

name: Foot Self-Myofascial Release (Foot-SMR)
description: A self-massage technique where you roll the bottom of your foot over a small object to release tension in the plantar fascia and calf complex, helping improve mobility and reduce tightness.
instructions: Sit on floor with affected leg crossed over other leg. Place a small object, such as a tennis ball or foam roller, under the bottom of your foot. Roll the object back and forth, applying moderate pressure, to release tension in the plantar fascia and calf complex. Focus on tender areas, holding for a few seconds to allow relaxation.

name: Agility Ladder Drills
description: A series of fast footwork patterns performed through an agility ladder to improve speed, coordination, and cardiovascular fitness.
instructions: Start by placing the agility ladder on a flat surface. Stand at one end of the ladder with feet shoulder-width apart. Perform the desired footwork pattern, such as high knees or lateral shuffles, through the ladder at a rapid pace. Focus on quick turnover and proper foot placement to complete the drill.

name: Single-Leg Machine Seated Calf Raise
description: A seated calf raise variation performed one leg at a time to isolate and strengthen the calf muscles, with emphasis on the soleus. Performed with controlled reps, typically in moderate to high rep ranges.
instructions: Sit at the machine with one foot on the platform and the other foot lifted off. Lower the platform down by dropping your heel, then raise it up by lifting your heel as high as possible, squeezing your calf muscle. Control the movement throughout the entire range of motion. Switch legs after completing the desired number of reps.

name: Smith Machine Calf Raise
description: A standing calf exercise performed in a Smith Machine, targeting the gastrocnemius by raising and lowering the heels under load for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Stand under the Smith Machine bar with feet shoulder-width apart on the edge of the platform. Raise up onto tiptoes, then lower heels back down to the starting position, squeezing calf muscles at the top. Keep knees straight and control the movement throughout the exercise. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Standing Calf Raises
description: The standing calf raise is a lower-leg exercise that targets the gastrocnemius muscles by lifting your heels off the ground while keeping your knees mostly straight. It can be performed unweighted or with added resistance and is often done for higher repetitions or time.
instructions: Stand on a step or platform with heels hanging off the edge. Keep your knees straight and raise up onto your tiptoes, lifting your heels as high as possible. Lower your heels back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Seated Calf Raise
description: A seated calf raise primarily targeting the calf muscles, especially the soleus, by lifting the heels against resistance while the knees are bent. Typically performed for moderate to high repetitions to build strength and muscular endurance in the lower legs.
instructions: Sit on the calf raise machine with knees bent and feet on the platform. Place your heels on the edge of the step and push the pads down with your knees. Lift your heels as high as possible, squeezing your calf muscles. Lower your heels back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Calf Press on the Leg Press Machine
description: Using a leg press machine, place the balls of your feet on the lower edge of the platform with your heels hanging off. Keeping your knees slightly bent but fixed, press the platform away by extending your ankles and lifting your heels, then lower under control to stretch the calves before repeating.
instructions: Place the balls of your feet on the lower edge of the leg press platform with your heels hanging off. Keep your knees slightly bent and fixed in position. Press the platform away by extending your ankles and lifting your heels, then lower under control to stretch the calves. Return to the starting position and repeat.

name: Calf Press
description: The calf press is a strength exercise that targets the calf muscles, especially the gastrocnemius, by pressing through the balls of the feet to raise and lower the heels under load. It is often performed for higher repetitions or time to build muscular endurance and definition in the lower leg.
instructions: Sit at the calf press machine with feet on the platform. Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the platform with heels hanging off. Press down through the balls of your feet to raise the heels, then slowly lower them back down to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Reverse Calf Raises
description: Stand with the balls of your feet on a raised surface under the Smith machine bar, heels hanging off the edge. With the bar resting securely across your upper back and shoulders, lower your heels toward the floor to stretch the calves, then press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, contracting the calves at the top before slowly lowering back down.
instructions: Stand under the Smith machine with the balls of your feet on a raised surface. Lower your heels toward the floor, stretching your calves. Press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, contracting your calf muscles. Slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hack Squat Calf Raise
description: The hack squat calf raise is a calf-focused exercise performed in a hack squat machine. With the shoulders supported by the pads and feet placed on the platform edge, you extend the ankles to raise your heels and then lower under control. This setup allows for heavier loading than many standing variations while keeping the upper body stable, and it is commonly performed for higher repetitions, such as 10–20 reps per set.
instructions: Stand in the hack squat machine with shoulders against the pads and feet on the platform edge. Place your feet so that your heels extend off the edge of the platform. Raise your heels by extending your ankles, then lower them back down under control. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping your upper body stable throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Leg Press Calf Raise
description: The leg press calf raise is a calf-focused exercise performed on a leg press machine, allowing heavy loading with added shoulder comfort compared to standing calf raises. It is typically performed for higher repetitions, such as 10–20 reps per set or more, to build calf strength and endurance.
instructions: Sit in the leg press machine with your feet on the platform, toes pointed upward. Press the platform away from you, extending your legs. Lift your heels as high as possible, raising your calves, then lower them back down to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Machine Seated Calf Raise
description: The machine seated calf raise is an isolation exercise that targets the calf muscles, especially the soleus, by performing ankle plantarflexion from a seated position with resistance. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions to build strength and muscular endurance in the lower leg.
instructions: Sit in the machine with feet on the platform and knees under the pad. Push the platform down with your heels to raise the weight, extending your ankles. Lower the platform back down to the starting position, stretching your calf muscles. Control the movement throughout the exercise to target your calf muscles effectively.

name: Peroneals Self-Myofascial Release (SMR)
description: Lie on your side with the outer lower leg (peroneals) resting on a foam roller or similar tool. Support your upper body with your hands or forearm and slowly roll from just below the knee to just above the ankle, pausing on tender spots for 20–30 seconds while breathing steadily. Adjust pressure by shifting more or less bodyweight onto the roller.
instructions: Lie on your side with your outer lower leg on a foam roller. Support your upper body with your hands or forearm. Slowly roll the foam roller from just below the knee to just above the ankle, pausing on tender spots for 20-30 seconds. Adjust pressure by shifting your bodyweight onto the roller.

name: Knee Circles
description: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and hands resting lightly on your thighs. Bend your knees slightly and bring them together, then slowly move your knees in a circular motion, tracing small circles in one direction for the desired number of repetitions before reversing the direction. Keep your heels on the ground and movements controlled to gently mobilize the knees and ankles while lightly engaging the calves.
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and hands on thighs. Bend knees slightly and bring them together. Move knees in small circular motions, first in one direction, then reverse direction, keeping heels on the ground and movements controlled. Keep the movement gentle to mobilize knees and ankles.

name: Standing Gastrocnemius Calf Stretch
description: Stand facing a wall with one foot stepped back and the heel firmly on the floor. Keep the back leg straight and the front knee slightly bent as you lean your hips and chest toward the wall until you feel a stretch in the calf of the back leg. Hold the stretch without bouncing, then switch sides.
instructions: Stand facing a wall with one foot stepped back and the heel on the floor. Keep the back leg straight and the front knee slightly bent. Lean your hips and chest toward the wall until you feel a stretch in the calf of the back leg. Hold the stretch and then switch sides.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Wall Calf Stretch
description: The wall calf stretch is a simple stretch targeting the calf muscles, performed by placing your hands on a wall and stepping one foot back to lengthen the calf without any equipment.
instructions: Stand facing a wall with one hand on the wall for balance. Step one foot back about a foot, keeping your heel on the ground and bending the front knee. Lean forward, stretching your calf muscle, and hold for 15-30 seconds. Switch legs and repeat to stretch the other calf.

name: Seated Calf Stretch
description: Sit on the floor with your legs extended in front of you. Loop a band, towel, or your hands around the ball of one foot. Keeping your knee straight and torso upright, gently pull the foot toward you until you feel a stretch along the calf. Hold for the desired time, then switch sides, maintaining steady breathing and avoiding bouncing.
instructions: Sit on the floor with legs extended in front of you. Loop a band or towel around the ball of one foot, keeping your knee straight. Gently pull your foot toward you until you feel a calf stretch, holding for the desired time. Switch sides and repeat, breathing steadily and avoiding bouncing.

name: Ankle Circles
description: Sit or stand and slowly rotate your ankle in a circular motion, drawing a full range of motion in both directions to gently mobilize the joint and warm up the lower leg.
instructions: Sit or stand with your foot relaxed. Rotate your ankle in a circular motion, first clockwise and then counterclockwise. Move slowly and control the movement to feel the full range of motion. Repeat for several repetitions to warm up your lower leg.

name: Calf Stretch Elbows Against Wall
description: Stand facing a wall with your elbows resting against it at shoulder height. Step one foot back, keeping the back leg straight and heel pressed toward the floor while the front knee bends slightly. Lean your hips toward the wall until you feel a stretch in the calf of the back leg. Hold for the desired time, then switch sides.
instructions: Stand facing a wall with elbows at shoulder height against it. Step one foot back, keeping the back leg straight and heel on the floor. Lean your hips toward the wall until you feel a calf stretch, holding for the desired time. Switch sides to stretch the other calf.

name: Standing Soleus And Achilles Stretch
description: Stand facing a wall with one foot slightly behind the other. Bend both knees while keeping your heels on the ground and lean gently toward the wall until you feel a stretch in the lower calf and Achilles of the back leg. Hold the stretch, then switch sides, keeping movements controlled and pain-free.
instructions: Stand facing a wall with one foot slightly behind the other. Bend both knees, keeping heels on the ground, and lean toward the wall. Hold the stretch when you feel it in your lower calf and Achilles, then switch sides. Keep movements controlled and pain-free.

name: Band Crossover
description: Stand between two anchored resistance bands set at about chest height. With a handle in each hand and arms slightly bent, step forward to create tension. Bring your hands together in front of your chest in a sweeping motion, crossing one hand slightly over the other, then slowly return to the starting position under control. Focus on squeezing the chest at the midpoint of the movement.
instructions: Stand between two anchored resistance bands at chest height. Hold a handle in each hand with arms slightly bent and step forward to create tension. Bring your hands together in front of your chest, crossing one over the other, squeezing your chest muscles. Return to the starting position slowly, controlling the movement.

name: Band-Resisted Bench Press
description: Lie on a bench and press a barbell or light bar upward while resistance bands add extra tension, emphasizing chest strength through the full range of motion.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor. Grip the barbell or light bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, ensuring the resistance bands are securely attached. Lower the bar to your chest, then press upward, extending your arms fully while keeping the bands taut. Control the movement throughout the entire range of motion.

name: Bench Press with Short Bands
description: A bench press variation using short resistance bands to add accommodating resistance, increasing tension as you press the weight away from your chest.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor. Grip the barbell with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, with short resistance bands attached to the bar and anchored to the bench or floor. Lower the bar to your chest, then press upwards, extending your arms fully, and control the return to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and back pressed against the bench throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Feet-Elevated TRX Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with the feet elevated in TRX/suspension straps, increasing the load on the chest, shoulders, and core while challenging stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with feet suspended in TRX straps. Engage your core and keep your body straight from head to heels. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your movements controlled and stable throughout the exercise.

name: Rusin Band Pull-Apart
description: A band pull-apart variation emphasizing controlled scapular retraction and shoulder stability by pulling a resistance band apart across the chest with strict form.
instructions: Hold a resistance band with both hands at shoulder height, arms extended to the sides. Pull the band apart, keeping arms straight, and focus on squeezing shoulder blades together. Maintain strict form and control throughout the movement, avoiding swinging or jerking. Return to the starting position slowly, keeping tension on the band.

name: Taylor Band-Assisted One-Arm Push-Up
description: A one-arm push-up variation using a resistance band for assistance, reducing load on the working arm while targeting the chest, shoulders, and triceps.
instructions: Start in a plank position with one hand on the ground and the other holding a resistance band anchored above you. Loop the band around your working arm to reduce the load. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and alternate arms with each rep.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Incline Band Bench Press
description: The incline band bench press is an upper-body pressing exercise performed on an incline that emphasizes the upper chest while also engaging the shoulders and triceps. Elastic resistance increases tension as you press upward, making it well-suited for moderate to high repetitions in chest or upper-body workouts.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with your back against the pad. Hold a resistance band in both hands, with the band anchored behind you. Press the band upward, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position, squeezing your chest muscles. Control the movement throughout the exercise to work your upper chest, shoulders, and triceps effectively.

name: Band Push-Up
description: The band push-up is an intermediate progression of the standard push-up that uses a resistance band across the upper back to increase loading through the top of the movement. It targets the chest, triceps, and shoulders while also engaging the upper back and core for stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with a resistance band across your upper back. Place your hands shoulder-width apart on the ground and engage your core for stability. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position, squeezing your chest and shoulders. Keep your movements slow and controlled throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Band Chest Fly
description: A single-joint chest isolation exercise using resistance bands. Performed standing or staggered stance, you anchor the band behind you at chest height, hold the handles with a slight bend in the elbows, and bring your arms together in front of your chest in a hugging motion, emphasizing a strong squeeze of the pectoral muscles before returning under control.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, anchoring the band behind you at chest height. Hold the handles with a slight bend in the elbows, keeping your arms at shoulder height. Bring your arms together in front of your chest in a hugging motion, squeezing your pectoral muscles. Return to the starting position under control, avoiding jerky movements.

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
description: The close-grip bench press is a barbell pressing variation that emphasizes the triceps while still engaging the chest and shoulders. By bringing the hands closer together than in a traditional bench press, more load is shifted to the triceps, making it an effective accessory for improving pressing strength and arm development. It can be performed with heavier loads for strength or moderate to high reps for muscle and strength endurance.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor. Grip the barbell with hands closer together than shoulder-width apart. Lower the bar to your chest, then press upwards, extending your arms fully, squeezing your triceps at the top. Return the bar to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Decline Barbell Bench Press
description: The decline barbell bench press is a compound upper-body exercise performed on a decline bench to emphasize the lower portion of the chest. Using a barbell, you lower the weight to the lower chest and press it back up, building strength and size in the pectorals while also engaging the shoulders and triceps.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with feet securely on the floor. Grasp the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower the barbell to the lower chest, then press it back up to the starting position, extending the arms fully. Control the movement throughout the exercise, avoiding jerky motions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Wide-Grip Bench Press
description: The wide-grip bench press is a barbell compound exercise that emphasizes the chest while also engaging the triceps and shoulders. By placing the hands wider than shoulder-width on the bar, the movement increases chest activation and can allow some lifters to press heavier loads compared to a standard grip. However, the wider hand position can place more stress on the shoulders, so controlled technique and appropriate loading are important. This exercise is commonly used as a primary chest builder or as an accessory movement to improve overall bench press strength.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with feet planted firmly on the ground. Grip the barbell with hands wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower the bar to your chest, then press upwards, extending your arms fully, while controlling the movement throughout. Breathe naturally and avoid arching your back or using momentum to lift the weight.

name: Wide-Grip Decline Barbell Bench Press
description: A barbell chest press performed on a decline bench with a wider-than-shoulder grip to emphasize the lower chest and increase range of motion.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with feet secured under the leg brace. Grip the barbell with hands wider than shoulder-width apart, hands facing forward. Lower the bar to your lower chest, then press upwards extending your arms fully, squeezing your chest muscles. Return the bar to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Incline Bench Press (Medium-Grip)
description: The barbell incline bench press with a medium grip is an upper-body compound exercise that emphasizes the upper chest while also engaging the shoulders and triceps.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with feet flat on floor. Grasp the barbell with a medium grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower the bar to upper chest, then press upwards extending arms fully, squeezing chest muscles. Return to starting position, controlling the bar's descent.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Neck Press
description: Lie on a flat bench holding a barbell with a wide grip. Lower the bar under control toward the base of your neck, keeping your elbows flared and shoulders retracted, then press the bar back up until your arms are extended. Focus on a light load and smooth, controlled motion to target the chest while minimizing shoulder strain.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding a barbell with a wide grip. Lower the bar under control toward the base of your neck, keeping your elbows flared and shoulders retracted. Press the bar back up until your arms are extended, using a light load and smooth motion. Focus on controlled movement to target the chest while minimizing shoulder strain.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Front Raise And Pullover
description: A barbell exercise that combines a front raise with a pullover to target the chest and shoulders while engaging the upper back and arms for stability. Performed lying on a bench, you raise the bar from over the hips to above the chest and then lower it back in an arc behind the head before returning to the start.
instructions: Lie on a bench with your feet planted on the floor. Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, holding it over your hips. Raise the bar straight up over your chest, then lower it in an arc behind your head, keeping your arms straight. Return the bar to the starting position, squeezing your chest and shoulders as you lower it back to your hips.

name: Board Bench Press
description: The board bench press is a powerlifting assistance movement for the traditional bench press. By limiting the range of motion with boards placed on the chest, it emphasizes the top portion of the press, allowing heavier loads to be used. This variation targets the chest and shoulders while strongly overloading the triceps and improving lockout strength.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with boards placed on your chest. Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower the bar to the boards, then press upwards, extending your arms fully to lockout. Control the bar throughout the entire range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Wide-Grip Decline Barbell Pullover
description: Lie on a decline bench holding a barbell with a wide overhand grip above your chest. Keeping your arms slightly bent, lower the barbell in an arc behind your head until you feel a stretch through your chest and lats, then pull it back over your chest to the starting position. Maintain control and keep your core braced throughout the movement.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench and hold a barbell with a wide overhand grip above your chest. Lower the barbell in an arc behind your head, keeping your arms slightly bent, until you feel a stretch in your chest and lats. Pull the barbell back over your chest to the starting position, maintaining control and keeping your core braced. Keep the movement slow and controlled throughout.

name: Barbell Guillotine Bench Press
description: Lie on a flat bench with a barbell held at shoulder-width grip. Lower the barbell slowly toward your upper chest or neck line, keeping your elbows flared out and wrists straight. Pause briefly just above the neck or upper chest, then press the bar back up to full extension without locking out the elbows, maintaining control throughout the movement.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with a barbell at shoulder-width grip. Lower the barbell slowly toward your upper chest, keeping elbows flared and wrists straight. Pause briefly just above the chest, then press the bar back up to full extension without locking out your elbows. Maintain control throughout the movement.

name: Incline Bench Press
description: The incline bench press is a barbell pressing exercise that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest while also engaging the shoulders and triceps. Performed on an inclined bench, it is commonly used for moderate to high repetitions (around 8–12 per set) as part of upper-body or chest-focused strength training.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with feet planted firmly on the floor. Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower the bar to your upper chest, then press upwards, extending your arms fully, squeezing your chest muscles. Return the bar to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bench Press
description: The bench press is a barbell-based upper-body strength exercise that primarily targets the chest while also engaging the triceps and shoulders. Performed lying on a bench, you lower the barbell to your chest under control and then press it back up to full arm extension. It is a key lift in powerlifting and a common measure of upper-body strength, effective for building both strength and muscle depending on the load and repetitions used.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor. Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, lowering it to your chest under control. Press the barbell back up to full arm extension, extending your arms fully. Breathe naturally and avoid arching your back throughout the movement.

name: AM Flat Bench Barbell Press
description: The flat bench barbell press is a compound strength exercise that primarily targets the chest while also engaging the triceps and shoulders. Performed lying on a flat bench, you lower the barbell to your mid-chest and press it back up to build upper-body strength and muscle mass.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with feet planted on floor. Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower the barbell to mid-chest, then press it upwards, extending arms fully, and squeeze chest muscles at top of movement. Return to starting position, controlling the descent of the barbell.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Incline Barbell Bench Press
description: Pressing movement performed on an incline bench using a barbell to target the upper chest and front shoulders.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with feet planted firmly on the floor. Grasp the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower the barbell to your upper chest, then press upwards extending your arms fully, targeting your upper chest and front shoulders. Return the barbell to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Spoto Press
description: The Spoto press is a bench press variation where the barbell is lowered under control and paused just above the chest before being pressed back up. This pause removes momentum, increases time under tension, and emphasizes strength and muscle development in the chest, triceps, and shoulders.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower the barbell under control to just above your chest, pausing for a brief moment. Press the barbell back up to the starting position, extending your arms fully. Control the entire movement to maintain proper form and avoid injury.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Plyo Push-Up
description: An explosive push-up variation performed with hands elevated on a kettlebell, emphasizing power and speed through the chest, shoulders, and triceps. It can be done for low reps to build upper-body explosiveness or higher reps for conditioning.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands gripping the handles of a kettlebell. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the kettlebell, keeping your core engaged. Explosively push up, extending your arms and propelling your upper body upward. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately lower into the next repetition.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Extended Range One-Arm Kettlebell Floor Press
description: Lie on your back on the floor holding a kettlebell in one hand with your arm extended above your chest. Lower the kettlebell in a controlled manner until your upper arm lightly touches the floor, then press it back up to full extension while keeping your core braced and shoulder stable. Perform all reps on one side before switching arms.
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor with a kettlebell in one hand, arm extended above your chest. Lower the kettlebell down in a controlled motion until your upper arm lightly touches the floor. Press the kettlebell back up to full extension, keeping your core braced and shoulder stable. Perform all reps on one side before switching arms.

name: Kettlebell Alternating Floor Press
description: A floor-based press performed while lying on your back, pressing kettlebells alternately to target the chest, triceps, and shoulders with a reduced range of motion that emphasizes lockout strength and shoulder stability.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Hold a kettlebell in each hand, with arms bent and kettlebells above your chest. Press one kettlebell upwards, extending your arm, then lower it back down and repeat with the other kettlebell. Alternate arms with each press, keeping your core engaged and shoulders stable throughout the exercise.

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Floor Press
description: Lie on your back on the floor holding a kettlebell in one hand with your elbow bent at about 45 degrees from your torso. Brace your core and press the kettlebell straight up until your arm is fully extended, keeping your shoulder stable against the floor. Lower the kettlebell under control until your upper arm lightly touches the ground, then repeat for reps before switching sides.
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor with a kettlebell in one hand and your elbow bent. Press the kettlebell straight up until your arm is fully extended, keeping your shoulder stable. Lower the kettlebell under control until your upper arm lightly touches the ground. Repeat for reps before switching sides.

name: Leg-Over Floor Press
description: Lie on your back on the floor holding a kettlebell in one hand with your knees bent and feet on the ground. Press the kettlebell straight up over your chest, keeping your wrist neutral and elbow under the weight. Lower the kettlebell until your upper arm lightly touches the floor, then press back up, keeping your core braced and shoulders stable throughout.
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor with knees bent and feet on the ground, holding a kettlebell in one hand. Press the kettlebell straight up over your chest, keeping your wrist neutral and elbow under the weight. Lower the kettlebell down until your upper arm lightly touches the floor. Keep your core braced and shoulders stable as you press back up to the starting position.

name: Pushups
description: A classic bodyweight upper-body exercise that builds strength and muscle in the chest, triceps, shoulders, and core by pressing the body away from the floor from a plank position.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart and feet hip-width apart. Engage your core and keep your body in a straight line from head to heels. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your movements controlled and smooth throughout the exercise.

name: Incline Push-Up
description: The incline push-up is a hands-elevated push-up variation where your body is aligned at an angle to the floor, reducing load compared to a standard push-up while still targeting the chest, shoulders, and triceps.
instructions: Place hands on an elevated surface, such as a bench or stairs. Position your body in a straight line from head to heels, with your hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the surface, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and your movements controlled throughout the exercise.

name: Hands-Elevated Push-Up
description: The hands-elevated push-up is a beginner-friendly push-up variation where the hands are placed on a bench or other stable elevated surface. Elevating the hands reduces the load compared to floor push-ups and shifts more emphasis to the triceps while still targeting the chest and shoulders. It can be used as an easier alternative to standard push-ups or as a mechanical dropset after reaching failure on floor push-ups.
instructions: Place hands on a stable elevated surface, such as a bench. Position body in a straight line from head to heels, engaging core muscles. Lower body until chest nearly touches the surface, then push back up to the starting position. Keep movements slow and controlled throughout the exercise.

name: Decline Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with the feet elevated on a stable surface, placing the body at a downward angle to increase emphasis on the upper chest and shoulders while maintaining a rigid plank position.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Place your feet on a stable decline surface, such as a bench or step, with your body at a downward angle. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, keeping your core engaged and body in a straight line. Push back up to the starting position, squeezing your chest and shoulder muscles.

name: Single-Arm Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with one arm, increasing the demand on chest, shoulders, triceps, and core for strength and stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with one hand on the ground and the other behind your back. Engage your core and lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground. Push back up to the starting position using the arm on the ground. Switch arms to work the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Close Push-Up to Wide Push-Up
description: A push-up variation that alternates between close-hand and wide-hand positions to emphasize the triceps in the close stance and the chest and shoulders in the wide stance.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands close together, about 6 inches apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Immediately move your hands to a wider position, about shoulder-width apart, and perform another push-up. Alternate between close and wide hand positions for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Wide-Grip Hands-Elevated Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with the hands placed wider than shoulder-width on an elevated surface, making the movement easier than floor wide-grip push-ups while emphasizing the chest. Ideal for beginners building pressing strength or as a mechanical dropset after standard push-ups.
instructions: Place hands wider than shoulder-width apart on an elevated surface, such as a bench or step. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the surface, keeping elbows close to your body. Push back up to the starting position, extending your arms fully. Keep your core engaged and body in a straight line throughout the movement.

name: Push-Ups With Feet On An Exercise Ball
description: Assume a push-up position with your hands on the floor and your shins or feet elevated on an exercise ball. Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels, brace your core, and lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows. Push back up to the starting position while maintaining control of the ball and avoiding sagging through the hips.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands on the floor and shins or feet on an exercise ball. Keep your body straight from head to heels and engage your core. Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows, then push back up to the starting position while controlling the ball. Maintain a straight line from head to heels throughout the exercise.

name: Feet-Elevated Push-Up
description: The feet-elevated push-up is a bodyweight push-up variation where the feet are raised on a stable surface, placing the body at a downward angle. This increases difficulty compared to standard push-ups and shifts more emphasis to the shoulders while still heavily targeting the chest and triceps.
instructions: Place your hands shoulder-width apart on the ground. Position your feet on a stable elevated surface, such as a bench or step. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and body in a straight line throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Isometric Wipers
description: An advanced wide-hand push-up variation where you shift your body side to side, lowering toward each hand in turn. This increases the load on each arm compared to standard push-ups and provides an intense stretch and challenge for the chest.
instructions: Start in a wide-hand push-up position with hands farther apart than shoulder-width. Lower your body toward one hand, keeping your elbows close to your body, then shift to the other side. Continue alternating sides, maintaining control and a straight line from head to heels. Keep your core engaged and avoid letting your hips sag throughout the movement.

name: Clock Push-Up
description: The clock push-up is a variation of the traditional push-up where you perform a push-up, then move your hands one step around an imaginary clock between each rep until you complete a full circle. This adds an endurance and core stability challenge while primarily working the chest, triceps, and shoulders.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands under shoulders. Perform a push-up, lowering your body until your chest almost touches the ground. After each push-up, move your hands one step around an imaginary clock, rotating your position. Repeat for a full circle, maintaining core stability and proper form throughout the exercise.

name: Incline Push-Up Depth Jump
description: A plyometric push-up variation performed with the hands elevated on a stable surface. From the top of an incline push-up, you explosively push away from the surface, briefly leaving contact, then land softly and immediately descend into the next rep to build upper-body power and chest strength.
instructions: Start in an incline push-up position with hands on a stable surface. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the surface, then explosively push away from it, briefly leaving contact. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately descend into the next rep. Keep your core engaged and control your movements to maintain proper form.

name: Wide Push-Up
description: The wide push-up is a push-up variation performed with the hands placed wider than shoulder-width apart. This increases emphasis on the chest while still engaging the shoulders and triceps. Maintain a straight line from head to heels, lower your chest toward the floor with control, then press back up without letting your hips sag. If you feel discomfort in the front of the shoulders, switch to a slightly narrower hand position.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower your chest toward the floor with control, keeping a straight line from head to heels. Press back up to the starting position without letting your hips sag. If you feel discomfort, adjust your hand position to a slightly narrower stance.

name: Sphinx Push-Up
description: The sphinx push-up is a challenging bodyweight push-up variation where you transition from a high plank down to your forearms and back up, placing greater emphasis on the triceps while still engaging the chest and shoulders. It is typically performed for reps or time in bodyweight or arm-focused workouts.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Lower your body down to your forearms, keeping elbows close to body and core engaged. Push back up to the starting plank position, extending arms fully and squeezing triceps. Repeat for desired number of reps, maintaining control and proper form throughout.

name: Close-Hands Push-Up
description: A bodyweight push-up variation performed with the hands placed close together under the chest, emphasizing the triceps while still engaging the chest and shoulders.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands closer than shoulder-width apart. Place hands directly under your chest with fingers facing forward. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, keeping elbows close to your body. Push back up to the starting position, extending your arms fully.

name: Tiger-bend Push-up
description: The tiger-bend push-up is an advanced push-up variation where you lower from a high plank onto your forearms, then press back up onto your hands and into a full push-up. This movement emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the chest, shoulders, and core for stability.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Lower your body down onto your forearms, keeping elbows close to body. Push back up to hands, straightening arms and extending elbows, then lower into a full push-up. Return to starting position, repeating the movement with control and proper form.

name: Plyo Push-Up
description: A dynamic bodyweight push-up variation where you explosively push off the floor so your hands leave the ground, building upper-body power, strength, and conditioning in the chest, triceps, and shoulders.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart and feet hip-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, keeping core engaged. Explosively push off the ground, extending your arms and lifting your hands off the floor. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately repeat the movement.

name: Close-Grip Hands-Elevated Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with the hands placed close together on an elevated surface such as a bench or box. Elevating the hands reduces overall difficulty compared to floor close-grip push-ups while increasing emphasis on the triceps and still engaging the chest and shoulders. Commonly used as a regression from standard push-ups or as a mechanical dropset after floor variations.
instructions: Place hands close together on an elevated surface, such as a bench or box. Position your body in a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the surface, keeping your elbows close to your body. Push back up to the starting position, extending your arms fully.

name: Suspended Push-Up
description: The suspended push-up is a bodyweight pushing exercise performed using suspension straps or gymnastic rings. It primarily targets the chest, shoulders, and triceps while heavily engaging the core and upper back for stabilization.
instructions: Hold suspension straps or gymnastic rings with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, keeping your core and upper back engaged for stability. Push back up to the starting position, straightening your arms and maintaining control throughout the movement. Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels throughout the exercise.

name: Push Up to Side Plank
description: A compound bodyweight exercise that combines a standard push-up with a side plank rotation. The push-up emphasizes the chest, triceps, and shoulders, while the transition into a side plank increases core engagement, especially in the abs and obliques. This movement also challenges shoulder stability and overall body control.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the plank position. Immediately rotate your body to one side, lifting your arm and leg off the ground, and hold for a brief moment. Alternate sides with each repetition, maintaining control and proper form throughout the movement.

name: Isometric Chest Squeezes
description: An isometric chest exercise where you press your palms together in front of your chest and squeeze hard to engage the pectoral muscles without moving your arms.
instructions: Stand or sit with good posture. Place your palms together in front of your chest. Press your palms together, squeezing your chest muscles as hard as you can, and hold for a few seconds. Release slowly to the starting position.

name: Side-to-side Push-up
description: A push-up variation where you step one hand and the opposite foot out to the side, lower into a push-up, then step back to center and repeat to the other side. This increases chest and shoulder activation while challenging core stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Step one hand and the opposite foot out to the side, lowering into a push-up while keeping core engaged. Push back up to plank position and step back to center, then repeat on the other side by stepping the other hand and opposite foot out. Keep movements slow and controlled to maintain balance and proper form.

name: Typewriter Push-Up
description: An advanced wide-hand push-up variation where you lower your chest toward one hand, shift across close to the ground, then toward the other hand, increasing unilateral chest and shoulder demand while providing a deep stretch.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower your chest toward one hand, keeping your body close to the ground. Shift your body across to the other hand, stretching your chest and shoulders. Push back up to the starting position, repeating on the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Diamond Push-Up
description: The diamond push-up is an advanced push-up variation performed with the hands close together under the chest, forming a diamond shape with the thumbs and index fingers. This position significantly increases the load on the triceps while still engaging the chest and shoulders.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands close together under your chest. Form a diamond shape with your thumbs and index fingers. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, keeping your elbows close to your body. Push back up to the starting position, squeezing your chest and triceps.

name: Judo Push-Up
description: The judo push-up is a dynamic push-up variation that blends elements of downward- and upward-facing dog. It increases demands on the shoulders and chest while also improving mobility through the hips and spine. It can be used in a dynamic warm-up, mobility-focused session, or as part of a bodyweight strength workout.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up and lift your hips into a downward-facing dog pose. From there, quickly swing your legs forward and land in a squat position, then place your hands on the ground and repeat the sequence. Keep your core engaged and movements smooth throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Superman Push-Up
description: A plyometric push-up variation where you explosively push off the floor, briefly lifting your hands (and sometimes feet) off the ground before landing and lowering into the next rep. This advanced push-up increases power and challenges core stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then explosively push off the floor, lifting hands and feet off the ground. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately lower into the next rep, keeping core engaged. Keep movements controlled and precise to maintain proper form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One-Arm Push-Up
description: A unilateral push-up variation performed with one arm to increase chest, shoulder, and core strength while challenging stability and balance.
instructions: Start in a plank position with one hand on the ground and the other behind your back. Place your feet shoulder-width apart and engage your core for stability. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Switch arms to work the other side.
