accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Clapping Push-Up
description: An explosive bodyweight push-up variation where you push off the floor with enough force to clap your hands before landing, building upper-body power, strength, and conditioning in the chest, triceps, and shoulders.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then explosively push off the floor. Clap your hands together before landing back in the plank position. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately repeat the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Staggered Push-Up
description: The staggered push-up is a bodyweight push-up variation performed with one hand positioned slightly forward and the other slightly back. This changes leverage and increases demand on the rear arm, chest, triceps, and core while still heavily engaging the shoulders. It challenges stability and unilateral strength more than a standard push-up.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart, but place one hand slightly forward and the other slightly back. Engage your core and keep your body straight from head to heels. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Switch hand positions and repeat to work both sides evenly.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Reverse-grip Hands-elevated Push-up
description: A beginner-friendly push-up variation performed with hands on an elevated surface and palms in a reverse (underhand) grip. Elevating the hands reduces the load compared to floor push-ups, making it a useful regression while still targeting the chest, triceps, and shoulders. Commonly done on a Smith machine bar or sturdy elevated surface, it can also be used in mechanical dropsets by raising the bar as fatigue sets in.
instructions: Place hands on an elevated surface with palms facing downwards. Position your body in a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core for support. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the surface, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your movements controlled and smooth throughout the exercise.

name: Triple Clap Push-Up
description: An explosive push-up variation where you push off the floor with enough power to clap your hands three times before landing and absorbing the impact back into a push-up position.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then explosively push off the floor. Clap your hands three times in the air, keeping your body straight. Land softly on the balls of your feet and hands, immediately absorbing the impact back into a push-up position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Side Kick-Through Push-Up
description: The side kick-through push-up is a dynamic full-body bodyweight exercise that starts in a bear crawl position, adds a push-up between transitions, and finishes each rep with a controlled leg kick-through to the side. It challenges upper-body pushing strength, core stability, hip mobility, and coordination, making it suitable for circuits, animal-flow style training, or as part of a dynamic warm-up.
instructions: Start in a bear crawl position with hands under shoulders and toes under hips. Lower your body into a push-up, keeping core engaged and legs straight. Quickly transition into a side plank, lifting one leg off the ground and kicking it through to the side in a controlled motion. Return to the starting bear crawl position and repeat on the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Swimming
description: Continuous swimming at a moderate pace to elevate heart rate and improve cardiovascular endurance while engaging the upper body, core, and legs.
instructions: Start in the water with your body in a horizontal position. Engage your core and use your arms to propel yourself forward, alternating strokes to maintain a moderate pace. Keep your legs relaxed and use a flutter kick to help with momentum. Breathe naturally, turning your head to the side to take breaths as needed.

name: Frog Push-Up
description: The frog push-up is an advanced push-up variation performed with the legs bent and hips elevated, placing greater emphasis on the triceps and shoulders while strongly engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with your hands shoulder-width apart. Bend your legs and lift your hips up, keeping your knees close to your chest. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your core engaged throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Wide Push-Up Plank Hold
description: The wide push-up plank hold is an isometric full-body exercise that emphasizes the chest, shoulders, and core by holding a plank position with the hands placed wider than shoulder-width.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands wider than shoulder-width apart. Place your hands directly under your shoulders with fingers facing forward. Engage your core and straighten your arms and legs, holding your body in a straight line from head to heels. Hold this position for the desired amount of time, maintaining control and proper form.

name: Close Push-Up
description: The close push-up is an upper-body strength exercise performed with the hands placed closer together than in a standard push-up, increasing emphasis on the triceps while still engaging the chest and shoulders.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands closer together than shoulder-width apart. Place your hands directly under your chest, with fingers facing forward. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, keeping your elbows close to your body. Push back up to the starting position, squeezing your chest and triceps.

name: Holman Seal Drag to Push-Up
description: Start in a prone position on the floor, then drag your body forward using your arms in a seal-like motion and transition into a push-up at the end of each drag. This combines upper-body pulling through the floor with a standard push-up to challenge the chest, shoulders, triceps, and core.
instructions: Start in a prone position on the floor with hands under shoulders. Drag your body forward using your arms in a seal-like motion, keeping elbows close to the ground. As you reach the end of the drag, transition into a push-up position with hands under shoulders and core engaged. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to complete the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Hand-Elevated Push-Up with Hand Row
description: A compound bodyweight exercise where you perform a push-up with your hands elevated on a stable surface, then row one hand back at the top of the movement to engage the back and core.
instructions: Place hands on a stable elevated surface, such as a bench or step. Perform a push-up by lowering your body until your chest almost touches the surface, then push back up to the starting position. At the top of the movement, lift one hand off the surface and row it back towards your side, keeping your core engaged. Alternate rows with each hand for each repetition.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Push-Up
description: A Holman Push-Up is a push-up variation that emphasizes chest and triceps strength while challenging core stability. From a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows, keeping your body in a straight line from head to heels, then press back up to the starting position under control.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows, keeping your body in a straight line. Press back up to the starting position under control, avoiding any arching or sagging of the back. Keep your core engaged throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Slow Hips-Up Chest Press
description: A controlled chest press performed while lifting the hips into a bridge position, emphasizing chest strength while engaging the glutes and core for stability.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with feet planted on the ground. Slowly lift your hips into a bridge position, squeezing your glutes and engaging your core. Hold the dumbbells over your chest and press them upwards, extending your arms fully. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Holman Jump-Out Push-Up with Hand Hop Back
description: From a high plank position, explosively jump your feet out wide as you lower into a push-up, then press back up and hop your hands backward slightly before resetting to the starting position. This dynamic variation increases chest and core demand while challenging coordination and power.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Explosively jump your feet out wide as you lower into a push-up, keeping your core engaged. Press back up to the starting position, then hop your hands backward slightly. Reset to the starting position, repeating the movement with control and power.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Froggy Push-Up to Donkey Kick
description: A compound bodyweight exercise that combines a wide-stance froggy push-up with a powerful donkey kick to challenge the chest, shoulders, core, and glutes while also demanding coordination and stability.
instructions: Start in a wide-stance plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body into a push-up, keeping your elbows close to your body, then push back up to the starting position. Immediately lift your right leg into a donkey kick, keeping your knee bent at a 90-degree angle, then quickly switch to the left leg. Alternate legs with each push-up repetition.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Push-Up to Sit Back
description: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line. Lower your chest toward the floor into a push-up, keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle. Press back up to the top of the push-up, then immediately shift your hips back toward your heels, bending your knees and reaching your arms forward into a sit-back position while keeping your hands on the floor. Pause briefly, then return to the high plank and repeat for the desired number of reps.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line. Lower your chest toward the floor into a push-up, keeping elbows at a 45-degree angle. Press back up, then shift hips back toward heels, bending knees and reaching arms forward into a sit-back position. Return to the high plank position to repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Sit Back Push-Up
description: From a push-up position, sit your hips back toward your heels while keeping your arms extended, then shift your weight forward and perform a push-up. This variation increases time under tension for the chest, shoulders, and triceps while adding a dynamic rocking motion.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart. Sit your hips back toward your heels, keeping arms extended and core engaged. Shift your weight forward, lowering your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Repeat, maintaining control throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Alternating Staggered Push-Up
description: A push-up variation where one hand is placed slightly forward and the other slightly back, alternating positions each rep to challenge the chest, shoulders, and triceps while increasing core stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with one hand slightly forward and the other slightly back. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, keeping your core engaged. Push back up to the starting position and alternate hand positions for the next rep. Repeat, continuing to alternate hand positions with each repetition.

name: Holman Foot-Up Diving Push-Up
description: A bodyweight push-up variation where the feet are elevated and the body dives forward and down, increasing load on the chest and shoulders while engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with feet elevated on a surface. Lower your body by diving forward and down, keeping your core engaged for stability. As you descend, allow your chest to nearly touch the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your movements controlled throughout the exercise.

name: Holman Narrow Push-Up
description: A close-hand push-up variation that emphasizes the chest and triceps by placing the hands narrower than shoulder-width while maintaining a straight body line from head to heels.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands narrower than shoulder-width apart. Place hands directly under chest and engage core to maintain a straight body line from head to heels. Lower body until chest nearly touches ground, then push back up to starting position. Keep elbows close to body throughout the movement.

name: Holman Dynamo Push-Up
description: A dynamic push-up variation that emphasizes explosive pressing strength and chest activation while challenging core stability and shoulder control.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, keeping core engaged and shoulders stable. Explosively push back up to the starting position, extending your arms fully and activating your chest muscles. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately repeat the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Jump to Push-Up
description: A dynamic full-body movement that combines a vertical jump with a push-up. Start standing, drop into a plank, perform a push-up, then explosively jump back to standing and immediately jump upward, repeating for reps.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart. Drop down into a plank position and perform a push-up, lowering your body until your chest nearly touches the ground. Quickly push back up and explosively jump your feet forward to return to standing, then immediately jump upward. Land softly on the balls of your feet and repeat for the desired number of reps.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Foot-Elevated Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with the feet elevated on a stable surface to increase resistance on the upper chest and shoulders while engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with feet elevated on a stable surface. Place hands shoulder-width apart on the ground, engaging core for stability. Lower body until chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep movements controlled and focused throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Feet-Elevated Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with the feet elevated on a stable surface to increase resistance on the upper body, emphasizing the upper chest and shoulders while engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Place your feet on a stable elevated surface, such as a bench or step. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position, squeezing your chest and shoulders. Keep your core engaged throughout the movement to maintain stability.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Total Fitness Plyo Push-Up
description: An explosive bodyweight push-up variation focused on developing upper-body power and strength by driving the hands off the floor each rep. It primarily targets the chest while heavily engaging the triceps and shoulders, and can be performed for low reps to build power or higher reps for conditioning.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then explosively push upward, driving your hands off the floor. Land softly on the balls of your hands and immediately lower into the next rep. Keep your core engaged and body straight throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Scapular Push-Up
description: The scapular push-up is an upper-body exercise that focuses on protracting and retracting the shoulder blades while maintaining straight arms, improving scapular stability and shoulder mobility. It is commonly used as a dynamic warm-up or in mobility-focused sessions.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Engage core and keep arms straight, lowering body slightly by protracting shoulder blades forward. Push back up to starting position by retracting shoulder blades together, maintaining a straight line from head to heels. Repeat for desired repetitions, focusing on controlled scapular movement.

name: Warrior Push-Up
description: A dynamic push-up sequence combining judo push-up, plank, side plank, mountain climber, diamond push-up, and regular push-up to challenge the chest and upper body from multiple angles while increasing core engagement and time under tension.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Perform a judo push-up, then immediately transition into a side plank, holding for a brief moment. Next, quickly move into a mountain climber, followed by a diamond push-up, and finish with a regular push-up, maintaining core engagement throughout. Repeat the sequence, alternating sides for the side plank to challenge your chest and upper body from multiple angles.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Taylor Roll-Over Push-Up
description: A dynamic push-up variation where you perform a standard push-up, then roll your body over to the opposite side into another push-up position, challenging chest strength, core stability, and coordination.
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Roll your body to one side, placing your hands and feet in a new push-up position, and immediately lower your body into another push-up. Repeat on the other side by rolling in the opposite direction.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Taylor One-Arm Push-Up Slow Negative
description: From a one-arm push-up position, slowly lower your chest toward the floor using one arm, emphasizing a controlled negative (eccentric) phase. Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels, core braced, and hips square. Lower as slowly as possible, then use both hands or a modified position to return to the start if needed.
instructions: Start in a one-arm push-up position with your body in a straight line. Lower your chest toward the floor slowly using one arm, keeping your core braced and hips square. Take your time, focusing on a controlled and slow descent. Return to the start using both hands or a modified position if needed.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30 Chest Push-Up To Isometric Hold
description: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and body in a straight line from head to heels. Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows, keeping them at about a 45-degree angle from your torso. Once your chest is just above the ground, hold the bottom position isometrically for a set time while maintaining tension through your chest, core, and glutes. Press back up to the starting position to complete the rep, then repeat for the desired number of repetitions and holds.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows, keeping them at a 45-degree angle from your torso. Hold the bottom position for a set time, maintaining tension through your chest, core, and glutes. Push back up to the starting position to complete the rep.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30 Chest Push-Up
description: A bodyweight push-up variation focused on the chest, performed for a target of 30 controlled repetitions to build upper-body strength and muscular endurance.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart and feet hip-width apart. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, keeping elbows close to your body. Push back up to the starting position, squeezing your chest muscles. Repeat for 30 controlled repetitions, maintaining proper form throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30 Chest Bench Push-Up
description: Place your hands on the edge of a bench slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, feet on the floor behind you so your body forms a straight line from head to heels. Lower your chest toward the bench by bending your elbows, keeping your core tight and elbows at about a 45-degree angle from your torso. Push through your palms to return to the starting position, maintaining a rigid plank throughout.
instructions: Place hands on the edge of a bench slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Position feet on the floor behind you, forming a straight line from head to heels. Lower your chest toward the bench by bending your elbows, keeping your core tight. Push through your palms to return to the starting position, maintaining a rigid plank throughout.

name: MetaBurn 3-Phase Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed in three controlled phases—lowering, pause/hold, and pressing up—to increase time under tension and build chest, shoulder, and triceps strength.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body down to the ground in a controlled motion, taking 2-3 seconds to reach the bottom phase. Pause for 1-2 seconds at the bottom, engaging your chest, shoulders, and triceps. Press back up to the starting position, squeezing your chest and extending your arms fully.

name: MetaBurn Fast Feet To Push-Up
description: From an athletic stance, perform quick fast feet in place, then immediately drop into a high plank and complete one push-up before returning to the standing position and repeating. Maintain a tight core and neutral spine throughout the transition from standing to the push-up and back.
instructions: Start in an athletic stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Perform quick, rapid steps in place, then immediately drop into a high plank position. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the plank position and quickly return to the standing athletic stance. Repeat the sequence, maintaining a tight core and neutral spine throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: MetaBurn Judo Push-Up To Pike Push-Up
description: A dynamic bodyweight push-up variation that flows from a judo push-up into a pike push-up, targeting the chest and shoulders while engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Perform a judo push-up by lowering your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then quickly push back up to the plank position. Immediately lift your hips up and back into a pike position, straightening your arms and legs, and perform a pike push-up. Lower your hips back down to the plank position to complete the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: MetaBurn Pike Push-Up
description: From a pike position with hips elevated and hands on the floor, bend your elbows to lower your head toward the ground, then press back up to straighten your arms. This variation emphasizes the shoulders and upper chest while engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Start in a pike position with hips elevated and hands on the floor. Bend your elbows to lower your head toward the ground, keeping your core engaged for stability. Lower down until your forehead nearly touches the ground, then press back up to straighten your arms. Return to the starting pike position, squeezing your shoulders and chest.

name: MetaBurn Push-Up To Frogger
description: From a high plank, perform a push-up, then explosively hop both feet forward to the outside of your hands into a deep squat (frogger) before hopping or stepping back to plank and repeating.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Perform a push-up, lowering your body until your chest nearly touches the ground. Explosively hop both feet forward to the outside of your hands, landing in a deep squat position. Hop or step back to the plank position and repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: MetaBurn Push-Up With Hand Side-Step
description: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line from head to heels. Perform a push-up by lowering your chest toward the floor, keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle. As you press back up, step one hand out to the side, then bring the other hand to follow so your body shifts laterally. Perform another push-up in this new position, then step your hands back to the starting position. Continue alternating side-steps with each push-up while maintaining a tight core and neutral spine.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line. Lower your chest toward the floor, keeping elbows at a 45-degree angle, then press back up. As you press up, step one hand out to the side and bring the other hand to follow, shifting your body laterally. Perform another push-up, then step your hands back to the starting position, alternating sides with each push-up.

name: MetaBurn Push-Up With Hand-Up Rotation
description: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line. Lower your chest toward the floor in a controlled push-up. As you press back up, rotate your torso to one side, lifting the same-side hand off the floor and reaching it toward the ceiling, forming a T-shape with your body. Return the hand to the floor, re-establish the plank, and repeat the push-up, rotating to the opposite side on the next rep. Maintain a tight core and neutral spine throughout.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line. Lower your chest toward the floor in a controlled push-up. As you press back up, rotate your torso and lift one hand off the floor, reaching it toward the ceiling. Return your hand to the floor and repeat, alternating sides with each rep.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: MetaBurn Sphinx Push-Up
description: A bodyweight push-up variation that emphasizes the triceps and chest by transitioning from a forearm plank position to a high plank, extending the elbows to lift the body while maintaining a rigid core.
instructions: Start in a forearm plank position with elbows under shoulders and core engaged. Push palms into ground to lift forearms, transitioning to a high plank position. Lower body until chest nearly touches ground, then extend elbows to return to high plank, keeping core rigid and maintaining control throughout.

name: MetaBurn Spiderman Push-Up
description: From a high plank position, lower into a push-up while simultaneously bringing one knee toward the elbow on the same side, mimicking a crawling motion. Alternate sides each rep to challenge the chest, shoulders, triceps, and core with added instability and hip mobility demand.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body into a push-up while bringing one knee toward the same-side elbow, keeping core engaged. Alternate sides with each rep, switching the knee and elbow. Focus on controlled movements and proper form throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Robertson Push-up to Down Dog
description: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line. Lower into a controlled push-up, keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the body. Press back up to the top of the push-up, then immediately shift your hips up and back into a Down Dog position, driving your chest toward your thighs and lengthening through the shoulders and hamstrings. Pause briefly, then return to the high plank and repeat for the desired reps.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line. Lower into a push-up, keeping elbows at a 45-degree angle. Press back up, then shift hips up and back into a Down Dog position, lengthening through shoulders and hamstrings. Return to the high plank position and repeat.

name: ACFT Hand-release Push-up
description: The ACFT hand-release push-up is a bodyweight strength exercise used in the Army Combat Fitness Test. From a prone position, you lift your hands off the ground at the bottom of each rep before pressing back up, increasing time under tension and range of motion for the chest, triceps, shoulders, and core.
instructions: Start in a prone position with hands shoulder-width apart and feet hip-width apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then lift your hands off the ground for a brief moment. Quickly place your hands back on the ground and push back up to the starting position, keeping your core engaged and body straight. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: 1.5-rep Push-up
description: The 1.5-rep push-up is an intermediate push-up variation where each repetition includes a full descent, a partial rise, a second descent, and then a full press to the top. This increases time under tension and builds upper-body strength and muscle, emphasizing the chest, shoulders, triceps, and core stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up halfway. From the halfway point, lower your body back down to the starting position, then push up to the top, fully extending your arms. Focus on slow and controlled movements to maintain proper form and engage your core throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: ACFT Triple-clap Push-up
description: The ACFT triple-clap push-up is an explosive plyometric push-up variation where you push off the ground with enough force to clap your hands three times before landing. It develops upper-body power and strength, emphasizing the chest, triceps, shoulders, and core stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then explosively push off the ground with enough force to lift your hands. Clap your hands three times before landing, keeping your core engaged and body straight. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately return to the plank position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: ACFT Superman Push-Up
description: An explosive bodyweight push-up variation where you push off the floor with enough force for both hands and feet to leave the ground briefly, targeting the chest, triceps, shoulders, and core for power and strength.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart and feet hip-width apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, keeping your core engaged. Explosively push off the ground with your hands and feet, lifting both off the floor briefly. Land softly on the balls of your feet and hands, immediately returning to the starting position.

name: Cross-over Push-up
description: A push-up variation performed with the hands on the floor and a small box or step to one side. After each push-up, you step one hand and the opposite foot over the box to transition laterally, then perform the next push-up on the other side. This increases core stabilization demands while maintaining a neutral spine and can be done for time or repetitions.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with one hand near a small box or step. Perform a push-up, keeping your core engaged and spine neutral. Step one hand and the opposite foot over the box to the other side. Repeat the push-up on the new side, alternating sides with each repetition.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Leverage Incline Chest Press
description: A machine-based incline chest press that targets the upper chest while providing guided resistance for improved stability and control, making it suitable for beginners.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your feet flat on the floor. Adjust the seat height to fit your body, with your chest against the pad. Grasp the handles and press them forward, extending your arms fully while squeezing your chest muscles. Return the handles to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Machine Chest Fly (Butterfly)
description: The machine chest fly, or butterfly, is an isolation exercise targeting the chest. Using a seated fly machine, you bring your arms together in front of your body in a hugging motion, usually after heavier compound presses like the bench press.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your back against the pad and feet flat on the floor. Grasp the handles with your arms extended to the sides at shoulder height. Press the handles together in front of you, squeezing your chest muscles as you bring your arms together. Return the handles to the starting position in a slow and controlled motion.

name: Leverage Chest Press
description: The leverage chest press is a machine-based upper body exercise that primarily targets the pectoral muscles while also engaging the shoulders and triceps.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your feet flat on the floor. Grasp the handles with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Press the handles forward, extending your arms fully, then slowly return to the starting position. Keep your back against the pad and core engaged throughout the exercise.

name: Decline Smith Press
description: The decline Smith press is a machine-based chest exercise performed on a decline bench under a Smith machine. It emphasizes the lower portion of the pectoral muscles while the fixed bar path provides added stability, making it suitable for controlled, moderate to high-rep sets in a chest or upper-body workout.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench under a Smith machine with feet securely on the floor. Grasp the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower the bar to your lower chest, then press upwards to extend your arms, squeezing your chest muscles. Return the bar to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Bench Press
description: The Smith machine bench press is a guided bar press performed lying on a bench under a Smith machine. The fixed bar path provides added stability compared to a free barbell, making it useful for focusing on chest development and controlling the load. It can be used with heavier weights for strength or moderate to high reps for muscle and strength endurance in chest or upper-body workouts.
instructions: Lie on the bench under the Smith machine with feet planted on the floor. Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, with arms fully extended. Lower the bar to your chest by bending your elbows, then press upwards to the starting position. Keep core engaged and control the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Leverage Decline Chest Press
description: A machine-based decline chest press that targets the lower portion of the chest while providing guided resistance for stability and control.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your feet flat on the floor and your back against the decline bench. Grasp the handles with your hands and press them downwards, extending your arms fully. Lower the weight back to the starting position in a controlled motion, squeezing your chest muscles. Breathe naturally and avoid arching your back throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Incline Bench Press
description: A guided incline pressing movement using a Smith machine to target the upper chest with added bar path stability, allowing focus on controlled reps and progressive overload.
instructions: Sit on the incline bench with your back against the pad and feet flat on the floor. Grasp the bar with a slightly wider than shoulder-width overhand grip. Lower the bar to your upper chest, then press upwards, extending your arms fully, keeping control throughout the entire range of motion. Return the bar to the starting position, squeezing your chest muscles at the top of the movement.

name: Smith Machine Close-Grip Bench Press
description: A guided bar press on the Smith machine using a narrow grip to emphasize the triceps while also working the chest and shoulders. Performed lying on a bench under the Smith bar, lowering it to the lower chest and pressing back up for controlled, moderate to high-rep sets.
instructions: Lie on the bench under the Smith machine with your feet planted firmly on the ground. Grasp the bar with a narrow grip, about 6-8 inches apart, and lower it to your lower chest. Press the bar upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Breathe naturally and avoid arching your back or using momentum to lift the weight.

name: Smith Machine Decline Press
description: The Smith Machine Decline Press is a machine-guided decline bench press variation that targets the lower portion of the chest. Using the fixed bar path of the Smith Machine provides added stability, allowing beginners to focus on pressing technique and controlled range of motion while safely loading the chest with moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Adjust the Smith Machine to a decline position and sit with your feet flat on the floor. Grasp the bar with a medium grip and lower your body to the bench, ensuring your shoulders are under the bar. Press the bar upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position, squeezing your chest muscles. Keep control throughout the entire range of motion.

name: Smith Machine Bench Press Throw
description: An explosive bench press variation performed in a Smith machine where a light load is pressed upward as powerfully as possible, releasing the bar briefly at the top before safely catching it on the way down. This movement is used to build upper-body pushing power and bar speed that carries over to the traditional bench press and other athletic pressing actions.
instructions: Lie on the Smith machine bench with feet planted firmly on the ground. Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, then press the bar upward as powerfully as possible. Release the bar briefly at the top of the movement, allowing it to float for a brief moment. Catch the bar on the way down, controlling its descent to the starting position.

name: Machine Chest Fly
description: A seated machine-based fly movement that targets the chest by bringing the arms together in a hugging motion. It provides guided resistance to isolate the pectoral muscles with minimal stabilization demands, making it suitable for controlled, moderate to high-rep hypertrophy work.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your feet flat on the floor. Grasp the handles with your arms extended to the sides at chest height. Press the handles together in a hugging motion, squeezing your chest muscles. Return to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Machine Bench Press
description: The machine bench press is a selectorized or plate-loaded pressing exercise that mimics the traditional bench press while providing a fixed path of motion. It primarily targets the chest and allows for different grips and widths to emphasize various areas of the upper body. It is commonly performed for moderate to high repetitions as part of a chest or upper-body workout.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your back against the pad and feet on the floor. Grasp the bar with your chosen grip width, squeezing your chest muscles to prepare. Press the bar away from you, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position in a controlled motion. Breathe naturally and avoid arching your back throughout the exercise.

name: Smith Machine Weighted Push-Up
description: The Smith machine weighted push-up is a push-up variation performed with the hands on a Smith machine bar and additional weight (such as a plate) placed on the upper back or shoulders. The bar is set high enough to allow a safe range of motion and to avoid getting pinned at the bottom. This movement increases loading on the chest, triceps, and shoulders compared to standard push-ups while maintaining a similar pressing pattern.
instructions: Set the Smith machine bar to a height that allows a safe range of motion. Place your hands on the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and position your body in a plank position. Add weight to your upper back or shoulders, then lower your body until your chest almost touches the bar, keeping your core engaged. Push back up to the starting position, extending your arms fully.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Decline Bench Press
description: The Smith machine decline bench press is a chest-focused pressing exercise performed on a decline bench within a Smith machine. The fixed bar path provides added stability compared to a free barbell, making it useful for targeting the lower portion of the pectoral muscles with controlled, moderate to high rep sets.
instructions: Lie on the decline bench with your feet secured under the footrest. Grasp the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip, squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower the bar to your lower chest, then press upwards, extending your arms fully, using a controlled and steady motion. Return to the starting position, keeping the bar on the Smith machine's track throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: AM Pec Dec
description: A seated machine chest fly that targets the pectoral muscles by bringing the arms together in a hugging motion, typically used for moderate to high reps in muscle-building upper-body workouts.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your back against the pad. Grasp the handles with your arms extended to the sides at shoulder height. Squeeze your chest muscles to bring the handles together in front of you, keeping your arms straight. Return the handles to the starting position in a controlled motion.

name: Chest Machine Flat Chest Press
description: Sit at the chest press machine with your back flat against the pad and feet planted. Grip the handles at chest level with elbows bent, then press the handles forward until your arms are almost straight without locking the elbows. Pause briefly, then slowly return to the starting position, keeping your shoulders down and chest lifted throughout the movement.
instructions: Sit at the chest press machine with your back flat against the pad and feet planted. Grip the handles at chest level with elbows bent. Press the handles forward until your arms are almost straight, then pause briefly. Return to the starting position slowly, keeping your shoulders down and chest lifted.

name: Smith Machine Push-Up
description: The Smith machine push-up is a bodyweight-style pressing exercise performed with your hands on the bar of a Smith machine. By adjusting the bar height, you can make the movement easier or harder, allowing for progressive overload and mechanical dropsets while targeting the chest, shoulders, and triceps with controlled form.
instructions: Stand in a Smith machine with the bar at chest height. Place your hands on the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the bar, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and body in a straight line throughout the movement.

name: Smith Machine Incline Chest Press
description: Pressing movement performed on a Smith machine with the bench set to an incline to target the upper chest while providing guided bar path for added stability.
instructions: Adjust the Smith machine bench to an incline position. Sit on the bench with your feet flat on the floor and grasp the bar with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Press the bar upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position, squeezing your chest muscles. Control the movement throughout the entire range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Chest Press
description: Pressing movement performed on a Smith machine to target the chest with guided bar path for added stability and control.
instructions: Sit at the Smith machine with feet flat on the floor. Grasp the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder width, squeezing your chest muscles. Press the bar upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position in a controlled motion. Keep your core engaged and back against the bench throughout the exercise.

name: Incline Chest Press Machine
description: Sit at an incline chest press machine with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor. Grip the handles slightly wider than shoulder-width, keeping your elbows at about a 45-degree angle from your torso. Press the handles forward and upward until your arms are almost straight without locking the elbows, then slowly return to the starting position under control, keeping tension on the chest throughout the movement.
instructions: Sit at the incline chest press machine with your back against the pad and feet flat on the floor. Grip the handles slightly wider than shoulder-width, keeping elbows at a 45-degree angle. Press the handles forward and upward until arms are almost straight, avoiding locked elbows. Return to the starting position slowly, keeping tension on the chest throughout the movement.

name: Chest Machine Fly
description: Sit at a chest fly machine with your back against the pad and feet flat on the floor. Grasp the handles with a slight bend in your elbows and arms out to the sides. Exhale as you bring the handles together in front of your chest in a wide hugging motion, squeezing your chest at the midpoint. Inhale as you slowly return to the starting position under control, keeping tension on the chest throughout the movement.
instructions: Sit at the chest fly machine with your back against the pad and feet flat on the floor. Grasp the handles with a slight bend in your elbows and arms out to the sides. Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest in a wide hugging motion, squeezing your chest at the midpoint. Inhale and slowly return to the starting position, keeping tension on the chest throughout the movement.

name: 30° Incline Smith Machine Chest Press
description: Pressing movement performed on a Smith machine with the bench set to a 30-degree incline to target the upper chest while providing guided bar path for stability.
instructions: Adjust the Smith machine bench to a 30-degree incline. Sit on the bench with your feet flat on the floor and grip the bar with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Press the bar upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position, squeezing your chest muscles. Control the movement throughout the entire range of motion.

name: Low-Cable Cross-Over
description: The low-cable cross-over is an isolation chest exercise performed on a cable machine, emphasizing the upper portion of the pectoral muscles. It is typically used in upper-body or chest-focused hypertrophy workouts, often paired with pressing and fly variations from multiple angles to fully develop the chest.
instructions: Stand facing a low-cable machine with cables at chest height. Hold a handle in each hand with arms extended to sides at shoulder height. Pull handles across body, meeting in front of chest, while keeping arms straight and squeezing chest muscles. Return to starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the cables.

name: Incline Cable Chest Fly
description: The incline cable chest fly is an isolation exercise performed on an incline bench using a cable machine to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It is typically used in upper-body or chest-focused workouts after heavier pressing movements or as a finishing exercise to fully fatigue the pectoral muscles.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with feet flat on floor. Hold a cable handle in each hand with arms extended to sides at shoulder height. Press handles upwards and together, squeezing chest muscles, then slowly return to starting position. Keep core engaged and avoid swinging arms throughout the movement.

name: Cable Crossover
description: The cable crossover is an isolation chest exercise performed on a cable machine. By adjusting the pulley height, you can emphasize different areas of the chest while maintaining constant tension through the full range of motion. It is commonly used in chest-focused strength routines as either a pre-exhaust movement before presses or as a finishing exercise to fully fatigue the pectoral muscles.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the pulleys at chest height. Grasp a handle in each hand with palms facing forward. Press the handles outward to full extension, squeezing your chest muscles, then return to the starting position in a slow and controlled manner. Keep your arms straight and core engaged throughout the exercise.

name: Incline Cable Chest Press
description: The incline cable chest press is a pressing movement performed on an incline bench using a cable stack to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It provides constant tension through the range of motion and is commonly used in chest-focused hypertrophy workouts as a primary lift, pre-exhaust exercise, or high-rep finisher, often paired with other presses or flyes to train the chest from multiple angles.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with feet flat on floor. Grasp cable handles with palms facing forward, keeping arms at shoulder height. Press handles forward, extending arms fully, then return to starting position in a controlled motion. Keep core engaged and back pressed against bench throughout the exercise.

name: Cable Chest Press
description: A chest press variation performed using a cable machine, allowing constant tension on the chest muscles throughout the movement while pressing the handles forward from a standing or seated position.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a handle in each hand at shoulder height, with palms facing forward. Press the handles forward, extending arms fully, then return to the starting position in a slow and controlled manner. Keep your core engaged and chest up throughout the exercise.

name: Single-arm cable cross-over
description: The single-arm cable cross-over is an isolation chest exercise performed on a cable machine. Working one side at a time helps improve muscular balance and allows you to focus on a strong contraction through the pecs, often used in upper-body or chest-focused hypertrophy workouts alongside presses or fly variations.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the cable at chest height. Hold the handle with one hand, keeping your arm straight. Pull the handle across your body, keeping your core engaged, until your hand reaches the opposite shoulder. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the cable.

name: Cable Cross-over
description: The cable cross-over is a chest isolation exercise performed on a cable machine to build size and strength in the pectoral muscles. By adjusting the pulley height, you can emphasize upper, middle, or lower chest fibers, making it a versatile option in chest-focused workouts. It is commonly used as a pre-exhaust movement before presses or as a finishing exercise to fully fatigue the chest from multiple angles.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the pulleys at chest height. Hold a handle in each hand with arms extended to the sides. Pull the handles together in front of you, keeping arms straight, until they meet or almost meet. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the cables.

name: Standing Cable Chest Press
description: A standing chest press performed with a cable machine, emphasizing controlled pushing to target the chest while engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the cable handle in each hand at shoulder height, with palms facing forward. Press the handle forward, extending arms and squeezing chest muscles, then slowly return to the starting position. Keep core engaged and maintain good posture throughout the exercise.

name: Lying Cable Chest Fly
description: The lying cable chest fly is an isolation exercise performed on a flat bench between two low cable pulleys. Lying on the bench, you bring the handles together in a wide hugging motion to emphasize the pectoral muscles through a large range of motion while maintaining constant tension from the cables. It is commonly used in chest-focused strength programs as a pre-exhaust movement before presses or as a finishing exercise to fully fatigue the chest.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench between two low cable pulleys with feet planted on the floor. Grasp a handle from each pulley with arms extended to the sides at shoulder height. Bring the handles together in a wide hugging motion, squeezing your chest muscles as you do so. Return to the starting position, maintaining control and constant tension on the cables.

name: Seated Cable Chest Fly
description: The seated cable chest fly is an isolation exercise performed on an upright bench using a cable stack to target the pectoral muscles. Bracing against the seat helps stabilize the torso so you can focus on stretching and contracting the chest through a controlled range of motion, often used alongside presses or other fly variations to train the chest from different angles.
instructions: Sit on an upright bench with feet flat on the floor. Hold a cable handle in each hand with arms extended to the sides at chest height. Press the cables forward, extending arms fully, then return to the starting position in a slow and controlled motion. Keep your back against the bench and core engaged throughout the exercise.

name: Single-Arm Incline Cable Cross-Over
description: The single-arm incline cable cross-over is an isolation chest exercise performed on an incline bench using a cable stack. Working one side at a time emphasizes the upper chest, helps address left-right imbalances, and allows for a strong peak contraction through the full range of motion. It is typically used in upper-body or chest-focused hypertrophy workouts, often after heavier pressing movements.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with your back against the pad. Hold a cable handle in one hand, with the cable at a height above your shoulder. Pull the handle down and across your body, squeezing your chest muscles as you lower it. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the cable.

name: High-to-Low Cable Chest Fly
description: The high-to-low cable chest fly is an isolation exercise that targets the lower portion of the chest by bringing the arms from a high position down and across the body using a cable machine. It is typically performed after heavy pressing movements to focus on chest definition and to work the pectorals through a different angle and constant tension.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the cables at a high position. Hold a handle in each hand with arms extended and palms facing down. Bring the handles down and across your body, keeping arms straight, until they are at a low position near your thighs. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking or swinging.

name: 30° Chest Low-To-High Cable Fly
description: Set an adjustable cable pulley to a low position on each side and stand in the center holding the handles with a slight forward lean. With a soft bend in your elbows, sweep your arms upward and inward in a 30° upward arc until your hands meet around upper-chest height, squeezing the chest at the top. Control the cables back down along the same path without letting the weights slam, keeping constant tension on the chest throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand in the center of the cable machine with a slight forward lean, holding the handles at a low position. Sweep your arms upward and inward in a 30° arc, keeping a soft bend in your elbows, until your hands meet at upper-chest height. Squeeze your chest at the top of the movement. Control the cables back down along the same path, keeping constant tension on the chest.

name: 30° Chest High-to-Low Cable Press
description: Set a cable machine so the handles are slightly above shoulder height and stand in a staggered stance facing away from the machine. With a slight forward lean and braced core, press the handles forward and down on a roughly 30° angle toward the lower chest, bringing the hands together in front of the body. Control the return as the cables move back up and out, keeping tension on the chest throughout the movement.

name: 30° Chest High-to-Low Cable Fly
description: Stand between two high cable pulleys set slightly above shoulder height, holding the handles with a slight forward lean and a staggered stance. With a soft bend in the elbows, sweep your arms downward and inward in an arc toward your lower chest, squeezing the chest at the bottom before slowly returning to the starting position under control. Maintain a stable torso and avoid using momentum to move the weight.
instructions: Stand between two high cable pulleys with a slight forward lean and staggered stance. Hold handles with a soft bend in the elbows. Sweep arms downward and inward in an arc toward your lower chest, squeezing at the bottom. Return to the starting position slowly and under control.

name: Chest Shoulder-Height Cable Fly
description: Stand between two cable stacks with the pulleys set at shoulder height, holding a handle in each hand. With a slight bend in your elbows and palms facing forward, step forward to create tension in the cables. Keeping your chest up and core braced, bring your hands together in front of your chest in a wide hugging motion, squeezing your chest at the midpoint. Slowly return to the starting position under control, maintaining the same elbow angle throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand between two cable stacks with pulleys at shoulder height, holding a handle in each hand. Step forward to create tension, keeping a slight bend in your elbows and palms facing forward. Bring your hands together in front of your chest in a wide hugging motion, squeezing your chest at the midpoint. Return to the starting position slowly, maintaining the same elbow angle throughout the movement.

name: Cable Pec Fly
description: A chest isolation exercise performed on a cable machine, bringing the arms together in front of the body to target the pectoral muscles through a controlled, continuous tension movement.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with cables at chest height. Hold a handle in each hand with arms extended to sides. Bring handles together in front of chest, squeezing pectoral muscles, then return to starting position in a controlled motion. Keep core engaged and maintain consistent tension throughout the exercise.

name: Chest Push (Medicine Ball)
description: Stand holding a medicine ball at chest height with elbows bent. In an explosive motion, push the ball straight forward away from your chest, fully extending your arms, then catch or retrieve the ball and repeat for reps. Focus on driving the movement from your chest and keeping your core braced.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest height with elbows bent. Push the ball straight forward, extending your arms fully and driving the movement from your chest. Keep your core braced throughout the motion. Catch or retrieve the ball and repeat for reps.

name: Medicine Ball Chest Pass
description: A partner-based plyometric exercise where you explosively pass a medicine ball from your chest to a partner and receive it back, building upper-body power and warming up the chest and shoulders.
instructions: Stand facing a partner with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a medicine ball at chest level with both hands. Explosively push the medicine ball forward to your partner, extending your arms and using your chest and shoulder muscles. Receive the ball back from your partner, absorbing the impact with your arms and chest, and repeat the motion.

name: Medicine Ball Sprawl to Chest Press
description: A dynamic combination exercise where you sprawl to the floor with your hands on a medicine ball, then quickly stand and perform a chest press with the ball. This movement challenges cardiovascular endurance while targeting the chest, core, shoulders, and upper back.
instructions: Start in a standing position with feet shoulder-width apart and hold a medicine ball at chest level. Drop down into a sprawl position, placing your hands on the medicine ball and kicking your feet back into a plank position. Quickly return to standing, then press the medicine ball forward at chest level, extending your arms. Return the medicine ball to the starting position and repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Medicine Ball Push-Up
description: The medicine ball push-up is a progression of the classic push-up that increases instability and range of motion. Placing one or both hands on a medicine ball challenges the chest, triceps, shoulders, and core more intensely while also engaging stabilizing muscles in the upper back.
instructions: Start in a plank position with one or both hands on a medicine ball. Place your hands shoulder-width apart on the ball, engaging your core and keeping your body straight. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ball, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your movements controlled and stable throughout the exercise.

name: Chest Push (Single Response)
description: Stand holding a medicine ball at chest height with elbows bent. In one explosive motion, push the ball straight forward away from your chest, fully extending your arms as you release it toward a partner or wall. Catch or retrieve the ball, reset your stance, and repeat for controlled, powerful repetitions.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest height with elbows bent. Push the ball straight forward, extending your arms and releasing it toward a partner or wall in one explosive motion. Catch or retrieve the ball and reset your stance. Repeat for controlled, powerful repetitions.

name: Chest Push with Run Release
description: From an athletic stance, hold a medicine ball at your chest, then explosively push it forward as if passing to a partner and immediately transition into a quick run or sprint release. This develops upper-body power and coordination with lower-body acceleration.
instructions: Stand in an athletic stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a medicine ball at your chest with both hands. Explosively push the medicine ball forward, then immediately transition into a quick run or sprint, keeping your arms relaxed and your core engaged. Focus on rapid turnover and powerful strides to maximize the exercise's cardio benefits.

name: Chest Push from 3 Point Stance
description: From a three-point athletic stance, hold a medicine ball at chest level and explosively push it forward like a chest pass, extending the arms to drive the ball straight out while maintaining a strong, stable base.
instructions: Start in a three-point athletic stance with feet shoulder-width apart and one hand on the ground. Hold a medicine ball at chest level with both hands. Explosively push the medicine ball forward, extending your arms and driving the ball straight out. Maintain a strong, stable base throughout the movement.

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
description: The dumbbell bench press is a classic strength exercise for building a bigger, stronger chest, often used as a primary movement in mass-building chest workouts.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor. Hold a dumbbell in each hand, with your arms extended above your chest and palms facing forward. Lower the dumbbells to your sides until your arms are bent at a 45-degree angle, then press them back up to the starting position. Breathe naturally and control the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Dumbbell Flyes
description: The dumbbell fly is a chest isolation exercise performed lying on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand. With a slight bend in the elbows, the arms open wide to stretch the chest, then close in a hugging motion to contract the pectoral muscles while minimizing shoulder and triceps involvement. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions as part of a chest or upper-body workout.
instructions: Lie on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Hold the dumbbells with a slight bend in your elbows and palms facing each other. Open your arms wide to the sides, stretching your chest, then bring them back together in a hugging motion, squeezing your chest muscles. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Decline Dumbbell Flyes
description: An isolation chest exercise performed on a decline bench using dumbbells to emphasize the lower portion of the chest through a wide arc of motion.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with feet securely locked under the pads. Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended overhead and palms facing each other. Lower the dumbbells to the sides in a wide arc, keeping arms straight, until you feel a stretch in your chest. Return the dumbbells to the starting position, squeezing your chest muscles.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Reverse-Grip Incline Dumbbell Bench Press
description: The reverse-grip incline dumbbell bench press is an upper-body strength exercise that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest by using a supinated (palms-facing-toward-you) grip on an incline bench. Lying back on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand, you press the weights upward while maintaining the reverse grip, then lower them under control to stretch the chest before pressing again. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8-12 reps per set, within upper-body or chest-focused workouts.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with feet flat on floor. Hold a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing towards you. Press the dumbbells upwards, extending arms fully, then lower them back down to the starting position under control. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Incline Dumbbell Flyes
description: An isolation chest exercise performed on an incline bench with dumbbells, emphasizing the upper chest through a wide, controlled arc of motion using lighter loads and higher reps for hypertrophy.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with feet flat on floor. Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended and palms facing each other. Lift dumbbells out to sides in a wide arc until arms are level with shoulders, then lower them back to starting position. Keep core engaged and control the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Neutral-grip Dumbbell Bench Press
description: The neutral-grip dumbbell bench press is a chest-focused pressing exercise performed lying on a bench while holding dumbbells with the palms facing each other. This grip keeps the elbows closer to the torso, increasing triceps involvement and reducing shoulder strain compared to a traditional pronated-grip bench press. It can be used with heavier loads for strength or moderate loads and higher reps for muscle growth.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor. Hold dumbbells with your palms facing each other and arms extended over your chest. Lower the dumbbells to your sides until your upper arms are parallel to the ground, then press upwards, extending your arms fully. Squeeze your chest and triceps at the top of the movement.

name: Decline Dumbbell Bench Press
description: The decline dumbbell bench press is an upper-body strength exercise emphasizing the lower portion of the chest by pressing dumbbells while lying on a decline bench. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8-12 reps per set, within chest-focused or upper-body workouts.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with feet flat on floor. Hold dumbbells over chest with palms facing forward. Lower dumbbells to sides of chest, then press upwards, extending arms fully. Control the movement throughout to avoid injury.

name: Single-arm kettlebell row
description: The single-arm kettlebell row is a muscle- and strength-building exercise that targets the back and core, similar to a single-arm dumbbell row but using a kettlebell for a different grip and loading feel.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell with one hand, standing with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend knees slightly and lean forward at the hips, keeping back straight. Lift the kettlebell to your side, keeping elbow close to your body, then lower it back down to the starting position. Repeat with the other arm to balance the exercise.

name: Hammer Grip Incline Dumbbell Bench Press
description: Lie back on an incline bench holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (hammer) grip, palms facing each other. With feet planted and shoulder blades pulled back, press the dumbbells up over your chest until your arms are extended without locking out. Lower the weights under control until your elbows are just below shoulder level, keeping the neutral grip throughout, then press back to the starting position.
instructions: Lie back on an incline bench with feet planted and shoulder blades pulled back. Hold a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing each other. Press the dumbbells up over your chest until arms are extended, then lower them under control until elbows are just below shoulder level. Keep the neutral grip throughout and repeat the motion.

name: Straight-Arm Dumbbell Pull-Over
description: The straight-arm dumbbell pull-over is a strength exercise that targets the chest and lats while also engaging the core. Lying on a bench, you hold a dumbbell with both hands above your chest and lower it in an arc behind your head with straight arms, then pull it back to the starting position. Keeping the arms straight increases the stretch on the lats and serratus area and demands more stability from the shoulders and core.
instructions: Lie on a bench with your feet planted on the floor. Hold a dumbbell with both hands above your chest, arms straight. Lower the dumbbell in an arc behind your head, keeping your arms straight, then pull it back to the starting position. Breathe naturally and control the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Incline Dumbbell Bench With Palms Facing In
description: Lie on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing each other. With feet planted and lower back supported by the bench, press the dumbbells up over your chest while keeping your elbows slightly tucked. Lower the weights under control until your elbows are just below bench level, then press back up, maintaining the neutral (palms-in) grip throughout.
instructions: Lie on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing each other. Plant your feet and support your lower back on the bench. Press the dumbbells up over your chest, keeping your elbows slightly tucked, then lower them under control until your elbows are just below bench level. Press back up to the starting position, maintaining the neutral grip throughout.

name: Incline Dumbbell Flyes - With A Twist
description: Lie on an incline bench set at about 30–45 degrees, holding a pair of dumbbells directly above your chest with a slight bend in your elbows and palms facing each other. Lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch across your chest, keeping your elbows softly bent and shoulders packed. As you bring the dumbbells back up, add a slight twist so your palms turn to face your feet at the top, squeezing your chest before returning to the starting position. Control the motion throughout and avoid letting the weights drop too low or bouncing at the bottom.
instructions: Lie on an incline bench with dumbbells above your chest, elbows slightly bent and palms facing each other. Lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping elbows softly bent and shoulders packed. Bring the dumbbells back up with a twist, turning palms to face your feet at the top, squeezing your chest. Control the motion throughout, avoiding jerky movements and bouncing at the bottom.

name: Bent-Arm Dumbbell Pull-Over
description: The bent-arm dumbbell pull-over is a classic upper-body exercise that targets the chest and lats while also engaging the serratus anterior. Performed lying across a bench or lengthwise on a bench, you lower a dumbbell in an arc behind your head with bent elbows, then pull it back over your chest. It is typically used in moderate rep ranges (8-12 reps) as part of an upper-body or chest-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on a bench with your feet planted on the floor. Hold a dumbbell over your chest with both hands and bent elbows. Lower the dumbbell in an arc behind your head, keeping your elbows bent at a 90-degree angle. Pull the dumbbell back to the starting position, squeezing your chest and lats.

name: Close-Grip Dumbbell Bench Press
description: The close-grip dumbbell bench press is a variation of the standard dumbbell bench press where the dumbbells are held closer than shoulder width. This narrower grip increases emphasis on the triceps while still targeting the chest and shoulders. It can be used with heavier loads for strength or moderate loads and higher reps for muscle growth.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor. Hold dumbbells with a close grip, about 6-8 inches apart, and position them over your chest. Lower the dumbbells to your chest, then press upwards, extending your arms fully, squeezing your chest and triceps. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Single-dumbbell Push-up
description: The single-dumbbell push-up is an intermediate push-up variation where both hands balance on the ends of a dumbbell. Elevating and narrowing the hand position increases instability and range of motion, making the chest, shoulders, triceps, and core work harder for strength and muscle gains.
instructions: Place a dumbbell on the ground and position your hands on either end of it. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the dumbbell, keeping your core engaged and body in a straight line. Push back up to the starting position, extending your arms fully and squeezing your chest and shoulder muscles. Keep your movements controlled and focused to maintain balance and proper form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One-Arm Flat Bench Dumbbell Flye
description: Lie flat on a bench holding a dumbbell in one hand directly above your chest with a slight bend in the elbow. Keeping your shoulder stable and elbow softly bent, slowly lower the dumbbell out to the side in an arc until you feel a stretch across your chest, then squeeze your chest to bring the weight back over your torso. Perform all reps on one side before switching arms, keeping your core braced and shoulders pressed into the bench throughout.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding a dumbbell in one hand above your chest with a slight bend in the elbow. Lower the dumbbell out to the side in an arc, keeping your shoulder stable and elbow softly bent, until you feel a stretch across your chest. Squeeze your chest to bring the weight back over your torso. Keep your core braced and shoulders pressed into the bench throughout, then repeat on the other side.

name: Dumbbell Floor Chest Fly
description: Lie on your back on the floor with a dumbbell in each hand, arms extended above your chest with a slight bend in the elbows. Slowly lower the weights out to your sides in a wide arc until your upper arms lightly touch the floor, feeling a stretch across your chest. Squeeze your chest to bring the dumbbells back together over your chest, maintaining the same elbow angle throughout the movement.
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor with a dumbbell in each hand, arms extended above your chest. Lower the weights out to your sides in a wide arc until your upper arms lightly touch the floor. Squeeze your chest to bring the dumbbells back together over your chest, keeping your elbows slightly bent. Maintain control throughout the movement to target your chest muscles effectively.

name: Dumbbell Fix Traveling Push-Up
description: Assume a high plank position with each hand gripping a dumbbell placed shoulder-width apart. Perform a push-up by lowering your chest toward the floor while keeping your core tight and body in a straight line. As you press back up, step one hand and the opposite foot laterally to the side, moving the dumbbells with your hands so they travel with you. Repeat the push-up in the new position, then travel back the other direction, alternating sides for the desired number of reps or distance.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands gripping dumbbells shoulder-width apart. Lower your chest toward the floor, keeping your core tight and body straight. As you press back up, step one hand and the opposite foot laterally to the side, moving the dumbbells with you. Alternate sides with each rep, repeating the push-up in each new position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Incline Dumbbell Chest Fly
