description: The incline dumbbell chest fly is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the upper portion of the chest by performing a fly motion on an incline bench with dumbbells. The inclined angle emphasizes the upper chest while reducing front-shoulder involvement. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8–12 reps per set or more.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with feet flat on floor. Hold dumbbells with palms facing each other and arms extended to sides at shoulder height. Lift dumbbells upwards in a circular motion, keeping arms straight, until they meet above chest. Lower dumbbells back to starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Dumbbell Push-Up Row
description: The dumbbell push-up row combines a push-up with a single-arm dumbbell row while maintaining a strong plank position. This movement trains the chest and back together while heavily challenging core stability and shoulder control, making it effective for time-efficient strength and muscle-building workouts.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands gripping dumbbells. Perform a push-up by lowering your body until your chest almost touches the ground. As you push back up, lift one dumbbell off the ground and row it towards your side, keeping your elbow close to your body. Alternate arms with each rep, maintaining a strong plank position throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Decline Dumbbell Fly and Press
description: The decline dumbbell fly and press is a chest-focused dumbbell complex performed on a decline bench. Each rep alternates between a wide-arc fly and a standard press, emphasizing the lower portion of the chest while also engaging the shoulders and triceps. Because the fly limits the load, it is typically done with moderate to high reps to create significant muscular tension using relatively light weights.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with dumbbells in each hand. Lower the dumbbells to the sides in a wide arc, keeping arms straight, then lift them back up to the starting position. Immediately press the dumbbells upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower them back down to the starting position. Alternate between the fly and press movements for the desired number of reps.

name: Incline Dumbbell Fly and Press
description: The incline dumbbell fly and press is a chest-focused dumbbell complex performed on an incline bench. Each rep alternates between a fly, emphasizing a deep stretch and contraction of the upper chest, and a press, allowing heavier loading through a multi-joint movement. This combination increases time under tension and metabolic stress for the chest, especially the upper pecs, even with relatively light weights. It is typically performed for moderate to high reps, such as 8-12 or more, within upper-body or chest-focused workouts.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with dumbbells in each hand. Perform a fly by lifting the dumbbells out to the sides until they are at a 45-degree angle, stretching your chest. Immediately follow with a press by lifting the dumbbells upwards, extending your arms fully. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position and repeat, alternating between fly and press movements.

name: Single-arm Decline Dumbbell Bench Press
description: The single-arm decline dumbbell bench press is a chest-focused strength exercise performed on a decline bench using one dumbbell at a time. It emphasizes the lower portion of the pectoral muscles while also engaging the shoulders, triceps, and core to stabilize the body against rotation. Typically done with moderate loads for 8-12 reps per arm, it is well-suited for intermediate lifters looking to improve unilateral strength and address muscle imbalances.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with your feet secured. Hold a dumbbell in one hand with your arm extended overhead. Lower the dumbbell to your side, keeping your elbow close to your body, then press it back up to the starting position. Alternate arms to complete the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm Incline Dumbbell Bench Press
description: The single-arm incline dumbbell bench press is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the chest while also challenging shoulder stability and core control. Performed on an incline bench with one dumbbell at a time, it is typically done with lighter loads for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench with your back against the pad. Hold a dumbbell in one hand with your arm extended overhead and your palm facing forward. Lower the dumbbell to your shoulder, then press it back up to the starting position, squeezing your chest muscles. Alternate arms with each repetition.

name: Dumbbell Chest Fly
description: The dumbbell chest fly is an isolation-focused chest exercise performed lying on a bench. With a slight bend in the elbows maintained throughout, the arms open wide to stretch the chest and then close in a hugging motion to contract the pectoral muscles, minimizing shoulder and triceps involvement. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions as part of an upper-body or chest-focused workout.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with dumbbells in each hand. Hold the dumbbells with a slight bend in your elbows and arms extended to the sides at shoulder height. Lower the dumbbells out to the sides until you feel a stretch in your chest, then lift them back up towards the center of your body in a hugging motion. Squeeze your chest muscles as your arms come together.

name: Machine Rear Delt Fly
description: The machine rear delt fly targets the rear deltoids and upper back by reversing the motion of a chest fly. Performed seated while facing the machine, you open the arms horizontally to the sides, focusing on squeezing the shoulder blades together. It is typically done with lighter resistance for higher repetitions to emphasize control and muscle activation.
instructions: Sit facing the machine with feet on the floor. Grasp the handles with arms extended and palms facing each other. Open arms to the sides, squeezing shoulder blades together, then return to the starting position in a controlled motion. Keep movements slow and focused to target the rear deltoids.

name: AM Dumbbell Bench Press
description: The dumbbell bench press is a classic strength exercise performed lying on a bench, pressing dumbbells from chest level to arm’s length to build size and strength in the chest.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor. Grip dumbbells with your palms facing forward and hold them over your chest. Press the dumbbells upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower them back to the starting position with control. Breathe naturally and avoid arching your back throughout the movement.

name: Holman Push-Up to Belly Touch
description: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and a dumbbell placed just outside one hand. Perform a push-up, lowering your chest toward the floor while keeping your body in a straight line. At the top of the push-up, reach one hand across your body to lightly touch the dumbbell near your belly area without rotating your hips. Return the hand to the floor and repeat, alternating the reaching hand each rep while maintaining core tension and stable hips.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and a dumbbell beside one hand. Perform a push-up, lowering your chest while keeping your body straight. At the top, reach one hand across to lightly touch the dumbbell near your belly without rotating your hips. Return the hand to the floor and repeat, alternating hands while maintaining core tension.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm Dumbbell Floor Press
description: The single-arm dumbbell floor press is a multijoint pressing exercise performed lying on the floor. With a reduced range of motion compared to a bench press, it emphasizes the chest, triceps, and shoulders while the single-arm setup increases core stabilization demands and helps address side-to-side strength imbalances.
instructions: Lie on the floor with your knees bent and feet flat. Hold a dumbbell in one hand with your arm extended overhead and your other arm at your side for support. Lower the dumbbell to your chest by bending your elbow, then press it back up to the starting position. Alternate arms with each rep to maintain balance and work both sides evenly.

name: Total Fitness Weighted Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with added weight on the upper back to increase resistance. It primarily targets the chest while also engaging the triceps, shoulders, upper back, and core. Typically, a partner places and stabilizes the weight plate for safety, and a spotter is recommended when using heavier loads.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Have a partner carefully place a weight plate on your upper back, securing it in place. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position, keeping your core engaged and body straight. Breathe naturally and avoid arching your back throughout the movement.

name: Incline Dumbbell Fly
description: Lie on an incline bench holding a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with a slight bend in your elbows. Slowly lower the weights out to the sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch across your chest, then squeeze your chest to bring the dumbbells back together over your chest, maintaining the elbow angle throughout.
instructions: Lie on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand above your chest. Hold the dumbbells with a slight bend in your elbows. Lower the weights out to the sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch across your chest. Bring the dumbbells back together over your chest, squeezing your chest and maintaining the elbow angle.

name: 30 Chest Dumbbells-Together Press
description: Lie on a bench holding a pair of dumbbells directly over your chest with palms facing each other and the bells touching. Keeping the dumbbells pressed together throughout, lower them under control toward the mid-chest, then press back up until your arms are extended. Maintain a slight arch in your lower back, feet planted, and shoulder blades retracted to emphasize the chest.
instructions: Lie on a bench holding dumbbells over your chest with palms facing each other and bells touching. Lower the dumbbells under control toward your mid-chest, keeping them pressed together. Press the dumbbells back up to the starting position with arms extended, maintaining a slight arch in your lower back and feet planted. Keep your shoulder blades retracted to target your chest muscles.

name: 30 Chest Dumbbells-Together Incline Bench Press
description: Lie on an incline bench holding a pair of dumbbells together directly above your upper chest with arms extended. Keep the dumbbells pressed together throughout the movement. Lower them under control toward your upper chest, keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from your torso. Pause briefly, then press the dumbbells back up while maintaining contact between them and keeping your shoulders retracted against the bench.
instructions: Lie on an incline bench holding dumbbells together above your upper chest with arms extended. Lower the dumbbells under control towards your upper chest, keeping elbows at a 45-degree angle. Pause briefly, then press the dumbbells back up while keeping them together. Maintain shoulder retraction against the bench throughout the movement.

name: 30-Degree Incline Dumbbell Fly
description: Lie on a 30-degree incline bench holding a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with a slight bend in your elbows. Lower the weights out to the sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch across your chest, then squeeze your chest to bring the dumbbells back together over your chest, maintaining the elbow angle throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bodyweight Flyes
description: The double-bar roll-out chest fly is a challenging bodyweight-style chest fly performed using two barbells as rolling handles. From a plank-like position, you extend your arms outward in a fly motion while maintaining a strong, braced core, then pull the arms back in to return to the start. This movement heavily targets the chest while also demanding significant core stability and shoulder control.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands on the ground. Extend your arms outward in a fly motion, keeping your core braced and shoulders stable. Lower your arms slowly, then pull them back in to the starting position, squeezing your chest. Keep your body straight and engaged throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Close-Grip EZ-Bar Bench Press
description: The close-grip EZ-bar bench press is a compound pressing exercise that emphasizes the triceps while also working the chest and shoulders. Performed lying on a bench with a narrow grip on an EZ bar, it is often used to build pressing strength and arm size, and may be combined in complexes with movements like pull-overs or skullcrushers.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the EZ-bar with your hands closer than shoulder-width apart. Lower the bar to your chest, keeping your elbows close to your body. Press the bar upwards, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position. Breathe naturally and avoid arching your back throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Chest Dip
description: A bodyweight dip performed on parallel bars with a forward torso lean and flared elbows to emphasize the chest while also engaging the triceps and shoulders. Commonly used for upper-body strength and muscle growth.
instructions: Stand in front of parallel bars with hands grasping the bars at shoulder width. Lower your body by bending your elbows until your arms are bent at a 90-degree angle, leaning forward to emphasize your chest. Keep your elbows flared and your core engaged throughout the movement. Push back up to the starting position by straightening your arms.

name: Drop Push
description: A beginner plyometric push-up variation where you start in a high plank, quickly lower your chest toward the floor, and then push back up explosively to build power and endurance in the chest and arms.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands shoulder-width apart. Quickly lower your chest toward the floor, keeping your core engaged and body in a straight line. Push back up explosively to the starting position, extending your arms fully. Land softly on the balls of your feet and repeat, maintaining control throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Forward Drag with Press
description: Walk forward while dragging a weighted sled or load and perform a pressing motion with the arms each step, focusing on driving through the legs and bracing the core to support the chest and shoulder press.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, grasping handles attached to the weighted sled. Take a step forward, dragging the sled, and press the handles forward, extending your arms. Drive through your legs, keeping your core braced to support the press. Repeat with each step, alternating legs and maintaining control throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Chain Press
description: A bench press variation where chains are added to the bar so resistance increases as you press upward, emphasizing lockout strength and power through the top range of the movement.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor. Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, feeling the chains' weight. Lower the bar to your chest, then press upward, extending your arms and locking out your elbows, focusing on explosive power through the top range. Control the descent back to your chest, repeating for desired reps.

name: Behind Head Chest Stretch
description: Sit or stand tall and interlace your fingers behind your head, keeping your elbows wide. Gently draw your elbows back and lift your chest to feel a stretch across the front of your chest and shoulders. Hold the position without arching your lower back, then relax and repeat.
instructions: Sit or stand tall with good posture. Interlace your fingers behind your head, keeping your elbows wide. Gently draw your elbows back and lift your chest to feel the stretch. Hold briefly, then relax and repeat.

name: Pass-through Stretch with Band
description: A shoulder mobility drill where you hold a resistance band at arm’s length in front of you with a wide grip and pass it over your head and behind your body, then return to the front. It helps improve shoulder mobility and activates the upper back and rotator cuff, commonly used as a warm-up for overhead lifting.
instructions: Hold a resistance band at arm's length in front of you with a wide grip. Pass the band over your head and behind your body, keeping your arms straight. Return the band to the starting position in front of you. Repeat the motion, focusing on smooth and controlled movements to stretch your shoulders.

name: Weighted Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with a weight plate placed on the upper back to increase resistance. It primarily targets the chest while also engaging the triceps, shoulders, upper back, and core. Typically performed with a spotter to safely position and remove the weight.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart and feet hip-width apart. Have a spotter carefully place a weight plate on your upper back. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position, keeping your core engaged and body straight. Exhale as you push up, and have your spotter remove the weight plate after completing the exercise.

name: Heavy Bag Thrust
description: From an athletic stance, drive your chest and arms explosively into a heavy bag, extending through the hips and pushing the bag away, then reset and repeat for powerful, plyometric chest work.
instructions: Stand in an athletic stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Drive your chest and arms explosively into the heavy bag, extending through the hips. Push the bag away from you, using your legs and core for power. Reset to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Decline Explosive Push-Up
description: A plyometric push-up variation performed with the feet elevated, emphasizing the upper chest and shoulders while developing explosive pressing power.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with feet elevated on a decline surface. Place hands shoulder-width apart and engage core muscles. Lower body to the ground, then explosively push up, extending arms fully and briefly lifting hands off the ground. Land softly on the balls of your hands to control the movement and repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Suspended Chest Fly
description: A chest isolation exercise performed with suspension straps, focusing on controlled horizontal abduction and adduction of the arms to strengthen the chest and shoulders while engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Hold the suspension straps with arms extended to sides at shoulder height. Lean forward slightly, engaging your core for stability. Perform a chest fly by lifting your arms together until they meet in front of you, then control the return to the starting position. Keep your arms straight throughout the movement.

name: Svend Press
description: The Svend press is a chest-focused pressing exercise where you squeeze a weight (such as plates or a dumbbell) between your hands while pressing straight out from the chest, emphasizing constant tension and muscle activation with relatively light loads.
instructions: Hold a weight, such as a plate or dumbbell, with both hands. Squeeze the weight between your hands, keeping your arms straight. Press the weight straight out from your chest, maintaining constant tension and muscle activation. Lower the weight back to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement.

name: Bar Push-Up Smith Machine
description: A hands-elevated push-up performed against a bar on a Smith machine, placing the body at an angle to the floor to reduce load and make the movement more accessible while targeting the chest and supporting muscles.
instructions: Stand in front of the Smith machine with the bar at chest height. Place hands on the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, with feet on the floor behind you. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the bar, keeping elbows close to your body. Push back to the starting position, extending your arms fully.

name: Plate-weighted Push-up
description: A push-up variation where weight is added by placing plates on the upper back to increase resistance. It primarily targets the chest while also engaging the triceps, shoulders, and core. A spotter is recommended to safely position and remove the plates and to monitor form as load increases.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart. Have a spotter carefully place weight plates on your upper back. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and maintain proper form throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Double-Bar Roll-Out Chest Fly
description: The double-bar roll-out chest fly is an advanced chest-focused strength exercise performed using two barbells as rolling handles. From a push-up position with hands on the barbell sleeves, you roll the bars outward into a wide fly position while maintaining a rigid plank, then pull them back in using the chest and core. This movement heavily targets the chest while demanding significant core stability, shoulder control, and full-body tension.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands on the barbell sleeves. Roll the bars outward into a wide fly position, keeping your plank rigid and core engaged. Pull the bars back in towards your body, squeezing your chest and maintaining control throughout the movement. Keep your entire body tense and stable throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Plate Dynamo Push-Up
description: A dynamic push-up variation performed with hands elevated or destabilized on plates to increase chest activation, shoulder stability, and core engagement. The movement emphasizes controlled lowering and powerful pressing while maintaining a rigid plank position.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands placed on plates or unstable surfaces. Lower your body down by bending your elbows, keeping your core engaged and your body in a straight line. Push back up to the starting position, extending your arms and maintaining control throughout the movement. Keep your shoulders stable and your chest activated throughout the exercise.

name: Holman Towel Drag Two Steps to Push-Up
description: From a push-up position with feet on a towel, walk your hands forward two small steps while dragging your feet, then perform a push-up and walk your hands back to the starting position. Repeat for controlled reps, keeping your core tight and body in a straight line.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with feet on a towel. Walk your hands forward two small steps while dragging your feet, keeping your core tight and body straight. Lower your body to perform a push-up, then walk your hands back to the starting position. Repeat for controlled reps, maintaining a straight line from head to heels.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Taylor Val Slide Push-Up/Fly Combo
description: A chest-focused bodyweight exercise performed in a push-up position using sliding implements under the hands to combine a push-up with a fly motion, increasing time under tension and core engagement.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands on sliding implements. Lower your body until your chest almost touches the ground, keeping core engaged. As you push back up, slide your hands outward to perform a fly motion, squeezing your chest muscles. Return to the starting position by sliding your hands back in and repeating the movement.

name: Exercise Ball Chest Stretch
description: A beginner-friendly chest stretch performed lying back over an exercise ball to open the pectoral muscles. Often used in warm-ups or cool-downs to improve chest mobility and posture. Intensity should be reduced or the stretch discontinued if it causes pain in the front of the shoulder.
instructions: Lie back over an exercise ball with your upper back and head supported. Place your feet flat on the floor for stability. Slowly let your chest sink downwards, stretching your chest muscles, and hold for 15-30 seconds. Breathe deeply and naturally, stopping if you experience any pain in the front of your shoulder.

name: Bosu Ball Push-Up
description: A push-up variation performed with hands on an unstable surface to increase chest activation and core stability. Typically done using a BOSU or similar unstable platform.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands on the dome side of the BOSU ball. Place hands shoulder-width apart and engage core for stability. Lower body until chest nearly touches the BOSU ball, then push back up to the starting position. Keep body in a straight line from head to heels throughout the exercise.

name: Feet-Elevated Push-Up on Ball
description: A push-up variation where the feet are elevated on a stability ball, increasing core and shoulder stabilization demands while placing greater emphasis on the chest and shoulders compared to standard floor push-ups.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands shoulder-width apart on the ground. Place your feet on a stability ball, engaging your core for balance. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Standing Alternating Band Speed Row
description: The standing alternating band speed row is an explosive back exercise performed with resistance bands. From a standing position facing the anchor point, you row one arm at a time in rapid alternation, focusing on pulling the elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades together. It is typically used for power or speed work, or as a high-rep burnout for the upper and middle back.
instructions: Stand facing the anchor point with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a resistance band in each hand, with the band at chest height. Pull one arm back, keeping elbow close to body, then quickly alternate to the other arm, focusing on rapid repetition and squeezing shoulder blades together. Keep core engaged and maintain controlled movement throughout the exercise.

name: Band Roundhouse Elbow
description: The band roundhouse elbow is an explosive rotational power exercise using resistance bands. It primarily targets the obliques and abs while training full-body coordination and striking mechanics. From an athletic stance with the band anchored behind you, rotate the hips and torso as you drive the elbow across the body in a roundhouse motion, then return under control. It can be used in ab workouts, martial arts conditioning, or general strength training.
instructions: Stand in an athletic stance with the resistance band anchored behind you. Rotate your hips and torso, driving your elbow across your body in a roundhouse motion. Keep your core engaged and maintain control throughout the movement. Return to the starting position under control, repeating the motion with power and precision.

name: Palms-down Wrist Curl Over Bench
description: The palms-down wrist curl over bench is a forearm isolation exercise performed with a barbell. With your forearms resting on a bench and palms facing down, you flex and extend at the wrists to build forearm strength and muscular endurance, typically using higher repetitions.
instructions: Sit on a bench with forearms resting on the edge and palms facing down. Hold a barbell with your hands shoulder-width apart. Slowly curl your wrists up, then lower them back down to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Palms-Up Wrist Curl Over Bench
description: The palms-up wrist curl over bench is a forearm isolation exercise performed with a barbell. With your forearms resting on a bench and palms facing up, you flex and extend at the wrists to build forearm strength and grip endurance, typically using higher repetitions.
instructions: Sit on a bench with forearms resting on the edge and palms facing up. Hold a barbell with an overhand grip, hands hanging off the edge of the bench. Curl your wrists up, lifting the barbell, then lower it back down to the starting position. Keep your forearms still and focus on wrist movement throughout the exercise.

name: Standing Behind-the-Back Wrist Curl
description: The standing behind-the-back wrist curl is a forearm isolation exercise performed with a barbell held behind the body. With arms extended and palms facing backward, the wrists are flexed to lift the barbell using the forearm muscles, then lowered under control. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions to build forearm strength and endurance.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hold a barbell behind your back with arms extended and palms facing backward. Hold the barbell with a firm grip and keep your upper arms still. Lower your wrists to lift the barbell up, then slowly lower it back down to the starting position under control. Keep your movements smooth and focused on using your forearm muscles.

name: Seated Finger Curl
description: The seated finger curl is a forearm isolation exercise performed while seated, using a barbell held in the hands with the forearms supported on the thighs or a bench. By allowing the barbell to roll down toward the fingertips and then curling the fingers to grip it back up, it targets the finger flexors and forearm muscles. Typically done for moderate to high repetitions, it helps build forearm strength and endurance, making it popular with rock climbers and athletes who need strong grip and forearm development.
instructions: Sit on a bench or chair with your forearms resting on your thighs. Hold a barbell with an overhand grip, allowing your arms to hang straight down. Let the barbell roll down toward your fingertips, then curl your fingers to grip it back up, squeezing your forearms. Lower the barbell back down to the starting position, repeating for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Seated Palms-Up Wrist Curl
description: The seated palms-up wrist curl is a forearm isolation exercise performed while seated, using a barbell with an underhand (supinated) grip. With the forearms resting on the thighs or a bench and wrists hanging off the edge, the lifter curls the barbell up by flexing the wrists, then lowers it under control. It is typically done for higher repetitions, such as 10-15 reps per set, to build forearm strength and grip endurance.
instructions: Sit on a bench with feet on floor. Hold a barbell with an underhand grip and rest forearms on thighs. Curl the barbell up by flexing wrists, then lower it under control. Keep movements slow and controlled throughout the exercise.

name: Bottoms-Up Clean From The Hang Position
description: From a standing position, hold a kettlebell in one hand with a neutral grip and the bell hanging at arm’s length. Hinge slightly at the hips, then explosively extend your hips and pull the kettlebell upward, guiding it into a bottoms-up position with the bell above the handle and your forearm vertical. Pause briefly to stabilize, then lower the kettlebell under control back to the hang position and repeat.
instructions: Stand with a kettlebell in one hand, hanging at arm's length. Hinge slightly at the hips, then explosively extend your hips to pull the kettlebell upward. Guide the kettlebell into a bottoms-up position with the bell above the handle and forearm vertical, pausing briefly to stabilize. Lower the kettlebell under control back to the hang position to repeat.

name: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl
description: Sit on a bench with your forearm resting on your thigh, palm facing up, holding a dumbbell. Let the wrist extend so the dumbbell lowers toward the floor, then curl your wrist upward to contract the forearm flexors. Lower under control and repeat, then switch arms.
instructions: Sit on a bench with your forearm resting on your thigh, holding a dumbbell with your palm facing up. Let your wrist extend to lower the dumbbell toward the floor. Curl your wrist upward to contract your forearm flexors, then lower the dumbbell under control. Repeat with the other arm after completing the desired number of repetitions.

name: Palms-Up Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
description: Sit on a bench holding a dumbbell in one hand with your forearm resting on the bench, palm facing up and wrist hanging off the edge. Keeping your forearm flat on the bench, slowly curl the dumbbell up by flexing your wrist, squeezing the forearm at the top. Lower the weight under control to a full stretch and repeat for reps, then switch arms.
instructions: Sit on a bench with forearm resting on it and hold a dumbbell in one hand, palm up. Keep your forearm flat and curl the dumbbell up by flexing your wrist. Lower the weight under control to a full stretch. Switch arms after completing the desired number of reps.

name: Seated Palms-Down Wrist Curl
description: The seated palms-down wrist curl is a forearm isolation exercise performed while seated, holding dumbbells with an overhand grip and curling only at the wrists. It is typically done for higher repetitions to build forearm strength and muscular endurance as part of a grip- or arm-focused workout.
instructions: Sit on a bench or chair with dumbbells in each hand, holding them with an overhand grip and palms facing down. Rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists extending over your knees. Curl your wrists up, keeping your forearms still, and then lower them back down to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Palms-Down Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
description: Sit on a bench holding dumbbells with your forearms resting on the bench, palms facing down and wrists hanging off the edge. Keeping your forearms flat on the bench, curl the dumbbells up by extending your wrists, then slowly lower them back down to the starting position to work the forearms.
instructions: Sit on a bench with forearms resting on it and dumbbells in hands, palms facing down. Let your wrists hang off the edge of the bench with your forearms flat. Curl the dumbbells up by extending your wrists, then slowly lower them back down to the starting position. Keep your forearms still and flat on the bench throughout the movement.

name: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
description: Sit on a bench with your forearm resting on your thigh or the bench, palm facing down and wrist hanging just past the edge. Holding a dumbbell, slowly curl your hand upward by extending the wrist, squeezing the forearm at the top. Lower the weight under control to the starting position and repeat before switching arms.
instructions: Sit on a bench with forearm resting on thigh or bench, palm facing down. Hold a dumbbell and let wrist hang just past the edge. Slowly curl hand upward by extending wrist, squeezing forearm at top. Lower weight under control to starting position and repeat on other arm.

name: Seated Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl
description: Sit on a bench with your forearms resting on your thighs, palms facing up, and a dumbbell in each hand. Let the dumbbells roll toward your fingers, then curl your wrists upward to contract the forearms. Lower under control and repeat for the desired reps.
instructions: Sit on a bench with forearms resting on thighs, palms facing up, and a dumbbell in each hand. Let the dumbbells roll toward your fingers. Curl your wrists upward to contract the forearms, then lower under control. Repeat for the desired reps.

name: Partner Farmer's Walk
description: The partner farmer's walk is a loaded carry where two partners alternate walking for a set distance or time while holding heavy dumbbells or other weights at their sides. One partner walks while carrying the weights, then passes them to the other partner, who repeats for the same distance or time.

name: Dumbbell Suitcase Carry
description: Walk while holding a single dumbbell at your side like a suitcase, keeping your torso upright and core braced to resist leaning toward the weight. This unilateral carry challenges grip strength, forearms, and the entire core, and works well as a finisher, in fat-loss circuits, or as an accessory to heavy lifts like the deadlift.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell at your side with your arm straight. Keep your torso upright and core braced to maintain balance. Walk forward with a normal gait, avoiding leaning toward the weight. Switch arms after a set distance to work both sides evenly.

name: Suitcase Dumbbell Carry
description: Walk while holding a single dumbbell at your side like a suitcase, keeping your torso upright and core braced to resist leaning toward the weight.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a dumbbell at your side with your arm straight, keeping your core braced and torso upright. Walk forward, taking small steps and keeping the dumbbell close to your body, avoiding leaning towards the weight. Keep your posture straight and stable throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Side Plank with Dumbbell External Rotation
description: An isometric side plank combined with a light dumbbell external rotation to train the core and shoulder stabilizers, especially the rotator cuff, while challenging balance and anti-rotation strength.
instructions: Lie on your side with feet stacked and hands under shoulders. Lift your hips off the ground, engaging your core, and hold a light dumbbell in your top hand. Rotate your top shoulder outward, keeping your elbow close to your body, and hold for a brief moment. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position, maintaining a stable side plank throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Rickshaw Carry
description: Walk while holding heavy weights at your sides to build grip strength, forearm endurance, and full-body stability. Maintain an upright posture, brace your core, and take controlled steps over a set distance or time.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding heavy weights at your sides. Keep your posture upright, bracing your core and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Take controlled steps forward, maintaining a steady pace over a set distance or time, keeping the weights close to your body. Keep your grip firm and your arms straight, avoiding any swinging or jerking motions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Farmer's Walk
description: The dumbbell farmer's walk involves walking while holding heavy dumbbells at your sides, building grip strength, core stability, and full-body conditioning. It is often used as a finisher, in fat-loss circuits, or as an accessory to heavy lifts like the deadlift.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Wrist Roller
description: Stand holding a wrist roller with both hands at shoulder height, arms extended in front of you. Slowly roll the weight up by flexing and extending your wrists until the rope is fully wound, then reverse the motion to lower it under control. Focus on smooth, controlled wrist movement without using your shoulders or body momentum.
instructions: Stand with a wrist roller held at shoulder height, arms extended in front of you. Hold the roller with both hands and slowly roll the weight up by flexing and extending your wrists. Keep the movement smooth and controlled, avoiding use of your shoulders or body momentum. Lower the weight back down under control, focusing on wrist movement only.

name: Standing Olympic Plate Hand Squeeze
description: Stand upright and hold an Olympic plate with your fingers and thumb, squeezing the plate to train grip and forearm strength. Maintain a neutral wrist, keep your shoulders relaxed, and hold the squeeze for time or repetitions before switching hands.
instructions: Stand upright with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold an Olympic plate with fingers and thumb, maintaining a neutral wrist. Squeeze the plate to engage grip and forearm muscles, keeping shoulders relaxed. Hold the squeeze for the desired time or repetitions before switching hands.

name: Plate Pinch
description: Stand upright and hold two weight plates together smooth-sides-out using just your fingers and thumb, pinching them tightly. Keep your shoulders relaxed, arms straight or slightly bent at your sides, and maintain a strong grip for the desired time before setting the plates down safely. This exercise targets grip strength and forearm endurance.
instructions: Stand upright with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold two weight plates together with your fingers and thumb, smooth sides facing out, and pinch them tightly. Keep your shoulders relaxed and arms straight or slightly bent at your sides. Maintain a strong grip for the desired time before setting the plates down safely.

name: Landmine Press and Catch
description: The landmine press and catch is an explosive single-arm pressing exercise using one end of a barbell anchored on the floor. You press the bar up and slightly forward with one arm, then release and catch it with the opposite hand, emphasizing power, coordination, and shoulder strength. It primarily targets the shoulders while also engaging the upper body and core for stability.
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the barbell with one hand and press it up and slightly forward, extending your arm fully. Release the barbell and quickly switch hands to catch it with the opposite arm. Lower the barbell back to the starting position and repeat with the same arm or alternate arms.

name: Plate Halo
description: A circular shoulder warm-up movement where you hold a weight in front of your chest and slowly rotate it around your head to mobilize the shoulder complex and upper back.
instructions: Hold a weight plate in front of your chest with both hands. Slowly rotate the plate in a circular motion around your head, keeping your arms straight. Rotate the plate first clockwise and then counterclockwise to mobilize your shoulder complex and upper back. Keep your core engaged and maintain control throughout the movement.

name: Bryant Wrist Curl
description: A forearm isolation exercise where you flex and extend the wrists against resistance to strengthen the wrist flexors and improve grip endurance.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell with your palm facing up. Rest your forearm on a flat surface with your wrist hanging off the edge. Curl your wrist up, lifting the dumbbell, then slowly lower it back down to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Seated Two-Arm Palms-Up Low-Pulley Wrist Curl
description: Sit facing a low pulley with a straight bar attached. Rest your forearms on your thighs or a bench with your palms facing up, wrists hanging just beyond your knees. Grip the bar and allow your wrists to extend so the bar rolls slightly toward your fingers, then curl your wrists upward by flexing your forearms. Pause briefly at the top, then lower under control and repeat.
instructions: Sit facing a low pulley with a straight bar attached. Rest your forearms on your thighs with palms up and grip the bar. Lower the bar by extending your wrists, then curl your wrists up by flexing your forearms. Return to the starting position under control.

name: Cable Wrist Curl
description: Sit or stand facing a low cable pulley with a straight bar attachment. Grasp the bar with an underhand grip and rest your forearms on your thighs or a bench, allowing your wrists to extend so the bar rolls toward your fingers. Curl the bar up by flexing your wrists, squeezing the forearms at the top, then slowly lower back to the starting position with control.
instructions: Sit or stand facing a low cable pulley with a straight bar attachment. Grasp the bar with an underhand grip and rest your forearms on your thighs or a bench. Curl the bar up by flexing your wrists, squeezing your forearms at the top. Lower the bar back down to the starting position with control.

name: Single-arm Cable Push-down
description: The single-arm cable push-down is an isolation exercise targeting the triceps one arm at a time, emphasizing strict form and a strong mind-muscle connection with moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the cable at chest height. Hold the cable handle with one hand and extend your arm fully, keeping your elbow close to your body. Lower the handle down towards your thigh by bending your elbow, then push it back up to the starting position, squeezing your triceps. Switch arms to complete the exercise on both sides.

name: Wrist Circles
description: Stand or sit with your arms extended in front of you and make slow, controlled circles with your wrists in both directions to gently mobilize the forearm and wrist joints.
instructions: Stand or sit with arms extended in front of you. Hold your arms straight and make small circles with your wrists. Circle your wrists in one direction, then switch to the other direction, moving slowly and controlled. Keep your arms still and focus on gentle wrist movements.

name: Kneeling Forearm Stretch
description: From a kneeling position, place your hands on the floor in front of you with fingers pointing toward your knees and gently lean back to stretch the forearms. Hold the stretch without bouncing and breathe steadily.
instructions: Kneel on the floor with your hands in front of you. Place your hands on the ground with fingers pointing towards your knees. Gently lean back to stretch your forearms, keeping your hands in place. Hold the stretch and breathe steadily, avoiding any bouncing movement.

name: Hip Extension with Bands
description: Attach a resistance band to a low anchor and around your hips, then hinge at the hips and extend them forward against the band’s resistance to fully contract the glutes while keeping your core braced and back neutral.
instructions: Attach a resistance band to a low anchor and around your hips. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hinge at the hips, keeping your back neutral. Extend your hips forward against the band's resistance, squeezing your glutes at the top. Keep your core braced throughout the movement.

name: Hip Lift with Band
description: Lie on your back with a resistance band looped just above your knees, feet flat and hip-width apart. Brace your core, press your knees slightly out against the band, and drive through your heels to lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees. Squeeze your glutes at the top, then lower your hips back down under control and repeat.
instructions: Lie on your back with a resistance band above your knees, feet flat and hip-width apart. Press your knees out against the band and drive through your heels to lift your hips. Squeeze your glutes at the top, forming a straight line from shoulders to knees. Lower your hips back down under control to repeat.

name: HM Monster Walk
description: A lateral and diagonal walking exercise using a resistance band around the legs to strengthen the glutes and hips while improving hip stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and a resistance band around your legs just above the knees. Hold the band with your hands if needed to maintain tension. Take small steps to the side, keeping your knees slightly bent, and walk laterally while maintaining band tension. Step diagonally and continue walking in different directions to target your glutes and hips.

name: Piriformis SMR
description: Piriformis self-myofascial release (SMR) uses a foam roller or similar tool to apply targeted pressure to the piriformis area in the upper gluteal region. This technique aims to reduce tightness and discomfort around the glutes and hips and is often paired with SMR for the IT band or TFL and follow-up stretching.
instructions: Sit on the floor with the affected leg crossed over the other. Place a foam roller under the gluteal region, targeting the piriformis area. Slowly roll the foam roller back and forth, applying moderate pressure to release tension. Hold on areas of discomfort for 20-30 seconds to allow for release.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Glute Bridge
description: The barbell glute bridge is a lower-body strength exercise that primarily targets the glutes and hamstrings. Performed lying on the floor with a barbell across the hips, it can be used as a main strength movement, a warm-up activation drill, or a high-rep finisher at the end of a lower-body workout.
instructions: Lie on the floor with knees bent and feet flat. Position a barbell across your hips, just above the pubic bone. Lift your hips up towards the ceiling, squeezing your glutes and pushing through your heels. Lower your hips back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
description: The barbell hip thrust targets the posterior chain, particularly the glutes, by driving the hips into full extension while supporting a weighted barbell across the hips.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench or platform with your feet flat on the floor. Place a barbell across your hips, just above the pubic bone, and hold it in place with your hands. Drive your hips upwards, squeezing your glutes and pushing your feet into the floor, then slowly lower the barbell back down to the starting position. Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kneeling Squat
description: From a tall kneeling position with hips extended, rest a barbell across your upper back and shoulders. Brace your core and squeeze your glutes as you sit your hips back toward your heels, keeping your torso upright. Drive your hips forward to return to the tall kneeling position, focusing on contracting the glutes at the top of the movement.
instructions: Start in a tall kneeling position with a barbell across your upper back and shoulders. Brace your core and squeeze your glutes as you sit your hips back toward your heels. Keep your torso upright and avoid arching your back. Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position, squeezing your glutes at the top.

name: Kneeling Jump Squat
description: From a tall kneeling position with a barbell on your upper back, explosively drive your hips forward and extend through the knees to jump from your knees up to your feet, landing in a squat position before standing tall. This movement emphasizes powerful hip extension and glute activation.
instructions: Start in a tall kneeling position with a barbell on your upper back. Explosively drive your hips forward, extending through the knees to jump up from your knees to your feet. Land in a squat position with your knees bent and back straight. Stand up tall to complete the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Thruster
description: A full-body kettlebell movement that combines a front squat with an overhead press. Holding the kettlebell in a goblet or bottoms-up position, you squat down and then drive up powerfully, using the leg drive to assist the press overhead. It can be used for strength, muscle-building, or conditioning depending on load and rep scheme.
instructions: Hold the kettlebell at chest level with both hands. Squat down, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes. Drive up through your legs, extending your hips and knees, and press the kettlebell straight up overhead. Return to the starting position, controlling the kettlebell as you lower it back to your chest.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Taylor Kettlebell Swing
description: A hip-dominant kettlebell swing variation emphasizing powerful hip extension to target the glutes and posterior chain while also engaging the core and shoulders for stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the kettlebell with both hands. Hinge at the hips, swinging the kettlebell back between your legs, then thrust your hips forward to swing it up to chest height. Keep your core engaged and back straight, using your hips to generate power. Control the swing, avoiding jerky movements, and repeat for desired repetitions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm triceps kick-back
description: The single-arm triceps kick-back is an isolation exercise targeting the triceps, performed by hinging at the hips and extending the working arm straight back to fully contract the triceps. It is typically done for moderate to high reps, such as 8-12 per arm, as part of an upper-body or arm-focused workout.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in one hand with arm bent and palm facing back. Hinge at the hips and lean forward slightly, keeping upper arm still. Extend the working arm straight back, keeping elbow close to body, to fully contract the triceps. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Holman Lateral High Knees to Quad Touch Jump
description: A dynamic lateral movement drill combining high knees with a jump and quad touch to build lower-body power, coordination, and conditioning. Step laterally while driving your knees high, then explode into a jump and quickly reach to touch your quads before landing softly and repeating.
instructions: Step laterally to one side, driving one knee high towards your chest. Quickly explode into a jump, then reach down to touch the quad of the same leg. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately repeat on the other side. Alternate legs with each repetition to maintain balance and generate power.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Suspended Hip Thrust
description: A hip-dominant bridge variation where the feet are elevated or suspended while the upper back is supported, emphasizing glute activation and hip extension.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench or platform with your upper back supported. Place your feet on a suspended surface, such as a bench or step, with knees bent at a 90-degree angle. Slowly lift your hips up, squeezing your glutes and pushing your feet down into the suspended surface, then lower back down to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-leg Cable Hip Extension
description: The single-leg cable hip extension is an isolation exercise that targets the gluteus maximus. Performed standing with a cable attached to one ankle, the working leg extends backward against resistance to emphasize hip extension and glute activation. It is commonly used as a burnout movement on lower-body days and is typically performed for moderate to high reps, such as 8-12 or more per set.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the cable attached to one ankle. Lift the working leg off the ground, keeping it straight, and extend it backward against the cable resistance. Slowly return to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise. Keep your core engaged and balance on the other leg to maintain stability.

name: Cable Pull Through
description: The cable pull through is a hip-hinge exercise that targets the glutes and hamstrings by pulling a cable attachment between the legs while maintaining a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand facing away from cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold cable handle with both hands and hinge at hips, keeping back straight and knees slightly bent. Pull handle through legs, squeezing glutes and pushing hips back. Return to starting position, controlling the movement and keeping core engaged.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Cable Rope Pull-Through Deadlift
description: The cable rope pull-through deadlift is a hip-hinge exercise that targets the glutes and hamstrings by pulling a rope attachment through the legs using a cable machine.
instructions: Stand facing cable machine with rope attachment at lowest setting. Hold rope with both hands and stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hinge hips and pull rope through legs, keeping back straight and core engaged, then return to standing position. Keep control throughout the movement, squeezing glutes and hamstrings at the top.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Cable Pull-Through
description: Stand facing away from a cable machine with the rope attachment set low between your legs. Hinge at the hips, keeping your back flat and knees slightly bent, then drive your hips forward to stand tall, squeezing your glutes at the top while keeping the cable path between your legs.
instructions: Stand facing away from the cable machine with the rope attachment between your legs. Hinge at the hips, keeping your back flat and knees slightly bent. Drive your hips forward to stand tall, squeezing your glutes at the top. Keep the cable path between your legs throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Glute-Ham Raise
description: The glute-ham raise is a posterior-chain exercise performed on a glute-ham developer to strengthen the hamstrings and glutes by extending the hips and flexing the knees against bodyweight resistance.
instructions: Lie on the glute-ham developer with legs secured under the roller. Lift your hips up by pushing against the pad, extending your hips and flexing your knees. Slowly lower your hips back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement. Keep your core engaged and focus on squeezing your glutes and hamstrings.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Glute Bridge
description: The glute bridge is a lower-body exercise that primarily targets the glutes while also engaging the hamstrings and lower back. It can be performed using just bodyweight or with added resistance such as a barbell, dumbbells, or resistance bands. It is effective for glute activation, can be used in warm-ups or main strength work, and can be performed for time or repetitions.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the ground. Slowly lift your hips up towards the ceiling, squeezing your glutes at the top of the movement. Lower your hips back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the entire motion. Repeat for desired repetitions, focusing on proper form and glute engagement.

name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
description: The single-leg glute bridge is a unilateral lower-body exercise performed lying on your back with one foot planted and the other leg extended. You drive through the heel of the working leg to lift your hips, targeting the glutes, hamstrings, and lower back while also challenging core stability.
instructions: Lie on your back with one foot planted and the other leg extended. Drive through the heel of the working leg to lift your hips, squeezing your glutes. Keep your core engaged and your body stable throughout the movement. Lower your hips back down to the starting position, controlling the descent.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Step-up with Knee Raise
description: The step-up with knee raise is a lower-body strength exercise focusing on powerful hip extension and stability. From the floor, step one foot onto a stable box or step, drive through the heel of the lead leg to stand tall, then lift the trailing leg up into a high knee position at the top before returning under control. It primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings and can be performed with bodyweight for higher reps as part of a dynamic warm-up, conditioning circuit, or lower-body workout.
instructions: Stand in front of a stable box or step with one foot on the floor. Step up onto the box with one foot and drive through the heel to stand tall. Lift the trailing leg up into a high knee position, then return to the starting position under control. Alternate legs with each repetition.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Glute Kickback
description: From a hands-and-knees position, extend one leg straight back and up by squeezing the glute, then lower with control and repeat before switching sides.
instructions: Start on hands and knees with a stable core. Lift one leg straight back and up by squeezing your glute. Lower your leg back down with control, without touching the floor, and repeat. Switch legs to work the other glute.

name: Standing Hip Extension
description: The standing hip extension is a beginner-friendly exercise that targets the glutes and hamstrings by extending the leg backward from a standing position. It is commonly used in dynamic warm-ups and can be performed for time or repetitions to activate the posterior chain.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Slowly lift one leg backward, keeping knee straight, until you feel a stretch in your glutes and hamstrings. Hold for a brief moment, then lower your leg back to the starting position. Repeat with the other leg, alternating to target both sides.

name: Lying Glute Stretch
description: The lying glute stretch is a floor-based stretch performed while lying on your back. One leg is rotated across the body while you gently pull the leg toward your chest to create a deep stretch in the glutes and hips. It is commonly used in warm-ups, cool-downs, or general mobility sessions to improve hip and glute flexibility.
instructions: Lie on your back with your arms at your sides. Bend one knee and cross it over your other leg, rotating your hip outward. Gently pull the crossed leg toward your chest, keeping your lower back pressed into the floor. Hold the stretch for 30 seconds and then switch legs.

name: Knee Across The Body
description: Lie on your back with legs extended. Bring one knee toward your chest, then gently guide it across your body toward the opposite side while keeping your shoulders on the floor. Hold the stretch, feeling it through the glutes and lower back, then return to the start and switch sides.
instructions: Lie on your back with legs extended. Bring one knee toward your chest, then gently guide it across your body toward the opposite side. Keep your shoulders on the floor and hold the stretch, feeling it in your glutes and lower back. Return to the start and switch sides.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated Glute Stretch
description: Sit upright on a bench or mat with both feet flat on the floor. Cross one ankle over the opposite knee, forming a figure-four position. Keeping your back straight, gently lean your torso forward until you feel a stretch in the glute of the crossed leg. Hold the position, breathing steadily, then switch sides.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench or mat with feet flat on the floor. Cross one ankle over the opposite knee, forming a figure-four position. Lean your torso forward, keeping your back straight, until you feel a stretch in the glute of the crossed leg. Hold and breathe, then switch sides.

name: One Knee To Chest
description: Lie on your back with legs extended. Gently pull one knee toward your chest using your hands, keeping the other leg straight and your lower back relaxed against the floor. Hold the stretch, breathing steadily, then switch sides.
instructions: Lie on your back with legs extended. Pull one knee toward your chest using your hands, keeping the other leg straight. Keep your lower back relaxed against the floor and hold the stretch, breathing steadily. Switch sides to stretch the other leg.

name: Neck Bridge Supine
description: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, then press through your feet and upper back to lift your hips, creating a gentle bridge while keeping your neck neutral and core engaged. Lower under control and repeat, focusing on glute activation and spinal stability rather than pushing into an extreme neck arch.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Press through your feet and upper back to lift your hips, creating a gentle bridge. Keep your neck neutral and core engaged, focusing on glute activation. Lower under control to the starting position, avoiding extreme neck arch.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Walking High Knee
description: Walk forward while driving one knee up toward hip height with each step, keeping your torso tall and engaging your core and glutes to lift and control the leg.
instructions: Start with an upright posture and engage your core. Walk forward, bringing one knee up toward hip height with each step. Keep your torso tall and drive your knees up quickly, landing midfoot or forefoot when stepping down. Focus on quick turnover and controlled leg lift to maintain a brisk pace.

name: Walking Butt Kicks
description: Walk forward while alternately kicking your heels up toward your glutes, maintaining an upright posture and controlled movement to warm up and activate the hamstrings and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and maintain an upright posture. Walk forward at a moderate pace, lifting one heel up toward your glutes with each step. Alternate legs with each step, keeping your knees slightly bent and movement controlled. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the exercise.

name: Walking Glute Stretch
description: A dynamic stretch where you walk forward while pulling one knee toward the opposite shoulder to stretch the glutes and hip area with each step.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Take a step forward with one foot and lift the opposite knee toward your opposite shoulder, stretching your glutes and hip area. Lower your knee back down as you step forward with the other foot, alternating legs with each step. Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout the exercise.

name: Lunge Kick
description: A dynamic lower-body exercise combining a reverse lunge with a front kick to build leg strength, power, and cardiovascular endurance. From standing, step back into a lunge, then drive through the front heel and kick the front leg forward before returning to the start position and repeating.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Step back into a lunge, keeping your front knee behind your toes. Drive through your front heel and kick your front leg forward, extending it fully. Return to the starting position and repeat with the other leg.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Curtsy Lunge
description: The curtsy lunge is a lower-body bodyweight exercise where you step one leg back and across behind the other, lowering into a lunge to target the hips and glutes. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8-12 reps per leg.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Take a large step back with one foot, crossing it behind the other foot. Lower your body into a lunge, keeping your front knee behind your toes and your back knee almost touching the ground. Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Alternating Standing Glute Stretch
description: The alternating standing glute stretch is a lower-body mobility and flexibility drill performed in a partial squat "figure 4" position. While standing, you cross one ankle over the opposite knee, sit the hips back into a shallow squat to feel a stretch through the glutes and hips, then alternate sides. It is commonly used in warm-ups or cool-downs to reduce hip and glute tightness and improve range of motion.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Cross one ankle over the opposite knee, forming a "figure 4" shape. Sit hips back into a shallow squat, feeling the stretch in glutes and hips. Alternate sides by switching the crossed ankle and repeating the stretch.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Side-lying Leg Lift
description: The side-lying leg lift is a bodyweight exercise targeting the hips and glutes. Performed lying on your side, you lift the top leg upward in a controlled motion to activate the glute muscles and hip stabilizers. It is commonly used as a glute activation drill, part of a lower-body warm-up, or in a lower-body circuit, typically for higher repetitions per leg.
instructions: Lie on your side with feet touching and hands under your head for support. Lift your top leg upward, keeping it straight, to a height that feels comfortable for your hip. Slowly lower your leg back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Repeat on the other side to target both legs evenly.

name: Lunge Glute Stretch
description: The lunge glute stretch is a lower-body mobility drill inspired by the yoga pigeon pose. From a lunge position, the front leg is brought across the body to target the glutes, hips, and piriformis while the arms stay straight for support. It can be used in a dynamic warm-up or as part of regular mobility work to improve hip and glute flexibility.
instructions: Start in a lunge position with your front foot facing forward. Bring your front leg across your body, keeping your back leg straight, to stretch your glutes and hips. Keep your arms straight for support and balance. Hold the stretch for a few seconds before returning to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Groiner
description: An explosive bodyweight movement from a plank position, driving one foot up outside the hand to dynamically stretch the hips while engaging the core, shoulders, and legs. Commonly used in athletic or fat-loss circuits for reps or time.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Engage core and lift one foot off ground, driving it up and out to side of hand. Quickly return to starting position and repeat with other foot. Alternate feet rapidly to complete the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: HM Alternating Donkey Kick
description: From an all-fours position, extend one leg back and up toward the ceiling while keeping the knee bent at 90 degrees, then lower with control and alternate legs to target the glutes.
instructions: Start on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Lift one leg back and up, bending knee at 90 degrees, then lower with control. Alternate legs, keeping core engaged and movements slow and controlled. Focus on squeezing glutes at the top of the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: HM Alternating Slow Step-Down With Chair
description: Stand facing away from a sturdy chair or step with one foot on the edge and the other hanging off. Slowly lower your hanging foot toward the floor by bending the standing leg, keeping your chest up and hips square. Lightly tap the heel to the ground without fully shifting weight, then press through the heel of the elevated leg to return to the starting position. Alternate legs, moving with control to emphasize glute engagement and stability.
instructions: Stand facing away from a sturdy chair with one foot on the edge and the other hanging off. Slowly lower your hanging foot toward the floor by bending the standing leg, keeping your chest up and hips square. Lightly tap your heel to the ground, then press through the heel of the elevated leg to return to the starting position. Alternate legs, moving with control to maintain stability.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: HM Right Side Iron Cross
description: Lie on your back with arms extended out to the sides in a T position. Lift your right leg straight up, then slowly lower it across your body toward the left side while keeping your shoulders flat on the floor. Pause briefly at the end range, feeling a stretch through the glutes and hamstrings, then return the leg to the starting position under control. Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a tight core and controlled breathing throughout.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms in a T position. Lift your right leg straight up, then lower it across your body toward the left side, keeping shoulders flat. Pause briefly at the end range, feeling the stretch in your glutes and hamstrings. Return your leg to the starting position under control, maintaining a tight core and controlled breathing.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: HM Single-Leg Bridge
description: Lie on your back with one knee bent and the other leg extended. Brace your core, drive through the heel of the bent leg, and lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knee. Keep the extended leg in line with your torso, pause at the top while squeezing your glutes, then lower under control. Repeat all reps on one side before switching.
instructions: Lie on your back with one knee bent and the other leg extended. Brace your core and drive through the heel of the bent leg to lift your hips. Lift until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knee, squeezing your glutes at the top. Lower under control, repeating on one side before switching to the other.

name: HM Hi-Low
description: A lower-body strength exercise targeting the glutes through a controlled hip hinge and extension pattern, typically moving from a higher to a lower hip position to emphasize glute activation and stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hinge hips and lower torso, keeping back straight and knees slightly bent. Push through heels to extend hips, squeezing glutes at the top of the movement. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement and maintaining glute engagement.

name: HM Kick-Back
description: From a hands-and-knees position, extend one leg straight back and slightly up by squeezing the glute, then return with control. Alternate sides to target the glutes while keeping the core braced and lower back stable.
instructions: Start on hands and knees with core braced and lower back stable. Lift one leg straight back and slightly up by squeezing the glute. Lower the leg back down with control, then alternate to the other side. Keep movements slow and deliberate to target the glutes effectively.

name: HM Left Side Iron Cross
description: Lie on your back with arms extended out to the sides in a T position. Lift your left leg straight up, then slowly lower it across your body toward the right side while keeping your shoulders on the floor, feeling a stretch and engagement through the glutes and hips. Control the movement back to the starting position and repeat for the desired reps before switching sides.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms in a T position. Lift your left leg straight up, then slowly lower it across your body toward the right side. Keep your shoulders on the floor and feel the stretch and engagement in your glutes and hips. Control the movement back to the starting position.

name: HM Left Side-Kick
description: From a standing position, shift your weight onto your right leg, brace your core, and lift your left knee up to hip height. Extend your left leg out to the side in a controlled kicking motion, focusing on squeezing the glutes and keeping your torso upright. Retract the leg back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions before switching sides.
instructions: Stand with feet together, shifting weight onto your right leg. Lift your left knee to hip height, bracing your core and keeping your torso upright. Extend your left leg out to the side in a controlled kicking motion, squeezing your glutes. Return your leg to the starting position and repeat.

name: HM Leg Lifts
description: Lie on your back with legs straight and core braced, then lift both legs upward until your hips and glutes engage, keeping your lower back pressed toward the floor. Control the movement as you lower your legs back down without letting them rest on the ground, maintaining tension in the glutes and core throughout.
instructions: Lie on your back with legs straight and core braced. Lift both legs upward until your hips and glutes engage, keeping your lower back pressed toward the floor. Control the movement as you lower your legs back down, maintaining tension in the glutes and core. Keep your legs slightly above the ground to avoid rest and maintain continuous tension.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Walking Lunge
description: The walking lunge is a lower-body strength exercise performed by stepping forward into alternating lunges while moving across the floor. It primarily targets the glutes, quadriceps, and hamstrings, while also challenging balance and core stability. It can be done for repetitions, time, or distance in the lower-body portion of a workout.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Take a large step forward with one foot, lowering your body until your back knee almost touches the ground. Push back up to the starting position and repeat with the other leg, alternating steps as you walk forward. Keep your front knee behind your toes and your back straight throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: High Plyometric Box Jump
description: Explosive jump from the floor onto a high box or platform, focusing on maximal power through the hips and legs, then stepping or jumping back down under control.
instructions: Stand in front of the box with feet shoulder-width apart. Explosively jump up onto the box, extending hips and legs to generate power. Land softly on the box with both feet, then step or jump back down to the starting position under control. Keep core engaged and maintain balance throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Unilateral Glute Bridge
description: Lie on your back with one knee bent and the other leg extended. Press through the heel of the bent leg to lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from shoulder to knee, keeping the extended leg in line with your torso. Squeeze your glute at the top, pause briefly, then lower under control and repeat before switching sides.
