Lower the dumbbell behind your head, stretching your triceps, then press it back up to the starting position, extending your arms fully. Focus on slow and controlled movements to target your triceps effectively.

name: Dumbbell Seal Row
description: The dumbbell seal row is a chest-supported rowing exercise performed on an elevated or inclined bench to target the middle back, especially the lats and rhomboids, while minimizing lower-back involvement. Lying face down on the bench, you row the dumbbells from a dead hang up toward your torso, focusing on squeezing the shoulder blades together. It is commonly used as an assistance movement for back strength and hypertrophy, typically in moderate to high rep ranges.
instructions: Lie face down on an elevated bench with your chest supported. Hold dumbbells with your arms extended and palms facing each other. Lift the dumbbells up towards your torso, squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Bent-over Dumbbell Rear Delt Fly
description: The bent-over dumbbell rear delt fly is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the rear deltoids by lifting dumbbells out to the sides while hinged at the hips. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions to improve shoulder balance and posterior shoulder development.
instructions: Hold dumbbells with palms facing each other. Bend at the hips and knees slightly, keeping back straight. Lift dumbbells out to the sides until they are at shoulder level, squeezing rear deltoids. Lower dumbbells back to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Band Resisted Dumbbell Row
description: A rowing exercise where a resistance band is anchored and looped around the dumbbell to increase tension throughout the pulling motion, targeting the lats and upper back.
instructions: Anchor the resistance band at shoulder height. Hold a dumbbell with the band looped around it, standing with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend knees slightly and lean forward, pulling the dumbbell towards your side until your elbow is at a 90-degree angle. Slowly return to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Holman Right Arm Weighted Half Jack
description: A dynamic unilateral dumbbell exercise where you perform a half jumping jack motion while pressing a dumbbell overhead with the right arm, targeting the lats and shoulders while engaging the core for stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in your right hand. Perform a half jumping jack motion by lifting your left foot off the ground and raising your left arm. As you do this, press the dumbbell overhead with your right arm, extending your arm fully. Lower your arm and return to the starting position, repeating the motion in a controlled and dynamic manner.

name: Holman Left Arm Weighted Half Jack
description: An intermediate unilateral strength exercise where you hold a dumbbell in the left hand and perform a half jumping jack motion, engaging the lats and upper body while maintaining core stability.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in your left hand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Perform a half jumping jack motion by raising your left arm and lifting your right leg off the ground. Keep your core engaged and maintain balance throughout the movement. Lower your arm and leg back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging the dumbbell.

name: Holman Renegade Row
description: A core-intensive dumbbell row variation performed from a high plank position, alternating single-arm rows while maintaining a stable, braced torso to target the lats and upper back.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Hold a dumbbell in one hand with arm extended, keeping torso stable and core engaged. Lift the dumbbell to side until elbow is at a 90-degree angle, then lower it back down and repeat with the other arm. Keep alternating arms and maintain a braced torso throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Renegade Row with Opposite Foot Step-Out
description: Start in a high plank position with a dumbbell in each hand, wrists under shoulders and feet hip-width apart. Step one foot out to the side to widen your base, then row the opposite-side dumbbell by pulling your elbow toward your hip while keeping your hips square to the floor. Lower the dumbbell under control, step the foot back to center, and repeat on the other side, alternating rows and opposite foot step-outs while maintaining a strong, braced core.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with a dumbbell in each hand, feet hip-width apart. Step one foot out to the side and row the opposite-side dumbbell by pulling your elbow toward your hip. Lower the dumbbell under control and step the foot back to center. Repeat on the other side, alternating rows and foot step-outs while keeping your core strong and hips square to the floor.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Renegade Row to Weighted Burpee
description: Start in a high plank position with a dumbbell in each hand, wrists under shoulders and feet set wider than hip-width for stability. Brace your core and perform a renegade row by pulling one dumbbell toward your ribcage while keeping hips square, then lower it back to the floor and repeat on the other side. After completing the rows, jump or step your feet toward your hands, keeping hold of the dumbbells, then explosively extend your hips and knees to stand and perform a small jump while pressing or keeping the dumbbells at your sides. Land softly, hinge at the hips, place the dumbbells back on the floor, and jump or step your feet back to the plank to begin the next repetition.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with a dumbbell in each hand, feet wider than hip-width. Perform a renegade row by pulling one dumbbell to your ribcage, then lower it and repeat on the other side. Jump your feet toward your hands, then explosively stand up with the dumbbells at your sides and perform a small jump. Land softly and return to the plank position to repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Weighted Double-Arm Cross Punch
description: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest height with elbows bent. Brace your core and rotate your torso as you punch one arm across your body on a slight diagonal, keeping the other hand near your chest. Retract the punch under control, then alternate sides in a rhythmic, controlled manner. Maintain a tall posture, avoid overextending the elbows, and keep the movement driven by the core and upper back rather than swinging the weights.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest height with elbows bent. Rotate your torso and punch one arm across your body on a slight diagonal, keeping the other hand near your chest. Retract the punch under control, then alternate sides in a rhythmic manner. Maintain a tall posture and engage your core to drive the movement.

name: Holman Standing Flat Back Row
description: Stand hinged forward at the hips with a flat back, holding dumbbells in each hand with arms extended toward the floor. Keeping your torso still and core braced, pull the dumbbells up toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and close to your sides, squeezing your lats at the top. Lower the weights under control to full extension without rounding your back, then repeat for the desired reps.
instructions: Stand hinged forward at the hips with a flat back, holding dumbbells in each hand. Keep your torso still and core braced, then pull the dumbbells up toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back. Squeeze your lats at the top of the movement. Lower the weights under control to full extension without rounding your back.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Total Fitness Dumbbell Isometric Row Hold
description: A bent-over dumbbell row variation where the weight is held isometrically at the top of the movement to build upper and mid-back strength and endurance.
instructions: Hold dumbbells with palms facing towards body. Bend knees slightly and lean forward at hips, keeping back straight. Lift dumbbells to sides until elbows are at a 90-degree angle, then hold the position for the desired time. Keep core engaged and maintain control throughout the hold.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Renegade Row
description: The dumbbell renegade row combines a dumbbell row with a high-plank position, training the lats and upper back while heavily challenging the core for stability. It is often used in time-efficient strength and muscle-building workouts and circuit-style training.
instructions: Start in a high-plank position with hands gripping dumbbells. Engage your core and maintain a straight line from head to heels. Lift one dumbbell off the ground, rowing it to your side while keeping your elbow close to your body. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position and repeat with the other arm.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: MetaBurn Bent-Over Row to Rear-Delt Raise
description: A compound dumbbell exercise performed in a bent-over position that combines a row to target the lats and upper back with a rear-delt raise to emphasize the rear shoulders and upper back stabilizers.
instructions: Hold dumbbells with palms facing body. Bend knees slightly and lean forward at hips, keeping back straight. Lift dumbbels to sides until elbows are at 90 degrees, squeezing lats and upper back, then raise dumbbels out to sides until at shoulder height, targeting rear delts. Lower dumbbels to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Weighted Pull-Up
description: The weighted pull-up is an advanced variation of the standard pull-up where additional resistance is added to the body, typically via a belt, vest, or holding a dumbbell or kettlebell. It primarily targets the upper back and lats while also heavily engaging the biceps and core to control the movement through a full range of motion.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Attach weights to your body using a belt, vest, or hold a dumbbell between your feet. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar, squeezing your lats and upper back. Lower yourself back down to a dead hang, controlling the movement throughout the entire range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Gironda Sternum Chins
description: A pull-up variation where you lean back and pull your lower chest or sternum toward the bar, emphasizing the lats and upper back while also engaging the biceps and core.
instructions: Hang from the bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Lean back slightly, keeping core engaged and legs straight. Pull yourself up, focusing on bringing your sternum towards the bar, squeezing lats and upper back. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Floor Rope Climb
description: The floor rope climb is a prone pulling exercise where you lie on the floor and use your arms to pull your body along a rope, keeping your feet in contact with the ground. It builds foundational strength for full rope climbs while targeting the back, lats, and biceps, and can be performed for time or repetitions.
instructions: Lie on the floor with the rope in front of you. Grasp the rope with both hands, keeping your feet in contact with the ground. Pull the rope towards your chest, using your arms and back to generate force. Keep your body straight and control your movements as you pull yourself along the rope.

name: Overhead Lat Stretch
description: A beginner stretch where you raise one or both arms overhead and gently lean to the side to lengthen the lat muscles along the side of the torso.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Raise one arm overhead with palm facing forward. Gently lean to the side, stretching the lat muscle, and hold for a few seconds. Repeat on the other side for balanced flexibility.

name: Around-the-world Pull-up
description: The around-the-world pull-up is an advanced pull-up variation that shifts more load to each individual arm and side of the back by moving your body in an arc at the top of the rep. This increases time under tension for the lats and upper back and can be used as a progression toward a one-arm pull-up or to add challenge and variety to pull-up training.
instructions: Start in a dead hang from a pull-up bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Initiate a pull-up, pulling yourself up until your chest nearly touches the bar. At the top of the rep, rotate your body in an arc, shifting your weight from one arm to the other, keeping your core engaged and lats contracted. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the entire range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: L-Sit Chin-Up
description: The L-sit chin-up is an advanced chin-up variation that combines a strict upper-body pull with a static L-sit hold. With the legs extended straight out in front of the body at hip height, it increases demand on the lats and biceps while heavily engaging the abs and hip flexors to maintain position. It is typically performed for low repetitions with a focus on control, full range of motion, and core tension throughout.
instructions: Hang from a chin-up bar with an overhand grip. Lift your legs straight out in front of you at hip height, engaging your core and hip flexors. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar, squeezing your lats and biceps. Lower yourself back down slowly, maintaining control and core tension throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Incline Straight-Arm Pull-Over
description: The incline straight-arm pull-over is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the chest and lats. Lying on an incline bench, you lower a weight in a controlled arc behind your head with straight or slightly bent arms, then pull it back over your chest, emphasizing a deep stretch through the chest and lats. It is typically performed for moderate to high reps as part of chest or back-focused training.
instructions: Lie on an incline bench with your feet planted firmly on the floor. Grasp the weight with straight or slightly bent arms, then lower it in a controlled arc behind your head. Pull the weight back over your chest, focusing on a deep stretch through your chest and lats. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Machine High Row
description: A machine-based rowing exercise that targets the upper and mid-back by pulling handles from a high position toward the torso. It emphasizes the lats and mid-back while providing guided resistance, making it easier to control form and focus on muscle contraction.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your feet flat on the floor. Grasp the handles with your arms extended and palms facing towards your body. Pull the handles towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together and focusing on your lats and mid-back. Return the handles to the starting position in a controlled motion.

name: AM T-Bar Row
description: A bent-over rowing exercise performed with a T-bar setup to target the lats and upper back, emphasizing a strong pull toward the torso while maintaining a stable hip hinge.
instructions: Stand over the T-bar with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the hips and knees, grasping the bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Pull the bar up toward your torso, squeezing your lats and upper back. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Assisted Single-Arm Pull-Up
description: An advanced vertical pulling exercise where you perform a pull-up primarily with one arm while using minimal assistance from the opposite hand, band, or support to help control the movement and build toward a full single-arm pull-up.
instructions: Start by hanging from a pull-up bar with one hand, using a resistance band or minimal assistance from the opposite hand for support. Engage your core and pull yourself up with the working arm, keeping your elbow close to your body. Control the movement as you lower yourself back down to the starting position. Use minimal assistance to help guide the movement and maintain proper form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Assisted Single-Arm Chin-Up
description: An advanced chin-up variation where you primarily pull with one arm while using minimal assistance from the opposite hand, band, or support to help complete the movement. This targets the lats and upper back while also challenging grip and core stability.
instructions: Grasp the chin-up bar with one hand and use minimal assistance from the opposite hand or a resistance band. Pull yourself up by bending your elbow and squeezing your lats, keeping your core engaged. Focus on using your target arm to do most of the work, with minimal help from the assisting arm. Lower yourself back down slowly to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Renegade Punch-Under
description: A core-focused renegade-style movement performed from a plank position, driving a punching motion under the torso to challenge the lats, shoulders, and trunk stability.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Grip a dumbbell in each hand, keeping arms straight. Drive one arm under your torso, punching upwards, while keeping the other arm stable. Alternate arms, maintaining a stable core and straight body line throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Renegade Row to Split Jump
description: Start in a high plank position with hands on the floor, feet hip-width apart, and core braced. Perform a single-arm row by pulling one hand toward your ribcage while stabilizing your hips, then return the hand to the floor. Immediately step or jump your feet forward, rise up, and perform an explosive split jump, switching legs in the air and landing softly. Return to the plank position and repeat, alternating the rowing arm and lead leg on each rep.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands on the floor and feet hip-width apart. Perform a single-arm row by pulling one hand toward your ribcage, then return it to the floor. Immediately step or jump your feet forward and rise up to perform an explosive split jump, switching legs in the air. Land softly and return to the plank position, alternating the rowing arm and lead leg on each rep.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Dirty Dog to Renegade Row
description: From a high plank position with hands on the floor and feet hip-width apart, lift one leg out to the side in a "dirty dog" motion while keeping hips level, then return it and perform a single-arm row by pulling the opposite hand toward your ribcage. Alternate sides each rep to challenge core stability, shoulder strength, and back engagement.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands on the floor and feet hip-width apart. Lift one leg out to the side, keeping hips level, then return it to the starting position. Immediately perform a single-arm row with the opposite hand, pulling toward your ribcage. Alternate legs and arms with each repetition.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Renegade Row to Side Punch to Forward Punch
description: A compound core and upper-body exercise performed from a plank position. You row one arm back, then extend it into a side punch and transition into a forward punch, challenging core stability, shoulder strength, and anti-rotation control.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands under shoulders and toes on ground. Row one arm back, keeping elbow close to body, then extend it into a side punch. Transition the arm into a forward punch, maintaining core stability and control. Repeat on the other side, alternating arms to challenge core and upper-body strength.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Close-Grip Pull-Down
description: The close-grip pull-down is a lat-focused cable exercise performed with an overhand grip and hands slightly closer than shoulder-width. The closer grip increases range of motion and time under tension compared to wider grips, making it effective for building back strength and muscle when performed for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Sit at the pull-down machine with knees securely under the knee pad. Grasp the bar with an overhand grip, hands slightly closer than shoulder-width apart. Pull the bar down towards your chest, squeezing your lats at the bottom of the movement. Release the bar back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Shotgun Row
description: The shotgun row is a single-arm cable row performed in a staggered stance, emphasizing the middle back and lats while engaging the core for stability. It is typically done for moderate to high reps (8-15) later in a back or upper-body workout.
instructions: Stand in a staggered stance with one foot forward. Hold a cable handle with the arm opposite your forward leg, keeping your elbow close to your body. Pull the handle to your side, squeezing your middle back and lats, then control the return to the starting position. Keep your core engaged for stability throughout the exercise.

name: Close-Grip Front Lat Pulldown
description: A cable-based pulldown variation using a close grip to emphasize the lats and mid-back while also engaging the biceps and forearms. Performed seated, you pull the bar or handle down toward the upper chest with controlled form to build back strength and width.
instructions: Sit at the lat pulldown machine with a close grip, hands about 6-8 inches apart. Pull the bar down toward your upper chest, squeezing your lats and keeping your elbows close to your body. Keep your core engaged and your back straight, avoiding any swinging or jerking motions. Lower the bar back to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Straight-Arm Rope Pull-Down
description: The straight-arm rope pull-down is a cable exercise targeting the lats and upper back. Performed standing with a slight hip hinge, you pull a rope attachment from shoulder height down toward the thighs while keeping the elbows nearly straight. This movement emphasizes a long stretch and strong contraction of the lats, with additional engagement of the upper back and core, and is typically done for moderate to higher reps.
instructions: Stand with a slight hip hinge, grasping a rope attachment at shoulder height. Keep your elbows nearly straight, then pull the rope down toward your thighs. Focus on squeezing your lats and upper back as you pull. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking or swinging.

name: Lat Pull-Down
description: The lat pull-down is a cable-based back exercise that targets the lats to build size and strength. Performed seated while pulling a bar down toward the upper chest, it allows controlled loading with moderate to high reps, typically 8–12 per set. Wrist straps can be used if grip strength is a limiting factor.
instructions: Sit at the lat pull-down machine with knees securely under the knee pad. Grasp the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip, or use wrist straps if needed. Pull the bar down toward your upper chest, squeezing your lats at the bottom of the movement. Release the bar back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Straight-Arm Pulldown
description: The straight-arm pulldown is a cable exercise performed in an upright or slightly bent-over stance to target the lats and upper back while engaging the core and triceps isometrically. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions, focusing on a controlled stretch and contraction of the lats throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip and straight arms. Pull the bar down towards your thighs, keeping your arms straight and squeezing your lats. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking or swinging.

name: Single-arm Kneeling Lat Pull-down
description: A unilateral lat pull-down performed from a kneeling position using a cable machine. This variation targets the lats while increasing core engagement and lower-body stability compared to the traditional seated version.
instructions: Kneel in front of a cable machine with one arm holding the handle. Pull the handle down towards your side, keeping your elbow close to your body, and squeeze your lats. Keep your core engaged and lower body stable throughout the movement. Return the handle to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking or swinging.

name: Elevated Cable Rows
description: Elevated cable rows are a seated pulling exercise using a cable stack to target the upper back, emphasizing the lats, traps, and rear delts while also engaging the biceps. Performed with a controlled squeeze of the shoulder blades, they are commonly used for muscle-building or as an accessory movement in strength programs.
instructions: Sit at the cable machine with feet flat on the floor and knees slightly bent. Grasp the cable handle with palms facing towards your body. Pull the handle towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together and keeping your elbows close to your body. Return the handle to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Full Range-Of-Motion Lat Pulldown
description: A cable-based pulling exercise where you sit and pull the bar down through a full range of motion to your upper chest, emphasizing controlled movement to fully lengthen and contract the lats.
instructions: Sit at the lat pulldown machine with knees securely under the knee pad. Grasp the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip. Pull the bar down to your upper chest, squeezing your lats and keeping your core engaged. Slowly return the bar to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the entire range of motion.

name: Kneeling Lat Pull-Down
description: The kneeling lat pull-down is a cable-based pulling exercise performed from a kneeling position to target the lats while also engaging the core and lower body for stability. Compared to the traditional seated version, the kneeling setup increases the demand on trunk control and posture while still emphasizing vertical pulling strength.
instructions: Kneel on the floor with knees wide apart and toes pointed. Hold the cable bar with hands shoulder-width apart and pull it down towards your chest, squeezing your lats. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the movement. Return to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Behind-the-neck pull-down
description: The behind-the-neck pull-down is a cable-based back exercise that targets the upper and middle back, emphasizing the lats and traps. It is typically performed by pulling a bar from overhead to behind the neck while seated, requiring significant shoulder mobility and control. Due to the shoulder position, it is generally recommended only for lifters with healthy shoulders and adequate mobility.
instructions: Sit at the cable machine with knees securely under the knee pad. Grasp the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip and pull it down behind your neck, squeezing your lats and traps. Keep your chest up and control the movement as you lower the bar. Return the bar to the starting position slowly and deliberately.

name: Straight-Arm Cable Pull-Over
description: The straight-arm cable pull-over is an isolation exercise targeting the lats and chest using a cable stack, typically performed lying on a flat bench. With elbows slightly bent and arms kept straight throughout the movement, you pull the cable from above your head toward your hips in a controlled arc, emphasizing a strong stretch and contraction in the lats. It is commonly used for moderate to high reps in muscle-building workouts and can be a joint-friendly alternative to dumbbell pull-overs or machine pull-overs.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with a cable stack above you. Grasp the cable handle with arms straight and elbows slightly bent. Pull the cable down toward your hips in a controlled arc, stretching and contracting your lats. Return the cable to the starting position slowly, maintaining control throughout the movement.

name: High-Cable Standing Row
description: The high-cable standing row is a standing pulling exercise that targets the lats and upper back using a high cable attachment. The elevated line of pull increases engagement of the lats, rhomboid region, and rear-shoulder area while challenging core stability.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the handle with palms facing down and arms extended. Pull the handle down towards your chest, squeezing your lats and upper back, while keeping your core engaged. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging.

name: Scapular Rows
description: Stand facing a cable machine with the handle set at mid-chest height. With arms straight and shoulder-width grip, retract your shoulder blades back and down without bending your elbows, then slowly allow them to protract forward. Focus on controlled scapular movement rather than pulling with the arms.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the handle at mid-chest height. Hold the handle with a shoulder-width grip and straight arms. Retract your shoulder blades back and down without bending your elbows. Slowly return to the starting position, focusing on controlled scapular movement.

name: Standing V-Grip Cable Rows
description: Stand facing a low cable machine with a V-grip handle attached. Hinge slightly at the hips with a soft bend in the knees, brace your core, and pull the handle toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and in toward your sides. Squeeze your back at the end of the movement, then slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position while maintaining control and a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand facing a low cable machine with a V-grip handle. Hinge at the hips and bend your knees slightly, bracing your core. Pull the handle to your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and in, squeezing your back at the end. Return to the starting position slowly, maintaining control and a neutral spine.

name: AM Close-Grip Pull-Down
description: The close-grip pull-down is a cable-based lat-focused exercise performed on a lat pulldown machine. Using a narrow grip, you pull the bar or handle down toward your upper chest, emphasizing the lats and mid-back while also engaging the biceps and forearms. It allows for adjustable resistance via the weight stack and is effective for building back strength and size.
instructions: Sit at the lat pulldown machine with knees securely under the knee pad. Grasp the bar with a close grip, hands about 6-8 inches apart. Pull the bar down toward your upper chest, squeezing your lats and keeping your core tight. Return the bar to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: AM Wide-Grip Pull-Down
description: A cable-based lat pull-down variation using a wide overhand grip to emphasize the lats and upper back. Performed seated while pulling the bar from overhead down toward the upper chest with controlled motion and full stretch at the top.
instructions: Sit at the cable machine with knees securely under the knee pad. Grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart or slightly wider. Pull the bar down toward your upper chest, squeezing your lats and upper back muscles. Return the bar to the starting position with controlled motion, stretching your lats at the top.

name: Reverse-grip lat pull-down
description: The reverse-grip lat pull-down is a lat-focused cable exercise using an underhand, relatively close grip to emphasize the lower portion of the lats and increase range of motion compared to wider overhand variations. It can be programmed with heavier loads for strength or higher reps for back hypertrophy.
instructions: Sit at the lat pull-down machine with your knees securely under the knee pad. Grasp the bar with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Pull the bar down towards your chest, squeezing your lats and keeping your core engaged. Release the bar back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Machine-Assisted Pull-Up
description: The machine-assisted pull-up uses a counterweight to reduce the effective load of a traditional pull-up, allowing you to train the vertical pulling pattern with controlled resistance. By adjusting the weight stack, you can increase or decrease assistance to match your strength level and focus on higher-rep hypertrophy work for the back. Perform with a controlled pull, squeezing the upper back at the top, then lowering under control through a full range of motion.
instructions: Adjust the weight stack to your desired assistance level. Sit at the machine with your knees on the pad and grasp the bar with an overhand grip. Pull yourself up, squeezing your upper back at the top, then lower down slowly and control the movement through a full range of motion.

name: Single-arm pull-down
description: The single-arm pull-down is a cable-based lat exercise performed one arm at a time, typically using a D-handle on a cable stack. It emphasizes unilateral lat engagement, helping improve mind-muscle connection, correct imbalances, and build upper-back strength and width.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the D-handle at chest height. Grasp the handle with one hand and pull it down towards your side, squeezing your lat muscles. Keep your core engaged and your arm close to your body as you pull. Release the handle back to the starting position and repeat with the other arm.

name: Single-Arm Machine High Row
description: The single-arm machine high row is a machine-based pulling exercise that targets the upper and mid-back, especially the lats and mid/lower traps. Using a selectorized or plate-loaded row machine, you sit with your chest supported, grasp one handle at shoulder or slightly above-shoulder height, and pull the handle down and back toward your torso, squeezing the shoulder blade toward the spine. This variation allows you to focus on one side at a time for better mind-muscle connection and balanced development, typically performed for moderate to high reps in back or upper-body workouts.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your chest supported and feet flat on the floor. Grasp one handle at shoulder height with your hand, keeping your arm straight. Pull the handle down and back towards your torso, squeezing your shoulder blade towards your spine. Release the handle back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Wide-Grip Machine Row
description: A seated rowing exercise using a cable or plate-loaded machine with a wide overhand grip to target the upper back and lats while maintaining a stable torso.
instructions: Sit at the machine with feet flat on the floor. Grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands wider than shoulder-width apart. Pull the bar towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together and keeping your torso stable. Return the bar to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking or swinging.

name: Robertson Half-Kneeling Landmine Press
description: A half-kneeling single-arm press using a landmine setup to build shoulder and upper-back strength while challenging core stability and anti-rotation control.
instructions: Start in a half-kneeling position with one knee on the ground. Hold the landmine handle with one hand, keeping your arm at shoulder height. Press the handle away from your body, extending your arm fully, then control the return to the starting position. Switch arms to work both sides evenly.

name: Pull-Ups
description: The pull-up is a multi-joint bodyweight exercise that builds strength and muscle in the upper back, lats, biceps, and core, and is often used as a test of relative bodyweight strength.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Engage your core and pull yourself up, squeezing your lats and pulling your chest to the bar. Focus on slow and controlled movements, avoiding swinging or jerking. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, stretching your arms fully.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Pull-up
description: A bodyweight vertical pulling exercise where you hang from a bar with an overhand grip and pull your chest toward the bar, targeting the upper back, lats, biceps, and core while developing relative strength.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip. Engage your core and pull your chest towards the bar, squeezing your lats and shoulders. Avoid swinging or jerking movements, using a slow and controlled motion to pull yourself up. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, stretching your arms fully.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Muscle Up
description: A compound pulling exercise where you explosively pull your body up to a bar and transition into a dip position above the bar, combining a pull-up and a straight-bar dip.
instructions: Hang from the bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Explosively pull yourself up, using your legs and hips to generate power, until your chest is above the bar. Transition into a dip position by rotating your shoulders and pushing yourself up, straightening your arms. Control your body as you lower back down to the starting hang position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Rope Climb
description: The rope climb is a bodyweight pulling exercise emphasizing upper-body strength and grip. Using a vertical rope, you pull your body upward primarily with the lats, biceps, and forearms while engaging the shoulders and core to stabilize and control the ascent and descent.
instructions: Find a sturdy rope and grasp it with both hands, feet flat on the ground. Pull yourself up with your arms, engaging your lats and core to lift your body. Use your legs to assist by wrapping them around the rope for support. Lower yourself down slowly, controlling the descent with your arms and core.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Wide-Grip Rear Pull-Up
description: A bodyweight pulling exercise performed with a wide overhand grip, pulling the chest up and behind the bar to emphasize the upper back and lats.
instructions: Hang from the bar with a wide overhand grip. Engage your core and pull yourself up, keeping your elbows close to your body. Pull your chest up and behind the bar, squeezing your upper back and lats. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Side To Side Chins
description: An intermediate chin-up variation where you pull your body up toward one hand, then shift your chin across the bar to the opposite hand, emphasizing unilateral lat and upper-back strength.
instructions: Hang from the bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Pull yourself up toward one hand, lifting your chin above the bar. Shift your chin across the bar to the opposite hand, keeping your core engaged and body straight. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, repeating on the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Neutral-grip Pull-up
description: The neutral-grip pull-up is a multijoint bodyweight pulling exercise performed with the palms facing each other. It targets the upper back and lats while also engaging the biceps and core, and is often more shoulder-friendly than straight-bar pull-ups.
instructions: Hang from a neutral-grip pull-up bar with palms facing each other. Engage your core and pull yourself up, squeezing your lats and upper back. Focus on slow and controlled movement, avoiding swinging or jerking. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, stretching your lats and keeping your core engaged.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kipping Muscle Up
description: A dynamic, explosive pulling and pushing movement where you swing (kip) to generate momentum, pull your body above the bar, and transition into a dip to lock out with straight arms on top of the bar.
instructions: Hang from the bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Swing your body to generate momentum, then pull yourself up and over the bar. Transition into a dip position, keeping your elbows close to your body. Lock out with straight arms on top of the bar, squeezing your chest and shoulders.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One Arm Against Wall Stretch
description: Stand side-on to a wall with your arm closest to the wall extended straight and palm placed flat against it at about shoulder height. Gently rotate your torso away from the wall while keeping the arm straight to stretch the lats and shoulder. Hold the stretch without bouncing, then switch sides.
instructions: Stand side-on to a wall with your arm closest to the wall extended straight and palm flat against it. Gently rotate your torso away from the wall, keeping your arm straight. Hold the stretch without bouncing for a few seconds. Switch sides to stretch the other arm.

name: One Handed Hang
description: The one handed hang is an advanced static hold performed by hanging from a pull-up bar with a single hand. It challenges shoulder stability, grip strength, and the lats while also engaging the core and surrounding upper-back muscles to maintain body control. This exercise is best suited for individuals with strong shoulders and grip, and should be approached progressively to reduce strain on the shoulder and elbow joints.
instructions: Find a sturdy pull-up bar and hang from it with one hand, gripping firmly. Engage your core and maintain a straight body line from head to heels. Slowly lift your other hand off the bar, holding the position for the desired time while keeping your shoulder down and away from your ear. Control your breathing and maintain stability throughout the hold.

name: Standing Side Bend Stretch
description: The standing side bend stretch is a dynamic stretch performed from a tall standing position, reaching one arm overhead and gently bending the torso to the opposite side to lengthen the obliques and lats. It is commonly used in warm-ups to prepare the upper body and core for lifting or athletics and can be performed slowly and controlled to improve lateral flexibility and mobility.
instructions: Stand tall with feet shoulder-width apart. Reach one arm overhead and gently bend your torso to the opposite side. Keep your arms and legs straight, stretching your obliques and lats. Return to the starting position and repeat on the other side.

name: Burpee to Pull-Up
description: A full-body plyometric movement combining a traditional burpee with a jumping pull-up, performed by dropping into a push-up, returning to standing, jumping to a pull-up bar, and completing a pull-up each rep. Commonly used in circuit and metabolic conditioning workouts.
instructions: Start in a standing position. Drop down into a push-up, kicking feet back into a plank position, then quickly return to standing. From standing, jump up to a pull-up bar and grasp it with hands, then complete a pull-up by pulling yourself up until your chin clears the bar. Immediately drop down to the ground and repeat the sequence.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Negative Pull-Up
description: The negative pull-up isolates the lowering (eccentric) phase of a pull-up. Starting at the top position with your chin over the bar, you slowly lower your body under control until your arms are fully extended. This builds strength in the back, lats, and arms, and is commonly used to progress toward full pull-ups or as a back-off exercise after regular pull-up sets.
instructions: Start at the top of a pull-up with your chin over the bar. Slowly lower your body down, keeping control throughout the movement. Take 2-3 seconds to fully extend your arms, stretching your lats and back. End with your arms fully extended and your body hanging straight down.

name: Chin-Up
description: The chin-up is a bodyweight pulling exercise performed on a bar with an underhand, narrower-than-shoulder-width grip. It primarily targets the upper back and biceps while also engaging the core for stability. This variation is often more biceps-focused and can feel more shoulder-friendly than an overhand pull-up.
instructions: Hang from a bar with an underhand grip, hands narrower than shoulder-width apart. Engage your core and pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar. Squeeze your upper back and biceps at the top of the movement. Lower yourself back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: HM Superman Lat Pull
description: Lie face down with arms extended overhead in a Superman position. Simultaneously lift your chest and legs slightly off the floor while pulling your elbows down toward your ribs in a lat pull motion, squeezing your lats and upper back. Slowly re-extend the arms overhead and lower the chest and legs with control, maintaining tension through the back throughout the movement.
instructions: Lie face down with arms extended overhead. Lift your chest and legs slightly off the floor, pulling your elbows down toward your ribs. Squeeze your lats and upper back, then slowly re-extend your arms and lower your chest and legs with control. Maintain tension through your back throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: AM Pull-Up
description: A bodyweight pulling exercise performed by hanging from a bar and pulling the chest toward the bar, targeting the upper back, lats, and biceps while engaging the core for stability. Commonly used as a test of relative strength in military and tactical fitness assessments.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Engage your core and pull yourself up by drawing your chest toward the bar. Focus on squeezing your lats and pulling with your back, not just your arms. Lower yourself back down to a dead hang, controlling the movement throughout the entire range.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Alternating Forward and Side Punch
description: Stand in an athletic stance with feet shoulder-width apart and hands in a guard position. Punch one arm straight forward, retract it, then punch the same or opposite arm out to the side at shoulder height, rotating slightly through the torso. Alternate forward and side punches in a controlled, rhythmic manner while keeping your core braced and shoulders down. Maintain light knee bend and avoid overextending the elbows.
instructions: Stand in an athletic stance with feet shoulder-width apart and hands in a guard position. Punch one arm straight forward, then retract and punch to the side at shoulder height, rotating your torso slightly. Alternate arms in a controlled, rhythmic manner, keeping your core braced and shoulders down. Maintain a light knee bend and avoid overextending your elbows.

name: Jump to Pull-Up
description: The jump to pull-up is a bodyweight pulling exercise where you use a small jump to help initiate each rep, allowing you to complete more total repetitions than strict pull-ups. Starting from the ground or a low box, you jump and grab the pull-up bar, using leg drive to assist the hardest part of the pull. This variation emphasizes the back and biceps while involving the legs and core for power and control, and is often used in circuits or conditioning workouts.
instructions: Stand under the pull-up bar with feet shoulder-width apart. Jump up and grab the bar with hands shoulder-width apart, using leg drive to assist. Pull yourself up, squeezing your back and biceps, then slowly lower yourself back down. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bent-over Scapular Slide
description: The bent-over scapular slide is an upper-body movement focused on improving scapular control and shoulder mobility. Performed in a hip-hinged position, you retract and protract the shoulder blades through a controlled range of motion, making it useful as a dynamic warm-up or light activation drill for the upper back and lats.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hinge at the hips, keeping back straight. Bend over at the waist, keeping arms straight and letting them hang. Slide shoulder blades together and then apart, moving in a slow and controlled motion. Keep core engaged and maintain a stable position throughout the exercise.

name: Iron Cross Stretch
description: The iron cross stretch is a bodyweight mobility drill performed lying on your back, swinging one leg across the body toward the opposite hand to improve hip and thoracic spine mobility.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended to the sides at shoulder height. Lift one leg and swing it across your body toward the opposite hand. Keep your lower back pressed into the ground and let your leg gently stretch your hip and thoracic spine. Hold for a brief moment before slowly returning to the starting position.

name: Total Fitness Standing Lat Stretch
description: A standing stretch targeting the lat muscles by lengthening the sides of the torso while maintaining an upright posture.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Reach arms up and interlock fingers, then lean to one side, keeping arms straight. Hold the stretch for a few seconds, feeling the length in your lat muscles. Return to center and repeat on the other side.

name: Catch and Overhead Throw
description: Stand holding a medicine ball at chest height. Partner or wall-throw the ball to you; catch it with soft knees, then immediately drive through your hips and shoulders to throw the ball overhead and slightly forward. Reset and repeat for explosive power and coordination.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest height. Catch the thrown ball with soft knees, absorbing the impact. Immediately drive through your hips and shoulders to throw the ball overhead and slightly forward. Reset to the starting position and repeat for a cardio workout.

name: Dynamic Back Stretch
description: A gentle, rhythmic stretching movement targeting the lats and upper back, performed by reaching the arms forward and side-to-side while hinging slightly at the hips to mobilize the back.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hinge slightly at the hips, keeping your back straight. Reach your arms forward and then out to the sides, moving in a slow and gentle rhythm. Keep your movements smooth and controlled, stretching your upper back and lats.

name: Chair Lower Back Stretch
description: Sit tall on a chair with your feet flat on the floor, then hinge forward from the hips and reach your hands toward the floor or your ankles, allowing your upper body to relax and gently stretch the lower back and lats. Hold the position while breathing slowly, then return to upright.
instructions: Sit tall on a chair with feet flat on the floor. Hinge forward from the hips and reach hands toward the floor or ankles. Allow your upper body to relax and gently stretch your lower back. Return to upright position slowly after holding the stretch for a few breaths.

name: Side-Lying Floor Stretch
description: Lie on your side with your bottom arm extended overhead and your top arm reaching forward to gently stretch the side of your torso and lats. Hold the position while breathing deeply, then switch sides.
instructions: Lie on your side with your bottom arm extended overhead. Place your top arm across your body, reaching forward to stretch your side. Hold the stretch, breathing deeply, then switch sides to repeat. Keep your movements slow and controlled to avoid injury.

name: Band Good Morning
description: A hip-hinge exercise using resistance bands to target the hamstrings and glutes while also engaging the lower back. Often used as a lighter alternative to barbell good mornings for higher-rep work and posterior chain strengthening.
instructions: Stand on the middle of a resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the ends of the band in each hand, with arms straight and palms facing backwards. Hinge at the hips, keeping back straight, and lower torso until you feel a stretch in the hamstrings and glutes. Return to standing, squeezing glutes and pushing hips back to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Band Deadlift
description: The band deadlift is a hip-hinge strength exercise that primarily targets the hamstrings while also engaging the glutes, lower back, and lats using resistance bands instead of free weights.
instructions: Stand on the middle of the resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the band handles with hands at shoulder width, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Hinge at the hips, lowering your torso and keeping the band taut, then lift the band up to hip level by squeezing your glutes and pushing your hips back. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Lower Back SMR
description: Lower back self-myofascial release (SMR) is a self-administered soft-tissue technique using a foam roller or similar tool to apply gentle, sustained pressure to the quadratus lumborum and spinal erector muscles of the lower back. The goal is to reduce tightness, improve tissue quality, and promote relaxation in this commonly tense area. It is often paired with SMR or stretching for the glutes and hips. Because direct pressure on the lower back can aggravate existing pain or injury for some individuals, it should be performed cautiously and discontinued if symptoms worsen.
instructions: Lie on your back with the foam roller under your lower back. Position the roller to target the quadratus lumborum and spinal erector muscles. Slowly roll the foam roller up and down, applying gentle pressure to release tension. Stop and hold pressure on areas of tension, breathing deeply to promote relaxation.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Deficit Deadlift
description: The barbell deficit deadlift is a compound pulling exercise performed while standing on an elevated surface to increase the range of motion. It emphasizes the posterior chain, particularly the glutes, hamstrings, and lower back, and is often used in powerlifting to build strength off the floor. Typically done with lighter loads than a conventional deadlift, it can be programmed for moderate to higher reps to develop both strength and muscle. If it causes back discomfort, reduce the height of the deficit and/or use a lifting belt.
instructions: Stand on an elevated surface with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a barbell with hands shoulder-width apart and bend down to grip it, keeping back straight and core engaged. Lift the barbell up to hip level, squeezing glutes and pushing hips back, then lower it back down to the starting position. Keep the movement controlled and slow to avoid straining your back.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Deadlift with Bands
description: A barbell deadlift performed with resistance bands attached to increase tension as the bar rises, targeting the posterior chain while reinforcing proper hip hinge mechanics.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart. Attach resistance bands to the barbell and anchors, ensuring even tension. Lift the barbell by hinging at the hips and pushing through the heels, keeping back straight and core engaged. Lower the barbell to the starting position, controlling the descent and maintaining tension in the bands.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Deadlift with Chains
description: A barbell deadlift variation where chains are attached to the bar, increasing resistance as you lift. This emphasizes powerful hip extension and lockout strength while targeting the posterior chain.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with chains attached, feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, keeping back straight and core engaged. Lift the bar by pushing hips back and down, then explosively extending hips to lockout position. Lower the bar to the starting position, controlling the chains' descent to avoid jerky movements.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Rack Pull with Bands
description: A partial deadlift variation performed from an elevated position in a rack with resistance bands attached to the barbell. This reduces the range of motion, emphasizes the upper portion of the pull, and increases resistance toward lockout to build lower back and overall posterior chain strength.
instructions: Stand in a rack with the barbell at mid-shin height and resistance bands attached. Grip the barbell with hands shoulder-width apart and feet shoulder-width apart. Lift the barbell up to lockout, squeezing your back and pushing your hips back, then lower it back down to the starting position. Keep your core tight and back straight throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Rack Pull
description: The rack pull is a partial deadlift performed with the barbell elevated on safety pins or blocks, reducing the range of motion. It emphasizes the glutes, hamstrings, and back while placing less stress on the hips and lower back than a full deadlift from the floor. It is commonly used to strengthen the deadlift lockout and as a higher-start deadlift alternative for those who experience discomfort pulling from the floor.
instructions: Stand in front of the barbell elevated on safety pins or blocks with feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the barbell with hands shoulder-width apart and bend knees slightly, keeping back straight. Lift the barbell up to hip level by squeezing glutes and pushing hips back, then lower it back down to the pins under control. Keep core engaged and maintain proper form throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated Good Mornings
description: Sit on a bench with your feet flat and a barbell resting across your upper back. Keep your chest up and core braced, then hinge forward at the hips while maintaining a neutral spine until you feel a strong stretch in your hamstrings and lower back. Pause briefly, then reverse the motion by driving through your hips and returning to an upright seated position.
instructions: Sit on a bench with feet flat and a barbell across your upper back. Keep your chest up and core braced, then hinge forward at the hips. Lower down until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings and lower back, pausing briefly. Return to an upright position by driving through your hips.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Stiff Leg Barbell Good Morning
description: With a barbell resting across your upper back, stand with feet about hip-width apart and knees only slightly bent. Hinge forward at the hips while keeping your back flat and core braced, lowering your torso until you feel a strong stretch in your hamstrings and glutes without rounding your lower back. Pause briefly, then drive your hips forward and return to the standing position under control.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and knees slightly bent, with a barbell across your upper back. Hinge forward at the hips, keeping your back flat and core braced, lowering your torso until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings and glutes. Pause briefly, then drive your hips forward to return to standing. Keep control throughout the movement to avoid injury.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Reverse Band Deadlift
description: A deadlift variation where resistance bands are attached above the barbell to reduce load at the bottom of the lift and increase it toward lockout, helping reinforce proper pulling mechanics and lockout strength.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the bar with hands shoulder-width apart and lift, keeping back straight and core engaged. As you lift, the resistance bands will increase tension, focus on squeezing glutes and pushing hips back to lockout. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30 Day Back Barbell Rack Pull
description: The barbell rack pull is a partial deadlift variation performed with the bar set on safety pins or blocks, targeting the lower back and upper posterior chain with a reduced range of motion. It allows heavier loading than a conventional deadlift while focusing on back strength and lockout power.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Deadlift
description: The kettlebell deadlift is a foundational hip-hinge strength exercise that targets the posterior chain using a kettlebell held between the feet. It is commonly used as a regression to the kettlebell swing and as an approachable way to learn proper deadlift mechanics and hip hinge technique.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and a kettlebell on the ground between your feet. Bend at the hips and grasp the kettlebell handle with both hands, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Lift the kettlebell up and stand up straight, squeezing your glutes and pushing your hips back. Lower the kettlebell back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: HM Kettlebell Swing
description: A dynamic hip-hinge exercise where you swing a kettlebell between your legs and up to chest height using powerful hip extension, targeting the posterior chain and core.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly pointed out. Hold a kettlebell with both hands, hanging down between your legs. Hinge your hips and swing the kettlebell back between your legs, then up to chest height using powerful hip extension. Keep your core tight and control the swing, avoiding excessive back arching.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Fix Dumbbell Deadlift
description: A hip-hinge deadlift variation using dumbbells to target the lower back and posterior chain while reinforcing proper form and control.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at sides. Hinge hips and bend knees slightly, keeping back straight and core engaged. Lift dumbbells off ground, squeezing glutes and pushing hips back, then lower dumbbells to starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Keep weight close to body and maintain proper form to target lower back and posterior chain.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Skier
description: A dynamic hip-hinge exercise mimicking a skiing motion while holding dumbbells at your sides. Swing the dumbbells back past your hips with a slight torso lean, then drive the hips forward to bring the weights up to about hip or mid-thigh height, keeping the arms mostly straight and outside the knees. Emphasizes powerful hip extension and posterior-chain endurance, often used in higher-rep sets for conditioning and fat loss.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at your sides. Hinge your hips and lean your torso slightly, swinging the dumbbells back past your hips. Drive your hips forward to bring the dumbbells up to hip or mid-thigh height, keeping your arms straight and outside your knees. Keep the movement dynamic and controlled, focusing on powerful hip extension.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Slow Deadlift
description: A controlled deadlift variation performed with dumbbells, emphasizing slow tempo to increase time under tension and improve lower back strength and hip hinge mechanics.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at sides. Hinge hips and slowly lower dumbbells to ground, keeping back straight and core engaged. Lift dumbbells back up to starting position, maintaining a slow and controlled tempo throughout the entire movement. Keep weight close to body and avoid jerking or bouncing the dumbbells.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Deadlift to Overhead Press
description: A compound dumbbell movement that combines a hip-hinge deadlift with an overhead press, targeting the posterior chain and shoulders while challenging core stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at sides. Hinge at hips and bend knees, lowering dumbbells to ground while keeping back straight.
