name: Partner Side Plank Band Row
description: An intermediate core and back exercise where two partners hold side planks facing each other while pulling on opposite ends of a resistance band in a rowing motion. This challenges the abs and obliques isometrically while the back and shoulders work dynamically to control the band tension.
instructions: Start in a side plank position with your partner, facing each other. Hold one end of the resistance band in each hand, with your arms extended and the band taut. Pull the band towards your chest in a rowing motion, keeping your core engaged and your body stable. Return to the starting position, controlling the band's tension throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Band-Assisted Chin-Up
description: The band-assisted chin-up is a chin-up variation performed with an underhand grip and a resistance band looped around the feet or knees to reduce effective bodyweight. It targets the upper back, lats, and biceps while engaging the core for stability, making it a useful progression toward unassisted chin-ups.
instructions: Loop a resistance band around the bar and your feet or knees to reduce your bodyweight. Grasp the bar with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar, squeezing your lats and core. Lower yourself back down to a dead hang, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Band-Assisted Pull-Up
description: Attach a resistance band to a pull-up bar and loop it under your knees or feet to reduce the load, then pull your chest toward the bar while keeping your core tight and shoulders engaged.
instructions: Attach a resistance band to the pull-up bar and loop it under your knees or feet. Grip the bar with hands shoulder-width apart and engage your core. Pull your chest toward the bar, keeping your shoulders down and away from your ears. Lower yourself back down slowly, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Reverse-Grip Band-Assisted Pull-Up
description: Attach a resistance band to a pull-up bar and place one or both feet or knees into the band for assistance. Using a reverse (underhand) grip about shoulder-width apart, pull your chest up toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your lats and mid-back at the top. Lower yourself under control to a full hang and repeat for reps, keeping your core tight and body stable throughout.
instructions: Attach a resistance band to a pull-up bar and place one or both feet into the band for assistance. Grip the bar with a reverse grip about shoulder-width apart. Pull your chest up toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your lats and mid-back at the top. Lower yourself under control to a full hang, keeping your core tight and body stable throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Band Bent-Over Row
description: The band bent-over row is a back-focused pulling exercise using resistance bands to strengthen the lats, mid and lower traps, and rear deltoids. Performed in a hip-hinged position, it targets the upper and middle back while also engaging the biceps and core for stability. It can be used for strength, muscle-building, or circuit-style conditioning.
instructions: Stand on the middle of a resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the hips and knees slightly, keeping your back straight, and hold the band's ends with your hands. Pull the band towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together and keeping your elbows close to your body. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the band.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Heavy Band Bent-Over Row
description: The heavy band bent-over row is a strength exercise targeting the upper and middle back using resistance bands. Performed in a hip-hinged position, you pull the band handles or ends toward your torso to build the lats, traps, and rear shoulders while also engaging the lower back and biceps for stability and assistance.
instructions: Stand on the middle of a heavy resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the hips and knees slightly, keeping your back straight. Hold the band handles with palms facing towards your body and pull them towards your torso, squeezing your shoulder blades together. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the band.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Reverse-Grip Bent-Over Row
description: The reverse-grip bent-over row is a compound barbell exercise that builds strength and size in the upper and lower back while also heavily engaging the lats and biceps. Using an underhand grip, you hinge at the hips, maintain a flat back, and pull the bar toward your lower ribs or waistline, emphasizing the lats and mid-back muscles.
instructions: Hold a barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Hinge at the hips and bend your knees slightly, maintaining a flat back. Pull the bar toward your lower ribs or waistline, squeezing your lats and mid-back muscles. Lower the bar back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One-Arm Long Bar Row
description: The one-arm long bar row is a landmine-style barbell row performed with one arm at a time to target the middle back. With one end of the barbell anchored near the ground, you hinge at the hips, grasp the free end of the bar, and row it toward your torso. This emphasizes the lats and mid-back while also engaging the biceps and grip.
instructions: Stand with one end of the barbell anchored near the ground. Hinge at the hips and grasp the free end of the bar with one hand. Row the bar toward your torso, keeping your elbow close to your body, then control the return to starting position. Switch arms to complete the exercise on both sides.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bent Over Barbell Row
description: The bent over barbell row is a compound strength exercise that targets the entire back, emphasizing the lats and lower back while also engaging the upper back and supporting muscles. Performed by hinging at the hips with a flat back and pulling the barbell toward the torso, it is commonly used to build back strength and size and as an accessory movement for deadlifts and other heavy pulls.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Hinge at the hips, keeping back flat and core engaged, and lean forward slightly. Lift the barbell to torso level, squeezing shoulder blades together and pulling with elbows close to body. Lower the barbell back to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Yates Row (Reverse Grip)
description: A bent-over barbell row variation performed with a reverse (underhand) grip and a more upright torso, targeting the middle back and lats while also engaging the biceps and forearms.
instructions: Hold a barbell with an underhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Bend your knees slightly and lean forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Lift the barbell to your chest, squeezing your middle back and lats, then lower it back to the starting position. Keep control throughout the movement, avoiding swinging or jerking the weight.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Pendlay Row
description: The Pendlay row is a barbell bent-over row variation where each repetition starts from a dead stop on the floor, eliminating momentum and emphasizing explosive pulling strength and upper-back development.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the hips and knees, gripping the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Lift the barbell to your chest, keeping your back straight and core engaged, then lower it back to the floor, coming to a complete stop before repeating. Keep the movement controlled and explosive, focusing on pulling with your upper back.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Straight Bar Bench Mid Rows
description: Lie face down on a flat bench with a barbell positioned underneath. Grasp the bar with an overhand grip, pull it up toward the underside of the bench by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together, then lower under control to full arm extension. This targets the mid-back while keeping the torso supported by the bench.
instructions: Lie face down on a flat bench with a barbell underneath. Grasp the bar with an overhand grip. Pull the bar up toward the underside of the bench by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower the bar back down to full arm extension under control.

name: Barbell Seal Row
description: The barbell seal row is a horizontal rowing exercise performed while lying face down on an elevated bench, pulling a barbell from a dead hang up toward the lower chest. It emphasizes the middle back and lats while minimizing lower-back involvement by providing full torso support. Typically used as an assistance lift for back strength and hypertrophy, it is commonly performed in moderate to high rep ranges (8–12+).
instructions: Lie face down on an elevated bench with your torso fully supported. Grasp a barbell with an overhand grip, hanging straight down from your hands. Pull the barbell up towards your lower chest, squeezing your middle back and lats. Lower the barbell back to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Yates Row
description: A bent-over barbell row variation performed with a more upright torso and an underhand grip to emphasize the middle back and lats while also engaging the biceps.
instructions: Hold a barbell with an underhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Bend your knees slightly and lean forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Lift the barbell to your chest, squeezing your middle back and lats, then lower it back down to the starting position. Keep control throughout the movement, avoiding swinging or jerking the weight.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Plate Row
description: A bent-over rowing exercise using a barbell plate or barbell to target the middle back by pulling the weight toward the torso while maintaining a flat back.
instructions: Hold a barbell plate or barbell with both hands, bend at the hips, and keep your back flat. Keep your knees slightly bent and your weight evenly distributed on both feet. Pull the weight towards your torso, squeezing your middle back muscles as you lift. Lower the weight back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Deadlift Bent Row Complex
description: A compound barbell complex that combines a deadlift with a bent-over row to train the entire posterior chain, emphasizing the upper and lower back and glutes using a moderate load.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Perform a deadlift by lifting the barbell up to hip level, squeezing glutes and pushing hips back. Bend knees slightly and lean forward at the hips to row the barbell towards your chest, keeping back straight and core engaged. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the entire range.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-Arm Landmine Row
description: A rowing exercise performed with one end of a barbell anchored to the floor, targeting the middle back by pulling the barbell handle or sleeve toward the torso with one arm at a time.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart facing the landmine anchor. Hold the barbell handle with one hand, keeping arm straight and core engaged. Pull the handle toward your torso, keeping elbow close to body, then slowly return to starting position. Alternate arms to complete the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
description: The barbell bent-over row is a compound pulling exercise that builds strength and size in the upper and lower back, with emphasis on the lats and spinal erectors. Performed by hinging at the hips with a flat back and rowing the barbell toward the lower ribs, it is commonly used as an accessory lift for deadlifts and overall upper-body strength development.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Hinge at the hips, keeping your back flat and core engaged, bending over to grab the barbell with your arms straight. Lift the barbell to your lower ribs, squeezing your lats and keeping your elbows close to your body. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging or jerking.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Deadlift Bent-Over Row Complex
description: A compound barbell complex that combines a conventional deadlift with a bent-over row to train the entire posterior chain, emphasizing the upper back, lower back, and glutes while also engaging the hamstrings and lats.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend down and grip the barbell with hands shoulder-width apart, keeping back straight and core engaged. Lift the barbell up to hip level, then bend knees slightly and lean forward at the hips to row the barbell to your chest, squeezing your lats and upper back. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, keeping control throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30 Back Barbell Deadlift
description: A barbell deadlift variation targeting the entire posterior chain, emphasizing the middle and lower back while also engaging glutes and hamstrings. Performed by hinging at the hips to lift the barbell from the floor to a standing position with a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart. Hinge at the hips and grab the barbell with an overhand grip, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Lift the barbell to a standing position, squeezing your glutes and pushing your hips back, while maintaining a neutral spine. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the entire range.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bent-Over Barbell Row
description: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a barbell with an overhand grip. Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to the floor, keeping your back flat and core braced. Pull the barbell toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back, squeezing your back muscles at the top. Lower the bar under control to full arm extension and repeat.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip. Hinge at the hips to 45 degrees, keeping your back flat and core braced. Pull the barbell to your lower ribs by driving your elbows back, squeezing your back muscles. Lower the bar under control to full arm extension.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: T-Bar Row
description: The T-bar row is a bent-over rowing exercise where you hinge at the hips and pull a barbell (set up as a T-bar) toward your torso to build middle and upper back strength.
instructions: Stand over the T-bar with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the hips and grasp the bar with hands shoulder-width apart, keeping back straight and core engaged. Pull the bar towards your torso, squeezing your shoulder blades together, then slowly lower it back to the starting position. Keep control throughout the movement to target your middle and upper back muscles.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Underhand Bent-Over Barbell Row
description: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a barbell with an underhand (supinated) grip. Hinge at the hips with a flat back until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to the floor. Keeping your core braced and back neutral, row the barbell toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and close to your sides. Squeeze your back at the top, then lower the bar under control to full arm extension without rounding your spine.

Row the barbell to your lower ribs by driving your elbows back, keeping your core braced and back neutral. Lower the bar under control to full arm extension, squeezing your back at the top and avoiding spine rounding.

name: Kettlebell Alternating Renegade Row
description: The kettlebell alternating renegade row is a compound strength exercise that combines a plank position with alternating kettlebell rows to train the back and core simultaneously. It challenges trunk stability, shoulder control, and anti-rotation strength while building upper-back and arm musculature.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands grasping kettlebells and feet shoulder-width apart. Engage your core and maintain a straight line from head to heels. Lift one kettlebell off the ground, rowing it to your side while keeping your elbow close to your body, then lower it back down. Alternate arms with each repetition, keeping your trunk stable and controlled throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Alternating Kettlebell Row
description: A bent-over rowing exercise performed with one kettlebell at a time, alternating arms to target the mid-back and lats while challenging core stability.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell in one hand with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend knees slightly and lean forward at hips, keeping back straight. Lift the kettlebell to side of chest, squeezing mid-back muscles, then lower and repeat with other arm. Alternate arms with each rep, maintaining core stability and control throughout the exercise.

name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Row
description: A bent-over rowing exercise using two kettlebells to strengthen the upper and mid-back while also engaging the biceps and core for stability.
instructions: Hold two kettlebells with palms facing your body. Bend your knees slightly and lean forward at the hips, keeping your back straight. Let your arms hang straight down from your shoulders, then lift the kettlebells to your sides until your elbows are at a 90-degree angle. Lower the kettlebells back to the starting position, squeezing your shoulder blades together and engaging your core for stability.

name: Kettlebell Bent-Over Row to Clean
description: A compound kettlebell movement that combines a bent-over row with an explosive clean. It primarily targets the lats and upper back while also engaging the biceps, shoulders, core, and lower body for power and stability. Performed for reps or time, it can be used for strength, muscle building, or conditioning.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell with both hands, standing with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the hips and knees, keeping back straight, and lower the kettlebell to just below knee level. Explosively pull the kettlebell up to chest height, rotating wrists and elbows to "catch" the kettlebell at the top of the movement. Stand up straight, squeezing shoulders and lats, to complete the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-Arm Kettlebell Row
description: A unilateral pulling exercise using a kettlebell to build strength and muscle in the back and core. Performed by hinging at the hips, supporting the body with one hand on a bench or thigh, and rowing the kettlebell toward the hip while keeping the torso stable.

Lift the kettlebell off the ground, rowing it towards your hip, squeezing your back muscles. Lower the kettlebell back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One-Arm Dumbbell Row
description: A unilateral rowing exercise performed with one knee and hand supported on a bench while the other arm rows a dumbbell toward the hip to build back strength and stability.

name: Incline Dumbbell Row
description: The incline dumbbell row is an upper-body pulling exercise performed face down on an incline bench. With a dumbbell in each hand, you row the weights toward your torso, squeezing the shoulder blades together to target the lats and upper back while also engaging the biceps. The incline position helps emphasize the lower portion of the lats and reduces lower-back strain compared with standing rows. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8-12 reps per set, in upper-body or back-focused workouts.
instructions: Lie face down on an incline bench with feet flat on floor. Hold a dumbbell in each hand, hanging arms straight down from shoulders. Lift dumbbells to sides, keeping elbows close to body, and squeeze shoulder blades together. Lower dumbbells back to starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging.

name: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row (Palms In)
description: Stand hinged at the hips with a flat back, holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing each other. Keeping your core braced and torso still, pull the dumbbells up toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back close to your sides, squeezing your back at the top. Lower the weights under control to full arm extension without rounding your spine, and repeat for the desired reps.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, hinged at the hips with a flat back. Hold a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing each other. Pull the dumbbells up toward your lower ribs, squeezing your back at the top. Lower the weights under control to full arm extension, keeping your core braced and spine straight.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Man-maker
description: The man-maker is a compound, full-body dumbbell sequence that combines a dumbbell front squat, military press, renegade row, and sprawl. It challenges strength, stability, and conditioning simultaneously and can be performed for rounds or for time as part of fat-loss or athleticism-focused workouts.
instructions: Start in a plank position with dumbbells under your hands. Perform a sprawl by jumping your feet back, then do a renegade row by lifting one dumbbell to your side. Next, jump your feet forward to a squat position and stand up, then press the dumbbells overhead in a military press. Finish by squatting down and placing the dumbbells on the ground to repeat the sequence.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Bent-Over Row
description: The dumbbell bent-over row targets the upper and middle back by hinging at the hips and pulling dumbbells toward the torso, emphasizing the lats, traps, and rear shoulders. It is effective for building strength and muscle and can be used in traditional strength training or higher-rep circuits.
instructions: Hold dumbbells with palms facing body. Bend at hips and knees slightly, keeping back straight. Lift dumbbells to sides until elbows are at a 90-degree angle, squeezing shoulder blades together. Lower dumbbells back to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Middle Back Shrug
description: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip. With a slight bend in the knees and a tall chest, hinge forward just a bit at the hips so your torso is slightly inclined. Keeping your arms straight and shoulders relaxed, squeeze your shoulder blades together and slightly down toward your back pockets, lifting the weights only by moving your shoulder blades, not your arms. Pause briefly at the peak contraction, then slowly release your shoulder blades back to the starting position under control. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with dumbbells at your sides and a slight bend in your knees. Hinge forward at the hips, keeping your chest tall and arms straight. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and down, lifting the weights with your back muscles, not your arms. Release slowly to the starting position, repeating for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Dumbbell Pull-Over to Skullcrusher
description: A compound dumbbell movement performed lying on a bench that combines a pull-over with a skullcrusher to target the chest, lats, and triceps in one continuous sequence.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor. Hold a dumbbell over your chest with both hands and extend your arms. Lower the dumbbell behind your head, stretching your lats, then pull it back up to the starting position and immediately lower it down towards your forehead, extending your elbows to target your triceps. Return the dumbbell to the starting position, squeezing your chest and lats.

name: Dumbbell Fix Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
description: A unilateral rowing exercise where you hinge at the hips and pull a dumbbell toward your torso with one arm at a time to build back strength and improve muscle balance.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in one hand. Hinge at the hips and bend knees slightly, keeping back straight. Pull the dumbbell towards your torso, keeping your elbow close to your body, then lower it back to the starting position. Repeat with the other arm, alternating sides to maintain balance and target the back muscles evenly.

name: Dumbbell Fix Bent-Over Dumbbell Row

Lower the dumbbells under control and repeat while maintaining a stable core and neutral spine.
instructions: Hinge at the hips with a flat back. Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended toward the floor. Pull the dumbbells toward your torso by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower the dumbbells under control to the starting position, keeping your core stable and spine neutral.

name: Decline Dumbbell Chest Fly
description: The decline dumbbell chest fly is an upper-body strength exercise that emphasizes the lower portion of the chest by performing a fly motion on a decline bench. Using lighter weights than a press, it is ideal for controlled, moderate-to-high rep hypertrophy work, focusing on stretching and contracting the chest through a wide range of motion.
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with feet securely locked under the pads. Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended and palms facing each other. Lower the dumbbells to the sides until you feel a stretch in your chest, then lift them back to the starting position in a controlled motion. Squeeze your chest muscles at the top of the movement.

name: HM Alternating Bent-Over Quick Row

name: Bent-Over Dumbbell Row

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row

Lower dumbbells toward feet, stretching hamstrings, then drive hips forward to stand tall. Return to starting position with control, avoiding jerky movements.

name: Single-Arm Bench Dumbbell Row

name: Unilateral Bent-Over Dumbbell Row

instructions: Lie on a bench with upper back supported and feet flat on floor, holding one dumbbell with both hands above chest.
