Keep core braced and lower back neutral throughout the movement.

name: 30° Incline Dumbbell Row
description: Lie chest-down on a bench set to roughly a 30-degree incline, holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended toward the floor. Pull the dumbbells up toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together, then lower under control. This targets the mid-back while minimizing lower-back strain.
instructions: Lie on a 30-degree incline bench with your chest down, holding a dumbbell in each hand. Pull the dumbbells up toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back. Squeeze your shoulder blades together as you lift, keeping control throughout the movement. Lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position under control.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Swing Row
description: From a hip-hinged position, swing a dumbbell slightly between your legs and then pull it back in a rowing motion toward your hip, focusing on squeezing the back muscles while keeping your core braced and spine neutral.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in one hand. Hinge at the hips, slightly swinging the dumbbell between your legs. Quickly pull the dumbbell back toward your hip, squeezing your back muscles and keeping your core braced and spine neutral. Repeat on the other side with the opposite arm.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: T-Bar Row with Handle
description: A bent-over rowing exercise performed using a T-bar setup and handle to target the middle back and lats while also engaging the biceps and rear shoulders.
instructions: Stand over the T-bar with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend knees slightly and lean forward at the hips, grasping the handle with an overhand grip. Lift the T-bar up to your chest, keeping elbows close to your body, then lower it back down to the starting position. Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated Cable Rows
description: The seated cable row is an upper-back exercise performed on a cable machine. From a seated position, you pull the handle toward your torso to train the lats, traps, rhomboids, and rear delts, with assistance from the biceps. It is commonly used for muscle-building or as an accessory movement in strength programs.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Upside-down Pull-up
description: The upside-down pull-up is an advanced bodyweight pulling exercise typically performed on rings or a bar in an inverted position. From a controlled upside-down hang, you pull your torso toward the anchor point, heavily engaging the lats, upper back, and biceps while the core stabilizes the inverted body. The pulling angle resembles a bent-over or upright row, depending on hip position, making it a challenging variation for back and arm strength and body control.
instructions: Hang upside-down from a bar or rings with hands shoulder-width apart. Engage your core to stabilize your body and pull your torso towards the anchor point. Focus on squeezing your lats and upper back as you pull. Control the movement as you return to the starting hang position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Inverted Row
description: The Smith machine inverted row is a bodyweight pulling exercise where you lie under the Smith bar, grasp it with an overhand or underhand grip, and pull your chest toward the bar. Adjusting the bar height changes the angle of your body and the difficulty: the lower the bar, the harder the movement. It primarily targets the lats and upper back while also engaging the biceps, forearms, and core for stability. Typically performed for moderate to high reps (8–12+ per set).
instructions: Lie under the Smith machine bar with your feet flat on the floor. Grasp the bar with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Pull your chest toward the bar, squeezing your lats and upper back, then control the return to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and body straight throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Lying T-Bar Row
description: A horizontal rowing exercise performed lying face down on a bench while pulling a T-bar or bar toward the torso to build mid-back thickness and overall pulling strength.
instructions: Lie face down on a flat bench with your feet secured. Grasp the T-bar handle with your hands shoulder-width apart. Pull the T-bar towards your chest, squeezing your mid-back muscles together. Lower the T-bar back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking or straining.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Suspended Row
description: The suspended row is a bodyweight pulling exercise performed with the body angled under a suspension trainer or similar setup. It targets the middle and upper back and biceps, and can be used for muscle-building sets or higher-rep conditioning, as well as a dynamic warm-up for upper-body pulling or pressing sessions.
instructions: Hang from a suspension trainer with hands shoulder-width apart. Lean back with feet planted on the ground, keeping your body straight. Pull yourself up by squeezing your shoulder blades and bending your elbows, lifting your chest to the handles. Slowly lower back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Inverted Row with Straps
description: A bodyweight pulling exercise performed by holding suspension straps and rowing your chest up toward your hands while keeping your body in a straight line, targeting the upper and mid-back.
instructions: Hold the suspension straps with hands shoulder-width apart. Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels. Pull your chest up towards your hands, squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower your body back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: One Arm Chin-Up
description: An advanced bodyweight pulling exercise where you perform a chin-up using primarily one arm, heavily challenging back, biceps, and grip strength.
instructions: Find a sturdy bar to hang from with hands shoulder-width apart. Reach up with one hand to grasp the bar, then lift your other hand to assist in finding balance. Slowly lift yourself up with one arm, keeping core tight, until your chin clears the bar. Lower yourself back down slowly to the starting position, then repeat with the other arm.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Sled Row
description: Stand facing a loaded sled with the straps or handles in your hands, hinge slightly at the hips, brace your core, and walk backward while pulling the sled toward you with powerful rowing motions, squeezing your shoulder blades together each step.
instructions: Stand facing the loaded sled with straps or handles in your hands. Hinge slightly at the hips and brace your core for stability. Walk backward while pulling the sled toward you with powerful rowing motions, squeezing your shoulder blades together with each step. Keep your movements controlled and focused on using your back and leg muscles to pull the sled.

name: Back Plate Hyperextension
description: A back extension performed on a hyperextension bench while holding a weight plate to increase resistance, targeting the lower and mid-back muscles.
instructions: Lie on a hyperextension bench with feet secured under the pads. Hold a weight plate across your chest or behind your head. Lift your torso up by squeezing your lower and mid-back muscles, keeping the movement slow and controlled. Lower your torso back down to the starting position, keeping the weight plate secure throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm Cable Seated Row
description: The single-arm cable seated row is a unilateral pulling exercise that targets the upper and mid-back by rowing a cable handle toward the torso from a seated position. It emphasizes the lats, traps, and rear shoulders while also engaging the biceps for assistance, making it effective for building back strength, correcting imbalances, and improving posture.
instructions: Sit at cable machine with feet on floor and one hand holding the cable handle. Keeping your back straight, pull the handle to your side, squeezing your shoulder blade towards your spine. Control the return to the starting position, avoiding jerky movements. Repeat with the other arm to maintain balance and symmetry.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Close-Grip Seated Cable Row
description: Sit at a cable row station with a close-grip handle, feet braced against the platform and knees slightly bent. Keeping your chest up and back neutral, pull the handle toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Pause briefly, then slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position with control.
instructions: Sit at a cable row station with a close-grip handle, feet braced against the platform and knees slightly bent. Keep your chest up and back neutral. Pull the handle toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Return to the starting position with control, slowly extending your arms.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30° Back Incline Face-Up Cable Pull-Down
description: Lie face-up on a bench set to about a 30-degree incline, positioned in front of a cable machine. With arms extended overhead toward the cable attachment, pull the handle down toward your upper chest while keeping your back pressed into the bench and your core braced. Focus on squeezing your back muscles at the bottom of the movement, then slowly return to the starting position under control.
instructions: Lie face-up on a 30-degree incline bench in front of a cable machine. Extend your arms overhead to grasp the cable handle. Pull the handle down toward your upper chest, squeezing your back muscles and keeping your core braced. Return to the starting position slowly and under control.

name: Kneeling High Cable Pull-Down
description: From a kneeling position facing a high cable attachment, pull the handle down toward your upper chest while keeping your torso upright and core braced to target the mid-back and lats.
instructions: Kneel facing a high cable attachment with feet wider than hip-width apart. Hold the handle with an overhand grip, keeping your torso upright and core braced. Pull the handle down toward your upper chest, squeezing your mid-back and lats. Return the handle to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking or swinging.

name: Back Leaning V-Bar Pull-Down
description: Sit at a lat pulldown station with a V-bar attachment and secure your thighs under the pads. Lean your torso slightly back while keeping your chest up and core braced. With an overhand neutral grip on the V-bar, pull the handle down toward your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your back muscles at the bottom. Control the weight as you let your arms extend overhead, maintaining the slight backward lean without swinging.
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown station with a V-bar attachment, securing your thighs under the pads. Lean your torso slightly back, keeping your chest up and core braced. Pull the V-bar down toward your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back. Control the weight as you return to the starting position, maintaining the slight backward lean.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Reverse-Grip Lat Pulldown
description: Sit at a lat pulldown station and grasp the bar with an underhand, shoulder-width grip. With your chest up and core braced, pull the bar down toward your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back, focusing on squeezing your back muscles. Control the bar as you return to the starting position with fully extended arms without letting your shoulders shrug up.
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown station with feet flat on floor. Grasp the bar with an underhand, shoulder-width grip, keeping chest up and core braced. Pull the bar down toward your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your back muscles. Control the bar as you return to the starting position with fully extended arms.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: 30 Back Standing Low Cable Bar Row
description: Stand facing a low cable pulley with a straight bar attachment. Grasp the bar with an overhand or neutral grip, step back to create tension, and hinge slightly at the hips while keeping your back straight and core braced. Pull the bar toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together, then slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position under control.
instructions: Stand facing a low cable pulley with a straight bar attachment. Grasp the bar with an overhand or neutral grip and step back to create tension. Hinge slightly at the hips, keeping your back straight and core braced, then pull the bar toward your lower ribs by driving your elbows back. Slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position under control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Standing Low Cable Rope Row
description: Stand facing a low cable pulley with a rope attachment, hinge slightly at the hips, and pull the rope toward your lower ribs while keeping your elbows close to your body and your back flat. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of the movement, then slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position.
instructions: Stand facing a low cable pulley with a rope attachment. Hinge slightly at the hips and keep your back flat. Pull the rope toward your lower ribs, keeping your elbows close to your body, and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Return to the starting position by slowly extending your arms.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Straight-Arm Cable Pulldown
description: Stand facing a cable machine with a straight bar attached to a high pulley. With arms straight and a slight bend in the elbows, pull the bar down in an arc from shoulder height to your thighs, focusing on squeezing your lats and keeping your back flat and core braced throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a straight bar attached to a high pulley. Keep your arms straight with a slight bend in the elbows and pull the bar down in an arc. Focus on squeezing your lats and keep your back flat and core braced throughout the movement. Lower the bar to your thighs, then control the return to the starting position.

name: Straight-Arm Rope Pulldown
description: Stand facing a cable machine with a rope attachment set high. With arms straight and a slight bend in the elbows, pull the rope down in an arc toward your thighs by engaging your lats and mid-back, then slowly return to the starting position under control.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with the rope attachment set high. Hold the rope with arms straight and a slight bend in the elbows. Pull the rope down in an arc toward your thighs by engaging your lats and mid-back. Return to the starting position slowly and under control.

name: Wide-Grip Seated Cable Row
description: Sit at a cable row station with a wide bar attachment. Place your feet on the platform, maintain a neutral spine, and grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip. Pull the bar toward your upper abdomen or lower chest by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together, then slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position under control.
instructions: Sit at a cable row station with a wide bar attachment. Place your feet on the platform and grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, maintaining a neutral spine. Pull the bar toward your upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Return to the starting position by slowly extending your arms under control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Rower
description: The rower is a gym machine that provides serious cardiovascular work while also targeting the legs, back, and arm muscles. It is effective for both high-intensity interval training and longer-duration endurance sessions, making it a versatile tool for building overall aerobic and muscular fitness.
instructions: Sit at the rower with feet strapped in and hands grasping the bar. Push with your legs, then pull the bar to your chest, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Return to the starting position by extending your arms and bending your knees. Keep a steady pace and rhythm to maintain a consistent workout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Bent-Over Row
description: The Smith machine bent-over row is an upper-body pulling exercise that targets the lats and mid-back using the guided bar path of a Smith machine. With a hinged torso and overhand grip, you row the bar toward your lower chest or upper abdomen to build back thickness and strength. It is typically performed for moderate to high reps, such as 8-12 per set, in upper-body or back-focused workouts.
instructions: Stand in a Smith machine with the bar at hip level. Hold the bar with an overhand grip and bend at the hips, keeping your back straight. Row the bar toward your lower chest or upper abdomen, squeezing your lats and mid-back. Lower the bar back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Leverage High Row
description: A machine-based rowing exercise that targets the lats and overall back by pulling handles from a high position toward the torso.
instructions: Sit at the leverage high row machine with feet flat on floor. Grasp the handles with palms facing towards body. Pull the handles down towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together and targeting your lats. Return the handles to the starting position in a controlled motion.

name: Machine Low Row
description: The machine low row is a strength exercise targeting the middle back, emphasizing the lats and mid-back muscles through a seated pulling motion.
instructions: Sit at the machine with your feet flat on the floor and knees slightly bent. Grasp the bar with your hands shoulder-width apart, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Pull the bar towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together and focusing on your middle back muscles. Return the bar to the starting position in a controlled motion.

name: Standing Lat Pull-Down
description: A cable-based lat pull-down performed from a standing position, emphasizing the lats and upper back while also engaging the core and lower body for stability.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip, keeping arms straight and engaging core for stability. Pull the bar down towards your thighs, squeezing lats and upper back muscles. Return the bar to the starting position, controlling the movement to maintain proper form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated Machine Row
description: Sit at a rowing machine with your chest supported or feet braced, grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, and pull them toward your torso by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Control the weight as you extend your arms to return to the starting position, keeping your chest up and core braced throughout.
instructions: Sit at the rowing machine with your chest supported or feet braced. Grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip. Pull the handles toward your torso by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Control the weight as you extend your arms to return to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Mixed Grip Chin-Up
description: Hang from a pull-up bar using a mixed grip (one hand supinated, one pronated) with arms fully extended and core braced. Pull your chest toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your back and biceps at the top. Lower under control to a full hang and repeat, alternating which hand is underhand between sets to balance the workload.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with a mixed grip, one hand facing forward and one hand facing backward. Pull your chest toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back. Squeeze your back and biceps at the top of the movement. Lower under control to a full hang, then repeat, alternating grip hands between sets.

name: Rhomboids SMR
description: Rhomboids self-myofascial release (SMR) is a soft-tissue technique for the mid-back using a foam roller or similar tool to apply pressure to tight trigger points in the rhomboid area, helping reduce tension and improve mobility around the shoulder blades.
instructions: Lie on the floor with a foam roller under your mid-back. Position the foam roller perpendicular to your spine, targeting the rhomboid area. Slowly roll the foam roller up and down, applying pressure to release tension in the muscle. Hold on areas of discomfort for 30 seconds to release trigger points.

name: Spinal Stretch
description: A seated or lying stretch focused on lengthening the muscles along the spine and middle back to reduce stiffness and improve mobility.
instructions: Sit on the floor with legs straight out in front of you. Slowly lean forward, reaching for your toes, and hold for a few seconds. Keep your knees slightly bent if necessary to avoid discomfort. Return to the starting position and repeat for several repetitions.

name: Upper Back Stretch
description: A mobility stretch performed by rounding the upper back and reaching the arms forward to lengthen the muscles between the shoulder blades and mid-back.
instructions: Stand or sit with good posture. Round your upper back, leaning forward slightly, and reach your arms forward. Keep your arms straight and let your hands dangle, feeling the stretch between your shoulder blades and mid-back. Hold for a few seconds to lengthen the muscles.

name: Middle Back Stretch
description: A static stretch performed to lengthen the muscles of the middle back by gently rounding and flexing the spine, helping to reduce stiffness and improve thoracic mobility.
instructions: Stand or sit with good posture. Place your hands behind your back and interlock your fingers. Gently lean back, rounding your middle back and stretching your chest forward, holding for 30 seconds. Breathe deeply and smoothly, feeling the stretch in your middle back.

name: HM Angel Stretch
description: A wall-based mobility drill where you stand with your back, head, and arms against a wall and slowly slide your arms up and down in a "snow angel" motion to open the chest and mid-back while improving shoulder mobility and posture.
instructions: Stand with your back, head, and arms against a wall. Slowly slide your arms up the wall in a wide, circular motion, keeping them straight. Continue the motion, lowering your arms back down to the starting position, repeating for several repetitions. Keep your shoulders and chest relaxed, focusing on smooth and controlled movements.

name: Foam Roll Upper Back
description: Lie on a foam roller placed horizontally under your upper back, knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Cross your arms over your chest or support your head lightly with your hands. Lift your hips slightly and slowly roll from the mid-back to just below the shoulders, pausing on tight or tender spots while keeping your core engaged and neck neutral.
instructions: Lie on a foam roller under your upper back with knees bent and feet flat. Cross your arms over your chest or lightly support your head with your hands. Lift your hips slightly and roll from mid-back to just below the shoulders, pausing on tight spots. Keep your core engaged and neck neutral throughout the movement.

name: Neutral-Grip Lat Pulldown
description: Sit facing the cable machine with a neutral-grip (palms facing each other) attachment. Grip the handles slightly wider than shoulder-width, keep your chest up, and pull the handles down toward your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back. Pause briefly, then slowly return to the starting position with control, maintaining a neutral spine throughout.
instructions: Sit facing the cable machine with a neutral-grip attachment. Grip the handles slightly wider than shoulder-width and keep your chest up. Pull the handles down toward your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back, then pause briefly. Return to the starting position with control, maintaining a neutral spine throughout.

name: 30 Back Pull-Up
description: Perform a standard pull-up focusing on engaging the middle back by pulling your chest toward the bar and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top. Maintain a controlled tempo and full range of motion for up to 30 total reps or sets of smaller rep counts to accumulate 30 quality repetitions.
instructions: Hang from the bar with hands shoulder-width apart. Pull yourself up by drawing your chest toward the bar, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top. Lower yourself back down to the starting position in a controlled manner. Repeat for up to 30 repetitions, focusing on a controlled tempo and full range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Reverse-Grip Pull-Up
description: Hang from a pull-up bar with an underhand, shoulder-width grip. From a dead hang with arms fully extended and shoulders engaged, pull your chest up toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your lats and mid-back. Pause briefly at the top, then lower yourself under control to the starting position without swinging. Repeat for the desired reps.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Pull your chest up toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your lats and mid-back. Pause briefly at the top, then lower yourself under control to the starting position. Keep your body still and avoid swinging throughout the exercise.

name: ACFT Medicine Ball Overhead Power Throw
description: An explosive full-body power exercise where you start in a semi-squat holding a medicine ball between the legs, then drive through the hips and core to scoop and throw the ball overhead and backward in one continuous motion. Commonly used in the Army Combat Fitness Test (ACFT) to assess power and conditioning.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart in a semi-squat position. Hold a medicine ball between your legs with both hands. Drive through your hips and core to scoop the ball up and throw it overhead and backward in one continuous motion. Keep your arms straight and release the ball at the highest point of the throw.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Upright Row with Resistance Bands
description: Stand on a resistance band with feet about shoulder-width apart, holding the ends of the band in front of your thighs with an overhand grip. Keeping the band close to your body, pull your hands straight up toward your chest, leading with your elbows so they rise higher than your wrists. Pause briefly at the top, then lower back down under control. Maintain an upright torso and avoid shrugging excessively or swinging.
instructions: Stand on a resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the band ends with an overhand grip in front of your thighs. Pull your hands up toward your chest, leading with your elbows, and pause briefly at the top. Lower back down under control, keeping your torso upright and avoiding excessive shrugging or swinging.

name: Band Seated Row
description: The band seated row is a resistance band alternative to the cable row that targets the upper back, including the lats, traps, and rear shoulders, with secondary emphasis on the biceps. Performed from a seated position with the band anchored in front of you, it can be used with heavier bands for strength and muscle-building or lighter bands for higher-rep, conditioning, or accessory work.
instructions: Sit on the floor with legs straight out in front of you. Anchor the resistance band at a stable point in front of you and hold the ends of the band in each hand. Pull the band towards your chest, keeping your back straight and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the band.

name: Barbell Shrug
description: The barbell shrug is a strength exercise that targets the trapezius muscles by elevating the shoulders straight up toward the ears while holding a barbell. It is typically performed with moderate to heavy loads for moderate to high repetitions to build upper-trap size and strength.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Hold the barbell at arm's length, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Lift the barbell by shrugging your shoulders straight up toward your ears, squeezing your trapezius muscles at the top. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Barbell Behind-the-Back Shrug
description: The barbell behind-the-back shrug targets the upper traps by holding a barbell behind the body and elevating the shoulders straight up toward the ears. Keeping the bar behind you encourages the shoulders to stay pulled up and back rather than rounding forward. It is commonly used in strength and muscle-focused upper-body training, often on shoulder or trap-focused days, and is typically performed with moderate to high reps such as 8–10 per set.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a barbell with an overhand grip behind your back, hands slightly wider than hip-width. Lift the bar by shrugging your shoulders straight up toward your ears, squeezing your traps at the top. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Clean Shrug
description: The clean shrug is a weightlifting movement where you hold a barbell at hip level with an overhand grip and explosively extend your hips and knees while shrugging your shoulders straight up toward your ears, then lower under control. It is typically used to build explosive power and trap strength for the clean and other Olympic lifts.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell at hip level with an overhand grip. Explode through hips and knees, shrugging shoulders straight up toward ears. Keep core tight and maintain control throughout the movement. Lower the barbell back to starting position under control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Snatch Shrug
description: Stand with a barbell using a wide snatch grip, bar over mid-foot and arms straight. With a flat back and braced core, extend your hips and knees explosively while keeping the bar close to your body, then shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears without bending your elbows. Control the bar back down to the starting position and repeat.
instructions: Stand with a barbell using a wide snatch grip, feet shoulder-width apart. Keep your back flat and core braced, then explosively extend your hips and knees. Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears without bending your elbows, keeping the bar close to your body. Control the bar back down to the starting position to repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm Inverted Row
description: The single-arm inverted row is a bodyweight pulling exercise performed using a fixed bar, such as a Smith machine bar, to target the lats and upper back. Lying under the bar and holding it with one hand, you keep your body in a straight line and pull your chest toward the bar, adjusting bar height to modify difficulty and angle.
instructions: Lie under the bar with your body in a straight line. Hold the bar with one hand, gripping firmly. Pull your chest toward the bar, keeping your core engaged and body straight, then control the return to starting position. Adjust the bar height to modify difficulty and angle as needed.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Sumo Deadlift High Pull
description: A dynamic kettlebell movement combining a wide-stance deadlift with an explosive upright pull. It targets the posterior chain and upper back, building power, strength, and conditioning depending on load and rep scheme.
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width apart, toes pointed outward. Hold a kettlebell with both hands, hanging down between your legs. Explosively lift the kettlebell up to chest level, pulling with your back and legs, then quickly return to the starting position. Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Standing Dumbbell Shrug
description: The standing dumbbell shrug targets the trapezius muscles by elevating the shoulders straight up toward the ears while holding dumbbells at the sides. It is commonly used to build upper-back strength and size and fits well in shoulder, back, or full-body strength routines.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at your sides. Keep your arms straight and lift the dumbbells by shrugging your shoulders up towards your ears. Avoid swinging or jerking the weights, instead use a slow and controlled motion. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.

name: Standing Dumbbell Upright Row
description: The standing dumbbell upright row is a strength exercise targeting the traps and shoulders. Performed standing with a dumbbell in each hand, the weights are pulled vertically along the body up toward chest height, emphasizing the upper back and shoulder muscles. It is commonly used with moderate to high repetitions, such as 8–12 reps per set or more.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing your body. Keep your back straight and core engaged, then lift the dumbbells up towards your chest, keeping them close to your body. Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades and upper back muscles as you lift. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Dumbbell Pull-Over
description: The dumbbell pull-over is a classic strength exercise performed lying on a bench, lowering a dumbbell behind the head and pulling it back over the chest to target the chest and upper back while also engaging the lats and core.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor. Hold a dumbbell over your chest with both hands and arms extended. Lower the dumbbell behind your head, stretching your chest and shoulders, then pull it back to the starting position, squeezing your chest and lats. Breathe naturally and control the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm Dumbbell Row
description: The single-arm dumbbell row is a unilateral back exercise that targets the lats and upper back, helping build strength and muscle with moderate to high reps per arm.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in one hand with your arm hanging straight down from your shoulder. Bend your knees slightly and lean forward at the hips, keeping your back straight. Lift the dumbbell to your side until your elbow is at a 90-degree angle, squeezing your lats and upper back. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position and repeat with the other arm.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Lying Face Down Neck Extension (Bodyweight Resistance)
description: Lie face down on a bench or mat with your head hanging just off the edge. Place your hands lightly on the back of your head or cross your arms under your forehead without pulling. Starting from a neutral position, slowly lift your head by extending your neck until it is in line with your spine, then lower back under control. Focus on small, controlled movements and avoid jerking or overextending the neck.
instructions: Lie face down on a bench or mat with your head hanging off the edge. Place your hands lightly on the back of your head or cross your arms under your forehead. Slowly lift your head by extending your neck until it's in line with your spine, then lower back under control. Focus on small, controlled movements to avoid jerking or overextending the neck.

name: Lying Face Up Neck Resistance
description: Lie on your back on a flat surface with your knees bent and feet planted. Place your hands lightly on your forehead to create gentle resistance, then slowly nod your head forward by bringing your chin toward your chest, keeping your shoulders relaxed against the floor. Pause briefly, then slowly return your head to the starting position without letting it drop. Focus on controlled movement and avoid using your arms to pull the head; they should only provide light resistance.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet planted. Place hands lightly on your forehead for gentle resistance. Slowly nod your head forward, bringing chin toward chest, then pause briefly. Return head to the starting position slowly, keeping movement controlled and using arms only for light resistance.

name: Seated Head Harness Neck Resistance
description: Sit upright on a bench with a head harness secured around your head and attached to resistance. Keeping your torso still and core braced, slowly flex your neck forward against the resistance, then return under control to the starting position. Perform for controlled repetitions to strengthen the neck muscles while avoiding any jerking motions.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with a head harness secured around your head. Keep your torso still and core braced. Slowly flex your neck forward against the resistance, then return under control to the starting position. Perform controlled repetitions to strengthen your neck muscles.

name: Cable Straight-Bar Upright Row
description: The cable straight-bar upright row is an isolation exercise targeting the traps and medial deltoids using a cable machine. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8-12 reps per set, within an upper-body or shoulder-focused workout. Due to the shoulder position involved, it may not be appropriate for individuals with existing shoulder issues or a history of shoulder injury.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the cable at chest height. Grasp a straight-bar attachment with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Pull the bar towards your chest, keeping elbows close to your body, and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Return the bar to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Cable Shrug
description: The cable shrug targets the traps with continuous tension from the cable, also engaging the shoulders and upper back. It is typically performed with moderate to lighter loads for higher-rep sets to build muscular endurance and hypertrophy in the upper traps.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with the cable at shoulder height. Hold a handle in each hand with palms facing your thighs. Shrug your shoulders up towards your ears, squeezing your traps at the top of the movement. Lower your shoulders back down to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Smith Machine Shrug
description: The Smith machine shrug is a trap-focused upper-body exercise where you stand under a fixed bar and elevate your shoulders straight up and down to target the trapezius muscles. It is typically performed with moderate to high repetitions as part of a shoulder or trap-focused workout.
instructions: Stand under the Smith machine bar with feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, palms facing your body. Lift the bar by shrugging your shoulders straight up, squeezing your traps at the top. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Leverage Shrug
description: The leverage shrug is a trap-focused strength exercise performed on a leverage or plate-loaded machine. Standing with arms extended and shoulders relaxed, you elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears in a controlled motion, then lower them back down to stretch the traps without using momentum.
instructions: Stand at the machine with feet shoulder-width apart and arms extended. Relax your shoulders and engage your core. Elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears in a controlled motion, then lower them back down to the starting position without using momentum. Keep the movement slow and focused to target the trap muscles.

name: Calf-Machine Shoulder Shrug
description: Stand on the calf-raise machine with the shoulder pads resting comfortably on your shoulders and feet about hip-width apart. Keep your arms relaxed at your sides and maintain a neutral spine. Without bending your elbows, elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears in a controlled shrugging motion, squeezing your upper traps at the top. Pause briefly, then slowly lower your shoulders back down to a full stretch. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding any bouncing or jerking.
instructions: Stand on the calf-raise machine with feet hip-width apart and shoulder pads on your shoulders. Keep your arms relaxed and maintain a neutral spine. Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears without bending your elbows, squeezing your upper traps at the top. Lower your shoulders back down slowly to a full stretch, repeating for the desired number of repetitions.

name: Smith Machine Behind-the-Back Shrug
description: The Smith machine behind-the-back shrug is a trap-focused strength exercise performed with the bar positioned behind the body. Standing inside the Smith machine with the bar resting behind your thighs, you elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears, squeezing the traps at the top before lowering under control. It is typically used for moderate to high reps (8-12+) in shoulder or trap-focused workouts to build upper trapezius size and strength while the fixed bar path provides added stability.
instructions: Stand inside the Smith machine with the bar behind your thighs. Hold the bar with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lift your shoulders straight up toward your ears, squeezing your traps at the top. Lower the bar back down to the starting position under control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Upright Row
description: The Smith machine upright row is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the traps and shoulders by pulling a fixed bar vertically along the body. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8–12 reps per set, within an upper-body or shoulder-focused workout.
instructions: Stand in a Smith machine with feet shoulder-width apart. Grasp the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, palms facing your body. Pull the bar up to your chest, keeping elbows close to your body, then lower it back to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the exercise.

name: Smith Machine Single-Arm Shrug
description: The Smith machine single-arm shrug is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the trapezius muscles by elevating one shoulder at a time against resistance. Using the fixed bar path of the Smith machine helps control the movement and focus on isolating the traps, typically performed for moderate to high repetitions in trap- or shoulder-focused workouts.
instructions: Stand in a Smith machine with the bar at shoulder height. Grasp the bar with one hand and hold it with your arm straight, keeping your core engaged. Shrug your shoulder up towards your ear, squeezing your trapezius muscle, then slowly lower it back down to the starting position. Repeat with the other arm to complete the exercise.

name: Isometric Neck Exercise - Sides
description: Sit or stand tall and gently press the side of your head into your hand while resisting the movement with equal force, holding the contraction without allowing the neck to move. This strengthens the lateral neck muscles isometrically and helps improve neck stability and posture.
instructions: Sit or stand tall with good posture. Place your hand on the side of your head, just above the ear. Gently press your head into your hand, resisting the movement with equal force, and hold for a few seconds. Keep your neck straight and still, without allowing any movement.

name: Isometric Neck Exercise - Front And Back
description: A static neck-strengthening exercise where you gently press your head forward into your hands, then backward, without moving the neck, to build isometric strength in the neck and upper back stabilizers.
instructions: Sit or stand with good posture. Place your hands on your forehead with fingers spread, gently pressing your head forward into your hands without moving your neck. Hold for a few seconds, then release and place your hands behind your head, gently pressing your head backward into your hands. Hold again for a few seconds, repeating the sequence to build isometric strength.

name: Neck Bridge Prone
description: Lie face down on a mat with your hands by your sides and gently press your forehead into the surface, lightly lifting your chest and shoulders off the ground to create a small bridge through the neck and upper back. Focus on controlled tension and avoid excessive pressure, keeping the movement slow and within a comfortable range of motion.
instructions: Lie face down on a mat with hands by your sides. Gently press your forehead into the surface and lift your chest and shoulders off the ground, creating a small bridge. Keep the movement slow and controlled, avoiding excessive pressure. Focus on gentle tension in the neck and upper back.

name: Side Neck Stretch
description: From a tall seated or standing position, gently tilt your head to one side, bringing your ear toward your shoulder until you feel a stretch along the opposite side of your neck. Hold without pulling on the head, then slowly return to center and repeat on the other side.
instructions: Sit or stand tall with good posture. Gently tilt your head to one side, bringing your ear toward your shoulder, until you feel a stretch. Hold the stretch without pulling on your head, then slowly return to center. Repeat on the other side.

name: Neck Self-Myofascial Release (Neck-SMR)
description: A self-myofascial release technique for the neck that uses gentle pressure and slow movements to reduce muscle tension and improve mobility around the upper back and shoulder area. Typically performed lying down or against a wall using bodyweight pressure on tight spots.
instructions: Lie on your back or sit with your back against a wall. Place your fingers or a foam roller on the tight spots in your neck, applying gentle pressure. Slowly roll or move your fingers from side to side, holding on tight spots for 30 seconds to release tension. Breathe deeply and slowly, feeling the relaxation in your neck and upper back.

name: Scapular Pull-Up
description: Hang from a pull-up bar with straight arms and engaged core, then draw your shoulder blades down and together to lift your body slightly without bending the elbows, before returning to a relaxed hanging position.
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with straight arms and engaged core. Draw your shoulder blades down and together to lift your body slightly. Keep your elbows straight and avoid swinging. Return to a relaxed hanging position, repeating the movement to target your scapular muscles.

name: Single-leg Depth Squat
description: The single-leg depth squat is a lower-body strength exercise performed on one leg to target the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings with an emphasis on control and stability. It is often used as a more accessible alternative to the pistol squat because it requires less flexibility while still challenging balance and unilateral leg strength.
instructions: Stand on one leg with the other foot lifted off the ground. Lower your body down into a squat, keeping your back straight and your core engaged. Control your descent and pause briefly at the bottom, then push through your heel to return to standing. Keep your knee in line with your toes and focus on balance and stability throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Squats - With Bands
description: A beginner-friendly squat variation using resistance bands to add light external resistance while targeting the quadriceps and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes facing forward, and the resistance band anchored above your head or at shoulder height. Hold the band's ends in each hand, with your arms at your sides, and your palms facing your thighs. Lower your body down into a squat, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes, then push through your heels to return to the starting position. Keep the band taut throughout the movement to maintain resistance.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hip Flexion with Band
description: Attach a resistance band to a low anchor and around the front of your ankle. Stand tall facing the anchor or slightly turned for comfort, holding onto a stable support if needed. Keeping your torso upright and core braced, lift your knee up in front of you against the band’s resistance, flexing at the hip without leaning back. Pause briefly at the top, then lower your leg under control to the starting position. Repeat for reps, then switch legs.
instructions: Stand facing the anchor with the resistance band around your ankle. Lift your knee up in front of you, keeping your torso upright and core braced. Pause briefly at the top, then lower your leg under control to the starting position. Repeat on both legs for the desired number of reps.

name: Forward Band Walk
description: Place a resistance band around your lower thighs or ankles, stand with feet hip-width apart, and slightly bend your knees into an athletic stance. Keeping constant tension on the band, step forward one foot at a time, maintaining alignment of knees over toes and avoiding the knees caving inward. Continue walking forward for the desired distance or number of steps while keeping your core braced and chest up.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and knees slightly bent. Place a resistance band around your lower thighs or ankles, keeping constant tension. Step forward one foot at a time, keeping knees over toes and core braced. Maintain good posture with chest up and walk forward for the desired distance.

name: Boss Everline Resistance-Band Pull Sprint
description: Attach a resistance band securely behind you at hip height, loop it around your waist, and move forward until there is light tension. Lean slightly forward with a strong, braced core and begin sprinting in place or driving forward in short bursts while the band provides backward resistance. Focus on powerful knee drive, quick ground contact, and maintaining an upright torso as you accelerate against the band.
instructions: Attach a resistance band securely behind you at hip height and loop it around your waist. Move forward until there is light tension and lean slightly forward with a strong, braced core. Begin sprinting in place or driving forward in short bursts, focusing on powerful knee drive and quick ground contact. Keep your torso upright as you accelerate against the band's resistance.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Banded Jump Squat
description: An explosive squat variation performed with a resistance band looped around the thighs just above the knees. The band increases hip and glute activation while the jump emphasizes power, strength, and conditioning in the lower body.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and a resistance band looped around your thighs just above the knees. Lower your body into a squat, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes. Explosively jump up from the squat position, extending your hips and knees. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately lower into the next squat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Speed Box Squat
description: A dynamic barbell box squat variation performed with added band resistance to develop lower-body strength and explosive power. The lifter descends under control to a box set around parallel depth, briefly pauses while maintaining tension, then drives up as fast as possible to emphasize speed and power through the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes facing forward, and the box behind you. Lower your body under control to sit on the box, keeping your back straight and knees in line with your toes. Pause briefly, maintaining tension, then explosively drive upward through your heels to return to standing. Focus on speed and power, extending your hips and knees quickly to complete the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: HM Banded Squat Toe Tap
description: Stand with a resistance band around your thighs and feet hip-width apart. Lower into a squat, keeping your chest up and knees tracking over your toes. From the squat position, tap one foot out to the side while maintaining tension in the band and keeping your weight centered, then return the foot to the starting position and repeat on the other side. Stay in a controlled squat throughout the movement to target the quadriceps and glutes while challenging hip stability.
instructions: Stand with a resistance band around your thighs and feet hip-width apart. Lower into a squat, keeping your chest up and knees over your toes. Tap one foot out to the side, maintaining tension in the band and keeping your weight centered, then return to the starting position and repeat on the other side. Keep your squat controlled throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Band Split Squat
description: The band split squat is a lower-body strength exercise using resistance bands to target the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings in a split stance. Leaning slightly back increases emphasis on the glutes and hamstrings, while a more upright torso shifts focus to the quads. It can be performed for time or repetitions and is commonly used in fat-loss or athletic performance circuits.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, one foot forward and one foot back, with a resistance band anchored at hip height. Hold the band with both hands at shoulder height, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Lower your body down into a lunge, keeping your front knee behind your toes and your back knee almost touching the ground. Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Band Front Squat
description: The band front squat targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings while also engaging the core and upper back to maintain an upright posture. Elastic band resistance increases as it stretches, matching the natural strength curve of the squat and challenging the lower body more at the top of the movement. Typically performed for moderate to high repetitions (8-12+ per set), stance width can be adjusted to reduce knee discomfort.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, band anchored at hip level. Hold the band with both hands at shoulder height, palms facing down. Lower your body into a squat, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes. Push through your heels to return to standing, squeezing your legs and glutes at the top.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Squat Hold with Band Pull-Apart
description: Hold a squat with thighs at or below parallel while simultaneously performing band pull-aparts to engage the upper back. This combo increases time under tension for the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings while strengthening the upper back and shoulders.
instructions: Hold a squat position with thighs at or below parallel. Hold a resistance band with both hands at shoulder height, arms extended. Pull the band apart, squeezing your shoulder blades together, while maintaining the squat position. Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Banded Squat Toe Tap
description: The banded squat toe tap is a squat variation using a resistance band with a lateral toe tap to increase activation of the hips and stabilizer muscles. From a banded squat position, you maintain tension in the band while alternately tapping one foot out to the side, then returning to the squat stance, keeping the core braced and knees tracking over the toes.
instructions: Stand on the middle of a resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Lower into a squat, keeping tension in the band and core braced. Tap one foot out to the side, keeping knees tracking over the toes, then return to the squat stance. Alternate feet, maintaining tension and control throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Band Overhead Squat
description: The band overhead squat is a full-body, functional movement that builds strength and stability through the legs, core, and shoulders. Using resistance bands instead of a barbell, it helps develop core control and overhead stability for Olympic lifting or CrossFit-style training.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a resistance band overhead with arms fully extended. Lower your body into a squat, keeping your back straight and your core engaged. Push through your heels to return to standing, maintaining control and stability throughout the movement. Keep the band overhead and arms straight throughout the entire exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Quadriceps SMR
description: Quadriceps self-myofascial release (SMR) uses a foam roller to apply pressure along the front of the thighs to help reduce tightness and improve tissue quality in the quadriceps. Typically performed lying face down with the roller under one or both thighs, you slowly roll from just above the knees up toward the hips, pausing on tender spots to allow the muscle to relax.
instructions: Lie face down with the foam roller under one or both thighs. Position the roller just above the knees and slowly roll it up toward the hips. Pause on tender spots to allow the muscle to relax and release tension. Repeat the process several times to help reduce tightness in the quadriceps.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Clean from Blocks
description: A barbell Olympic lift variation where the bar starts elevated on blocks, reducing the pull range and emphasizing explosive leg and hip drive to lift the bar to the shoulders in a front rack position.
instructions: Stand in front of blocks with barbell at knee level. Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder width, keeping back straight and core engaged. Explosively drive through your legs and hips to lift the bar up, receiving it in a front rack position across your shoulders. Keep your elbows high and close to your body to control the bar and maintain balance.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Full Squat
description: The barbell full squat is a compound lower-body exercise performed with a barbell on the upper back or traps. It targets the quadriceps while heavily engaging the glutes, hamstrings, and core to build overall lower-body strength and power. It can be trained with heavy low-rep sets for strength or higher reps for muscle and work capacity.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward. Hold the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, resting it on your upper back or traps. Lower your body down into a squat, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes, until your thighs are parallel to the ground. Push through your heels to return to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Back Squat to Box
description: The barbell back squat to box is a lower-body strength exercise where you squat down under control to a box set at a chosen height, lightly sit or touch, then drive back up to standing. Using the box helps customize squat depth and reduces the stretch reflex at the bottom, emphasizing control and power through the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. It can be used with heavier loads for strength and power or moderate loads and higher reps for muscle building.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart behind a box. Hold a barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, resting it on your upper back. Lower your body down into a squat, touching your glutes to the box, then drive through your heels to return to standing. Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Push-Press
description: The push-press is an overhead barbell press that uses leg drive to help propel the weight through the sticking point of a strict press, making it a powerful movement for developing explosive strength and shoulder power.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the barbell at shoulder height. Lower your body slightly by bending your knees, then explosively drive upward through your legs. Press the barbell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hang Clean
description: From a standing position holding a barbell at arm’s length, hinge at the hips and slightly bend the knees to let the bar hang just above the knees. Explosively extend the hips and knees while shrugging the shoulders to drive the bar upward, then quickly drop under the bar and catch it on the front of the shoulders in a quarter squat before standing tall.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell at arm's length. Hinge at the hips and bend the knees slightly, letting the bar hang just above the knees. Explosively extend the hips and knees, shrugging the shoulders to drive the bar upward, then quickly drop under it. Catch the bar on the front of the shoulders in a quarter squat, then stand tall.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Walking Lunge
description: The barbell walking lunge challenges balance and lower-body strength by targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings while engaging the core. Using a barbell allows for progressive overload and can be performed for moderate to high repetitions for strength, conditioning, and mental toughness.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and hold a barbell with hands shoulder-width apart. Take a large step forward with one foot and lower your body until your back knee almost touches the ground. Keep your front knee behind your toes and push back to the starting position, then repeat with the other leg. Alternate legs with each step, maintaining balance and control throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Olympic Squat
description: A barbell squat variation performed with a more upright torso and deeper range of motion, emphasizing the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes and core for stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward. Hold a barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Lower your body down into a squat, keeping your weight in your heels, until your thighs are parallel to the ground. Push through your heels to return to standing, squeezing your quadriceps and glutes at the top of the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Narrow-Stance Squat
description: The narrow-stance squat is a lower-body strength exercise that emphasizes the quadriceps by placing the feet closer together than in a traditional squat. Because of the stance, many lifters will not be able to squat as deep, which is normal. It can be performed for time or repetitions as part of any strength-focused workout.
instructions: Stand with feet together or about 6 inches apart. Lower your body down into a squat, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes. Descend to a depth where your thighs are parallel to the ground or as low as comfortably possible. Push through your heels to return to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Box Squat with Bands
description: A squat variation where you sit back onto a box or bench while using resistance bands attached to the barbell to increase tension and improve power and control through the squat range of motion.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart behind a box or bench. Attach resistance bands to the barbell and hold it with an overhand grip. Lower your body down into a squat, sitting back onto the box while keeping your back straight and knees in line with toes. Push through your heels to return to the starting position, squeezing your legs and glutes.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Weighted Jump Squat
description: A squat variation where you explosively jump upward while holding a barbell, focusing on power and strength in the legs and hips.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with hands shoulder-width apart. Lower your body into a squat, keeping back straight and knees behind toes. Explosively jump upward, extending legs and hips, while holding the barbell. Land softly on the balls of your feet, then repeat the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Squat with Chains
description: A barbell back squat performed with chains attached to the bar to increase resistance as you stand up, emphasizing power development and strength through the full range of motion.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing forward or slightly outward. Hold the barbell with chains attached, resting it on your upper back. Lower your body down into a squat, keeping back straight and knees behind toes, then forcefully stand up, extending hips and knees to lift the barbell with chains. Control the movement throughout the entire range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Squat
description: The barbell back squat is a compound lower-body strength exercise performed with a barbell on the upper back. It primarily targets the quadriceps while heavily engaging the glutes and hamstrings, with support from the core and upper body to stabilize the load. It can be trained with heavy low-rep sets for maximal strength or higher reps for muscle and work capacity.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart under the barbell. Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, squeezing shoulder blades together to stabilize the load. Lower body down into a squat, keeping back straight and knees behind toes, then push through heels to return to standing position. Keep core engaged and maintain control throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Front Squat
description: The barbell front squat is a compound lower-body exercise where the barbell is held across the front of the shoulders. This position shifts emphasis to the quadriceps and glutes while demanding significant core and upper-back engagement to keep the torso upright. It can be used as a primary strength movement, an alternative to the back squat, or an accessory lift for squats and deadlifts.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly outward. Hold the barbell across the front of your shoulders, resting it on your collarbone. Lower your body down into a squat, keeping your back straight and core engaged, then push through your heels to return to standing. Keep your torso upright and control the movement throughout the entire range.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Forward Lunge
description: The barbell forward lunge is a lower-body strength exercise where you step forward and lower into a lunge while supporting a barbell across your upper back. It primarily targets the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes and hamstrings, and challenges balance and core stability. It can be loaded heavily for strength or used in moderate rep ranges for muscle development, either on its own or as part of a barbell complex or circuit.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hold a barbell across your upper back. Take a large step forward with one foot and lower your body down into a lunge, keeping your back knee almost touching the ground. Keep your front knee behind your toes and your weight in your heel, then push back up to the starting position. Alternate legs with each rep.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Box Squat
description: The Smith machine box squat is a guided squat variation performed to a box or bench using a Smith machine. The fixed bar path provides added stability, making it useful for focusing on quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings with controlled depth. It can be used with heavier loads for strength or moderate to high reps for muscle and strength endurance as part of a lower-body or full-body routine.
instructions: Stand in a Smith machine with the bar at shoulder height. Place a box or bench behind you to control the depth of the squat. Lower your body down to the box, keeping your back straight and knees in line with your toes, then push through your heels to return to standing. Keep the movement slow and controlled throughout the entire range of motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Step-Up
description: The barbell step-up is a lower-body strength exercise that targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings by stepping onto an elevated surface while supporting a barbell on the upper back. It builds unilateral leg strength, power, and stability while closely mimicking functional movements like climbing stairs.
instructions: Stand in front of a step or bench with a barbell across your upper back. Place one foot on the step and push through your heel to step up, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Bring the other foot up to meet the first foot, then step back down to the starting position with control, alternating legs with each rep. Keep your knees in line with your toes and avoid letting them extend past your toes.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Bulgarian Split Squat
description: The barbell Bulgarian split squat is a single-leg lower-body exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps while also working the glutes and hamstrings. With the rear foot elevated, you descend into a lunge position while supporting a barbell on your upper back, allowing greater loading than with dumbbells. This variation challenges balance and unilateral strength, helping address muscle imbalances between legs.
instructions: Stand with your back to a bench or step, placing one foot on the elevated surface behind you. Hold a barbell with an overhand grip across your upper back, resting it on your shoulders. Slowly lower your body into a lunge position, keeping your front knee behind your toes and your back leg straight, then push through your front heel to return to standing. Keep your core engaged and balance throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Hang Clean - Below the Knees
description: From a standing position, hold a barbell with a shoulder-width grip and lower it to just below the knees by hinging at the hips and slightly bending the knees. Explosively extend the hips and knees to drive the bar upward, then quickly drop under the bar, catching it in a front rack position with elbows high. Stand up fully to complete the rep, then lower the bar back below the knees under control for the next repetition.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with a shoulder-width grip. Lower the bar to just below the knees by hinging at the hips and slightly bending the knees. Explosively extend the hips and knees to drive the bar upward, then quickly drop under the bar to catch it in a front rack position. Stand up fully to complete the rep, controlling the bar as you lower it back down for the next repetition.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Hack Squat
description: The barbell hack squat is a deadlift-style squat variation performed with the barbell positioned behind the legs. This setup shifts emphasis to the quadriceps and glutes while also engaging the hamstrings and lower back for stability. It can be used as a free-weight alternative to the machine hack squat or as a primary lower-body strength and hypertrophy movement.

instructions: Start in a standing position with dumbbells by your sides. Drop down into a squat position and place your hands on the ground, then kick your feet back into a plank position. From the plank position, quickly return your feet to the squat position and stand up, then curl the dumbbells up to your shoulders, squeezing your biceps. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position and repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Slow Weighted Reverse Lunge
description: A controlled reverse lunge performed with dumbbells, emphasizing slow tempo to increase time under tension and build strength and stability in the quadriceps and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding dumbbells at your sides. Take a slow step back with one foot, lowering your body until your back knee almost touches the ground. Keep your front knee behind your toes and your weight in your front heel, pushing back up to the starting position at the same slow tempo. Alternate legs with each rep, maintaining control and balance throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Slow Weighted Plie Squat
description: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width apart and toes turned out. Hold a dumbbell with both hands at chest level. Keeping your torso upright and core braced, slowly bend your knees and lower your hips straight down into a deep plie squat, tracking knees over toes. Pause briefly at the bottom, then drive through your heels to return to standing, squeezing your glutes and quadriceps at the top. Move with controlled, slow tempo throughout the set.
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width apart and toes turned out. Hold a dumbbell at chest level with both hands. Slowly lower into a deep plie squat, keeping torso upright and knees over toes. Push through heels to return to standing, squeezing glutes and quadriceps at the top.

name: Holman Weighted Lunge Jump with Biceps Curl

Land softly in a lunge on the opposite leg and lower the dumbbells back down under control. Repeat, alternating legs and maintaining an upright torso and stable core throughout.

name: Holman Weighted Lunge Jump with Chest Press
description: From a split lunge stance holding dumbbells at chest level, explosively jump and switch legs in the air while pressing the weights forward, then land softly back into a lunge and repeat, alternating legs each rep.

name: Holman Weighted Half Jack to Biceps Curl
description: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Step your feet out wider than hip-width as you simultaneously raise the dumbbells to perform a biceps curl, keeping your elbows close to your torso. Step your feet back together as you lower the dumbbells under control to the starting position. Maintain a slight bend in the knees and a braced core throughout, repeating in a rhythmic, controlled half-jumping-jack motion.
instructions: Stand tall with a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Step your feet out wide and raise the dumbbells to perform a biceps curl, keeping elbows close to your torso. Step your feet back together and lower the dumbbells under control to the starting position. Maintain a slight knee bend and braced core throughout the movement.

name: Holman Weighted Half Jack to Chest Press
description: From a standing position holding dumbbells at your sides, step or hop your feet out into a half jumping jack stance while simultaneously bringing the dumbbells up to chest height. As your feet come back together, press the dumbbells straight out from your chest, then return them to the starting position and repeat in a controlled, rhythmic manner.

As your feet come back together, press the dumbbells straight out from your chest. Return the dumbbells to the starting position and repeat in a controlled, rhythmic manner.

name: Holman 180 Jump to Renegade Side Raise
description: A dynamic combination move that starts with a 180-degree jump squat and transitions into a renegade position with a dumbbell side raise, targeting the legs, core, and shoulders while challenging coordination and power.
instructions: Start in a squat position with feet shoulder-width apart. Jump up and rotate 180 degrees, landing softly on the balls of your feet. Immediately transition into a renegade position with hands on the ground and one hand holding a dumbbell. Raise the dumbbell out to the side until your arm is at shoulder height, then lower it back down and repeat on the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Holman Right Reverse Lunge Biceps Curl to Right Knee-Up Biceps Curl
description: From a standing position holding dumbbells at your sides, step your right foot back into a reverse lunge while performing a biceps curl. Drive through your left heel to stand up, bringing your right knee up toward your chest as you perform another biceps curl. Maintain an upright torso and controlled tempo throughout.
instructions: Stand with dumbbells at your sides. Step your right foot back into a reverse lunge while curling the dumbbells up toward your shoulders. name: Holman Roll-Back to Squat Thrust to Renegade Row

From the plank, perform a renegade triceps press by lifting one dumbbell off the ground and extending your arm. Alternate arms with each repetition, maintaining core stability throughout the movement.

name: Holman Weighted Narrow Squat Jump

name: Holman Diagonal Deadlift
