name: Holman Weighted Burpee to Overhead Alternating Reverse Lunge

Return to standing, then alternate legs for the next reverse lunge. Repeat the full sequence for the desired reps, maintaining control and a tight core throughout.

name: Holman Squat Thrust to Alternating Front Raise

name: Dumbbell Racked Reverse Lunge

This targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings while challenging the core and upper back to maintain an upright posture. Typically performed for moderate to high reps per leg as part of a lower-body workout.
instructions: Hold dumbbells in a racked position at your shoulders. Step backward with one foot, lowering your body until your back knee almost touches the ground. Keep your front knee at a 90-degree angle and your back straight, engaging your core for balance. Push through your front heel to return to standing, alternating legs with each rep.

name: Total Fitness Dumbbell Reverse Lunge

instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at sides. Take a large step backward with one foot, lowering body until back knee almost touches ground. Keep front knee behind toes and back knee bent at 90 degrees. Push through front heel to return to starting position.

name: Total Fitness Dumbbell Step-Up

name: Bear Crawl Sled Drag
description: The bear crawl sled drag is an advanced variation of the bear crawl that adds external resistance by dragging a sled or similar load. It can be used in short, powerful bursts or as part of longer conditioning circuits.
instructions: Start in a bear crawl position with hands and feet on the ground. Attach a sled or load to your waist or feet, ensuring a secure and comfortable connection. Crawl forward while dragging the sled, keeping your core engaged and your body low to the ground. Maintain a steady pace and control your breathing throughout the exercise.

name: Bicycling

name: Hex-Bar Deadlift
description: The hex-bar deadlift is a deadlift variation performed with a hexagonal (trap) bar that allows you to stand inside the bar and hold the handles with a neutral, palms-in grip. Because the load is aligned more closely with your center of gravity than in a conventional barbell deadlift, it typically reduces shear stress on the lower back while heavily engaging the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, along with the lower and upper back and grip.

name: Rear Leg Raises

name: Thigh Killa

Quickly return to the starting position, switching the forward foot. Repeat on the other side, alternating arms and legs to maintain balance and generate power.

This exercise is commonly used in dynamic warm-ups or fat-loss and conditioning circuits.
instructions: Stand with feet together, holding a medicine ball at chest height. Jump your feet out wide while pressing the ball overhead, extending your arms fully. Quickly return your feet to the starting position, lowering the ball to your chest or hips. Repeat the movement rhythmically, focusing on explosive power and speed.

name: Alternate Leg Diagonal Bound
description: From an athletic stance, bound forward and diagonally onto one leg, landing softly with the knee slightly bent. Immediately push off that leg to bound forward and diagonally to the opposite side, alternating legs in a rhythmic, controlled pattern. Focus on soft landings, maintaining balance, and driving explosively off the ground with each bound.
instructions: Start in an athletic stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Bound forward and diagonally onto one leg, landing softly with a slightly bent knee. Immediately push off that leg to bound to the opposite side, alternating legs in a rhythmic pattern. Focus on soft landings and explosive takeoffs to maintain balance and control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Sit Squats
description: From a seated position on a chair or bench, place your feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart. Engage your core, lean slightly forward, and press through your heels to stand up to a full upright position. Then slowly lower your hips back down to the seat with control, lightly touching before standing again. This controlled sit-to-stand motion helps build leg strength and reinforces proper squat mechanics.
instructions: Sit on a chair or bench with feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Engage your core and lean slightly forward, then press through your heels to stand up. Slowly lower your hips back down to the seat with control, lightly touching before standing again. Keep the movement slow and controlled to build leg strength and proper squat mechanics.

name: Frog Hops
description: A dynamic lower-body plyometric exercise where you squat down into a frog-like position and explosively hop forward or upward, targeting the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes and calves.
instructions: Start in a squat position with feet shoulder-width apart. Lower your body down into a deep squat, keeping your back straight and hands on the ground. From this position, explosively hop forward or upward, using your legs to propel yourself. Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately repeat the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Lateral Hop to Sprint
description: A plyometric conditioning drill where you hold light dumbbells at your sides, perform quick lateral hops, then immediately transition into a short weighted sprint. This exercise challenges lower-body power, core stability, and shoulder endurance while elevating heart rate for fat-loss or athletic conditioning.
instructions: Hold light dumbbells at your sides with palms facing thighs. Perform quick lateral hops from side to side, keeping feet together and knees slightly bent. Immediately transition into a short sprint, driving your arms and legs forward while maintaining control. Land softly on the balls of your feet to absorb the impact and repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Smith Machine Lunge Sprint
description: A lower-body power exercise performed in a Smith machine, alternating explosive lunge jumps with the bar on the upper back. The fixed bar path reduces balance demands while allowing you to load the movement more heavily than bodyweight jump lunges.
instructions: Stand in a Smith machine with the bar on your upper back. Position your feet shoulder-width apart, then step forward with one foot into a lunge position. Lower your body, then explosively jump up, switching your feet to the other lunge position. Repeat, alternating legs quickly to mimic a sprinting motion.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Sissy Squat
description: The sissy squat is a bodyweight squat variation where you lean back and allow the knees to travel forward over the toes, placing a strong stretch and load on the quadriceps. It is typically performed with bodyweight and can be done in partial or full range of motion depending on knee comfort and control.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Lean back slightly, allowing knees to extend forward over toes. Lower body down into a squat, keeping back straight and knees in line with toes, then push through heels to return to standing position. Keep core engaged and control the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Wall Ball Toss
description: The wall ball toss is an explosive full-body exercise using a medicine ball. From a squat position, you drive through the legs and hips while pressing the ball upward to a target on a wall, then catch it as it returns and flow directly into the next squat. Common in CrossFit, it can be performed for time or for repetitions to build power, conditioning, and coordination.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, facing a wall with a target. Hold a medicine ball at chest level, then lower into a squat position. Explosively drive through your legs and hips to press the ball upward to the target, catching it as it returns. Immediately flow into the next squat, repeating the motion for desired repetitions or time.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Bosu Ball Squat
description: Stand on the flat side of a BOSU or similar unstable surface with feet about shoulder-width apart, brace your core, and lower into a squat by bending your hips and knees while keeping your chest up. Push through your heels to return to standing, maintaining balance and control throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand on the flat side of a BOSU with feet shoulder-width apart. Brace your core and lower into a squat by bending your hips and knees, keeping your chest up. Push through your heels to return to standing, maintaining balance and control. Keep your movements slow and controlled to ensure stability throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Exercise Ball Bulgarian Split Squat
description: A single-leg squat variation where the rear shin and foot rest on an exercise ball while the front leg performs a squat, emphasizing the quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes with a significant balance challenge.
instructions: Stand with an exercise ball behind you. Place the top of your rear foot on the ball, with your leg straight, and step forward with your front foot. Lower your body into a squat, keeping your back straight and front knee behind your toes, then push back up to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and balance throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Internal Rotation with Band
description: Stand sideways to a fixed anchor with a resistance band attached at elbow height. Hold the band in the hand closest to the anchor, elbow bent to 90 degrees and tucked to your side. Starting with your forearm pointing out to the side, rotate your arm inward across your body while keeping your elbow pinned to your torso. Control the band as you return to the starting position and repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides.
instructions: Stand sideways to a fixed anchor with a resistance band at elbow height. Hold the band with the hand closest to the anchor, keeping your elbow bent and tucked in. Rotate your forearm inward across your body, keeping your elbow pinned to your torso. Return to the starting position, controlling the band, and repeat on the other side.

name: Band Pull Apart
description: The band pull-apart is an upper-back and rear-shoulder exercise using a resistance band to create constant tension, helping activate the traps, rear delts, and upper back before upper-body lifting.
instructions: Hold a resistance band with both hands at shoulder width. Pull the band apart, keeping arms straight, to stretch the band across your chest. Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together as you pull the band apart. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the band.

name: Back Flyes with Bands
description: Stand with a resistance band anchored in front of you at chest height, holding one end in each hand with arms extended slightly in front. With a slight bend in your elbows and stable posture, pull your arms out and back in a wide arc until your hands are in line with your shoulders, squeezing your upper back and rear shoulders. Control the band as you return to the starting position and repeat for the desired reps.
instructions: Stand with a resistance band anchored at chest height, holding one end in each hand with arms slightly extended. Keep a slight bend in your elbows and maintain stable posture. Pull your arms out and back in a wide arc until hands are in line with shoulders, squeezing your upper back. Control the band as you return to the starting position.

name: Lateral Raise with Bands
description: Stand on a resistance band and hold the handles at your sides with a slight bend in your elbows. Keeping your core braced and shoulders down, raise your arms out to the sides until they reach shoulder height, then lower under control. This targets the shoulder muscles with constant band tension.
instructions: Stand on a resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the band handles at your sides with a slight bend in your elbows. Raise your arms out to the sides until they reach shoulder height, keeping your core braced and shoulders down. Lower your arms back down to the starting position under control.

name: Shoulder Press with Resistance Bands
description: Stand on the middle of a resistance band with feet about shoulder-width apart, holding one end of the band in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward. Brace your core and press your hands straight up overhead until your arms are fully extended, keeping your wrists stacked over your shoulders. Lower back down under control to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
instructions: Stand on the middle of a resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold one end of the band in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward. Press your hands straight up overhead until arms are fully extended, keeping wrists stacked over shoulders. Lower back down to the starting position under control.

name: External Rotation with Band
description: Stand with a resistance band anchored at elbow height and your working elbow bent to 90 degrees, tucked close to your side. Hold the band with your forearm across your body and rotate your forearm outward, keeping your elbow pinned to your side and shoulder down and back. Control the band as you return to the start position and repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides.
instructions: Stand with a resistance band at elbow height and bend your working elbow to 90 degrees, keeping it close to your side. Hold the band with your forearm across your body. Rotate your forearm outward, keeping your elbow pinned and shoulder down. Control the band as you return to the start position.

name: Rusin Banded Over-And-Back
description: Stand tall holding a resistance band with a wide, overhand grip in front of your thighs. Keeping your elbows straight and ribs down, raise the band up overhead and continue moving it behind your body until it reaches your lower back or as far as comfortable, then reverse the motion to return to the start. Move slowly and under control, focusing on opening the shoulders and upper back without arching the lower back.
instructions: Stand tall with a resistance band held in front of your thighs, using a wide overhand grip. Raise the band up overhead, keeping your elbows straight and ribs down. Move the band behind your body to your lower back, then reverse the motion to return to the starting position, focusing on slow and controlled movement. Keep your core engaged to maintain a neutral spine throughout the exercise.

name: Rusin Banded Face Pull
description: Attach a resistance band to a stable anchor at upper-chest or face height. Grip the band with both hands using an overhand or neutral grip, step back to create tension, and stand tall with a slight bend in the knees. Pull the band toward your face by driving your elbows out and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together while keeping your shoulders down. Pause briefly, then slowly return to the starting position under control. Focus on using the rear shoulders and upper back rather than your arms.
instructions: Attach a resistance band to a stable anchor at upper-chest height. Grip the band with both hands and step back to create tension, standing tall with a slight knee bend. Pull the band toward your face by driving your elbows out and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together. Return to the starting position slowly, keeping control and focusing on using your rear shoulders and upper back.

name: Jordan Shallow Band-Resisted Thread the Needle
description: A band-resisted rotational shoulder and upper-back drill performed on the floor in a quadruped position. The band adds light resistance as you thread one arm under the body and rotate, promoting shoulder stability, thoracic rotation, and control through the mid-back.
instructions: Start in a quadruped position on the floor with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Loop a resistance band around one hand and anchor it to a stable object. Thread the banded arm under your body, rotating your torso and keeping your hips stable. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement and repeating on the other side.

name: Seated Banded Barbell Shoulder Press
description: Sit upright on a bench with a barbell held at shoulder height and resistance bands anchored under the bench or your feet and looped around the bar. Brace your core, keep your back tall, and press the barbell straight overhead until your arms are extended without locking out. Lower the bar under control back to shoulder level and repeat for the desired reps, maintaining tension in the bands throughout.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with a barbell at shoulder height. Keep your core braced and back tall, then press the barbell straight overhead until your arms are extended. Lower the bar under control back to shoulder level, maintaining tension in the bands. Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding locked-out joints.

name: Band Shoulder Press
description: The band shoulder press is a shoulder-strengthening exercise using resistance bands as an alternative to the dumbbell or barbell shoulder press. The elastic resistance provides dynamic tension throughout the range of motion, challenging the deltoids while requiring the core to stabilize the body. It can be performed for lower reps to build strength or higher reps for muscle endurance and conditioning, and is often used as a main shoulder movement or as an accessory to pressing exercises.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a resistance band in both hands at shoulder height. Press the band straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the band back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the range of motion. Keep your core engaged to maintain stability and proper form.

name: Band Lateral Raise
description: The band lateral raise is a shoulder exercise that targets the middle head of the deltoids using resistance bands. Performed standing, you step on the band and raise your arms out to the sides until they reach shoulder height, then lower under control. The band provides constant, increasing tension through the range of motion, making it ideal for warm-ups, activation, or accessory shoulder work in the 8–12+ rep range.
instructions: Stand on the middle of the resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the ends of the band in each hand at your sides. Raise your arms out to the sides until they reach shoulder height, keeping elbows straight. Lower your arms back down to the starting position under control.

name: Band Upright Row
description: The band upright row is a shoulder and trap exercise using resistance bands to provide dynamic tension through the full range of motion. It is typically performed standing by stepping on the band, gripping it with both hands, and pulling upward toward the chest or collarbone while keeping the elbows higher than the wrists. This variation is often easier on the shoulders than a barbell upright row and is commonly used for moderate to high repetitions to build muscle and endurance.
instructions: Stand on the resistance band with feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the band with both hands, keeping palms facing your body. Pull the band upward toward your chest, keeping elbows higher than wrists. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the band.

name: Staggered-Stance Band Shoulder Press
description: A resistance band overhead press performed in a staggered stance to target the shoulders while engaging the core for stability. The staggered stance reduces lower-back strain compared to a parallel stance and helps maintain balance as you press the band overhead for controlled reps.
instructions: Stand in a staggered stance with one foot forward and one foot back. Hold a resistance band in both hands at shoulder height, with the band at chest level. Press the band straight up over your head, extending your arms fully and engaging your core for stability. Lower the band back to the starting position, controlling the movement to complete one rep.

name: Single-arm banded landmine press
description: The single-arm banded landmine press is an upper-body pressing exercise performed with a barbell anchored in a landmine and a resistance band for added tension. It primarily targets the shoulders while also engaging the triceps, upper chest, and core for stability. This movement is typically done in moderate rep ranges to build strength and muscle in the deltoids.
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with the barbell at shoulder height. Hold the barbell with one hand, engaging your core for stability. Press the barbell away from your body, extending your arm fully, then control the return to the starting position. Repeat on the other side to ensure balanced development.

name: Band Front Raise Rear Fly Row Combo
description: A continuous-loop band exercise that combines a front raise, rear fly, and row to target all heads of the shoulders while also engaging the arms, chest, back, and core. Performed in sequence without rest, it provides constant tension for a time-efficient shoulder burnout or accessory movement on upper-body days.
instructions: Hold a continuous-loop band in both hands at shoulder height with palms facing down. Perform a front raise by lifting the band upwards, keeping arms straight, then immediately move into a rear fly by lifting the band out to the sides and back. Next, bend elbows to pull the band towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together in a rowing motion. Return to the starting position and repeat the sequence without rest.

name: Jordan Shallow Foam Roller Thoracic Extension
description: Lie on a foam roller placed horizontally under your upper back (thoracic spine), knees bent and feet flat. Support your head lightly with your hands, keep your hips down, and gently extend your upper back over the roller, pausing briefly before returning to neutral. Move the roller slightly up or down the thoracic spine to mobilize different segments while keeping the motion controlled and pain-free.
instructions: Lie on a foam roller under your upper back with knees bent and feet flat. Support your head lightly with your hands and keep your hips down. Gently extend your upper back over the roller, pausing briefly, then return to neutral. Move the roller up or down your thoracic spine to mobilize different segments, keeping the motion controlled and pain-free.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Jordan Shallow Thread the Needle (with Foam Roller)
description: From a quadruped position with a foam roller under one arm, reach the working arm under your torso, gently rolling the foam roller forward as you rotate your upper back and shoulder toward the floor. Pause briefly at the end range, feeling a stretch through the shoulder and upper back, then reverse the motion to return to the starting position. Repeat for controlled reps, then switch sides.
instructions: Start in a quadruped position with a foam roller under one arm. Reach the working arm under your torso, rolling the foam roller forward as you rotate your upper back and shoulder toward the floor. Pause briefly at the end range, feeling the stretch, then reverse the motion to return to the starting position. Repeat on the other side to maintain balanced mobility.

name: Clean and Press
description: The clean and press is a full-body barbell lift combining a floor-to-shoulder clean with an overhead press. The bar is pulled from the floor through three phases to the front rack position, then driven overhead to full lockout. It develops total-body power, strength, and coordination and can be programmed for low reps for maximal strength or moderate to higher reps for conditioning.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart. Lift the bar from the floor to the front rack position, keeping your back straight and core engaged. From the front rack, drive the bar upwards to full lockout overhead, extending your arms and standing tall. Lower the bar back to the front rack and then to the floor, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Clean and Jerk
description: The clean and jerk is a full-body Olympic weightlifting movement performed in two phases: first the clean, where the barbell is pulled explosively from the floor and received in the front rack position, then the jerk, where the bar is driven overhead to a locked-out position. Typically done for low repetitions with a focus on power, speed, and precise technique, it develops total-body strength, coordination, and athleticism.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart. Explosively pull the bar up to the front rack position, receiving it with elbows high and core engaged. From the front rack, drive the bar upwards, extending the hips and legs to propel it overhead to a locked-out position. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, maintaining control throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Military Press
description: The military press is a compound barbell overhead press that primarily targets the shoulders to build size and strength. Performed standing with the barbell pressed from the upper chest to full lockout overhead, it can be trained with heavy loads for strength or moderate reps for muscle growth as part of full-body or upper-body routines.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the barbell at shoulder height. Press the barbell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the barbell back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated Barbell Shoulder Press
description: The seated barbell shoulder press is a classic overhead pressing exercise that targets the shoulders while minimizing lower-body involvement. Performed seated, it emphasizes the deltoids and triceps, helping build upper-body strength and muscle. The seated position also reduces assistance from the legs and can decrease the tendency to overextend the lower back during the press.
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support, holding a barbell at shoulder height. Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Press the barbell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully, then lower it back to the starting position in a controlled manner. Keep your core engaged and avoid arching your back throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Snatch-Grip Behind-The-Neck Overhead Press
description: A barbell overhead press performed from behind the neck using a wide snatch grip, emphasizing the shoulders and upper back while improving overhead stability and mobility.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a barbell with a wide snatch grip behind your neck, resting on your traps. Press the barbell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully and squeezing your shoulders. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Standing Bradford Press
description: The standing Bradford press is a shoulder-focused barbell exercise that alternates pressing the bar in front of the head and behind the neck without fully locking out the elbows. It can be used as a warm-up for other pressing variations or as a primary muscle-building movement for the shoulders, typically performed for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell at shoulder height. Press the bar forward, extending arms but not fully locking out elbows, then lower it behind your neck. Alternate pressing the bar in front of your head and behind your neck, keeping control throughout the movement. Keep your core engaged and back straight to maintain proper form.

name: Barbell Shoulder Press
description: The barbell shoulder press is a classic strength exercise performed seated or standing to build and strengthen the shoulders, particularly the front delts, while also heavily engaging the triceps to press the barbell overhead.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, palms facing forward. Press the barbell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully, then lower it back down to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and back straight throughout the exercise.

name: Barbell Rear Delt Bent-Over Row
description: The barbell rear delt bent-over row is a strength exercise that targets the rear shoulders and upper back. Using a lighter barbell than a traditional bent-over row, you hinge at the hips, keep your back flat, and row the bar out and slightly away from the body to emphasize the rear delts and upper back. It is typically performed for moderate to higher repetitions, such as 8-15 per set, within a back or upper-body workout.
instructions: Hinge at the hips with a flat back and hold a lighter barbell with an overhand grip. Bend your knees slightly and lean forward, keeping your core engaged. Row the bar out and slightly away from your body, targeting your rear delts and upper back. Lower the bar back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Barbell Upright Row
description: The barbell upright row is a strength exercise that targets the traps and shoulders by pulling a barbell vertically from hip level toward the upper chest, helping build size and strength in the upper back and shoulder region.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Hold the barbell at hip level with palms facing the body. Lift the barbell straight up to upper chest level, keeping elbows higher than the shoulders. Lower the barbell back to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Bradford/Rocky Presses
description: A shoulder pressing variation where the barbell is moved in a short arc from just in front of the head to just behind it without fully locking out the elbows, keeping constant tension on the shoulders.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell at shoulder height. Press the barbell forward and upward in a short arc, keeping elbows slightly bent. Move the barbell from just in front of your head to just behind it, maintaining constant tension on your shoulders. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Lying Rear Delt Fly
description: The lying rear delt fly is an upper-body isolation exercise performed face down on a bench to target the rear deltoids. Lying prone minimizes body momentum, increasing tension on the shoulders throughout the movement. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8-12 or more, within upper-body or shoulder-focused workouts.
instructions: Lie face down on a flat bench with arms extended to sides. Hold dumbbells with palms facing each other. Lift arms out to sides until they are at a 45-degree angle to your body, squeezing rear deltoids. Lower arms back to starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Machine Shoulder (Military) Press
description: The machine shoulder press is an upper body strength exercise that primarily targets the deltoids by pressing weight overhead along a guided path.
instructions: Sit at the machine with feet on the floor and grip the handles at shoulder height. Press the weight straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the weight back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Keep your core engaged and avoid arching your back during the exercise.

name: Standing Barbell Press Behind Neck
description: The standing barbell press behind the neck is a shoulder-focused strength exercise performed standing with a barbell lowered behind the head and pressed overhead. It primarily targets the shoulders while also engaging the traps and triceps. Due to the shoulder position, it may not be suitable for individuals with existing or previous shoulder issues.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Lower the barbell behind your head, keeping your elbows close to your ears. Press the barbell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully and squeezing your shoulders. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid injury.

name: Barbell Front Raise
description: The barbell front raise is an upper-body isolation exercise that targets the front of the shoulders. Performed standing with a barbell held in front of the thighs, the weight is lifted to shoulder height with controlled form, typically for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with hands shoulder-width apart. Hold the barbell in front of your thighs with palms facing your thighs. Lift the barbell straight up to shoulder height, keeping your elbows slightly bent and core engaged. Lower the barbell back to the starting position with control.

name: Overhead Bar Front Raise
description: The overhead bar front raise is a shoulder and upper-trap isolation exercise performed by lifting a barbell from thigh level up in front of the body to an overhead position with controlled form. It emphasizes the anterior (front) deltoids and upper trapezius, and is typically used with moderate weight for higher repetitions (8–12+ reps) on upper-body or shoulder-focused training days.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell at thigh level with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Lift the barbell straight up in front of you, keeping arms straight, until it reaches overhead position. Slowly lower the barbell back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Keep core engaged and avoid swinging or jerking the weight.

name: Incline Barbell Shoulder Protraction
description: The incline barbell shoulder protraction is an upper-body strength and mobility exercise focused on the scapula and serratus anterior. Performed lying face down on an incline bench, you extend your arms with a barbell and actively protract (push forward) the shoulders without bending the elbows. This helps improve shoulder stability, pressing strength, and overall shoulder health. It is typically done with light weight for higher repetitions.
instructions: Lie face down on an incline bench with your chest against the pad. Grasp a barbell with your arms extended and hands shoulder-width apart. Push the barbell forward, protracting your shoulders without bending your elbows, then slowly return to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and control the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Behind-the-head Push-Press
description: The behind-the-head push-press is an overhead barbell press variation performed from a back rack position. A shallow dip and drive with the legs helps accelerate the bar overhead, emphasizing explosive power and shoulder strength. It is commonly used in Olympic weightlifting training, often for low repetitions to focus on power, timing, and movement quality.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hold a barbell in a back rack position. Lower your body slightly with a shallow dip, then explosively drive upward with your legs. As you extend your legs, press the barbell straight up over your head, releasing it from your shoulders and extending your arms fully. Control the barbell as you lower it back to the starting position behind your head.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Rack Delivery
description: From a standing position with the barbell in your hands, practice moving the bar smoothly into the front rack position on your shoulders, focusing on keeping your elbows high, core braced, and wrists relaxed while maintaining control throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Move the bar up to your shoulders, keeping elbows high and core braced. Focus on relaxing your wrists and maintaining control as you guide the bar into the front rack position. Keep your back straight and engage your core to support the weight.

name: Smith Incline Shoulder Raise
description: A shoulder isolation exercise performed on a Smith machine with the bench set to an incline, targeting the front and middle deltoids by raising the bar along a fixed path.
instructions: Sit on the incline bench with your back against the pad. Grasp the Smith machine bar with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Raise the bar straight up, keeping your arms straight, until your arms are at a 45-degree angle to your body. Lower the bar back down to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Tall Muscle Snatch
description: Stand tall holding a barbell with a wide snatch grip. Without using your legs to dip or drive, explosively shrug and pull the bar upward, keeping it close to your body. Rotate your elbows under and punch the bar overhead to a locked-out position while remaining fully upright, then lower under control. Focus on an aggressive upper-body pull and smooth turnover without using leg drive.
instructions: Stand tall with a wide snatch grip on the barbell. Explosively shrug and pull the bar upward, keeping it close to your body. Rotate your elbows under and punch the bar overhead to a locked-out position, remaining fully upright. Lower the bar under control to the starting position.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Axle Clean And Press
description: A full-body strongman movement where you lift an axle or barbell from the floor to the shoulders (clean) and then press it overhead, emphasizing shoulder strength, power, and coordination.
instructions: Stand over the axle with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the hips and knees to grip the axle with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Explosively lift the axle to shoulder height, then press it straight up over your head. Lower the axle back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Jerk Balance
description: From a split stance with the barbell in the front rack position, dip slightly and drive the bar overhead while stepping the front foot forward into a deeper split, focusing on balance, timing, and receiving position for the jerk.
instructions: Stand in a split stance with the barbell in the front rack position. Dip slightly and drive the bar overhead, immediately stepping the front foot forward into a deeper split. Keep your core engaged and focus on balance as you receive the bar overhead. Control your movement to maintain stability and proper form.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Seated EZ-bar Shrug
description: The seated EZ-bar shrug targets the trapezius muscles by elevating the shoulders while seated, helping build upper trap size and strength with controlled, moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with your feet on the floor. Hold an EZ-bar with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Lift the EZ-bar by shrugging your shoulders, keeping your arms straight, and hold for a brief moment. Lower the EZ-bar back to the starting position, squeezing your shoulder blades together.

name: Single-arm Barbell Lateral Raise
description: The single-arm barbell lateral raise is an isolation shoulder exercise that emphasizes the lateral deltoids while challenging stability due to the uneven load of the barbell. Performed one arm at a time, it is typically done for moderate to high repetitions (8–15 per set) within a shoulder-focused strength workout.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell in one hand at your side. Lift the barbell out to the side until it's at shoulder height, keeping your arm straight. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Switch arms to complete the exercise on both sides.

name: AM Upright Row
description: The barbell upright row is a strength exercise that targets the traps and shoulders by pulling a barbell vertically from hip level to upper chest, helping build bigger and stronger upper back and shoulder muscles.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Keep your back straight and core engaged, lifting the barbell to upper chest level. Pull the barbell upwards, keeping elbows higher than the bar, and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Standing Barbell Shoulder Press
description: The standing barbell shoulder press is a compound movement used to build size and strength in the shoulders. It can be trained as a strength lift or in traditional muscle-building rep ranges as part of full-body, shoulder-focused, or upper-body training.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Lift the barbell to shoulder height, resting it on the front of your shoulders. Press the barbell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm Incline Cable Cross-over
description: The single-arm incline cable cross-over is an isolation chest exercise performed on an incline bench using a cable stack. Working one side at a time emphasizes the upper chest, helps correct side-to-side imbalances, and allows you to focus on a strong peak contraction through the full range of motion. It is typically used in upper-body or chest-focused hypertrophy workouts, often paired with presses or fly variations to hit the chest from multiple angles.
instructions: Sit on an incline bench facing a cable stack. Grasp the cable handle with one hand at shoulder height. Pull the handle down and across your body, extending your arm fully and squeezing your chest. Return to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid jerking the cable.

name: Barbell Bear Complex
description: The barbell bear complex is a full-body barbell sequence combining a power clean, front squat, push press, back squat, and a second push press from behind the neck. It develops total-body strength, power, and conditioning when performed for multiple rounds with controlled rest.
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet shoulder-width apart. Perform a power clean to bring the barbell to your shoulders, then immediately lower into a front squat. From the front squat, push press the barbell overhead, then lower it behind your neck and perform a back squat. Finally, push press the barbell overhead again from behind your neck to complete the complex.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Incline Face-Up Straight-Bar Front Raise
description: Lying face-up on an incline bench, hold a barbell with straight arms hanging toward the floor and raise it in an arc until your arms are perpendicular to your torso, targeting the front of the shoulders. Lower under control to the start position and repeat.
instructions: Lie face-up on an incline bench. Hold a barbell with straight arms hanging toward the floor. Raise the barbell in an arc until your arms are perpendicular to your torso, squeezing your shoulder muscles. Lower the barbell under control to the starting position.

name: Standing Behind-The-Head Shoulder Press
description: Press a barbell overhead from behind the head while standing, targeting the shoulders with an emphasis on the medial and posterior delts. Maintain a tight core and stable stance throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Position the barbell behind your head at shoulder height. Press the barbell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully and squeezing your shoulder muscles. Lower the barbell back to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement.

name: Single-arm kettlebell push-press
description: The single-arm kettlebell push-press combines a lower-body dip and drive with an overhead press using one arm. Each set begins with a clean to bring the kettlebell to the rack position, then you use leg drive to help press the weight overhead, lowering it back under control. It can be used for strength, muscle-building, or conditioning, and often appears in circuits or kettlebell complexes.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and clean the kettlebell to the rack position. Lower your body slightly by bending your knees, then drive through your legs to help press the kettlebell overhead with one arm. Extend your arm fully and briefly pause at the top of the movement. Lower the kettlebell back to the rack position under control, ready to repeat with the same arm or switch to the other arm.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Military Press
description: Stand tall holding a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward and core braced. Press both kettlebells overhead until your arms are fully extended without arching your lower back, then lower them under control back to shoulder level and repeat.
instructions: Stand tall with a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height, palms facing forward, and core braced. Press both kettlebells straight up over your head until your arms are fully extended. Avoid arching your lower back during the press. Lower the kettlebells back to shoulder level under control and repeat.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Alternating Kettlebell Press
description: Press one kettlebell overhead at a time while keeping the other at shoulder level, alternating sides to build shoulder strength and stability.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder level. Press one kettlebell straight up over your shoulder, extending your arm fully. Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position and immediately press the other kettlebell overhead, alternating sides with each rep. Keep your core engaged and maintain control throughout the exercise.

name: Single-arm Kettlebell Snatch
description: The single-arm kettlebell snatch is a dynamic full-body movement where a kettlebell is driven from between the legs or from the floor to an overhead lockout in one explosive motion. It develops power, strength, and conditioning, particularly in the shoulders, hips, and posterior chain.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, kettlebell on ground between your feet. Hinge hips and grab kettlebell with one hand, keeping back straight and core engaged. Explosively lift kettlebell, keeping arm straight, and bring to overhead lockout position. Lower kettlebell back to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Single-arm kettlebell clean and jerk
description: The single-arm kettlebell clean and jerk is a compound lift where the kettlebell is cleaned from the floor or swing position to the rack, then driven overhead with a dip under the weight before standing to lockout. It can be used for strength, muscle-building, or conditioning, and is often performed for higher-rep sets or in complexes.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, kettlebell on the ground between your feet. Hinge at the hips to grab the kettlebell with one hand, swinging it back between your legs. Quickly swing the kettlebell up to rack position, then dip under it to drive it overhead, locking out your arm. Stand up straight to complete the lift, controlling the kettlebell throughout the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Pirate Ship
description: A rotational kettlebell exercise where the weight is raised in an arc to the side of the body in both directions, emphasizing controlled shoulder movement and core engagement, particularly the obliques.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell with both hands, standing with feet shoulder-width apart. Swing the kettlebell to one side, raising it in an arc to chest level, keeping your core engaged and shoulders controlled. Rotate your torso to follow the kettlebell, then slowly lower it back down to the starting position. Repeat on the other side, maintaining a smooth and controlled motion throughout the exercise.

name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Clean
description: A full-body power exercise where you swing two kettlebells from between your legs up to the rack position at shoulder height, using hip drive and a quick pull to guide the bells smoothly to your shoulders.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward. Hold a kettlebell in each hand with palms facing your body, hanging down between your legs. Hinge your hips and swing the kettlebells back between your legs, then explosively drive your hips forward to generate power and pull the kettlebells up to the rack position at shoulder height. Keep your back straight and core engaged as you guide the kettlebells to your shoulders.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Double Kettlebell Push Press
description: The double kettlebell push press combines a powerful leg drive with an overhead press using two kettlebells. After cleaning both kettlebells to the rack position, you dip slightly at the knees and hips, then drive through the legs to help press the weights overhead. This allows you to handle heavier loads than a strict press and can be used for strength, muscle-building, or conditioning in circuits and complexes.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height. Dip slightly at the knees and hips, then drive through the legs to generate power. Press the kettlebells straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the kettlebells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Seated Press
description: A seated overhead press using a kettlebell to build shoulder strength while reducing lower-body involvement and helping stabilize the core.
instructions: Sit on a flat surface with feet planted firmly on the ground. Hold a kettlebell with both hands at shoulder height. Press the kettlebell straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging.

name: Double Kettlebell Jerk
description: From a front rack position with a kettlebell in each hand, dip slightly through the knees and hips, then explosively drive the weights overhead using leg drive and a quick drop under the bells to lock out the arms before standing tall.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height. Dip slightly through the knees and hips to load your legs. Explosively drive the kettlebells overhead, using leg power and quickly dropping under the bells to lock out your arms. Stand tall, squeezing your legs and glutes to complete the movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Arnold Press
description: A shoulder press variation performed with a kettlebell where you start with palms facing you at shoulder height and rotate the wrists outward while pressing overhead, targeting all heads of the deltoids.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell at shoulder height with palms facing you. Press the kettlebell straight up while rotating your wrists outward so palms face forward at the top. Keep your core engaged and avoid arching your back. Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position with control, rotating your wrists back to the original position.

name: Double-Kettlebell Windmill
description: The double-kettlebell windmill is an advanced core and shoulder stability exercise where one kettlebell is held overhead and the other is held below the torso while hinging at the hips. It emphasizes controlled rotation, shoulder stability, and hip mobility rather than speed or conditioning, and is best performed with focus and without fatigue.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell in each hand, with one arm extended overhead and the other arm extended down towards the ground. Hinge at the hips, keeping your back straight and core engaged. Rotate your torso, keeping your arms straight, to one side and then the other, focusing on controlled movement and stability. Keep your eyes on the kettlebell overhead and move slowly to maintain balance and control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Split Snatch
description: From a standing position, swing a kettlebell between your legs with one arm, then explosively drive through your hips to pull the kettlebell upward and punch it overhead while simultaneously moving your feet into a split stance. Stabilize the kettlebell overhead with a locked-out arm, then carefully return it to the starting position and repeat for the desired reps before switching sides.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and hold a kettlebell with one arm. Swing the kettlebell between your legs, then explosively drive through your hips to pull it upward. Punch the kettlebell overhead, moving into a split stance, and stabilize it with a locked-out arm. Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position and repeat on the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Para Press
description: A unilateral overhead press performed with one kettlebell to build shoulder strength, stability, and control while engaging the core for support.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a kettlebell in one hand at shoulder height with elbow bent. Press the kettlebell straight up over your shoulder, extending your arm fully. Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Split Jerk
description: From a front-rack position with one kettlebell, dip slightly through the knees, then drive explosively through the legs and hips to press the kettlebell overhead while splitting the legs into a lunge stance. Lock the arm out overhead, stabilize, then bring the feet back together and lower the kettlebell to the rack position under control.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell in one hand at shoulder height with your arm bent. Dip your knees slightly, then explosively drive through your legs and hips to press the kettlebell overhead. Split your legs into a lunge stance as you lock your arm out overhead. Bring your feet back together and lower the kettlebell to the starting position under control.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Military Press To The Side
description: Stand tall holding a kettlebell in one hand at shoulder height with your palm facing forward and wrist stacked over the elbow. Brace your core, keep your ribs down, and press the kettlebell straight overhead until your arm is fully extended without leaning back or shrugging excessively. Lower the kettlebell under control to the starting position and repeat for reps before switching sides.
instructions: Stand tall with a kettlebell in one hand at shoulder height, palm facing forward. Brace your core and press the kettlebell straight overhead until your arm is fully extended. Lower the kettlebell under control to the starting position. Repeat for reps on one side before switching to the other side.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Jerk
description: From a standing position, hold a kettlebell in one hand at shoulder height with your elbow close to your body. Dip slightly by bending your knees, then explosively drive through your legs and hips to help press the kettlebell overhead, locking out your elbow. Briefly stabilize at the top, then lower the kettlebell back to the starting rack position under control and repeat for reps before switching arms.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hold a kettlebell in one hand at shoulder height. Dip slightly by bending your knees, then explosively drive through your legs and hips to press the kettlebell overhead. Lock out your elbow and briefly stabilize at the top. Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position under control and repeat for reps before switching arms.

name: Double Kettlebell Snatch
description: An explosive full-body movement where you swing two kettlebells from between your legs up overhead in one fluid motion, finishing with the bells locked out above your shoulders.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand with palms facing your body. Swing the kettlebells back between your legs, then explosively drive your hips forward to generate power. Quickly lift the kettlebells up to shoulder height, then press them overhead, locking out your arms. Keep your core engaged and control the kettlebells throughout the entire movement.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Jerk
description: From a front-rack position, dip slightly through the knees and hips, then drive the kettlebells overhead using leg power and finish by locking out the shoulders and elbows before lowering back to the rack.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height. Dip slightly through the knees and hips, then explosively drive the kettlebells upwards using leg power. Extend the shoulders and elbows to lock out the kettlebells overhead. Lower the kettlebells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Kettlebell Upright Row
description: The kettlebell upright row is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the traps and shoulders by pulling a kettlebell vertically from hip level toward the chest. It is typically performed with light to moderate weight for higher repetitions as an accessory movement in shoulder or upper-body workouts and may not be suitable for individuals with existing shoulder issues.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a kettlebell with both hands at hip level, palms facing body. Lift the kettlebell straight up to chest level, keeping elbows close to body and core engaged. Lower the kettlebell back to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Kettlebell Upright Row and Press
description: A compound kettlebell movement combining an upright row into an overhead press to build shoulder strength and upper-body conditioning.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell with both hands at thigh level. Perform an upright row by lifting the kettlebell to chest height, keeping elbows close to body. From the row position, press the kettlebell straight up over head. Lower the kettlebell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Single-arm Kettlebell Thruster
description: The single-arm kettlebell thruster combines a front squat and an overhead press using one kettlebell. Starting from a clean into the rack position, you perform a full squat and then drive the kettlebell overhead in one powerful motion. This compound movement can be used for strength, muscle-building, or conditioning, and challenges the entire body with an emphasis on the shoulders and legs.
instructions: Start by cleaning the kettlebell into the rack position. Lower your body into a full squat, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes. Drive through your heels to return to standing, simultaneously pressing the kettlebell overhead with a straight arm. Control the kettlebell as you lower it back to the rack position to complete the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Double-Kettlebell Shoulder Press
description: The double-kettlebell shoulder press is a strength-focused overhead pressing exercise where two kettlebells are cleaned to the rack position and then pressed overhead. It can be performed for low-rep strength work or moderate-rep muscle-building sets, and is also common in circuit-style or complex-based training.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Clean two kettlebells to the rack position, with one kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height. Press the kettlebells straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the kettlebells back to the rack position, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Double-Kettlebell Push-Press
description: The double-kettlebell push-press is a compound overhead pressing exercise where two kettlebells are cleaned to the shoulders and then driven overhead using a powerful leg drive followed by an arm press. It targets the shoulders and upper body while engaging the legs and core to assist with power and stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand. Clean the kettlebells to your shoulders, keeping your core engaged and back straight. Drive through your legs to generate power, then press the kettlebells straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the kettlebells back to your shoulders, controlling the movement throughout.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Dumbbell Front Raise to Lateral Raise
description: A shoulder-focused dumbbell complex combining a front raise and a lateral raise in one sequence. Perform a controlled front raise to shoulder height, lower the dumbbells, then perform a lateral raise to shoulder height. This movement emphasizes the anterior and middle deltoids, making it an efficient accessory or burnout exercise on upper-body days.
instructions: Hold dumbbells at your sides with palms facing your thighs. Raise the dumbbells forward to shoulder height, then lower them back down. Immediately raise the dumbbells out to the sides until they are at shoulder height again. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the entire sequence.

name: Single-arm Palm-in Dumbbell Shoulder Press
description: A unilateral overhead press performed with a neutral grip, pressing one dumbbell overhead at a time to build shoulder strength and stability. Can be done standing or seated and trained with moderate to heavy loads in typical hypertrophy rep ranges.
instructions: Stand or sit with good posture. Hold a dumbbell in one hand with a neutral grip, palm facing your body. Press the dumbbell straight up over your shoulder, extending your arm fully. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Standing Palms-In Shoulder Press
description: The standing palms-in shoulder press is a dumbbell overhead pressing exercise that targets the shoulders with a neutral (palms-in) grip. It can be used with heavier loads for strength or higher reps for muscle-building and conditioning.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at shoulder height with palms facing each other. Press the dumbbells straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid swinging or jerking. Keep your core engaged and maintain good posture throughout the exercise.

name: Seated Dumbbell Press
description: The seated dumbbell press is a shoulder-strengthening exercise performed while seated, pressing dumbbells overhead to build muscle and strength in the shoulders with assistance from the triceps.
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with feet planted firmly on the ground. Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward. Press the dumbbells straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement to avoid injury.

name: Standing Dumbbell Shoulder Press
description: The standing dumbbell shoulder press is a compound upper-body exercise that targets the shoulders while also engaging the core for stability. Performed standing, it allows for natural shoulder movement and can be loaded progressively for muscle and strength gains.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at shoulder height. Engage your core and keep your back straight. Press the dumbbells straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Single-arm Lateral Raise
description: The single-arm lateral raise is a shoulder isolation exercise that targets the middle head of the deltoid. Performed standing while holding a dumbbell in one hand, you raise the arm out to the side until it reaches shoulder height, then lower under control. It can be done holding onto a stable object for balance or free-standing to increase core stability demands, typically for moderate to high reps per side.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in one hand. Hold onto a stable object for balance if needed, or stand free for increased core challenge. Raise the dumbbell out to the side until your arm reaches shoulder height, keeping your elbow slightly bent. Lower the dumbbell back down to the starting position under control, repeating on the other side.

name: Power Partials
description: A shoulder exercise using partial range of motion to keep constant tension on the deltoids, typically performed standing with dumbbells raised through the strongest portion of the lift.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at shoulder height. Raise the dumbbells a few inches, focusing on the top portion of the lift. Lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement. Keep constant tension on the deltoids by avoiding full extension or lockout.

name: Incline Dumbbell Reverse Fly
description: The incline dumbbell reverse fly is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the rear shoulders and upper back. Performed lying chest-down on an incline bench, you raise light dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc to emphasize the posterior deltoids and postural muscles. It is typically done with lighter weights for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Lie chest-down on an incline bench with feet flat on floor. Hold light dumbbells with arms extended and palms facing each other. Raise dumbbells out to sides in a wide arc, squeezing rear shoulders and upper back. Lower dumbbells back to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Overhead Dumbbell Front Raise
description: The overhead dumbbell front raise is a shoulder exercise where you lift dumbbells from your thighs up to directly overhead, emphasizing the anterior deltoids while also engaging the traps and upper back. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions and can be used as a warm-up, primary shoulder builder, or burnout finisher on shoulder-focused days.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at thigh level. Lift the dumbbells straight up over your head, keeping arms straight and core engaged. Raise the dumbbells to a position directly overhead, squeezing your shoulder muscles at the top. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press
description: The seated dumbbell shoulder press is a strength exercise targeting the shoulders, with significant assistance from the triceps. Performed seated with dumbbells pressed overhead, it allows a more natural arm path than a barbell and can feel more shoulder-friendly. It can serve as a primary upper-body pressing movement or as an accessory lift later in a workout.
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support, holding dumbbells at shoulder height. Press the dumbbells straight up over your head, extending your arms fully. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, keeping control throughout the movement. Keep your core engaged and avoid arching your back throughout the exercise.

name: Alternating Standing Shoulder Press
description: The alternating standing shoulder press is a dumbbell exercise performed from a standing position, pressing one arm overhead at a time. It primarily targets the shoulders and can be used with heavier loads for strength or higher reps for muscle-building and conditioning.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height. Press one dumbbell straight up over your shoulder, extending your arm fully. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position and repeat with the other arm, alternating arms with each rep. Keep your core engaged and maintain good posture throughout the exercise.

name: Single-arm Incline Rear Delt Raise
description: The single-arm incline rear delt raise is an upper-body isolation exercise that targets the posterior deltoids and upper back postural muscles. Performed lying chest-down on an incline bench and raising a dumbbell out to the side with a slight elbow bend, it helps improve shoulder stability, posture, and muscular balance between sides. It is typically done with light weight for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Lie chest-down on an incline bench with your feet flat on the floor. Hold a dumbbell in one hand with a slight elbow bend. Raise the dumbbell out to the side until your arm is at a 45-degree angle to your body, squeezing your rear deltoid. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position and repeat with the other arm.

name: Alternating Dumbbell Front Raise
description: The alternating dumbbell front raise is a single-joint shoulder exercise that targets the anterior (front) deltoids by lifting one dumbbell at a time in front of the body. Alternating sides allows brief rest between reps, often enabling higher total volume and better focus on form and control.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Lift one dumbbell forward and upward to shoulder height, keeping your arm straight. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position and repeat with the other arm, alternating sides with each rep. Keep your core engaged and maintain control throughout the exercise.

name: Alternating Deltoid Raise
description: The dumbbell front raise to lateral raise combines two movements to strengthen the anterior and middle deltoids of the shoulders.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at your sides. Raise one dumbbell to the front until it's at shoulder height, then lower it back down. Immediately raise the same dumbbell out to the side until it's at shoulder height, then lower it back down. Alternate arms with each repetition, keeping control throughout the entire movement.

name: Single-Dumbbell Front Raise
description: The single-dumbbell front raise is an isolation exercise that targets the anterior (front) head of the shoulders. Performed one arm at a time, it helps address strength and size imbalances between sides and is typically done for moderate to high reps (8–12+) in shoulder or upper-body workouts.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold a dumbbell in one hand with arm at side and palm facing thighs. Raise the dumbbell straight out in front of you to shoulder height, keeping arm straight. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position and repeat with the other arm.

name: Arnold Press
description: The Arnold press is a dumbbell shoulder press variation that adds forearm rotation throughout the movement. Starting with the palms facing toward the body at shoulder height, the lifter rotates the hands outward while pressing overhead, targeting all three heads of the deltoids. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions, such as 8–12 or more, within upper-body or shoulder-focused workouts.
instructions: Hold dumbbells at shoulder height with palms facing your body. Press the dumbbells straight up while rotating your hands outward, so your palms face forward at the top. Keep your upper arms still and only move your forearms and hands during the rotation. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, reversing the rotation to complete the exercise.

name: Single-arm Standing Shoulder Press
description: The single-arm standing shoulder press is a strength exercise that targets the shoulders and triceps by pressing one dumbbell overhead at a time from a standing position. It allows many lifters to handle more weight per arm than with a two-dumbbell press, making it effective for both strength and muscle-building when performed for moderate to higher reps.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in one hand at shoulder height. Press the dumbbell straight up over your shoulder, extending your arm fully. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout. Switch arms to complete the exercise on both sides.

name: Dumbbell Front Raise
description: The dumbbell front raise is a single-joint shoulder exercise that primarily targets the anterior (front) deltoids. Performed standing or seated, you lift dumbbells from your thighs to shoulder height with straight or slightly bent arms, focusing on controlled movement and avoiding swinging. It is typically done for moderate to high repetitions (8–12+ per set) in upper-body or shoulder-focused workouts.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at thighs with palms facing thighs. Lift dumbbells straight out to shoulder height with straight or slightly bent arms. Avoid swinging and focus on controlled movement, squeezing front shoulder muscles at the top. Lower dumbbells back to starting position, keeping control throughout the entire range of motion.

name: Bent-over Dumbbell Rear Delt Row
description: A bent-over rowing variation performed with dumbbells to target the rear (posterior) and side (medial) deltoids. With a hip hinge and flat back, the lifter pulls the dumbbells out and slightly back in line with the rear shoulders, emphasizing the backside of the shoulder for more balanced development compared to pressing-focused routines.
instructions: Hold dumbbells with palms facing each other. Bend at hips with flat back, keeping knees slightly bent. Pull dumbbells out and back, targeting rear shoulders, then lower to starting position with control. Keep core engaged and maintain posture throughout the exercise.
accessibilityRequirements: Not Suitable for Lower Back Issues

name: Incline Dumbbell Front Raise
description: The incline dumbbell front raise is an upper-body strength exercise targeting the shoulders. Performed face down on an angled bench, it allows a greater range of motion than standing or upright-seated raises and hits the shoulders from a different angle. It is typically done for moderate to high reps, such as 8-12 reps per set, as part of upper-body or shoulder-focused training.
instructions: Lie face down on an incline bench with dumbbells in each hand. Hold the dumbbells with palms facing your thighs. Raise the dumbbells forward and upward, keeping arms straight, until they are at shoulder height. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Side Lateral Raise
description: The side lateral raise is an isolation strength exercise targeting the lateral head of the shoulders using dumbbells. Performed standing or seated, you lift the weights out to your sides until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor, helping build wider, more defined shoulders and improving shoulder stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at your sides. Lift the dumbbells out to your sides, keeping arms straight, until they are at shoulder height and parallel to the floor. Avoid swinging or jerking the weights, using controlled movement instead. Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position, squeezing your shoulder muscles as you lift and lower.

name: Dumbbell External Shoulder Rotation
description: A lightweight dumbbell exercise targeting the rotator cuff and shoulder stabilizers, commonly used as a warm-up or prehab movement before shoulder or chest pressing workouts.
instructions: Hold a lightweight dumbbell in each hand with arms at sides and palms facing thighs. Bend elbows to 90 degrees, keeping upper arms still and only rotating forearms. Rotate dumbbells outward, keeping control throughout the movement, until palms face forward. Slowly return to the starting position, squeezing shoulder blades together.

name: Single-arm incline lateral raise
description: The single-arm incline lateral raise is an isolation shoulder exercise performed lying sideways on a 45-degree incline bench. Using a light dumbbell, you raise the working arm out to the side to target the lateral head of the shoulders through an extended range of motion with strict form, typically for moderate to high repetitions.
instructions: Lie sideways on a 45-degree incline bench with your feet flat on the floor. Hold a light dumbbell in one hand with your arm at your side. Raise the dumbbell out to the side until your arm is at shoulder level, keeping your elbow straight. Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position, controlling the movement throughout the exercise.

name: Seated Rear Delt Fly
description: The seated rear delt fly is an upper-body strength exercise that targets the rear deltoids of the shoulders. Sitting reduces body momentum, helping isolate the shoulder muscles. It is typically performed for moderate to high repetitions (8–12+ reps) in upper-body or shoulder-focused workouts.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench with feet on floor. Hold dumbbells with arms extended and palms facing each other. Lift dumbbells out to sides until at shoulder level, squeezing rear deltoids. Lower dumbbells back to starting position, controlling the movement throughout.

name: Dumbbell Raise
description: A shoulder isolation exercise where you raise dumbbells away from your body to strengthen and build the deltoid muscles. Typically performed standing with a slight bend in the elbows, lifting the weights to about shoulder height under control.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at your sides. Keep a slight bend in your elbows and lift the dumbbells away from your body. Raise the dumbbells to about shoulder height, keeping control throughout the movement.
